Channel 6
Channel 11
2,412 MHz
2,437 MHz
2,462 MHz
Wireless LANs
IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi™)
Wireless ASC
Networks
IEEE 802.15.4
(ZigBee™)
Channel
15
20
25 26
2,400
2,425
2,450
2,475
2,480
MHz
MHz
MHz
MHz
MHz
Clear Channel
Clear Channel
Clear Channel
Clear Channel
the same power source for the actual mechanical or electri-
cal system being controlled, such as a terminal box with a
fan, lighting panel, exhaust fan or heating coils. Therefore, a
dedicated power trunk did not have to be pulled to also feed
the ASC and radio to realize the full benefits of wireless.
The beauty of mesh is that it no longer requires installers to
ensure that any two specific ASCs and their associated radios
in a given network are within range of one another, or that no
obstructions exist between them now or in the future. In many
controller network applications, where engineers can review a
design drawing and verify that the project’s characteristics are
conducive to a mesh network (see sidebar, Checklist: Is Mesh
Technology a Good Fit?), the installation becomes as simple
as plug-and-play. This is the case with our three examples,
each of which represents a sensible, flexible and cost-effective
adaptation of a wireless mesh ASC network into a building
automation system.
Assembly Plant Avoids Disruption
When the 400,000 ft2 (37 000 m2) Volvo loader assembly
plant (Photograph 2) near Asheville, N.C., was built in 1977,
there wasn’t the overriding concern for HVAC control effi-
ciency that exists in today’s facility designs. Approximately
four years ago, the decision was made to improve the safety,
working environment, and energy efficiency of the plant
by adding major improvements to the HVAC and lighting
systems using the most current technologies. However, until
these stand-alone systems could be networked and operated
remotely from a central control point, the improvements and
efficiencies that could be achieved was limited.
Some concerns included the potential safety issues associ-
ated with a major, traditional hardwiring project, as well as
the interruptions the work could cause to the assembly lines.
A hardwired network would have required running wire and
conduit over long distances (in many cases, more than 100 ft
[30 m] between controllers) throughout the 30 ft (9 m) high
ceilings in most of the facility. Wireless mesh became the
technology enabler for solving this problem.
The challenge was to network and control from a central
location all of the existing equipment that required time-
consuming, manual on/off operation. Previously, the facility
management staff would manually switch off each unit in
certain sections of the four-building complex after each shift.
This equipment included unit heaters, rooftop air-conditioning
Checklist: Is Mesh Technology a Good Fit?
By evaluating certain project characteristics, engineers
can make a rough determination whether or not a wireless
mesh network is a good fit for their application. Some of
the key questions include:
• Will the wireless devices be located in a grid-like ar-
rangement or in a long, narrow configuration, e.g., a
commercial office vs. an airport terminal? (A grid-like
arrangement is more conducive to mesh topology.)
• Will the wireless devices be located in a dense con-
figuration or are RF obstructions between them are
such that two or three other wireless devices are
within communications range of each wireless device?
Generally, the denser the configuration and fewer RF
obstructions between devices, the better.
• Are other wireless devices used in the facility? If so,
what frequencies are used? You may want to consult
with the IT department to determine if a frequency
channel management plan is in effect.
Photo 1 (left): ASC radio mounted on underside of terminal box. Figure 2 (right): 2.4 GHz frequency band: frequency channels used
by 802.11 and 802.15.4 networks.
38
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
October 2006
units and office split systems. (Primary lighting circuits were
operated by a time clock.)
A total of 86 controllers and their associated radios were
installed in the ceiling of the four buildings for fan control
and gas heat control of the gas-fired unit heaters. Additionally,
another six controllers and associated radios were installed
in each electrical penthouse to operate the existing lighting
circuits. Finally, for each wireless network, a corresponding
radio was mounted at one of the supervisory building con-
trollers. Four building controllers and their associated radios
required for this project are located on the plant floor, while
a fifth is located in an equipment room off of the plant floor.
The wireless networking allows each controlled component
to be accessible from multiple locations as determined by the
facility staff. The staff can monitor and control the space tem-
perature at each unit heater from any BAS workstation. They
also can provide floor personnel with local control, prevent
local control, or give limited local control at each area.
The benefits include significant reductions in energy use and
operating costs, lower project costs to complete the needed
retrofit and minimal, if any, disruptions to production lines.
The total time needed to complete the wireless retrofit—six
to seven weeks—was considerably shorter than it would have
been with a hardwired network installation. It was consider-
ably safer, as well.
School Upgraded on a Budget
Last year, South Carolina’s Horry County school district
embarked on a four-year program to renovate its 46 schools.
The district began the program at its Conway Education
Center (Photograph 3), a 45,000 ft2 (4180 m2), single-story
elementary school built in the 1950s and expanded over several
decades. The 25-classroom school is a typical K–12 facility
with long wings, cinderblock walls, and suspended ceilings
hanging approximately 4 ft (1.2 m) below the roof deck. Some
of the block walls are filled with concrete to act as firewalls.
The aging BAS, as well as the existing network cabling infra-
structure, all needed to be replaced or upgraded.
HVAC control was either nonexistent or a time-consuming
chore. The energy management staff couldn’t control the tem-
perature or perform scheduling, overriding or remote monitor-
ing. If teachers worked late, the staff didn’t have any way of
adjusting the temperature to accommodate them.
The only control means was through a central panel in the
electrical room. If a teacher turned on one override for a zone,
12 or 15 heat pumps might be turned on at the same time.
If someone on the energy management staff wanted to see
temperatures and humidity levels, he would have to drive to
the school and go into the individual classrooms.
Another problem was that the county’s infrastructure im-
provement budget did not support the construction cost of
cutting through walls to run new cable and busway to the
various sections of the school.
The goal was to move from an electromechanical control
system to a direct digital control system that managed the
heating, air conditioning, lighting, and energy use of the entire
building. Challenges included finishing the installation and
deploying the new BAS in only three weeks (to work around
the summer school schedule) while minimizing the impact
to the existing structure. That’s when it was decided that a
wireless ASC network was the only solution.
Even with such a compressed work schedule, the school
district was able to meet its deadline by completing the instal-
lation in only one week and within its limited budget.
Challenges still existed. For example, post-installation
checkout of the new wireless mesh system revealed two ra-
dios located at the end of the wing had not established robust
communications with the rest of the network. As a result, a
repeater radio was installed in the hallway between the two,
which corrected the problem immediately.
The school was able to complete the upgrade to its BAS in
a shorter period of time compared to a hardwired network,
Photo 2 (left): Volvo loader assembly plant near Asheville, N.C. Photo 3 (right): Conway Education Center in Conway, S.C.
40
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
October 2006
the number of penetrations to walls and ceilings was reduced
significantly, and the school realizes the cost savings from
more efficient control. Engineers can perform remote moni-
toring of day and night cooling setpoints, night heating data,
and many other points via room controllers. Moreover, with
the wireless system, it’s possible to add a room controller,
move a classroom or add a water heater without having to run
a cable. It’s a simple matter of installing the wireless device
exactly where it’s needed.
Engineering Firm Showcases Its Own Building
The third wireless mesh controller example involves DEC
Engineers, a consulting me-
chanical engineering firm
that owns and operates in a
17,800 ft2 (1650 m2) building
in San Diego (Photograph
4). In the winter of 2006,
the firm totally retrofitted
the building’s mechanical
systems to modernize and
suit its specific needs. The
firm’s goal was to remain in
the forefront of mechanical
and HVAC design by setting
an example with its own
building.
The mechanical systems
for the building are not those you would see in a normal
smaller office building. For example, the facility has a chilled
water plant, custom double-wall air-handler unit (AHU) with
HEPA filters and CO2 monitoring for demand-controlled
ventilation. This is all part of the firm’s plan to practice what
it specifies for its clients in its own facility.
The main AHU is controlled by an Ethernet-enabled super-
visory building controller mounted in the building’s mechani-
cal room. A radio run from this controller is mounted in the
ceiling to support the ASC network
A central trunk extends down from the building’s AHU
exposing ductwork, variable air volume (VAV) boxes and
linear diffusers. The controllers’ transmitters are clearly vis-
ible from ground and mezzanine levels. The building was
designed specifically so that customers could see the wire-
less mesh controller network with its transmitters in place
and working.
The wireless mesh network has operated nearly problem-
free for the last eight months. Although all of the other wireless
systems and devices are close to the wireless controllers, no
interference problems have occurred.
Conclusion
In each case study, site surveys were not required due to the
relatively RF-friendly layout and environment each presented.
A review of the floor layout, location and general layout of
the radios, and review of the thickness and types of materials
used in the construction, were the only initial assessments
required to confirm that a robust mesh network would be
formed with little or no additional effort. In two of the three
case studies (plant and commercial office), the number of
obstructions was minimal, and radios were mounted gener-
ally in wide-open spaces. Once the radios were installed and
powered up, they automatically established a robust mesh
network. For the school, only one additional repeater radio
was required even though significant amounts of concrete and
steel obstructions were present. Checkout of the networks
confirmed that each radio was communicating with multiple
neighbors, creating a highly
reliable mesh network.
In all three case studies,
various RF systems and de-
vices were present, e.g., wire-
less LANs, wireless security
systems, wireless inventory
control systems, microwaves,
and cell phones. However,
none of the cases experienced
any issues with interference.
It’s important to point out
that the environment may not
always be as RF-friendly as
these case studies. Where it
is not obvious that the tech-
nology would be a good fit, and if the facility already exists,
an informal site survey could be performed to determine if
wireless is a viable alternative. Such a survey might con-
sist of simply powering up a few radios and locating them
throughout the facility in a manner that approximates their
positions to support a mesh controller network. Then, during
final deployment, if the system cannot establish an adequate
number of communication links, additional repeater radios
can be installed into “holes” in the mesh, or existing radios
and/or antennas can be repositioned as needed.
As HVAC engineers gain experience in deploying wireless
mesh networks, they will become more adept in identifying
facilities and applications where the technology is a good fit.
Also, as more adaptable wireless devices are developed, such
as battery-powered wireless room temperature sensors that
can communicate with their associated ASC via the existing
wireless ASC mesh network, the technology will become more
attractive. As a result, mesh technology’s acceptance and use
will continue to increase.
References
1. Raimo, J. 2005. “Wireless mesh networks using HVAC
controllers.” ASHRAE Journal 47(7):32–36.
2. Hubler, T. 2005. “Worry-free wireless networks.” Net-
worked Controls, Heating/Piping/Air-Conditioning Engineer-
ing Magazine October.
Photo 4: DEC Engineers in San Diego.
50
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
October 2006
Smoke Management
In the 2006 IBC
By Jeffrey S. Tubbs, P.E.
F
or some building spaces, smoke management is an important part of the overall fire and
life-safety strategy. Smoke management requirements within the International Building
Code (IBC)1,2 continue to evolve, and some dramatic changes were made in the 2006 version.
The most recent updates allow greater design flexibility for atrium spaces. This flexibility
requires a more comprehensive approach to the design of the smoke management system.
A majority of the revised provisions affect the exhaust method for atrium smoke manage-
ment. However, smoke management within underground buildings and provisions related to
the location of fire command centers also have been updated.
This article will review smoke management requirements
within the International Building Code, and discuss the revised
provisions related to fire command centers, smoke management
in underground buildings and atrium spaces.
Where is Smoke Management Required?
The 2006 edition of the IBC requires smoke management
systems within atrium spaces, underground buildings, hospi-
tals and detention facilities, windowless detention facilities,
warehouse buildings, legitimate stages, high-rise stairs, and,
where necessary, for smoke-protected assembly seating (IBC
Section 1025.6.2.1).
• Atrium Spaces. Smoke management is required within
atrium spaces connecting more than two stories per IBC
Section 404.4.
• Underground Buildings. Underground buildings require
smoke management and smoke-proof enclosures, depend-
© 2006, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal Vol. 48, Oct. 2006. For personal use
only. Additional reproduction, distribution, or transmission in either print or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s prior written permission.
October 2006
ASHRAE Journal
51
About the Author
Jeffrey S. Tubbs, P.E., is a an associate principal with Arup and is the staff
group leader of the Westborough, Mass., office.
ing upon the below-grade depth of the floor levels (IBC
Sections 405.4, 405.5, and 405.8.2).
• Hospitals and Detention Facilities. IBC Sections 407.4
and 408.6 require passive smoke barriers in hospitals and
similar facilities (I-2 Occupancies), and in detention facili-
ties (I-3 Occupancies).
• Windowless Detention Facilities. Windowless detention
and correctional facilities, reformatories, prisons and jails
require an engineered natural or mechanical smoke man-
agement system (Section 408.8).
• Warehouse Buildings. Smoke and heat venting is required
by IBC Section 910 for one-story Group F-1 and S-1 ware-
houses with compartments larger than 50,000 ft2 (4645 m2)
and for buildings with high-piled combustible storage.
• Legitimate Stages. Certain stages are required to be pro-
vided with a proscenium curtain to separate the stage from
the crowd, as well as with stage ventilation. Ventilation can
be accomplished through automatically opening vents, or
through a smoke management system in accordance with
IBC Section 909.
• High-Rise Buildings. IBC Section 403.13 requires smoke-
proof exit enclosures to help maintain tenable egress paths
in high-rise buildings. Sections 909.20 and 1020.1.7 in-
clude design requirements for smoke-proof enclosures.
• Smoke-Protected Assembly Seating. In some large
assembly facilities with seating, all of the occupants
may not be in an immediate danger from fire or other
incidents. IBC Section 1025.6.2.1, Smoke-Protected
Seating, recognizes this and allows reduction in egress
widths when substantiated through a life-safety evalua-
tion conducted in accordance with the NFPA 101®: Life
Safety Code.® 3
Fire Command Rooms and Smoke Management
In the earlier editions of the International Building Code,
a firefighter’s smoke control panel is required to be located
within a fire command room. This effectively mandates fire
command rooms for all buildings with smoke management.
The 2006 edition clarifies this provision. Smoke control pan-
els are required to be located within a fire command room
for high-rise buildings and buildings with smoke-protected
seating (IBC Section 909.16). In other buildings, the panel is
required to be located in an approved location adjacent to the
fire alarm control panel.
The code change was justified as follows: buildings with
atrium smoke exhaust systems do not require the full range
of fire and life-safety systems required within a high-rise fire
command room. Since these other systems are not required,
the space required by a dedicated fire command room is not
necessary.
Underground Building Smoke Management Design
The 2003 edition of the IBC4 includes provision for un-
derground buildings with and without compartments. For
compartmented buildings with floors 30 ft (9 m) below the
level of exit discharge, a smoke exhaust system is required.
For buildings without compartments with floors 30 ft (9 m)
below the level of exit discharge, a smoke control system is
required (IBC Section 405.5). Underground buildings larger
than 1,500 ft2 (140 m2) with floor levels 60 ft (18 m) below
the level of exit discharge require a minimum of two smoke
compartments. Smoke exhaust and smoke control are defined
as follows.
• Smoke Exhaust. Smoke exhaust is defined within IBC
Section 909.21 and is used for buildings with compart-
ments. Smoke exhaust requires six air changes per hour
through independent exhaust systems serving each com-
partment. Although this provides a means to clear smoke
after an event, it may or may not meet the expected level
of life safety provided by other portions of Section 909.
Additionally, Section 909.21 is the only portion of the
section to address smoke exhaust systems.
• Smoke Control (IBC 2003 edition). Active smoke
control includes any of the smoke management methods
allowed by Section 909: pressurization method, exhaust
method, and airflow method. For spaces without re-
quirements for compartments, any of the active smoke
control methods would be allowed. These provisions
also include a comprehensive set of design and testing
requirements.
The reference from Section 405.5 to Section 909.21 causes
confusion. IBC Section 405.5 requires a smoke exhaust sys-
tem. Section 909.21 is the only portion of Section 909 to ad-
dress smoke exhaust systems. Thus, it is unclear if all portions
of Section 909 applied to these systems. Further, the exhaust
rate followed the outdated six air changes per hour criteria,
which has based exhaust rates upon building size rather than
expected fire conditions.
To address these concerns, the 2006 edition of the IBC deleted
Section 909.21. Now, smoke management systems for under-
ground buildings are required to be designed in accordance
with all of the requirements of Section 909. This allows flex-
ibility with the design, but also requires the level of robustness
required for other smoke management systems, such as special
inspection to review the installation.
Exhaust Method
In many cases, smoke management using the exhaust method
is necessary to allow safe egress from the upper levels of large
open spaces such as atria and multilevel malls. The 2006 edi-
tion of the IBC includes a number of changes relating to the
exhaust method.
Design Fire. Within the earlier editions of the IBC, exhaust
systems were required to be based upon a design fire size of
5,000 Btu/s, unless an engineering analysis was conducted and
concluded that a smaller fire was appropriate. The 5,000 Btu/s
52
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
October 2006
(88 kW) design fire was justified by a statistical analysis of
sprinkler-protected retail shops and an assumed 50 Btu/s/ft2
(9.5 kW/m2) average fuel load over a 10 ft by 10 ft (3 m by
3 m) area.5 Atrium spaces and other similar spaces have a
range of uses and possible fuel loads. In spaces with low ceil-
ing heights protected with sprinklers, a 5,000 Btu/s (88 kW)
design fire may be larger than necessary, as sprinklers would be
expected to control the fire growth. Conversely, in tall spaces,
a design fire larger than 5,000 Btu/s might be expected. Thus,
in the earlier version of the IBC, the prescribed fire may or
may not have been conservative. To address this, the 2006
edition of the IBC requires design fires to be determined
as part of an engineering analysis and to be developed by a
registered professional.
Calculating Exhaust Smoke Layer Height. In the 2000
and 2003 editions of the IBC, exhaust systems are required
to maintain a calculated smoke layer height 10 ft (3 m) above
egress walkways.4,6 In some buildings, ceiling heights are less
than 10 ft (3 m). In these cases, the 10 ft (3 m) requirement
cannot be applied. NFPA 92B: Standard for Smoke Manage-
ment Systems in Malls, Atria and Large Spaces and other design
guides allow a calculated height of 6 ft (1.8 m). 2006 IBC has
changed the calculated height to 6 ft (1.8 m).
Adoption of NFPA 92B. Before the 2005 Edition, NFPA
92B: Guide for Smoke Management Systems in Malls, Atria
and Large Spaces,7 was developed as a set of recommended
practices. However, it was written in non-mandatory language,
and thus could not be adopted by codes. This resulted in detailed
design criteria being included within the codes. NFPA 92B was
revised to a standard in the 2005 edition.8 The 2006 edition of
the IBC now adopts the NFPA 92B standard and has removed
the detail design criteria. The new approach allows more design
flexibility, with a corresponding wider range of approaches.
Figure 1 compares the 2003 with the 2006 design process.
• Mechanical exhaust to maintain a layer height. This
was the approach required by the 2003 edition of the IBC,
except the minimum calculated smoke layer height is now
6 ft (1.8 m) above the walking surfaces.
• Gravity smoke venting to maintain a layer height. De-
sign guides have included provisions to use natural gravity
venting to vent smoke. This is now allowed within the
IBC framework. The smoke venting systems is required
to maintain the smoke layer 6 ft (1.8 m) above the walking
surfaces with this approach.
• Passive smoke reservoir filling. Buoyancy causes smoke
to collect and fill the upper portions of the space. In large
spaces, smoke may not fill down to a point that would af-
fect egress paths during the expected egress times. This
approach takes advantage of this and allows the elimination
of smoke management if the smoke reservoir is sufficiently
large to allow occupants to exit before the smoke layer
descends to 6 ft (1.8 m) above the egress paths.
• Mechanical exhaust or gravity vents to slow the layer
descent. In some cases, a lower exhaust quantity or smaller
area of gravity vents can be provided to slow the descent of
the smoke layer to allow safe egress. As with the passive
filling method, this requires keeping the smoke layer to 6
ft (1.8 m) above the egress paths during the time required
for evacuation.
When designing to the NFPA 92B, similar algebraic cal-
culation methods to those within the 2003 edition of the IBC
are available. Those familiar with smoke management provi-
sions within previous editions of the IBC will recognize the
familiar axisymmetric, balcony spill and window spill plume
equations. This standard also provides clarification as to
when these equations apply. It is also of note that NFPA 92B
makes it clear that designers need to review smoke filling to
illustrate that design conditions are not exceeded before the
exhaust fans are fully operational and up to full capacity. Note
that an ASHRAE research project (TRP-1247, Balcony Spill
Plumes) is underway to investigate the validity of the balcony
spill plume equations.
Both the IBC and NFPA 92B include requirements for
testing and commissioning smoke management systems. The
testing requirements from the IBC will govern.
Compartment fire models, CFD analysis, and scale-model
approaches are specifically allowed by the NFPA 92B standard.
Users are cautioned that IBC Section 909.8.1 requires that the
“lowest horizontal surface of the accumulated smoke layer” be
maintained 6 ft (1.8 m) above egress walking surface. Therefore,
while CFD analysis techniques are used, these methods need
to illustrate that the layer is maintained and that people do not
need to walk through smoke. It is likely that solutions requiring
Figure 1: IBC design process for exhaust systems: 2003 vs. 2006. Taken
with permission from the June 2006 issue of Building Safety Journal and is copyright
2006 by the International Code Council.
54
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
October 2006
occupants to evacuate through smoke will
need to follow an “Alternate Means and
Methods” approach.
Another change that occurs in the ref-
erence to NFPA 92B is the requirement
for the review of the number and size of
exhaust vents needed to prevent plughol-
ing, which occurs when the exhaust vents
have velocities such that cool lower air
is exhausted with the hot upper layer air.
This may short-circuit the design and may
make the design less efficient.
This discussion represents a brief
overview of NFPA 92B. Refer to NFPA
92B for additional information and
guidance.
Supply Makeup Air Velocity. The pre-
vious editions of the IBC limited makeup
air to 200 fpm (1 m/s). This is eliminated
within the 2004 supplement to the IBC.
However, NFPA 92B mandates the supply
air be limited to 200 fpm (1 m/s) unless a
“higher makeup air velocity is supported
by” an engineering analysis.
An ASHRAE research project (TRP-
1300, Maximum Velocity of Makeup Air
for Smoke Management Systems in Atria
and Other Large Spaces) is underway
to investigate the maximum velocity of
makeup air in atrium smoke control ap-
plications. This research should provide
recommendations on this topic.
Integrating Smoke Management
with Evacuation Times. In previous
editions of the IBC, smoke management
systems were required to be operational
for at least 20 minutes after detection.
Section 909.4.6 of the 2006 edition of
the IBC modified this requirement to 1.5
times the evacuation time or 20 minutes,
whichever is less.
Summary
In general, new requirements found
within the 2006 edition of the IBC
allow greater design flexibility. Since
Figure 2 (at left): Natural ventilation through operable vents justified through smoke movement analysis. Figure 3 (at right): Passive system
justified through smoke filling and evacuation analyses.
NFPA 92B is now referenced, design-
ers can choose among several methods
for atria and similar large open spaces.
This flexibility puts more responsibil-
ity on the designer, as the designer
will need to select the appropriate
concepts and be responsible for de-
veloping the design fire. Additionally,
designers need to review smoke filling
to be sure that the design conditions
are not exceeded before the fans are
fully operational.
References
1. ICC. 2006. International Building Code,
2006 ed. Falls Church, Va.: International
Codes Council.
2. ICC. 2006, “Smoke Management in the
2006 IBC.” Building Safety Journal. Falls
Church, Va.: International Codes Council.
3. NFPA 2006. NFPA 101: Life Safety
Code.® Quincy, Mass.: National Fire Protec-
tion Association.
4. ICC. 2003. International Building Code,
2003 ed. Falls Church, Va.: International
Codes Council.
5. Morgan and Chandler. 1981. “Fire sizes
and sprinkler effectiveness in shopping com-
plexes and retail premises.” Fire Surveyor.
10:23–28.
6. ICC. 2000. International Building Code,
2000 ed. Falls Church, Va.: International
Codes Council.
7. NFPA 2000. NFPA 92B: Guide for
Smoke Management Systems in Malls, Atria
and Large Spaces. Quincy, Mass.: National
Fire Protection Association.
8. NFPA 2005. NFPA 92B: Standard for
Smoke Management Systems in Malls, Atria
and Large Spaces, Quincy, Mass.: National

68
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
October 2006
n aging population that depends more and more on air
conditioning and medical equipment, increased use
of electronics requiring uninterrupted power, and in-
creased concerns about the reliability of grid-supplied power
have all contributed to a growing demand for home emergency
power systems. Grid transmission congestion led to a five-fold
increase in electricity purchasing transactions that could not
be completed between 1998 and 2002.1 Also, electric grid
capacity margins have decreased significantly over the past
twenty years, increasing the risk of an outage. These margins
are expected to decrease further over the next decade, from
around 20% to 10%.2
Several types of residential emergency power systems exist,
including:
• Energy storage systems;
• Portable power generators;
• Standby generators; and
• Residential combined heat and power systems (Micro-
CHP).
Table 1 summarizes some key characteristics of the four
emergency backup systems discussed.
Residential energy storage systems typically use batteries;
although other energy storage methods, such as flywheels, are
in development (commercial systems exist). They are relatively
simple, but can operate only for limited durations without re-
charging. Usually, they are only used for short-term supply of
small critical loads, such as computers. Whole-house systems
are available, but even these larger systems are not usually ex-
pected to power large loads, such as air conditioning, electric
cooking, and electric heat.
Most portable power generators use low-cost gasoline- or
diesel-fueled internal-combustion (IC) engines. They are rela-
tively inexpensive, but can be difficult to set up, need liquid fuel
storage, require regular testing and maintenance for reliability,
and can only serve plug loads unless hard-wired by an electri-
cian. Because they typically use liquid fuel, they require periodic
refueling during an outage to continue providing power, which
may not always be feasible. Although some people attempt to
By Robert Zogg, P.E., Member ASHRAE; Tyson Lawrence; Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; and
James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
Residential Emergency
Power Systems
A
use them for emergency power, e.g., to power a few plug-in ap-
pliances during a power outage, they perhaps are best suited for
non-emergency, intermittent, grid-independent power.
Standby generators are turnkey power systems that also
generally use IC engines, but the similarities to portable power
systems end there. They are permanently mounted, usually
outdoors, and tend to be larger than portable generators, both
physically and in capacity. Most units use natural gas or propane
fuel, and start up automatically when grid power fails. They also
automatically start periodically to ensure proper functioning.
Unlike portable and standby power generators, Micro-CHP
systems provide everyday energy savings by capturing heat
rejected from a generator to meet space- or water-heating loads.
When designed properly, they also can provide power during
a grid outage.3
Sizing Considerations
Current residential emergency power systems have a broad
range of capacities, from 1 kWe to 40 kWe. Small battery unin-
terruptible power supplies systems can provide as little as 100
W for an hour, but true home emergency power battery systems
range from 3.5 kW to 11 kW and provide power to a limited set
of appliances for periods ranging from a few hours to as long as
a couple of days. Typically, portable power systems have genera-
tion capacities of 5 kWe or less, while the bulk of the market for
standby generators ranges from 5 kWe to 12 kWe.
Generators can be wired to cover only critical household
circuits, or they can provide whole-house backup power. Manu-
facturers usually provide sizing guidelines that indicate what
household loads can be serviced by a given generator capacity.
They generally assume that the generator capacity must be
sufficient to simultaneously meet the rated power levels of all
connected loads.
Analyses show, however, that total average demand for a typi-
cal U.S. household equals only about 1 kWe. Higher energy use
typically occurs in the evening and when large loads (e.g., air
conditioning or electric cooking) are used. This allows a 1 kWe
or 2 kWe system to meet the critical loads in most homes.

cluding air conditioning.
For example, the average hourly power draw profile for a
3,000 ft2 (279 m2) home is usually 1 kW or less and rarely
exceeds 2kW (Figure 1). Consequently, larger generators op-
erate at a small fraction of their rated capacity at most times,
leading to significant part-load efficiency penalties.
Using an intelligent load-management system and a small
energy-storage system, it is possible to design a backup power
system around the average power draw value instead of a theo-
retical peak value.4
Smaller generators have several key advantages, including:
• smaller physical size;
• lower noise (depending on technology)
• potentially lower cost; and
• improved generation efficiency.
From an energy perspective,
maintaining high generation ef-
ficiency is particularly important
for Micro-CHP systems because
they operate for many more
hours per year than dedicated
emergency power systems.
Prime Mover Selection
All of the discussed systems
except for energy storage use a
prime mover to convert fuel to
mechanical energy or directly
to electrical energy. In doing
so, the prime mover also rejects heat—either to the ambient
air or to a heat-recovery system that supplements household
space and water heating.
While most existing systems use IC engines due to their
technical maturity, alternative prime movers such as Stirling
engines, fuel cells, Rankine engines, thermophotovoltaics,
and thermoelectric systems are being developed. Each has had
reasons why it has yet to achieve widespread market penetration
for emergency power provision.
The low generation efficiencies of Rankine engines, thermo-
photovoltaics, and thermoelectric systems limit their practical
application in emergency power systems. In theory, some fuel
cells can achieve high generation efficiencies, but they will
likely be too expensive for standby use in the near future.
Stirling engines have achieved limited market penetration
due to their relative technical immaturity. Recent develop-
ments in Stirling technology indicate, however, some signifi-
cant advantages for emergency power applications. Because
Stirling engines use external combustion, their combustion
process can be more readily
controlled to limit emissions.
External combustion also al-
lows for fuel flexibility. In
principle, Stirling engines are
inherently less complicated
than IC engines and, have the
potential to be substantially
quieter.
Energy Savings Potential
On-site power systems that
operate only during grid out-
ages inherently increase energy
consumption because they serve loads that would otherwise
not be met. Distributed generation systems (systems without
heat recovery that also operate during non-emergency periods)
only save energy if their generation efficiency exceeds that for
grid power (less transmission and distribution losses).
70
ASHRAE Journal
October 2006
For residential-scale applications, only solid oxide fuel cell
systems hold promise for exceeding the efficiency of the grid,
and they have yet to enter the residential market. In addition,
energy storage systems have charge and discharge inefficiencies
that increase energy consumption.
However, with a proper controls and operating strategy, it may
be possible in some utility service areas to lower energy con-
sumption with energy storage systems by charging the system
when grid power can be efficiently generated and discharging
the system when grid power is least efficient (e.g., during peak-
demand periods).
On the other hand, Micro-CHP systems with generation
efficiencies between 10% and 25% (lower heating value) can
reduce home energy consumption and costs by approximately
10% to 15%, based on an analysis of U.S. homes using natural
gas-fired appliances.4
Market Factors
One study estimates the annual U.S. market for all residential-
scale generators is about 1 million units and growing. Of these,
approximately 200,000 are standby generators.4
Energy storage systems can cost between $7,000 and $15,000,
including installation, depending the on size and complexity of the
installation, putting them at the top of price scale. While portable
power systems typically cost around $300 to $500, they do not
provide convenient and reliable emergency power for many house-
holds. Standby generators typically have installed costs that range
from $4,000 to $6,000 for the most common capacities,4 while the
installed cost of micro-CHP systems now entering the U.S. market
is about $12,000 (not including subsidies and incentives).5
References
1. Owens, D.K. 2003. Testimony before the U.S. House Committee
on Energy and Commerce. Sept. 4.
2. “2005 Long-Term Reliability Assessment: The Reliability of
Bulk Electric Systems in North America.” North American Electric
Reliability Council (NERC). ftp://www.nerc.com/pub/sys/all\_updl/
docs/pubs/LTRA2005.pdf.
3. Zogg, R., K. Roth, and J. Brodrick. 2005. “Combined heat and
power for residences.” ASHRAE Journal, 47(7):142–143.
4. Zogg, R. 2006. “Research, development, and demonstration of
micro-CHP systems for residential applications—phase I.” Draft final
report to the U.S. Department of Energy. August.
5. Guyer, E. 2006. Presentation to the Massachusetts Institute of
Technology Energy Club. August 9.

cipals with TIAX LLC, Cambridge, Mass. Tyson Lawrence is
a technologist at TIAX. James Brodrick, Ph.D., is a project

ASHRAE Journal
45
By Stephen Lasher; Robert Zogg, P.E., Member ASHRAE; Eric Carlson; Patrick Couch; Matthew Hooks;
Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
PEM Fuel Cells
For Distributed Generation
W
hen used in distributed generation (DG) applications,
fuel cells have the potential to save energy and reduce
emissions depending on the fuel-cell technology
used,1,2 and their inherent fuel-flexibility could help address
energy shortage issues through energy diversity.3 In addition, fuel
cells have the potential to be quieter, more reliable, and have lower
maintenance costs than most technologies used for DG.
Similar to batteries, fuel cells are electrochemical devices
that convert chemical energy directly into electric power. Un-
like batteries, both the cathode and anode reactants of a fuel
cell are constantly replenished by air and fuel, respectively.
Some of the fuel cell technologies under consideration for DG
applications include solid oxide, molten carbonate, phosphoric
acid, and polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) fuel cells. This
article addresses PEM fuel cells (PEMFC). Subsequent articles
will discuss other fuel cell options.
In a PEMFC both the anode and cathode are comprised of
platinum-based electrocatalysts supported on carbon particles.
The electrolyte is a polymeric membrane that conducts protons.
PEMFCs operate at lower temperatures than other fuel cells (i.e.,
60°C to 90°C [140°F to 194°F] vs. 200°C to 1000°C [392°F
to 1,832°F] ), which limits their combined heat and power ap-
plications. PEMFCs likely will not be able to provide the high
waste heat temperatures required for absorption cooling and, in
most cases, space heating. On the other hand, their fast startup,
simplicity of operation, zero emissions (when operating on
hydrogen [H2]), and potential for low capital and maintenance
costs have attracted interest for DG applications.
How Will PEMFCs Be Fueled?
Ideally, PEMFCs can operate on a H2-rich fuel (i.e., “re-
formate”) generated from fossil fuels such as coal, natural
gas, gasoline and landfill gas, or alcohols in conjunction with
fuel processing. In practice, PEMFCs require very clean H2 to
prevent poisoning of platinum catalysts from impurities, such
as carbon monoxide (ppm levels) and sulfur compounds (ppb
levels). For this reason, ultra-pure (>99.999%) H2 is the fuel
of choice for PEMFC systems.
Investigators are evaluating several H2 production, transporta-
tion, and storage options that eventually could meet the demands
of future H2 vehicles and stationary PEMFC systems. This
includes both large-scale, central H2 production options (typi-
cally 50 to 500 tons per day [TPD] H2 output) and small-scale,
distributed production options (typically <  3 TPD H2 output).
Leveraging the benefits of large central H2 production options
for distributed PEMFC power systems will require an extensive
H2 delivery network between and within cities and towns, and
along highways. Transportation options include compressed
gaseous H2 delivery via tube trailers or pipelines, and liquid H2
or chemical hydride delivery via trucks, barges, or rail.
Alternatively, H2 generation can occur on a smaller scale
at or near the point-of-use to avoid storage and transportation
from a central plant. Potential distributed production options
include natural gas steam methane reforming and grid-based
water electrolysis.
The DOE has developed cost models to estimate the required
H2 selling price for various production options for fueling
future H2 vehicles. Models and documentation are available
on the DOE-supported H2A Web site: www.hydrogen.en-
ergy.gov/h2a\_analysis.html. As can be seen in Figure 1, H2
production costs of at least $1.75/kg can be expected, even
assuming high H2 demand. Delivery for central plant options
would likely increase the cost by at least $0.50/kg to $2.25/kg
or $17/MMBtu (HHV).
Although central coal gasification is projected to be the
lowest­-cost production option in the long-term, it will not
likely be economical for metro-area-based H2 demands of
less than about 100 TPD (enough to generate 69 MWe or fuel
200,000 H2 vehicles) due to the poor scalability of production
and large-scale delivery of H2. Therefore, coal gasification and
other central plant options are not expected to fuel stationary
PEMFC systems until significant H2 demand (e.g., from H2
vehicles or PEMFC power systems) develops.
Instead, distributed H2 production via on-site reforming
will be the most attractive nearer-term option for stationary
PEMFC systems. This process would likely use natural gas
© 2006, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal Vol. 48, Nov. 2006. For personal use
only. Additional reproduction, distribution, or transmission in either print or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s prior written permission.
46
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2006
Figure 1: Required hydrogen selling price for production only.

Hydrogen Price $/MMBtu (HHV)
as the feedstock because it is relatively easy to reform to H2
and readily available at or near most DG sites, and potential
sites, in the U.S.
Energy and Emissions Considerations
PEMFCs efficiently convert H2 to electricity (40% vs. 30%
for DG technologies using conventional fuels). On the other
hand, generating (and in some cases storing and transporting)
H2 is relatively inefficient compared to conventional fuels.
Therefore, the overall primary energy efficiency and green-
house gas (GHG) emissions from PEMFC power systems that
operate on H2 produced via distributed natural gas reforming
are not significantly better—and can be worse—than conven-
tional DG technologies or the electric power grid.4
In addition, demand for natural gas has increased signifi-
cantly and its cost has increased sharply in recent years, due
in large part to greater use for power production. Therefore,
incremental demand for natural gas will likely require ad-
ditional liquefied natural gas (LNG) imports, which have
higher emissions and energy security concerns than domestic
natural gas.
Despite the primary energy challenges, PEMFC have
potential to significantly reduce criteria pollutant emissions
compared to conventional DG power systems. PEMFC do not
produce nitric oxide (NOx) and sulfur oxide (SOx) emissions,
the latter because of the required sulfur cleanup in the fuel
processing step.
When operating on pure H2, they also do not emit hydrocar-
bons, carbon monoxide, particulate matter, or other emissions
at the point of use. These low emissions can greatly facilitate
siting relative to conventional DG power systems.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
November 2006
ASHRAE Journal
47
Cost Challenges
Fuel costs are the largest component (more than 70%) of
the overall cost of generating electricity for most combustion-
based DG applications.5 The potential for higher efficiency and
resulting lower operating costs of fuel cells are primary reasons
for their development for DG applications. TIAX analysis has
shown that fuel costs must be $10/MMBtu (HHV) or less to be
competitive with the electric power grid and achieve break-even
economics with electricity at $0.09/kWh (assuming 40% HHV
efficiency [Figure 2]).
However, generating ultra-pure H2 is costly. Cost projections
based on DOE H2A models for distributed natural gas-based
H2 production assuming H2 will probably not cost less than
$2.25/kg or $17/MMBtu (HHV) (Figure 1). Distributed H2
production costs presented here are based on the H2A models
for transportation applications that have been modified by
excluding capital and operating costs associated with storage,
compression, and dispensing to H2 vehicles.
This distributed H2 production cost translates into an equiva-
lent cost of electricity of at least $0.13/kWh based on fuel costs
alone (assuming 40% HHV efficiency). This does not include
capital, replacement, or other operating and maintenance costs
for the fuel cell power system. In the near-term, stationary
PEMFC power systems are projected to have higher capital costs
than combustion-based DG systems, adding to the overall cost
of electricity.
Promising DG Markets
Although the economic viability of PEMFC power systems
for most DG applications hinges on the development of an
extensive hydrogen infrastructure, some smaller but more
promising nearer-term stationary power applications exist.

Advertisement formerly in this space.
48
ASHRAE Journal
November 2006
Among these applications, high-value
backup power could be the most attrac-
tive. End-users in the telecom, finance,
health-care, emergency response, retail
and services industries may be willing
pay a premium for reliable backup power.
In these applications, on-site reforming or
hydrogen storage could fuel the PEMFC
power system. Although the cost of this
system will exceed that of alternatives,
the reliability and ease of siting due to
near-zero emissions, could make PEMFC
systems more attractive than conventional
backup power systems.
Another promising application is to use
a PEMFC to generate power at locations
that have low-cost or “negative value” H2-
rich fuel available. For example, certain
chemical processes result in large-scale
(5 to 10 TPD) flaring of H2-rich gas (e.g.,
chlor-alkali plants). A few demonstration
efforts are underway to capture this op-
portunity H2 for storage and resale and/or
for fueling PEMFCs to generate power.
Because the H2 is a byproduct of another
process, the economics of using the H2 in
PEMFCs improves.
A promising future application for
stationary PEMFCs is as part of a “Hy-
drogen Energy Station.” Energy stations
would be locations with distributed H2
production to provide fuel to H2 vehicles
and stationary PEMFC power systems.
This integrated design could meet the
needs of groupings of residential or
commercial customers. Benefits might
include early fuel infrastructure support
for zero emissions vehicles (ZEV) and
cleaner electricity production. Energy
Stations could result in a more sustainable
environment and a potential economic
benefit via emission trading programs.

1. Zogg, R., K. Roth, and J. Brodrick. 2005.

Calculation Tools for the
Ventilation Rate Procedure
Standard 62.1
Screenshot from the 62MZCalc spreadsheet included with the Standard 62.1-2004 User’s Manual.
A
NSI/ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2004 has made significant ad-
vancements in calculations and directives to help improve
outdoor air delivery to the occupants. These changes and additional
calculations, especially for multizone systems, have made it more
difficult for engineers to determine the required outdoor air quanti-
ties. In the past, it was easy for the engineer to apply the 5 cfm
(2.4 L/s), 10 cfm (4.8 L/s), or 15 cfm (7.1 L/s) times the number
of occupants to arrive at the recommended outdoor air quantity.
However, it has become evident that this simplistic approach is not
adequate to provide good indoor air quality.
To improve ventilation air delivery,
HVAC engineers should incorporate
the calculations and directives of the
standard into the normal design pro-
cess. Several tools exist to make the
ventilation rate procedure calculations
less difficult.
Ventilation Air Delivery Problem
During the evolution of the current
standard, the Standing Standards Project
Committee (SSPC) 62.1 and researchers
quantified several factors that added to
the degradation of indoor air quality.
As indicated in Figure 1, the delivery
of outdoor air (OA) from the air-handling
By Steven M. Miller, P.E., Member ASHRAE
© 2006, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal Vol. 48, Nov. 2006. For personal use
only. Additional reproduction, distribution, or transmission in either print or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s prior written permission.
November 2006
ASHRAE Journal
17
unit to the breathing zone can be reduced significantly, due to
a combination of operating conditions:
• The reduction of primary airflow by the variable air vol-
ume terminal unit decreases the supply air quantity to the
zone and consequently reduces the amount of outdoor air
delivered to the zone. The energy saving with VAV systems
has often been at the cost of indoor air quality.1
• Delivery of the primary air to the breathing zone is de-
pendent on the performance of the air diffusers. If the
diffusers are not selected to drive the primary air down to
the breathing zone at a sufficient velocity, less OA will be
available at the occupied level.
• Even if the diffusers are selected to deliver the recommend-
ed 150 fpm (0.8 m/s) velocity at the breathing level, based
on cooling air quantity, the delivery can be significantly
changed when the supply air system switches to heating
mode. This can happen when a heating coil at the VAV
terminal is activated, which creates a buoyancy effect and
short-circuits the supply air back to the return register at
the ceiling level. (ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals,
Chapter 33, recommends limiting the heating discharge
temperature differential of ceiling mounted diffusers to
approximately 15°F (8°C). At higher temperatures, the
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 55-2004, Thermal Environ-
mental Conditions for Human Occupancy, maximum
temperature gradient of 5°F [3°C] in the occupied zone
could be exceeded.)
• A short-term occupancy increase in a zone, such as an
extended meeting in an office or in conference rooms,
reduces the per-person OA quantity for that zone.
SSPC 62.1 and ASHRAE researchers have addressed the
problems associated with delivery of ventilation air to the
breathing zone, and have revised the standard to account for
these problems. The results of the calculations included in
Standard 62.1-2004 will provide a minimum value for outdoor
air quantity that we can use as the required outdoor air quantity
for each air-handling system. In the past, we did not have a
good tool to quantify the recommended outdoor air volume. As
a result, we have seen several instances of building occupant
complaints and sick building syndrome. By complying with the
directives in the standard, we can start with reliable baseline
values for ventilation air quantities. From this baseline, we can
(optionally) make changes to our system designs that improve
the ventilation air delivery efficiency and consequently improve
the indoor air quality even further.
A common complaint from engineers is that Standard 62.1-
2004 is too time consuming and difficult to apply. However, new
tools are available, including the new 62.1 User’s Manual with
its associated practice spreadsheet, and a third-party compliance
software package (both available at www.ashrae.org/bookstore).
These tools help with application of the standard’s procedures,
and make the process less time consuming. The engineer can use
these tools to optimize the ventilation air quantity. Optimization
can help avoid the potentially excessive air quantities that can
occur using other direct calculation methods.
Outdoor Air Calculation Procedures
Standard 62.1-2004 includes two alternatives for determining
the recommended OA quantity: the ventilation rate procedure
and the IAQ procedure.2
The ventilation rate procedure is a prescriptive procedure
that determines OA intake rates based on the occupancy
category, number of occupants, and floor area. The floor area
OA requirement is based on contaminant sources and source
strengths typical for the occupancy category. The procedure
also accounts for system ventilation efficiency (including the
fraction of outdoor air discharged to the zone), as well as the
ventilation effectiveness of the air-distribution devices.
The IAQ procedure is a design procedure that determines OA
intake requirements and other system design parameters based
on analysis of contaminant sources, contamination concentra-
tion targets, and perceived acceptability targets. Compliance
through this procedure is not within this article’s scope.
In most cases, the ventilation rate procedure is the more
straightforward method of determining the recommended
OA rate. Typically, the engineer does not have all of the in-
formation needed to perform the IAQ procedure at the early
design stages.
To improve ventilation air delivery, HVAC engineers should incorporate
the calculations and directives of the standard into the normal design
process. Several tools exist to make the ventilation rate procedure
calculations less difficult.
18
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2006
Outdoor Air Quality
Prior to completing ventilation design using either design
procedure, the engineer is required to investigate the local
outdoor air quality and review the findings with the building
owner or representative. This requires three steps of investiga-
tion and documentation.
1. Regional Air Quality. The status of compliance with
national ambient air quality
standards shall be determined
for the geographic area of the
building site for a status of
either attainment or non-at-
tainment for each pollutant
listed in the standard, Table
4-1. Compliance status for the
region can be found at www.
epa.gov.
2. Local Air Quality. An
observational survey of the
building site and immedi-
ate surroundings shall be
conducted to identify local contaminants that could enter the
building. The survey should include items listed in the standard
and the 62.1 User’s Manual, including date, time, area surveyed,
description of nearby facilities, observation of odors or irritants,
visible plumes or air contaminants, sources of vehicle exhaust,
and prevailing wind.
3. Documentation. The information gathered during outdoor
air quality investigation must be documented in a report and
reviewed with the building owners or their representatives.
The documentation must include a conclusion by the engineer
relative to the acceptability of outdoor air quality.
If the investigation indicates non-attainment for one or more
of the applicable pollutants or if a local source of contamina-
tion exists, the engineer should consider remedial measures
including air-cleaning devices for the specific pollutants and
using appropriate air intake locations.
Ventilation Rate Procedure
The ventilation rate procedure addresses essentially three
types of air-handling systems for the purpose of determining
required OA ventilation.
1. Single-Zone Systems. One air handler supplies a mixture
of outdoor air and recirculated air to only one zone.
2. 100% Outdoor Air Systems. One air handler supplies
only outdoor air to one or more zones.
3. Multizone, Single Supply Recirculating Systems. One
air handler or central system supplies a mixture of outdoor air
and recirculated return air to more than one zone from a single
location (e.g., reheat, single-duct VAV, single-fan dual-duct, and
multizone).
Simplified OA Calculation Tasks
Calculation of the Standard 62.1-2004 outdoor air quantity
typically can be reduced to eight to 10 tasks.
1. Establish the ventilation zones and tabulate the associ-
ated floor areas. Ventilation zones are one or several occupied
spaces that have a similar occupancy category, similar occupant
density, similar air-distribution effectiveness, and similar zone
primary airflow per unit area. Ventilation zones are not neces-
sarily the same as thermal control zones that are used in load
calculations, but they can be the same in many cases.
2. Select the appropriate
occupancy category for each
of these ventilation zones
from the list in Table 6-1 of
the standard.
3. Determine the occupan-
cy of each ventilation zone.
Standard 62.1-2004 includes
an estimated default value
based on the zone floor area
and the occupant density for
the occupancy category. If the
number of people expected to
occupy the zone during typi-
cal usage cannot be accurately predicted, these default values
can be used to calculate the required outdoor air quantities. If
the number of people can be determined (e.g., by using the
architect’s programming, by counting the number of chairs
or desks in a zone, or other means), the actual number should
be used in the calculations. (If the engineer is using the 2003
International Mechanical Code, which takes its table from the
pre-Addendum n version of the standard, the engineer must use
the default values as minimums, not defaults.) If the occupancy
is expected to increase at times, such as for extended meetings
in an office, in conference rooms, or other areas within the
zone, the anticipated peak occupancy for the zone should be
included in the calculations, along with a time-related multiplier
to allow for the short-term occupancy.3 Engineering judgment
is needed to establish the anticipated number of people as ac-
curately as possible.
4. Calculate the outdoor air required in the breathing zone,
based on the combination of occupant-related requirements and
area-related requirements. These calculations are performed
automatically with the 62.1-2004 User’s Manual spreadsheets or
with the available third-party compliance software programs.
5. Determine the correct air distribution effectiveness
value from Table 6-2 of the standard for each ventilation
zone. The correct selection should be based on the expected
worst case for each zone. For example, if a zone could have a
supply air temperature more than 15°F (8°C) above the room
temperature when a terminal reheat coil is energized, then the
corresponding effectiveness number should be selected. This
has a smaller effectiveness number (0.8), which will require a
larger quantity of outdoor air than if ceiling supply of cool air
had been selected.
6. Establish the design primary airflow to the zone from
load calculations. This includes outdoor intake air and recircu-
lated air from the air-handling system, but does not include air
Intended Ventilation Air Delivery
to Occupants
Effective Ventilation Air
Deficit at the ‘Breathing
Zone’ Due to Combination
Of Operating Conditions
1. Application of VAV,
Reduction to Minimum
Primary Air Quality
2. Less Than Optimum Air
Diffusion Effectiveness
3. Air Buoyancy Effect (Warm Air
Short-Circuiting to Return at Ceiling)
4. Short-Term Increase
In Occupancy (Meeting or
Conference)
Figure 1: Effective ventilation diagram.
20
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2006
transferred or air recirculated to the zone by other means (such
as locally recirculated airflow). Also, establish the minimum
primary airflow to the zone, which is the minimum expected
primary airflow when a variable volume device is at minimum
design flow rate.
7. If the air-handling system includes secondary recirculation
at the zone, it is necessary to perform additional calculations to
determine the outdoor air requirements, as shown in Appendix
A of the standard. This provision is for systems that provide part
of their ventilation by recirculating air from other zones without
directly mixing it with outdoor air (e.g., dual-fan dual-duct,
fan-powered mixing box, fan-powered VAV box, and transfer
fans for conference rooms). The quantity (cfm or L/s) of sec-
ondary recirculation airflow to zone should be determined
by the airflow volume of the secondary system fan or blower.
Figure 2 shows a diagram describing the secondary recircula-
tion system.4 With secondary recirculation systems, it is also
necessary to determine the estimated portion of secondary
recirculation air from the common return. This air quantity
will be a portion of the previously established quantity of sec-
ondary recirculation airflow to zone. This air quantity should
be based on engineering judgment and depends on whether the
air-handling return system is ducted or is a ceiling plenum type
of return system.
8. Calculate the system ventilation efficiency, based on the
ventilation zone with the minimum value of zone ventilation
efficiency. This will require calculating the zone ventilation
efficiency of several zones (or possibly all zones) to determine
the one with the minimum ventilation efficiency, also described
as the ventilation-critical zone. This ventilation-critical zone
is the zone that requires the largest fraction of outdoor air in
the primary airstream. Since this zone determines the outdoor
air requirement for the associated air-handling system, the
Appendix A calculations should be used, rather than using the
less accurate Table 6-3 values. If the ventilation efficiency of
this ventilation-critical zone is unnecessarily low, the outdoor
air requirement of the air-handling system can be unnecessar-
ily high, which could result in wasted energy and construction
costs. (Appendix A calculations can be simplified by using the
previously mentioned tools.)
9. If the ventilation-critical zone has a zone ventilation ef-
ficiency that is significantly lower than the next lowest zone
ventilation efficiency, the engineer should provide the extra
time and effort to optimize the zone ventilation efficiencies.
As noted in Standard 62.1-2004, “The designer may increase
the zone supply flows during the design process, particularly to
the critical zones requiring the highest fraction of outdoor air,
and thereby reduce the system outdoor air intake requirements
determined in the calculation, sometimes dramatically.”5
Typically during design, the VAV minimum is set by factors
such as minimum controllable operating point of the VAV box,
by office standards that may dictate a minimum air change
rate; by minimum air quantity per unit area; or by air volumes
required for building heating, depending on the system type.
In some instances, the minimum for one zone might be signifi-
cantly smaller, proportionally, than minimums for other zones.
This can create an unnecessarily large outdoor air requirement
at the air-handling unit.
Optimization improves the ventilation efficiency of the
ventilation-critical zone and can significantly reduce the out-
door air requirement of the associated air-handling system.
Analysis of the ventilation-critical zone(s) should include trad-
eoff comparisons between energy saved at the air-handling unit
with lower outdoor air quantity, vs. the potential of increased
heating required at the zone level to avoid overcooling when
the minimum airflow is increased.
See the Optimization Process sidebar for an example.6 Op-
timization involves an iterative (multiple-trial) procedure to
arrive at the most economical outdoor air quantity, while still
complying with the requirements of Standard 62.1-2004.
With experience, the optimization process can be completed
in a short time. As can be seen from this small example, im-
proving the ventilation efficiency of the ventilation-critical
zone has reduced the required OA from the original 3,046 cfm
(1438 L/s) to the final required OA of 1,638 cfm (773 L/s).
And, the requirements of the ventilation rate procedure are
still being met.
10. Calculate the design outdoor intake quantity based
on the uncorrected outdoor air intake and the final system
ventilation efficiency. This value will be used as the outdoor
air intake quantity for the air-handling system. The previously
mentioned tools perform these calculations automatically.
Ensure Outdoor Air Will Be Provided to Occupants
After completing the ventilation calculations and establish-
ing the outdoor air quantity for each air-handling system, it is
essential that the engineer make provisions to ensure the HVAC
equipment actually provides the required outdoor air quantity.

Third Step. The minimum has

Fourth Step. The minimum for
the third zone has been increased
to 2,700 cfm (1274 L/s). This re-

reduces the required OA to 1,638
cfm (773 L/s). Note also that the

(yellow highlight) has shifted to the
fifth zone (0.40 in this example). The
“Required OA” reduction from the
previous step is now only 79 cfm
(37.3 L/s), so further optimization is
not necessary. (If further optimiza-
tion had been indicated, the user
would now switch to the fifth zone
and increase the minimum airflow
[Vdz] to that zone.)
The mechanical ventilation systems are to be designed and
controlled to maintain at least the required minimum outdoor
airflow under any load condition. Standard 62.1-2004 states,
“VAV systems with fixed outdoor air damper positions must
comply with this requirement at minimum supply airflow.”7
In the interest of energy conservation, this requirement would
indicate that OA dampers on VAV systems should be controlled
rather than fixed. Otherwise, the OA quantity at full-load
operation could be excessive. OA damper control should include
airflow measurement devices to ensure the required air quan-
tity is being provided under all load conditions. Specifications
should include provisions for initial test and balance of OA
quantities. Operations and maintenance manuals should include
prescribed schedules for periodic testing and adjustment of the
OA dampers and controls over the life of the systems.
Strategies to Improve Ventilation Efficiency
Standard 62.1-2004 has pointed out several areas that cause
reduced ventilation efficiency. The engineer has the opportunity
during the design phase to create the most favorable conditions
for effective ventilation air delivery.
• Select diffusers and registers to deliver the primary air
to the breathing zone at recommended velocities, and at
worst-case conditions. This could include using diffusers
that automatically adjust discharge-free areas to maintain
distribution patterns as air volume is decreased.
• Revise the method of building heating (such as hydronic
baseboard heating), so it is not necessary to provide ceiling
supply of heating air. Alternately, if using ceiling supply,
limit the heating air differential temperature by increasing
the heating airflows.
• Use underfloor air systems and floor supply of cooled air
with ceiling return systems.
• Revise VAV minimums based on optimization of ventilation-
critical zones. The engineer also should verify that ad-
equate heating is available for zones that are increased
during optimization to avoid overcooling of the spaces.
• Add heat/cool energy recovery equipment, so even larger
24
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2006
amounts of outdoor air can be introduced without a ma-
jor increase in operating costs. This issue is addressed
further in ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1-
2004, Energy Standard for Buildings Except Low-Rise
Residential Buildings.
• Consider using zone-level recirculating systems, such as
fan-powered mixing boxes, which can essentially “borrow”
unused outdoor air from adjacent zones.
• Apply sound engineering judgement on each project to es-
tablish the final outdoor air quantities for each air-handling
system. The OA quantity that is calculated using the Stan-
dard 62.1-2004 procedures is to be considered a minimum
or baseline quantity. Any additional OA introduced into
the primary airstream likely will improve indoor air qual-
ity even more than the standard has prescribed. Balancing
the operation and energy costs with indoor air quality is
still a consideration. However, when
Standard 62.1-2004 is used, we can be
reasonably confident that our projects
will be afforded the standard of care that
they deserve.
Summary
Using Standard 62.1-2004 requires ad-
ditional work by the engineer. The old “10
cfm (4.8 L/s) per person” rule is history.
Engineers depend on organizations like
ASHRAE to provide design recommen-
dations based on extensive research and
on the expertise of technical committee
members. Ventilation calculations are no
less important than heating and cooling
calculations, and should be a standard
part of the design process.
By using the tools and guidelines
discussed here, the calculations and
documentation can be reduced to an
easily manageable list of tasks. These
tasks are further simplified once heating
and cooling load calculations have been
performed because they use many of the
same input values.

This article addresses the problem of energy consumption
for the warm-up period after setback as a mathematical task
of optimum control and then provides the solution backed by
experimental data, which shows real energy savings.

About the Authors
Yuri A. Tabunschikov is a doctor of technical science, professor, and a member
of the Russian Academy of Architecture and Construction Sciences. Marianna M.
Brodatch is a professor at the Moscow Architectural Institute (State Academy).

roviding optimal control of energy consump-
tion for the warm-up period after a tempera-
ture setback is significant in regards to heating
system controls. However, the applications of
setback mode are based upon the analysis of
internal air temperature change depending on the
thermal inertia characteristics of the enclosing
structures and the heat supply mode.
This only addresses the direct issue because it only answers
the question of what happens if a decision is made in given
conditions and then provides the value of a chosen efficiency
criterion. Solutions that resolve the reverse issue are concep-
tually more difficult. These can answer the question: which
solution must be selected for the efficiency criterion to reach
its maximum value. In our case, it is necessary to choose an

V = volume of heated space, m3;

c · r = volumetric heat capacity of air, J/(m­3 · °C);

The first item on the right side of Equation 1 relates to both
the walls and windows. The second item includes heat losses
due to air exchange.
Let TR,st be the initial internal air temperature where t = 0,
and TR,end be the final internal air temperature (where t = t1).
Function Q(t) in the section [0, t1] will be called control.
Control Q(t) will be called allowable if the function Q(t) is
piecewise continuous in section [0, t1], and its values do not
exceed the limits of a certain U set. It is obvious that any
permissible control is limited.
Let us rewrite Equation 1 as follows:
dTR = f (TR, Ti, Q, t)
dt
(3)
In the theory of optimal control, it is assumed that the right
side of Equation 3 is a continuous function and has continu-
ous first derivatives with respect to TR, Ti, t for any values in
the section [0, t1].
Let us define the problem of energy consumption optimiza-
tion in heating a building and examine it as an optimal control
task as follows: we need to find such control Q(t) of energy
consumption spent on a temperature change in a building,
and such solution to the system of equations of heat balance
of a given building as a united power system that satisfy the
initial conditions upon which the function
(4)
achieves the smallest possible value.
Equation 4, which needs to be minimized, determines the
energy consumed for heating the building.
Control Q(t), which provides a solution to the given problem,
is called an optimal control and corresponds to the change of
temperature from the initial TR,st to the final TR,end, while the
corresponding temperature change trajectory, TR(t), is called
an optimal trajectory.
The optimal control solution of energy consumption for the
warm-up period after the setback as a mathematical task of
optimum control was first solved and described in two publica-
tions1,2 using the method of L.S. Pontriagin.3
The following result was obtained: the minimization of
energy consumption for the warm-up period after setback
when the time of change between the initial and the required
temperatures of the building is minimal (maximal quick-
action method).
To put this condition into effect for a given building, it is
necessary to further examine the heat balance component and
to estimate heat storage figures.
For the majority of residential and social buildings, the mini-
mization of power spent on heating can be achieved when the
following two statements are implemented.
Statement One: The building must be heated using abso-
lutely maximum power of the given heating devices.
Statement Two: The heating of the building must be started from
the surfaces of enclosing structures with highest heat capacity.
In most cases this relates to the internal surfaces of the en-
closing structures, which as a rule are the most cooled (e.g.,
minimization of heating time can be achieved by rapidly heating
the internal surfaces of the enclosing constructions by means
of convection trailing jets).
For the power consumption control to converge to optimal,
Statements One and Two must be carried out simultaneously.
Usually, executing only one of them in heating a building (air
plus enclosing structures) gives a far from optimal result.
However, at the time, some aspects of this solution raised
serious doubts among applied mathematicians and, therefore,
required further substantiation, which was provided by work
done by the author and his colleague.4,5
However, a mathematical conclusion cannot be convincing
for scientific researchers and experts unless it is either verified
by direct calculations or, and this is a necessity, confirmed by
specific experimental research.
28
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2006
Therefore, an experiment is needed to demonstrate the effec-
tiveness of optimal power consumption control determined by our
solution and Statements One and Two. This is done by quantitative
comparisons of power used to heat a given building.
To conduct the experiment and to check Statement One (the
building must be heated using absolutely maximum power of
the given heating devices), it is required to have two identical
facilities and to organize the process of simultaneous heating of
the facilities applying the sources of different power. Otherwise,
if only one source is used, it is required to divide the experiment
into two stages and use a source that ensures different power
levels for the two stages.
To check Statement Two (the heating of the building must be
started from the surfaces of enclosing structures with highest
heat capacity), we need two identical facilities with identical
heating devices. In the first case, these would primarily heat the
internal surfaces of the enclosing structures, e.g., by means of
intensive convection jets. And, in the other case, these would
primarily heat the internal air of facility.
The experiment was conducted in the climate chamber of the
JAGA research center, which meets these requirements.
Two identical facilities are in the climate chamber (Figure 1).
Each is 4 m by 4 m (13 ft by 13 ft) with an inclined roof and
height of 2.7 m to 3.7 m (9 ft to 12 ft). In the climate chamber
it is possible to maintain the air temperature within in the range
from –15°C to +30°C (5°F to 86°F).
The window openings are located in Wall 2 (an area of
12.60 m2 [135 ft2]); in Wall 3 (an area of 1.56 m2 [17 ft2]); in
Wall 4 (an area of 2.52 m2 [27 ft2]). There are multiple glass
units with aluminum window casements that have equivalent
heat transfer resistance coefficients equal to 0.6 m2 · K/W (3.4
ft2 · h · °F/Btu). The walls are 300 mm thick (1 ft). The equivalent
heat transfer resistance coefficient for the walls is 2.7 m2 · K/W
(15.3 ft2 · h · °F/Btu) and 2.6 m2 · K/W (14.8 ft2 · h · °F/Btu) for
the covering. No air exchange occurs in the facilities.
To measure the temperature in each facility, the following
tools are used:
• Twenty-five sensors for measuring the internal air tempera-
ture, positioned at five points (I, II, III, IV, V) in the height
extent of the facilities. Sensors I, II, III, IV are located at
a distance of 27 cm (11 in.) from the wall surface;
• Five sensors for measuring the temperature of the internal
surface of Wall 1; and
• Five temperature sensors on the internal surface of Win-
dow 2.
Sensors for measuring the temperature of the facilities are
located on five levels: at 0.1 m (4 in.), 0.6 m (2 ft), 1.1 m (3.6
ft), 1.6 m (5.2 ft), and 2.1 m (6.8 ft) from the floor. The layout of
the temperature sensors is shown on Figure 1. The temperature
sensors on the internal surface of the enclosing structures are
indicated by traditional numerals, and the internal air tempera-
ture sensors are indicated by Roman numerals.
When processing the results of this experiment, the follow-
ing equation was taken for the measurement of temperature t
of the facility:

Figure 2: Consumption of energy on heating the facility where the heat-
ing device is on the internal surface of the enclosure structures (dotted
line) and where the device is in the middle of the facility (solid line).
