Barrier Isolator
ISO Class 5
Barrier Isolator
Ante/Buffer Room
(ISO Class 8)
Barrier Isolator (a glove box) is designed to provide clean
makeup air to the workspace and a seamless barrier between
the workspace and the operator.
Buffer Area/Zone/Room is a clean space containing the
primary engineering control equipment, and to which the sec-
ondary engineering controls are applied.
Cleanroom is any space where provisions are made to control
particulates and/or microbes concentrations at or below speci-
fied limits (typically the buffer area, antespace, or anteroom).
It corresponds to ASHRAE’s definition of “clean space.”
Compounded Sterile Preparations (CSP) are products
administered to patients that require some type of manipulation
prior to administration.
ISO Class2 is a measurement of room air cleanliness. ISO
Class 5 corresponds to Class 100; ISO Class 7 to Class 10,000;
and ISO Class 8 to Class 100,000. Class numbers correspond to
the number of particles per ft³ at 0.5 microns or larger.
Primary Engineering Controls are measures taken to pro-
duce the environment in a LAFW or barrier isolator.
Risk Level (low, medium or high) pertains to the risk of the
product becoming contaminated, not to a danger presented to
the operator.
Secondary Engineering Controls are measures taken to
produce the environment in the spaces occupied by staff and
the LAFW or barrier isolator.
Space Configuration
Several ways exist to configure the pharmacy, equipment and
the HVAC system to meet USP 797 requirements. The specific
configuration has a significant impact on the HVAC system.
The variables involved are risk level, type of equipment used
(LAFW, vs. barrier isolator), and the cleanliness classification
of the pharmacy. The basic configurations are:
• An ISO Class 5 clean space, with no LAFW or barrier
isolator equipment (Figure 1a);
• An LAFW located in an ISO Class 8 clean space with an
ante space, but without an anteroom (Figure 1b);
• An LAFW located in an ISO Class 8 clean space with an
anteroom (Figure 1c);
• A barrier isolator located in a non-ISO rated space (Figure
1d); and
• A barrier isolator located in an ISO Class 8 space (Figure
1e).
Scenario 1 (Figure 1a) is unlikely to be implemented. This
arrangement is expensive initially from the standpoint of
finishes, and operationally from the standpoint of cleaning
the space and controlling operator-generated particulates.
Laminar flow clean benches, biological safety cabinets and
barrier isolators are all specifically designed to provide the
required ISO Class 5 space, and can minimize the impact of
the room finishes and operator procedures on the sterile pro-
cesses. This option does provide the most flexibility in that
low, medium and high-risk procedures can all be performed
on the benchtop without the spatial limitations of an LAFW
or barrier isolator.
Scenario 2 (Figure 1b) is probably the best option for
most existing facilities. Modifications can be made to the
HVAC system to achieve the ISO Class 8 classification, and
LAFW
LAFW
Figure 1: A) Scenario 1, workbenches in ISO Class
5 cleanroom; B) Scenario 2, LAFWs in ISO Class 8
clean space, with ante space; C) Scenario 3, LAFWs in
ISO Class 8 cleanroom, with anteroom; D) Scenario 4,
barrier isolator in Non-ISO rated space; E) Scenario
5, barrier isolator in ISO Class 8 clean space.
38
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
February 2007
the room can be configured to provide a buffer space for the
actual preparation work, and an ante space for storage and
product organization work. This scenario will work for low
and medium risk CSPs.
Scenario 3 (Figure 1c) is the same as Scenario 2 except a
wall is added to physically separate the buffer space from the
ante space. This would allow the performance of high-risk pro-
cedures. However, as can be determined from the operational
sections of USP 797, most high-risk procedures can be avoided
with procedural modifications.
Scenario 4 (Figure 1d) makes use of a barrier isolator to
compensate for the lack of an ISO Class 8 room. This would
be an advantageous option if it were cost prohibitive to achieve
an ISO Class 8 space with the existing room configuration
or HVAC system. Another advantage is that procedures at all
three risk levels can be performed. The disadvantage is the
limitation on throughput caused by the additional operator
time required to perform procedures in a glovebox.
Scenario 5 (Figure 1e) is basically the same as Scenario 4,
but the room is arranged to have an ISO Class 8 classification.
This arrangement is recommended (but not required) by USP
797 when a barrier isolator is used. However, this scenario is
unlikely because, once the expense of an ISO Class 8 room
is incurred, a need no longer exists to use a barrier isolator,
unless it is required for personnel protection from hazardous
or infectious agents. An LAFW will allow compliance at a
lower cost.
Most existing hospital pharmacies are served by central
air-handling systems. If the system also serves patient care
space, the air-handling unit probably already has high-
efficiency final filters. Even if it does not serve patient care
areas, it probably already has a higher than normal level of
filtration. Therefore, for most healthcare applications, Scenarios
2 and 3 are the most technically feasible and affordable, while
still yielding an efficient workspace. Therefore, the remainder of
this article will focus on how to achieve compliance with USP
797 assuming that the intended result is an ISO Class 8 clean
space, with LAFWs providing the ISO Class 5 compounding
environment.
How to Achieve an ISO Class 8 Clean Space
Once the decision has been made that the pharmacy should
meet a cleanliness level that meets or exceeds ISO Class 8, the
design team must consider many factors. One might assume
that, since the ISO standard applies to concentrations of par-
ticulates in the air, this must be primarily an HVAC engineer-
ing issue. To the contrary, the necessary provisions to achieve
ISO Class 8 affect the pharmacy staff first, and the facility’s
architecture (i.e., space planning, finishes, etc.) second.
To yield an ISO Class 8 clean space, all sources of particulates
must be controlled. Examples of these sources are the workers,
any shedding surfaces (permanent or mobile), the compounding
process itself, the supply air to the pharmacy, and infiltration
through doors, cracks and penetrations. The following are some
measures (both HVAC-related and not) that are used to control
these sources of particulates.
Control Measures (Non-HVAC)
Operator Procedures. While not the focus of this article,
the standard operating procedures (SOPs) that the pharmacy
operators follow must be carefully assessed, and periodically
reassessed to ensure that the worker’s particulate generation
is minimized as much as possible. USP 797 includes recom-
mended SOPs that pharmacies should use as a baseline. Some
activities yield increased particulate generation rates, such
as gowning and writing.3 Those types of activities should be
confined to the antearea, or outside of the pharmacy altogether.
For worker activities that must occur within the clean space,
the operations should be configured so as to minimize the par-
ticulate generation to the maximum extent possible.
USP 797 requires that the air quality, on a particulate count
basis, be checked in the LAFWs, barrier isolators, buffer rooms,
anteareas, and anterooms at least every six months. Airborne
microorganisms are required to be evaluated monthly at various
locations (weekly for high risk). When the results from the envi-
ronmental monitoring indicate that the counts of particulates are
too high, or that the colony-forming units are trending upwards,
the SOPs are often the first place to look for resolution.
Surfaces and Materials. All permanent surfaces, including
ceilings, walls and floors, should be monolithic, nonshedding,
and washable. All penetrations through the room barrier by
conduits, lights, diffusers, sprinkler heads, etc., should be
completely sealed. The devices themselves should be sealed
and washable. All mobile materials and equipment in the clean
space, such as supply carts and workers’ garments, need to be
nonshedding.
Cleaning Procedures. The cleaning supplies and procedures
that are used to sterilize the clean areas must be closely reviewed
to minimize any particulate or microbial generation during the
cleaning procedure, and any residual generation that occurs
after (i.e., such as from off-gassing). The processes should be
reviewed thoroughly and coordinated with the facility’s envi-
ronmental systems department. In many cases it is determined
that low-shedding type mop heads need to be used to maintain
an ISO Class 8 environment. As with the operator SOPs, clean-
ing procedures often are reassessed when the results from the
environmental monitoring indicate increased particulate or
microorganism levels.
Control Measures (HVAC)
Supply Air Quality. The air being supplied to the pharmacy
must be cleaner than the desired air quality of the room itself
to mitigate the generation of particulates inside of the room
(e.g., by workers). Means of calculating the required level of
filtration exist that would be required for the supply air given a
room change rate, a particulate concentration of the air entering
the filters, an internal particulate generation rate, and a desired
room air quality. However, since the internal particulate genera-
40
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
February 2007
tion rates are difficult to predict, 99.97% efficient HEPA filters
typically are used since they are known to successfully yield
ISO Class 8 spaces for most applications. The HEPA filters can
be located centrally, such as in the air-handling unit, or locally,
such as at the pharmacy supply diffusers. Twenty-five percent
efficient prefilters should be located somewhere upstream of the
HEPA filters to increase the usable life of the more expensive
HEPA filters. Also, the conditions of the HEPA filters need to
be routinely checked for loading; the increased air pressure
drop caused by the loading of HEPA filters can have a profound
impact on the supply air volume, which in turn can impact room
pressurization levels.
If the air-handling system
is dedicated to the pharmacy,
consideration should be given
to locating the HEPA filters in
the air-handling unit. This re-
duces maintenance cost (since
filters are easier to change in
a mechanical room than in a
ceiling plenum or clean space
and typically fewer filters are
used when located centrally).
Additionally, variable fre-
quency drives on the supply
fans can enhance system
performance since a constant
airflow can be delivered while compensating for the loading of
the HEPA filters. When HEPA filters are centrally located, take
care to ensure that contaminants are not introduced downstream
of those filters. For example, duct lining and sound attenuators
should not be used downstream of the filters. If humidification
is required to be added to achieve design humidity levels, the
humidifiers should use clean steam.
Room Air Changes. The number of air changes required to
achieve ISO Class 8 can vary depending on room geometry,
supply air quality, and the projected level of internal particulate
generation. If the supply air passes through HEPA filters before
being introduced into the space, then 20 to 30 air changes per
hour is typically sufficient to achieve ISO Class 8.4
Room Airflow Distribution. The characteristics of the
airflow within a pharmacy are an integral aspect of the clean
space’s performance. Airflow patterns are essential in keeping
the particulate counts down throughout the space, and also
help to effectively wash particulates away from the workers.
Low velocity, non-aspirating-type diffusers and displacement
ventilation design should be used to help prevent air turbu-
lence in the room. The placement of LAFWs and diffusers is
also important.
LAFWs should be located in non-traffic paths and away from
doors to prevent movement of operators and the opening/closing
of doors from influencing the airflow patterns at the face of the
LAFW. Diffusers should be mounted away from LAFWs, such
that the supply air velocity does not exceed 50 fpm (0.25 m/s)
at the face of the LAFW.5 In general, to maximize the room
ventilation effectiveness the HVAC designer should take care
in selecting diffuser locations in relation to where the air will
be exhausted, and low exhaust is not normally necessary.
Room Pressurization. To prevent particulates from infiltrat-
ing through doors, cracks and penetrations, the clean spaces
must be kept at positive pressure in relation to neighboring
spaces. The ultimate intent is that the location where the sterile
compounding occurs is at the highest pressure, and the room
pressures cascade lower farther away from the most sterile
location. The differential pressure across a room barrier causes
airflow to move in the desired
direction, which is from the
cleaner space to the dirtier
space. The target should be a
minimum pressure differential
across each room barrier of
0.01in. w.g. (2.5 Pa).6
Digital differential pressure
monitors should be provided
at the entrance to the buffer
room and/or anteroom. These
devices can send a signal to the
building automation system,
which enables the differential
pressure to be trended over
time, thereby highlighting a
degrading differential pressure before it becomes an operational
problem. Digital monitors with alarms are discouraged when
there is not an anteroom before the buffer room, since when-
ever the door has been opened to the buffer room its pressure
will equalize with the adjacent space, causing frequent alarms.
If digital monitors are not preferred or applicable, designers
might elect to provide physical indicators (i.e., ball-in-tubes,
or flutter-strips), to signal pharmacy operators that there is a
potential contamination problem.
Room Temperature and Humidity Control. Occupant
comfort is always a concern, but the temperature and humidity
must also be kept low enough to prevent workers from sweating,
which increases human particulate and microbial generation
rates. The garb that workers are projected to wear must be
explored during design, since thicker garments will warrant
lower room air temperatures. In the absence of specific design
criteria, whenever possible, HVAC designers should ensure
that the system can produce buffer room temperatures at least
as low as 68°F (20°C), with the relative humidity maintained
at or below 50% RH. These conditions are required due to the
additional garb worn by workers, similar to that in an operat-
ing room.7
Implementation in Existing Facilities
Hospital renovations are inherently complicated. Mainte-
nance of operations, infection control, and unknown existing
conditions contribute to this complexity. A pharmacy renova-
Airflow (From Ceiling Plenum or Duct)
Supply Duct
Speed Controller
Ceiling
Airflow
(To Room)
Blower
Pre-Filter (Optional)
HEPA
Filter
Figure 2: Fan/filter unit.
42
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
February 2007
tion for USP 797 compliance involves these items, plus the
additional technical challenges associated with higher levels
of air filtration, higher air change rates, differential pressure,
and additional exhaust requirements.
The addition of a new air-handling unit is often the simplest
solution, but for many facilities might not be the most cost-
effective solution. In that case, perhaps the most difficult feature
to include is HEPA filtration of the supply air. HEPA filters im-
pose a significant air pressure drop on an existing air-handling
unit. Many facilities may be tempted to merely install HEPA
filter diffusers. However, this will cause either less-than-desired
airflow in the pharmacy, or balancing problems in the remain-
der of the air-handling unit zone. A better approach is the use
of fan/filter units (Figure 2). The fan provides the additional
static pressure needed to compensate for filter air pressure drop.
Ceiling plenum space and acoustical considerations will require
evaluation. The integral blower generates a considerable amount
of noise, particularly at the manufacturers’ recommended
maximum airflows (typically equivalent to 100 fpm [0.5 m/s]
through the diffuser face), which can be mitigated by using
more fan/filter units at lower face velocities.
Prefilters only should be provided with the fan/filter units
if the inlet air has not already passed through roughing filters,
such as if the air is being drawn directly from the plenum space,
Hospital renovations are inherently complicated. Maintenance of
operations, infection control, and unknown existing conditions con-
tribute to this complexity.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
44
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
February 2007
since the prefilters present maintenance and access issues. For
example, if a fan/filter unit has a prefilter that can only be ac-
cessed from an ISO Class 8 space (such as through a ceiling
access panel or light fixture), the space will be briefly exposed
to the dirty air in the ceiling plenum, and the pharmacy will have
to be temporarily shut down for the amount of time needed to
replace the filters and to bring the space particulate counts back
down to ISO Class 8 levels. If HEPA filters are located at the
air-handling unit, existing ductwork downstream of the filters
should be cleaned and existing duct lining removed.
If the existing air-handling system cannot be rebalanced to
provide additional airflow in the pharmacy, the challenge of
providing additional air changes can be solved in much the
same way as the filtration issues. The fan/filter units discussed
previously also can provide the additional airflow recirculated
from the space. Unlike most health-care applications where
patient safety and infection control are
the primary issues, the concern in the
pharmacy is primarily particulate con-
trol. Therefore, ventilation air quantities
are not as important, and the additional
supply air can be recirculated through the
local HEPA filters, and not through the
central air-handling unit.
Maintaining the pharmacy at a posi-
tive pressure with respect to all adjacent
spaces (not just the adjacent spaces with
a communicating door) is typically just
a matter of balancing the space for more
supply air than return air. Some sealing
of cracks in the room enclosure may be
required. If any LAFWs are exhausted to
the outside, obtaining positive pressure is
more of a challenge.
The lower temperature and humidity
requirements due to operator clothing
can be a challenge for an existing air-
handling system. The additional supply
air changes provided for particulate
control will help, but probably will not
be sufficient. A lower supply air tempera-
ture will likely be required. This could
possibly be achieved by rebalancing the
chilled water flow to the cooling coil. If
not, a new cooling coil may be required.
If the supply air temperature is reduced,
the impact on existing reheat coil capacity
must also be evaluated.
Future Issues
Currently, a change to USP 797 has
been proposed that would increase the
buffer area requirements from ISO Class
8 to ISO Class 7.8 This change would
have a significant impact on the HVAC
systems and would require different solu-
tions than those outlined here. The two
sources noted as the basis for this change
deal primarily with the manufacturing
process. It is not clear that this change
would provide substantial improvement
in protecting the sterility of CSPs.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
46
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
February 2007
Conclusion
While an initial reading of USP 797 might be intimidating,
compliance is not that difficult. The ISO Class 8 requirements,
while substantially cleaner than typical office space, are not that
much cleaner than normal patient care spaces. Fortunately for
HVAC system designers, with a little planning and attention
to detail, an existing pharmacy facility may be brought into
compliance. The pharmacy professionals have the more difficult
task of complying with the new procedural issues.
References
1. United States Pharmacopoeia. 2004. USP General Chapter
<797>, Pharmaceutical Compounding—Sterile Preparations.
2. ISO 14644-1-1999, Classification of Air Cleanliness.
3. 2003 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications, Chapter 16,
Clean Spaces, 3.
4. 2003 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications, Chapter 16,
Clean Spaces, 11.
5. 2003 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications, Chapter 14,
Laboratories, 5.
6. Bare, A.R. 2006. “Pressurization strategies for biocontainment.”
2007 R&D Laboratory Design Handbook (11):59–63.
7. American Institute of Architects. 2006. AIA Guidelines for
Design and Construction of Health Care Facilities.
8. www.usp797.org.
Unlike most health-care applications where patient safety and infec-
tion control are the primary issues, the concern in the pharmacy is
primarily particulate control.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
48
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
February 2007
Modulate Coil
Heat Transfer
By Robert Koenig, Life Member ASHRAE
W
hen the purpose of the coil is comfort conditioning, modu-
lating coil heat transfer in an air handler is essential. In the
mind of owners and occupants, reliable and accurate temperature
regulation establishes the performance quality of the system.
Varying the volume of water flowing
through a coil establishes the primary
rate of heat transfer. It follows that pre-
cise regulation of water flow is a critical
function of temperature control in the
space it serves. With few exceptions,
current design practice impedes modu-
lation, resulting in cyclic control at the
unit, fluctuating temperature in the space
served and inefficient operation.
In theory, calculated design load is the
condition during which capacity meets the
maximum load requirement without adjust-
ment of any kind. Only when load dimin-
ishes and the system is operating under part
load is modulation required to change the
rate of heat transfer from the coil. Control
valves, when properly sized, set the design
flow rate and provide the means to modulate
coil heat transfer under part load.
When specifying pressure drop for
valve sizing, most systems seldom, if
ever, require calculated design capac-
ity and to a lesser degree, the installed
capacity. If design capacity is required,
it rarely represents more than 1% or
2% of total operating hours. In general,
hydronic systems operate below 50% of
design volume a high percentage of total
operating hours. That means friction in
the distribution piping and accessories
except the control valve will be less than
25% of calculated design. Under this
condition the valve/pump curve in Figure
2 illustrates that the differential pressure
(DP) at the valve will be slightly above
design pump head. At this point, valve
sizing determines how well coil heat
transfer can be modulated.
Since coil heat transfer is nonlinear
with respect to flow rate, valves have been
designed to counteract the nonlinear char-
acteristic of the coil. The characterization
referred to as equal percentage produces
a counteracting nonlinear flow curve re-
sulting in a near linear heat transfer from
the coil with respect to valve stem travel.
Figure 1 shows the relationship of flow to
stem travel for three valve plug designs
(shapes). The curve shown as “modi-
fied linear (equal percentage) two-way
valve” is shown as a compromise between
modified linear and equal percentage as
it more closely represents what a slightly
oversized valve will produce.
About the Author
Robert Koenig is an HVAC system analyst with
special interest in equipment application and perfor-
mance. He was a guest speaker on hydronic system
design and control in the 1960s and ’70s at most of
ASHRAE’s western U.S. chapters.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, February 2007. © Copyright 2007 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating
and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed elec-
tronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
February 2007
ASHRAE Journal
49
The term equal percentage is meaningless unless the pressure
drop remains reasonably constant throughout the full stroke of the
valve. Maintaining the DP valves are sized for is most significant
when system load is below 25% of design. Flow in the coil will
be less than 12% of design volume, pump head is at the top of its
curve and pipe, coil, and accessory resistance is minimal. Valve
pressure drop is near the top of the pump pressure curve and flow
modulation is at its most critical stage. Only if the valve is sized
for the pressure drop to which it is exposed (pump head), will a
specified flow characteristic be producible.
To facilitate equal percentage flow, the distribution piping and
pump selection must be engineered to minimize pump head.
The head required to meet flow demand under maximum load
conditions is an engineering option. Pipe sized to minimize head
will result in minimal differential pressure variation throughout
the system under varying load conditions facilitating valve per-
formance to meet its published/specified flow characteristic.
Variation in DP has significant influence on valve perfor-
mance because it changes the flow rate through the valve and
coil without regard for terminal load or space condition. This
results in a change in space temperature causing the control
point to shift away from setpoint, suggesting the controller is
out of calibration, prompting maintenance calls.
A valve sized for less than the actual head (DP) is oversized.
Oversized valves allow flow to exceed the intended maximum
(design) rate (Figure 2). The results are system overflow, tem-
perature change in the space that is more rapid than the sensor
can react to, pump overloading, and low differential temperature
(DT) at startup and when uncontrolled cyclic action occurs. A
portion of the characterized valve plug becomes ineffective,
rendering a published and/or specified flow characteristic invalid
regardless of valve port design. These facts are not altered by
types or sequence of control including direct digital control
(DDC), which provides many features designed to improve
equipment and system performance, not to overcome and mask
design deficiencies.
The importance of proper valve sizing is demonstrated in
Figure 1, which shows comparative valve capacity for a valve
sized and operating at the actual specified DP and capacity
of the same valve when it is subjected to significantly greater
pressure drop. The modified linear (equal percentage) two-way
valve curve approximates the difference in valve capacity rela-
tive to stem travel and the percentage of design coil capacity
it provides. Note the reduction in stem travel as DP increases.
The graph and scales at right are based on a specified valve
capacity of 10 gpm (6.3 L/s) at 4 psi (27.5 kPa) drop. The first
scale shows capacity when the valve is sized for 4 psi (27.5
kPa), representing the actual pressure drop it is subjected to.
The next two columns show capacity when the same valve is
subjected to 100 ft (298 kPa) and 75 ft (224 kPa) respectively.
The capacity of the valve is two-and-a-half to more than three
times the specified capacity when subjected to the higher dif-
ferential pressure. Specifying control valves to be sized at an
arbitrary low pressure drop is unrealistic, an engineering error
and costly for the owner to bear long term. Specifying control
valves to produce equal percentage flow characteristics in sys-
tems with variable speed pumps is comparable to specifying
the pump to produce a flat curve. Specifications do not change
the laws of physics.
Consider a valve specified for heating at 100,000 Btu (29.3
kW), 10 gpm (0.631 L/s) sized for a specified 3 psi (41.3 kPa)
drop and pump head is specified at 100 ft. (298 kPa). The valve
would require a 5.77 Cv rating. Assuming the nearest standard
Cv available is 6, the valve would have a capacity of 39.5 gpm
(2.5 L/s). A system design of 300 gpm (19 L/s) with 30 valves
would have a flow potential of more than 900 gpm (56.8 L/s).
The pump will require excessive motor horsepower at startup
and continue until control valves begin reducing flow unless
balance valves are installed and set to limit flow.
Compare this to the same system having pipe that is one size
larger to reduce friction loss approximately 50% and calculat-
ing the head requirement based on pipe and equipment only,
excluding the control valve. The pump head is 50 ft (149 kPa),
the valve is sized for the design pump head, valve Cv is 2.14.
Assuming the closest Cv is 2.5, the valve capacity is 11.6 gpm
(80 L/s) near the pump. The system now has a design flow
potential of 348 gpm (21.96 L/s), providing not only overflow
protection but also motor overload protection and reduced
energy consumption.
Since the valve drop was not included in the head calcula-
tion, using design head for drop when sizing the valve doubles
friction through the longest terminal circuit. The flow rate will
be reduced to 71% (approximate) of design. Since the available
valve Cv is slightly higher than calculated, the flow rate will be
8.2 gpm (0.517 L/s). The graph in Figure 2 indicates a reduction
or shortage in heat transfer approximating 2% for heating and
8% for cooling. The shortage only occurs if the entire system
requires calculated design capacity or at startup if all control
valves are fully open. Considering standard valve and coil size
is incremental, the larger rather than smaller will be selected
to meet specified capacity, and safety factors applied during
design, capacity shortage is highly improbable.

Imagine a system where overflow is eliminated, manual
balancing is unnecessary, designed valve flow characteristics
are producible, DP variation is minimized, DT is predictable,
pump motor loading is limited, pump impeller trimming is un-
necessary, calibration and startup (commissioning) is simplified
and performance quality is ensured. Many hours of contractor
labor can be saved (eliminated) if the system does not require
manual balancing and adjustments associated with high head
and oversized valves.
An article in ASHRAE Journal1 addresses the effect of reduced
flow to coils (less than design). Based on a computer simula-
tion, it states in part, referring to coils: “Those furthest from the
pump will have flow rates below design (73% for chilled water
and 75% for hot water).” It goes on to say that “At first glance,
one might expect that the low flow rates at the extremes of the
system could cause problems.” Reference is made to non-linear
coil performance affecting capacity: “In this case, the reduced
chilled water flow (73%) results in a coil capacity that is 89%
of the design capacity (based on the coil manufacturers’ rating
program). The reduced hot water flow (75%) results in a coil
capacity that is 96% of the design capacity (based on the VAV
box manufacturers’ reheat coil rating program), assuming sup-
ply water temperatures are at design conditions. These capacity
shortages are most likely within the error (or safety factor) of the
engineers’ design load calculations and are unlikely to result in
any significant increase in warm-up or cool-down times.” These
values compare with those in Figure 2.
In question is the practice of specifying valve drop at a small
percentage of design pump head. It is false to assume sizing
valves for low DP provides capacity protection. Oversized
valves are a major cause of coil freezing in hot water systems
and low temperature-differential in chilled water systems. Over-
sized valves near the pump short circuit the system, overload the
pump(s) and frequently leave units near the end of the circuit
with inadequate flow. Valves sized for design pump head pre-
vent excessive overflow, providing uniform water distribution
throughout the system when the distribution piping is properly
sized, without the added cost of manual balancing.
System design should be for the benefit of the owner/
occupant not for protecting against a capacity shortage for 1%
or 2% of time the system may be required to meet a calculated
design condition. In no way does this discount the importance
of system capacity to satisfy realistic load requirements. It is
most important to provide the most accurate temperature in the
space served by the most effective and efficient means possible.
Assuming airside design is correct, the waterside, coil, thermo-
stat and control valve become the controlling mechanism of the
space they serve. Designing a distribution system and properly
applying terminal equipment to ensure the system facilitates
efficient, reliable and accurate temperature control under all
load conditions defines system engineering.
When calculated pump head exceeds 50 ft (149 kPa), increase
the distribution pipe size. On average, increasing pipe one size
will decrease resistance nearly 50%. The cost may be 75% to
100% higher for labor and material. This applies to distribution
piping cost only. To minimize the impact on first cost, consider
sizing control valves for 100% of design pump head. The system
is now self-balanced. The cost of balancing valves and labor,
differential pressure control, and variable frequency drive for
pumps is eliminated. The benefit from improved system and
personnel performance can be significant. Energy and mainte-
nance cost is reduced over the life of the building.
When control valves are sized for design pump head, pump
selection and operation is important. Head rise from design
flow to shutoff should be as low as possible and pump speed
should be constant to minimize differential pressure variation.
Pumps should be sized and arranged for staging (Figure 3) to
minimize energy consumption. For example, knowing volume
Figure 1: Valve flow characteristics for three-plug types.
Figure 2: Coil capacity, pump head, system curve and valve DP.

(on average) will be less than 25% of design the majority of total
operating hours consider having three pumps sized for 20%,
30%, and 50% of design flow, staged on/off by flow controller
(FS) located in the supply main ahead of the secondary pumps.
In the cooling mode the flow sensor (FS) also can control the
bypass valve to maintain minimum flow to the chiller.
Three properly sized pumps will provide 100% of design ca-
pacity and reasonable backup if one pump fails. When the system
is operating below 20% of design flow, only the pump with the
smallest motor will operate minimizing energy consumption. For
a 500 gpm (31.5 L/s) system, a pump operating at 50 ft (149 kPa)
head and 75% efficiency will likely have a 10 hp (7.45 kW) motor.
At 20% or 100 gpm (6.3 L/s), a proper pump with 2 hp (1.49 kW)
motor will suffice. Compare that to a 25 hp or 30 hp (18.6 kW or
22.4 kW) motor on a typical 100 ft (298.7 kPa) head system.
For heating and cooling changeover systems, a 10% pump
is advisable because the system will likely operate on heating
a larger percentage of total hours, and coils sized for cooling
will have excessive heating capacity and require less flow.
The control valves also are oversized for heating. Fortunately,
the flow characteristic is maintained because the DP remains
relatively constant, facilitating accurate and stable control
(assuming constant pump speed). These systems are prone to
freeze problems in cold climates due to low flow if not prop-
erly controlled. Cyclic control adds to the problem when the
temperature sensor dissipates heat slower than the coil allowing
the coil to freeze while the valve remains closed.
As a means of maintaining reasonably constant DP when
system size indicates the primary equipment will generate
significant resistance and impose excessive DP variation, it is
advisable to pump the primary and secondary sections semi-
independently with pumps in series. Figure 3 shows a variable
speed pump(s) to offset resistance in the primary loop. When
volume generates a pressure drop across the primary equipment,
differential pressure controller (DP) will detect a change and
activate Variable Speed Pump 4 to maintain a predetermined set-
point. In so doing, pressure drop across the primary equipment
will have minimal effect on differential across the secondary
circuit. The alphabetical markings in Figure 3 are for valves,
check or motorized as best suited for the situation. Three-way
valves will minimize DP variation, but compared to two-way,
they increase first cost, sacrifice operating efficiency and their
flow characteristic is not conducive to refined coil control.
Modulating flow in coils presents a distribution problem
within the coil. Using a circulating pump to maintain constant
volume and arranging the control valve to modulate water tem-
perature can avoid freeze-up and stratification on the leaving
side of the coil or coil bank. Another option is lowering heating
or raising cooling water temperature. A third is a coil modulator
(manifold type valve) that changes the effective heat transfer
surface of the coil on an individual tube basis that ensures uni-
form water distribution. The modulator is designed to bypass
part of the tube length for heating and provides circuiting op-
tions for cooling to effect greater heat transfer per unit of water
to increase DT optimizing boiler and chiller efficiency.
When the sole purpose of a coil is preheating outside air, it
is advisable to install coils sized for low temperature rise, 20°F
to 25°F (11°C to 14°C), in series to avoid overheating. Modula-
tion is not recommended in this case. Each coil should be two
position (on/off) controlled by an entering air sensor set at 35°F
(1.5°C). Preheat coils generally are used to eliminate problems
associated with mixing outside and return air. “Do Averaging
Sensors Average?” in ASHRAE Journal2 has diagrams showing
stratification in a conventional air-handling unit using a standard
mixing plenum. Stratification is an inherent characteristic of two
airstreams entering a plenum through separate ducts with louver
or parallel blade dampers regulating volume. Averaging sensors
provide a false sense of security with regard to air mixing and coil
freeze protection. Average does not protect the coldest point.
Mixing outside and return air should be a priority to ensure
uniform distribution of ventilation air throughout the system. If
thorough mixing takes place, no need exists to preheat outside air
for conventional air-handling systems. Based on 20% outside air,
it would be an unusual weather occurrence that produces a mixed
air temperature below 40°F (4°C). Since conventional mixing
plenums do not mix, two airstreams’ stratification is an inherent
problem for coil freeze and air quality protection. Centrifugal fans
act as a centrifuge and tend to separate airstreams of different
Figure 3: Primary-secondary pumping.
Figure 4: Air distributor type mixing plenum.
54
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
February 2007
density (temperature). Two airstreams entering the fan parallel to
the shaft produce three streams leaving with the two outside being
nearly the same and the center noticeably different depending on
the differential between the entering airstreams.
To ensure proper mixing of two airstreams, a distributor-type
mixing box should be used. In a conventional mixing plenum, two
airstreams have a single interface area. The distributor (Figure 4)
divides the airstreams into alternating layers of outside and return
air multiplying the interface area to facilitate rapid heat transfer and
mixing as the air enters the fan plenum and passes through the fan.
For example, at 25% outside and 75% return air, each 8 in. (20.3
cm) section will be delivering a 2 in. (5.08 cm) layer of outside
air and a 6 in. (15.24 cm) layer of return air alternately across the
mixed air plenum, facilitating rapid heat transfer, thorough mixing
and ensuring uniform distribution of ventilation air.
Conclusions
Modulating coil heat transfer is the basis of accurate and
stable space temperature control. Precise modulation requires
sizing control valves at design pump head. The valve drop
should not be included in the design head calculation.
• Variable speed pumps should be applied in a manner that
minimizes differential pressure variation at the terminal
control valve, not to vary it.
• Owners and occupants realize the greatest benefit from
skillful engineering during part load operation, below
50% of design, which represents the majority of total
operating hours.
• The best investment an owner can make and the best as-
surance a designer can provide that the system will be
efficient, reliable, controllable, simple to operate and less
expensive to maintain is a piping system sized to facilitate
low head pumping.
References
1. Taylor, S.T. and J. Stein. 2002. “Balancing variable flow hydronic
systems.” ASHRAE Journal 44(10):17–24.
2. Avery, G. 2002. “Do averaging sensors average?” ASHRAE
Journal 44(12):42–43.
Bibliography

Water System. ASHRAE Journal 32(10):30–31.
Shiming, D. 2002 “Sizing replacement chiller plants.” ASHRAE
Journal 44(6):47–49.
Zhang, Z.J. 1993. “Another look at traditional control valve sizing
practice.” ASHRAE Journal 35(2):38.
1970. “Sizing control valves for hydronic systems.” Building Sys-
tems Design May.

JOURNAL

F e b r u a r y , 1 9 9 7
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, February 1997. © Copyright 1997 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers,
Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
Challenges in Electrical Power Measurements
Of Adjustable Speed Drives and Motors
By Alexander Domijan, Jr., Ph.D.
Member ASHRAE
and Dariusz Czarkowski, Ph.D.
1 Introduction
he numerous advantages of adjustable speed drives
(ASDs) have caused a rapid growth of their applications in
HVAC equipment. Power electronic converters, which are the
heart of ASDs, shape current and voltage waveforms both at
power-grid--ASD and ASD--motor terminals making the wave-
forms nonsinusoidal. The character of waveform distortion is
different for various types of ASDs [1]. Accurate measurements
of electrical power under nonsinusoidal waveform conditions
pose a serious challenge for engineers and scientists. The power
information is, however, very important for efficiency optimiza-
tion of HVAC equipment.
There are two kinds of problems associated with power
measurements. One is to select an instrument best suited to
precisely measure electrical quantities under a particular type
of distortion, the other is to interpret correctly the results. In
the instrumentation area, this paper presents the principle of
operation of modern solid-state power analyzers, their param-
eters and technical limitations. Accuracy of three selected
meters in ASD efficiency measurements is experimentally
investigated. The pitfalls of the fast Fourier transform (FFT)
method used for harmonic analysis are discussed. Regarding
the theoretical interpretation of the measurements, power def-
initions under nonsinusoidal conditions are reviewed. Particu-
lar stress is put on reactive power, compensation of which
increases equipment efficiency. Nowadays, there is no com-
monly agreed upon reactive power definition. Several defini-
tions are presented, discussed and illustrated by an ASD
waveform example. Conclusions and suggestions for further
research are given.
2 Equipment for Power Measurement and Analysis
The contemporary power meters and analyzers are digital
solid-state instruments. They sample current and voltage wave-
forms at a very high frequency reaching hundreds of kilohertz.
The samples are converted to a digital representation with ana-
log-to-digital (A/D) converters and stored in an internal memory.
The stored information is processed by digital signal processing
(DSP) integrated circuits and displayed, printed out, or sent to
external devices. DSP circuits can perform a great variety of
mathematical operations that allows for obtaining characteristic
parameters of acquired voltages and currents. Modern power
analyzers are able to provide the user with rms and mean values
of voltage and current, real power, reactive power, apparent
power, power factor, true power factor, voltage and current crest
factor, frequency, peak current and voltage, peak inrush current,
and many more. Units equipped with an integrator function dis-
play energy consumption, maximum demand, reactive energy
consumption, and current consumption.
Frequency domain analysis is an invaluable tool for analysis
of distorted waveforms. Most of the contemporary power ana-
lyzers can calculate 50 harmonics in accordance with the
IEEE-519 standard. Knowledge of harmonic content is impor-
tant not only for investigating the behavior of various electric
power apparatus but also for determining the accuracy of the
measurement [2]. The accuracy that reaches 0.05% at frequen-
cies close to the fundamental, degrades with an increase in fre-
quency.
The
important
topic
of
frequency
domain
decomposition is discussed in the next section.
Modern power meters are equipped with a number of analog
and digital inputs and outputs. For instance, analog inputs from
torque and speed transducers are used for determining a motor
or a drive efficiency. Analog outputs allow for displaying mea-
sured waveforms on an external oscilloscope. Data can be dig-
itally sent to an external printer or computer. Finally, a power
analyzer can be remotely controlled through digital inputs.
Although contemporary power meters and analyzers are
powerful specialized microcomputers, the increasing variety
and operating frequency of power electronic devices, ASDs
among them, call for a careful choice in selecting a meter that
suits the specific needs of a user. Table 1 presents basic perfor-
About the Authors
Alexander Domijan, Jr., Ph.D., was raised in Boonton, N.J. Dr.
Domijan obtained his B.S. degree in electrical engineering from the
University of Miami, his master’s degree in electric power engi-
neering from the Renssealaer Polytechnic Institute, Troy, N.Y.,
and the doctorate in electrical engineering from the University of
Texas at Arlington. He is presently a professor of electrical and
computer engineering at the University of Florida. He also is direc-
tor and founder of the Florida Power Affiliates and the Power Qual-
ity and Power Electronics Laboratory.
Dariusz Czarkowski received a master’s degree in electronics en-
gineering and the master’s degree in electrical engineering from
University of Mining and Metallurgy, Cracow, Poland. He re-
ceived a master’s degree in electrical engineering from Wright
State University. Dr. Czarkowski graduated with a Ph.D. from the
Florida Power Affiliates Program at the University of Florida and
is presently an assistant professor at the Polytechnic University in
New York.
T
F e b r u a r y , 1 9 9 7
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
33
mance parameters that can be expected
from power analyzers which are cur-
rently on the market.
Three meters, each from a different
manufacturer,
were
experimentally
tested in efficiency measurements of
three types of ASDs used in HVAC
equipment, namely, PWM induction
drive,
dc-brushless
drive,
and
switched-reluctance drive. Meter 1 was
a several years old design which is pres-
ently considered the workhorse in the
industry. The maximum sampling fre-
quency for the 3-phase configuration is
95 kHz. Meter 2 was a new model
claimed by its manufacturer to have a unique anti-aliasing fil-
ter. The maximum sampling frequency of this unit is 104 kHz.
Meter 3, also a new design, has the maximum sampling fre-
quency of 70 kHz. A multichannel PC-based data acquisition
system was used as a reference meter. The sampling frequency
of the reference meter was selected to be 250 kHz. The refer-
ence meter was calibrated using a NIST traceable standard
with an accuracy of approximately 35 parts per million. Table
2 gives the efficiency of the drives obtained with the reference
meter and percentage errors of the three tested commercial
meters. It can be seen that only the efficiency of the dc-brush-
less drive was measured accurately by Meter 2 and Meter 3.
This poor performance of the commercial meters can be
attributed mainly to their too low sampling frequency. Other
factors contributing to poor performance are the use of the
equivalent waveform method, which should be abandoned in
ASD work. The fundamental power definitions used to process
the waveforms may not be appropriate under nonsinusoidal
and unbalanced conditions. Further, there is difficulty in syn-
chronizing independently to either input or output frequency,
which is important in efficiency measurements of motor-drive
systems when using multiphase meters.
3 Spectrum Analysis of Voltage and
Current Waveforms
3.1 Fast Fourier Transform
In modern digital power meters and analyzers, the measured
voltage or current signal is sampled N times per period.
According to Fourier analysis, the samples of the time domain
signal x(t) can be expressed as
(1)
where
(2)
The Nyquist criterion states that the sampling frequency
should be at least twice the highest harmonic frequency of the
signal. However, to obtain accurate results with not a very long
sampling interval, it is usually suggested in practice that the
sampling frequency be about 10 times the highest harmonic
frequency. Per the IEEE 519 standard, measurements of up to
50 harmonic components are required. This implies that for
the fundamental 60 Hz component, instruments with a sam-
pling frequency of at least 30 kHz should be used.
A fast procedure for calculating coefficients X(fk) is known
as the fast Fourier transform (FFT). To speed up the calcula-
tion process, the FFT algorithm takes advantage of the peri-
odic character of terms e−j2πkn/Nand uses so called ‘butterfly’
matrix computational techniques [3]. This name has its origin
in a graphical representation of the FFT flow chart where com-
putation of each coefficient X(fk) reminds one of the wings of
a butterfly. The “butterfly” operations are performed by dedi-
cated integrated circuits.
3.2 Sources of Errors in FFT
Application of instruments that use the FFT for signal pro-
cessing without understanding the limitations of this method
may lead to erroneous results. The FFT method assumes that
the analyzed waveforms are periodic and stationary, the sam-
pling interval must be exactly an integer multiple of a wave-
form period, and the waveform does not contain so called
“non-integer harmonics”, that is, components with frequencies
that are not integer multiples of the fundamental frequency.

3 4
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
F e b r u a r y , 1 9 9 7
Obviously, the Nyquist sampling criterion must also be satis-
fied. Three major pitfalls associated with the FFT can be dis-
tinguished [4]: aliasing, picket-fence effect, and leakage.
3.2.1 Aliasing
Aliasing occurs when high-frequency harmonics of the sig-
nal are translated by FFT into low-frequency components.
This problem can be alleviated by increasing the sampling fre-
quency. However, even when Nyquist the criterion is satisfied,
aliasing may still occur. In this case, it is referred to as
“pseudoaliasing.” Harmonic analyzers are equipped with
low-pass antialiasing filters. However, the nonideality of those
filters causes distortions of the measured signal. Another anti-
aliasing technique is comparing the measurement results at
different sampling frequencies. This solution adds greatly to
the complexity of meters.
3.2.2 Picket-Fence Effect
The picket-fence effect takes place when the analyzed
waveform contains frequency components that are not integer
multiples of the fundamental frequency. Such “non-integer
harmonics” may remain unrecognized by a meter or their con-
tribution to the power transfer may be erroneously accounted
for. Non-integer harmonics are present when a motor-drive
system is not completely in its steady state. There were also
reports on a presence of substantial non-integer harmonics in
the steady-state of, for instance, a synchronous motor supplied
from a load commutated six-pulse inverter.
3.2.3 Leakage
The leakage effect describes the apparent spreading of energy
from one frequency to adjacent ones. It occurs when the sampling
interval is not exactly an integer multiple of a waveform period. In
reality, the frequency of the fundamental component has some
fluctuations that cause a truncation of the signal not exactly at the
end of a cycle when the sampling interval is set constant based on
the expected value of the fundamental frequency. Leakage is
reduced by applying window techniques, that is, multiplying the
analyzed waveform by an appropriate time domain function.
There is, however, no optimum window shape for all applications.
4 Power Definitions
4.1 Single-Phase Sinusoidal Situation
Consider the sinusoidal voltage
and cur-
rent
, where V and I are the rms values
of voltage and current, respectively. The instantaneous power,
describing the rate at which the electric energy is transferred to
or from the circuit, is given by
(3)
where P is the averaged value of p known as active power
(4)
and Q is the reactive power defined as
(5)
The instantaneous power p(t) obeys the Principle of Energy
Conservation, i.e., in any electric circuit, the total instanta-
neous power is the sum of the instantaneous powers in all the
circuit components. The apparent power U = VI is equal to the
product of the rms voltage and the rms current. The phasor
power is given by
(6)
The amplitude of the phasor power
is
equal to the geometrical sum of the active power and the
reactive power. Hence, for the sinusoidal case U = S. The
efficiency of the system is expressed by the power factor PF =
P/S = cosφ.

component. Currently, P is calculated taking into account the
first 50 harmonics (per IEEE-519-1992). The definition given
by (7) is in full agreement with the physical interpretation that
the active power is the average value of the instantaneous
power. Hence, the active power definition does not stir any
controversies among engineers and scientists.
Reactive Power: Various definitions of reactive power are
discussed in Section 4.2.2.
Apparent Power: In a single-phase two-wire circuit, the
apparent power is equal to the product of the rms voltage and
the rms current
(8)
Apparent power has the property of magnitude only. Its sign
is ambiguous, usually positive for convenience. The defini-
tions of apparent power in polyphase circuits are given in Sec-
tion 4.2.3.
Phasor Power: The phasor power is equal to the phasor sum
of the values of the phasor power for every harmonic
(9)
The amplitude S of the phasor power S is equal to the square
root of the squares of the active power and the reactive power
(10)
Distortion power: The distortion power D is defined as a
scalar quantity equal to the square root of the difference of the
squares of the apparent power and the amplitude of the phasor
power
(11)
The sign of the distortion power is not determined. Usually,
it is assumed to be the same as the sign of the active power.
Vector power: The vector power U is a vector which has the
active power, reactive power, and distortion power as its rect-
angular components
(12)
The magnitude of the vector power
is equal to the apparent power.
Power factor: Power factor PF = P/U is the ratio of active
power to apparent power. The displacement power factor is the
cosine of the angular difference between the fundamental com-
ponents of voltage and current.
4.2.2 Reactive Power
This section presents only the most recognized and impor-
tant definitions of reactive power. For more definitions, e.g.,

Kimbark’s reactive power, Depenbrock’s reactive power, and
Enslin and Van Wyk’s reactive power, please, refer to [7] and
[5]. This plethora of reactive power definitions indicates how
controversial and important this topic is.
First harmonic reactive power: The first harmonic reactive
power is defined as
(13)
where V1, I1, and φ1 are the rms voltage, current, and phase
shift angle of the first harmonic component. The first har-
monic reactive power is important in analyzing energy rela-
tions at fundamental frequency with the rest of the frequencies
treated as noise. However, this approach is not valid for switch-
ing loads, i.e., power electronics equipment.
Budeanu’s reactive power: The most common definition of
reactive power is called the Budeanu’s reactive power
(14)
This definition was intended to be analogous to the defini-
tion of nonsinusoidal active power P. However, the Budeanu’s
reactive power has no physical meaning and may lead to false
conclusions. Therefore, it has been suggested that the use of
the Budeanu’s reactive power should be abandoned [6]. The
above mentioned reference gives several very educational
examples of the deficiencies in Budeanu’s reactive power.
The circuit of Figure 1 is based on one of those examples.

, where ω =
2π × 60 rad/s. When the switch is OPEN, the apparent power
supplied to the series combination of R = 1Ω and L = 10Mh is
S = 2.0456 kVA and the Budeanu’s reactive power is QB =
1.9627 kVA. When the switch is CLOSED, the apparent
power is S = 1.8609 kVA and the Budeanu’s reactive power is
compensated to zero by a shunt capacitance C = 235.8. Despite
the total compensation of Budeanu’s reactive power, the power
factor of the source PF = P/S changes only slightly from
0.1234 to 0.1357 which makes the Budeanu’s definition mean-
ingless.
Fryze’s reactive power: This and following definitions of
reactive power are based on the concept of current splitting
[5]. Fryze decomposed the load current into two components
i = ia + ir, called the active and reactive currents. The reactive
current is the component that does not contribute to the net
energy transfer. The active, reactive, and apparent power are
defined in terms of the rms values of currents ia and ir, and
the rms voltage V
(15)
Qf always has a positive sign. Leading and lagging power fac-
tors cannot be determined. Compensation of Fryze’s reactive
power can be accomplished by injection of the compensating
current −ir.
Sharon’s reactive power: Sharon’s reactive power definition
is based on the concept of a reactive current that can be com-
pensated by an optimal combination of capacitances and
inductances. The Sharon’s reactive power is expressed by

Kusters-Moore reactive power: For the purpose of this def-
inition, the load current i = ia + iqC + IqCr is decomposed into
three components: active, capacitive reactive, and residual
reactive currents. The power of the resulting equivalent capac-
itor given by
(17)
and was suggested by Kusters and Moore as the definition of
the reactive power. The Kusters-Moore’s reactive power is a
signed quantity. If the capacitive reactive power happens to be
positive then there is no equivalent capacitor that can compen-
sate it. In this case, an inductive reactive power can be defined.
4.2.3 Apparent power in polyphase circuits
Applications of apparent power: The apparent power is
used in power engineering for several purposes ([8] p. 37):
1. Revenue metering.
2. To define the maximum ratings of electrical apparatus
such as transformers, generators, motors, etc.
3. As a meaningful quantity in the general theory of electri-
cal networks.
4. As a reference value for power factor calculations.
5. As a reference value for network analysis on a per unit
basis.
Definitions of apparent power: In the IEEE Standard Dic-
tionary [9], the apparent power is defined as a magnitude of the
vector power
(18)
where P is the active power P = ΣkPk,QB is the Budeanu’s reac-
tive power (see its definition and comments in Section 4.2.2)
ΣkQB, k, D is the distortion power D = ΣkDk, k denotes the k-th
phase, and sign (D) = sign (P).
Another quantity used, although not defined explicitly, in
the IEEE Standard Dictionary
(19)
A physical interpretation can be assigned to the apparent
power. It can be said that the apparent power is a maximum
active power which can be delivered by the source to a
non-reactive load [8]. There are several definitions of such an
apparent power possible for a polyphase system. The system
can be considered as a whole or as a sum of separate phases.
In a “phase-by-phase” approach the maximum active power is
Pmax = ΣkPmax, k with conditions Ik
2 = constant, and Vk

stant, where Ik and Vk are rms values of current and voltage of the
k-th phase, respectively. The apparent power of the system turns

(20)

39
induction motor at rated load and 1800 rpm speed. The input of
the drive is a 3-phase rectifier. (The University of Florida
Power Quality Lab is the only facility in the world that can gen-
erate high power synchronized arbitrary and unbalanced three-
phase voltage and current waveforms to apply to devices under
test and development—such as ASDs, motors, transformers,
meters, relays, etc. [11]). It can be seen in Figure 2(a) that the
input voltage to the rectifier is practically sinusoidal. The pul-
sating input current is rich in odd harmonics, especially 5th,
7th, 11th, 13th, 17th, and 19th. However, for both input voltage
and current, the Fourier analysis up to 50 harmonics is suffi-
cient. The output of the drive is a 3-phase PWM inverter whose
phase voltage and current waveforms are depicted in Figure
2(b). The spectrum of the switched rectangular voltage extends
beyond 50 harmonics. Table 3 gives the values of apparent,
active, and various reactive powers calculated from the
recorded data under various load and speed conditions. It
should be stressed that the presented numbers are for a selected
single period of fundamental frequency. Since the system is in
a dynamic steady-state, no conclusions regarding, for instance,
the drive or the motor efficiency can be drawn. Efficiency
study requires averaging over a few tens of periods.
It can be seen in Table 3 that the values of different reactive
powers are spread over a wide range. The first harmonic reac-
tive power is close in value to the Budeanu’s reactive power
because for the presented cases the fundamental component
dominates either in voltage or in current spectrum. The Fryze’s
reactive power is usually displayed by contemporary power
analyzers as the reactive power measurement. The authors sub-
mit that it may be possible to further optimize motor drive effi-
ciencies via appropriate use of measurement equipment and
power definitions, and the authors are doing additional
research with this goal in mind.
5 Conclusions
Challenges in measurement of electrical power inputs to
variable speed motors and their solid-state power converters
have been presented. It becomes obvious that to perform an
accurate measurement, a high technical expertise in power
meters, their parameters, and limitations as well as a thorough
understanding of electrical power phenomena under distorted
waveform conditions is required from a user.
The topics presented address the principle of operation and
basic parameters of modern power meters and analyzers.
Experimental accuracy results of ASD efficiency measure-
ments by three commercial power meters are given. The
sources of measurement errors are pointed out with particular
emphasis on sampling frequency and harmonic analysis. It
should be noted that as measurement equipment manufactures
become more and more aware of difficulties in measurements
of highly nonsinusoidal signals they respond by continuously
improving their products. A very controversial topic of power
definitions for nonsinusoidal waveforms is reviewed and illus-
trated by examples.

Guide for Service to Equipment Sensitive to Momentary Voltage Dis-
turbances,” IEEE Standard 1250-1995, pp. 1-34.
20. D. Zaninelli, and A. Domijan, “IEC and IEEE Standards on
Harmonics and Comparisons,” Proceedings of the NSF Conference
on Unbundled Power Quality Services in the Power Industry, pp. 46-
52, November 17-19, 1996.

ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
February 2007
Using Molten-Carbonate Fuel Cells
For Distributed Generation
By Robert Zogg, P.E., Member ASHRAE; Eric Carlson; Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; and
James Brodrick, Ph.D.; Member ASHRAE
W
hen used in distributed generation (DG) applications,
fuel cells have the potential to save energy and
reduce emissions, and their inherent fuel-flexibility
also could help address energy shortage issues through energy
diversity.1,2 In addition, fuel cells have the potential to be
quieter, more reliable, and have lower maintenance costs than
most technologies used for DG.
This is the fourth in a series of articles on fuel cells for DG
applications. It addresses molten-carbonate fuel cells (MCFCs).
Earlier articles addressed PEM fuel cells,3 solid-oxide fuel
cells,4 and phosphoric-acid fuel cells.5
Characteristics and Development Status
MCFCs are high-temperature fuel cells (operating at 600°C
to 700°C [1,100°F to 1,300°F]) that use a molten-carbonate-
salt electrolyte retained in a ceramic matrix.6,7 As shown in
Figure 1, MCFCs recycle carbon dioxide from the exhaust
stream to maintain the electrolyte. Unlike low-temperature fuel
cells (such as PEM fuel cells), MCFCs can reform (convert to
hydrogen) fuels such as natural gas within the fuel-cell stack,
lowering system cost and com-
plexity. Due to their high toler-
ance for carbon monoxide and
carbon dioxide, developers are
optimistic that MCFCs will be
able to internally reform even
gasified coal, assuming that
sulfur, particulates, and other
impurities can be managed.
Some developers are using,
however, multistage reform-
ers, including a small, external
reformer and several reformer
zones within the fuel-cell stack.
The external reformer also traps
sulfur, greatly reducing the pos-
sibility of sulfur contamination
of the stack.
There is one major MCFC
developer/manufacturer in the
U.S., one in Italy, and several
in Japan, targeting generation
capacities of 250 kW to mul-
tiple megawatts.8,9 The U.S. manufacturer and its international
partners have more than 50 customer sites, with more than 124
GWh generated globally.9 The same manufacturer claims 90%
availability (fleet-wide) in 2005, and projects 95% availability
in 2007.9 On the other hand, even 95% availability implies that
the system cannot be called upon to operate during more than
400 hours per year, which may be unacceptable in some DG
applications.
MCFC’s high stack temperatures and a highly corrosive elec-
trolyte impact stack life. Developers are exploring corrosion-
resistant materials and cell designs that increase life without
degrading performance. Early installations used stacks having
life expectancies of at least three years. Next-generation stacks
are expected to last five years.
Energy and Emissions Considerations
Typical MCFC electric generation efficiencies are in the range
of 40% to 50% (higher heating value), corresponding to about
45% to 55% based on lower heating value.10 Efficiencies vary
depending on manufacturer and capacity. MCFCs reject heat at a
relatively high temperature, mak-
ing it feasible to add a bottoming
cycle (typically a gas turbine or
microturbine), boosting electric
generation efficiencies to the
range of 60% to 70% (higher
heating value), corresponding
to about 65% to 75% based on
lower heating value. These effi-
ciencies compare favorably with
the national average efficiency
of grid-generated electricity—
31.5% (higher heating value)
in 2004, including transmission
and distribution losses.11 There-
fore, MCFCs can save energy
compared to using grid-supplied
electricity. High heat rejection
temperatures also make MCFC
good candidates for Combined
Heat and Power (CHP) systems,
where reject heat is used to sup-
ply building thermal loads.

The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, February 2007. © Copyright 2007 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is
presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
64
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
February 2007
As with all fuel-cell technologies,
MCFC will realize dramatic emissions re-
ductions, with roughly order-of-magnitude
reductions in both SO2 and NOx emissions
compared to the current national-average
generation mix.
Cost Challenges
The U.S.-based MCFC manufacturer
reports cost reductions for sub-MW-scale
plants from about $10,000/kW in 2003
to $4,600/kW in 2006, and target costs
of $3,200/kW to $3,500/kW in 2007 for
2 MW plants.9 The manufacturer suggests that costs can
decrease another 25% to 30% if annual production levels
increase to 50 MW. These costs are high relative to con-
ventional DG technologies, which tend to cost $600/kW to
$1,000/kW.12
As discussed earlier, developers continue to address MCFC
stack life and durability issues. Stack life is a key factor in de-
termining MCFC operating costs. For example, Figure 2 shows
Cost of Electricity (COE) projections for MCFC as a function
of stack life, assuming an installed cost of $2,500/kW (achiev-
able only through further cost reductions and higher-volume
production) and that stack replacement costs account for 40%
of the total system installed cost. Natural-gas prices used are
national averages for 2005.13 Extending stack life from three
to five years lowers COE by about 12%. If stack life can be
increased to 10 years, COE decreases by 20%.
Promising DG Applications
Despite the high cost of MCFC compared to conventional DG
technologies, its high electric generation efficiencies and ultra-
low emissions may help justify its use in DG applications where
grid-supplied electricity is particularly expensive and where low
emissions are valued. Its capacity range is best suited for large-
commercial, institutional, and industrial DG applications.

power for residences.” ASHRAE Journal 47(7):142 – 143.

Program, U.S. Department of Energy, Washington, D.C.
Due to their high tolerance for carbon monoxide and carbon di-
oxide, developers are optimistic that MCFCs will be able to inter-
nally reform even gasified coal, assuming that sulfur, particulates,
and other impurities can be managed.

ASHRAE Journal

March 2007
Photo: Aéroports de Montréal
Heating Montréal’s Airport
A high-efficiency heating plant was installed as the existing one could no longer keep pace with the airport’s growth.
I
n 2000, Aéroports de Montréal (ADM) expanded its existing air
terminal to cope with increasing passenger traffic. The overall
surface area is doubled, which meant that the existing plant could
no longer meet the needs of the terminal.
Jacques Lagacé, P.Eng., and Pierre Roussel,
P.Eng., from the firm Bouthillette Parizeau, are
partners in Consortium Bouthillette Parizeau/
Pageau Morel/Groupe HBA Inc. in Montreal, QC,
Canada. They won first place in the 2007 ASHRAE
Technology Awards.
The original air terminal was built in
1960. A common practice then was to
provide heating energy to the terminal by
a remote central heating plant located away
from the runways and control tower. This
was done to prevent the vapor plume, given
off by exhaust combustion gases during
low outdoor temperatures, from hindering
activities of air traffic controllers. These
vapor plumes commonly are formed by the
condensation of water droplets in suspen-
sion in subcooled combustion gases.
The heating plant was used to generate
high temperature water (HTW) (400°F
[204°C]) routed to the main terminal
building via an underground tunnel
almost one mile (1.6 km) long. Over
its course, the HTW piping was subject
to substantial heat losses, which were
dissipated to the exterior by means of
several ventilation systems. These losses
accounted for an appreciable loss of ef-
ficiency of the main plant. Furthermore,
the total capacity of the existing plant
proved to be insufficient to meet the
needs of the expansion. Considering the
heat losses, the considerable investments
necessary to increase the capacity of the
existing heating plant, and the limited
possibilities of installing energy-saving
By Jacques Lagacé, P.Eng., Member ASHRAE, and Pierre Roussel, P.Eng., Member ASHRAE
About the Authors
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2007. © Copyright 2007 American Society of Heat-
ing, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may
not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
March 2007
ASHRAE Journal
29
measures, the decision was made to build
a new thermal plant integrated into the
new construction. This only could be
done if a dependable plume elimination
system was provided.
Figure 1 shows the general arrange-
ment of the air terminal, including the
main thermal plant and the location of the
control tower. This plant houses all the
equipment required to produce chilled
water (CW) (44°F [6.7°C]), low tem-
perature water (LWT) (105°F [40.6°C])
and medium temperature water (MTW)
(240°F [115.6°C]). The equipment was
selected based on individual energy ef-
ficiency and heat recovery potential to
keep operating expenses at a minimum.
Four new chillers providing a total
cooling capacity of 2,260 tons (7946 kW)
with an average power demand of
0.53 kW/ton (0.15 kW/kW) are comple-
mented by the existing chillers and are
connected to a common chilled water distribution loop extend-
ing more than 1.3 miles (2.1 km) in length.
The new chillers are connected in series by pairs to increase
the temperature differential to 18°F (10°C). The first chillers
(1,330 ton [4676 kW]) reduce the chilled water temperature
from 60°F to 49°F (15.6 to 9.4°C). The second chillers (930 ton
[3270 kW]) lower the temperature from 49°F to 42°F (9.4 to
5.6°C). This 18°F (10°C) temperature differential allows the
reduction of the chilled water flow rate, and consequently, the
size of distribution piping.
The heat generated by the first 1,330 ton (4676 kW)
chillers is released to the atmosphere using dedicated
cooling towers. The heat from the second 930 ton
(3270 kW) chillers is reclaimed (17,080 MBtu/h or
5004 kW) to generate part of the LTW. Although when
heating loads are low or nonexistent, the heat is rejected,
partly or completely through the cooling towers.
A schematic showing the principle of production of heat
carrying fluids summarizes the complexity of the installation
(Figure 2). Heat distribution equipment using low temperature
water are commonly found to be heating coils, heat plate ex-
changers (for 100% fresh air systems), small unit heaters and
a few convectors. This low temperature water is generated by
reclaiming the heat from the chillers or other heat recovery
equipment and is supplemented, if necessary, by injection
of medium temperature water. Injection takes place at local
pumping stations.
The terminal’s main source of heating uses medium tempera-
ture hot water. Equipment having large heating loads such as
air curtains, vestibules or garage doors in the luggage handling
facilities is connected to the medium temperature network.
Medium temperature water is produced by four 450 hp
(4414 kW) boilers (water tube type) heating the water from
165°F to 240°F (74°C to 115.6°C) at full load conditions. Each
boiler is equipped with a dual fuel burner (natural gas and Num-
ber 2 oil), providing the possibility of obtaining combustion
efficiencies of 82% with natural gas and 84% with oil.
The main constraint for the new boiler room was that the
plume produced by the exhaust gases during cold weather
operation must be invisible, so as not to hinder the activities
of the air traffic controllers. This plume, usually dense and
opaque, is caused by condensation of vapor found in exhaust
gases when mixed with cold and/or humid outside air. In
standard boiler operation, this water vapor is exhausted
to the atmosphere and gives a net efficiency value that
reflects the difference between the higher and lower
heating values of the fuel used.
As shown in Figure 1, the proximity of the boil-
ers’ exhaust stacks to the air control tower presented
a challenge.
After several analyses, the piping diagram and equipment
used for the treatment of exhaust gases from boilers and energy
recovery are summarized in Figures 3 and 4.
The plume reduction process is complex. The first step is to
run the boilers’ flue gases (±340°F [±171°C]) through the direct
contact economizer. Water, called gray water, is sprinkled in
direct contact with the flue gas. The gray water is cooled from
140°F to 50°F (60°C to 10°C) by passing it through a series
of heat plate exchangers. The major part of the heat recovered
is supplied to the low temperature water loop through a dedi-
cated heat plate exchanger Ex1. Heat plate exchanger Ex2 is
supplied with chilled water at 44°F (6.7°C) Therefore, the
temperature of saturated gas leaving this exchanger will be
60°F (15.6°C). Technical data for the exchanger requires that
effluent gases’ temperature averages 10°F (5.6°C) higher than
the gray water temperature. Heat given off to the chilled water

Figure 1: General arrangement of Aéroports de Montréal.
30
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
loop is recovered by the chillers capable
of heat recovery. The overall efficiency of
the combustion process obtained amounts
to 99%. The direct contact economizer al-
lows reclamation of a large part of the heat
contained in the flue gases, which is usu-
ally lost to the atmosphere (4,270 MBtu/h
or 1251 kW per boiler).
The primary purpose of the gray water
circulated in the direct contact economizer
is to absorb the latent and sensible heat
contained in the hot exhaust gas from
the boilers. In doing so, it also absorbs
a large part of pollutants that otherwise
would be released in the atmosphere. An
automatic chemical treatment system
neutralizes the acidic portion of the grey
water before being rejected to the sewers.
30
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
Figure 3: Piping diagram and equipment used for energy recovery and the treatment of exhaust gases from boilers.
Figure 2: Principle of production of heat carrying fluids.
The direct contact economizers act as air scrubbers for hot flue
gases and helps reduce by a significant amount greenhouse gas
emissions.
The 60°F (15.6°C) saturated flue gases leaving the direct con-
tact economizer have to be mixed with hot dry air so the mixture
can be exhausted to the atmosphere with minimal chances of
32
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
standard equipment rather than costly, complicated custom-built
units. The main expenditure for the exhaust treatment system
is the direct contact economizer. Considering standard equip-
ment is installed, maintenance is simplified and straightforward
since the operations and maintenance staff is familiar with such
equipment. All four groups of “boiler–direct contact econo-
mizer–blower–pumps–heat exchangers–dilution systems” are
identical. That concept provides huge
flexibility in operation because only
three of the four groups are sufficient
to supply the heating demand. At
any time, a group can be shut down
to be inspected or to maintain part
of the system or because a problem
occurs.
An airport must maintain services
in almost any situation. To prevent
the loss of the heating and cooling
plant, a large part of the controls
operation can be carried out directly
on the equipments, even if the direct
digital controls automated system
is down. When the heat generated
by the heating plant exceeds the
demand, the heat is rejected into the fresh air system. This
avoids using conventional cooling towers to reject the heat, and
it allows more fresh air to be brought into the building. This
results in better indoor air quality, better energy efficiency and
reduction of the possibility of creating a vapor plume associated
with using cooling towers.
