forming a visible plume (under normal winter conditions).
The psychrometric chart seen in Figure 5 represents the pro-
cess lines in the exhaust flue gases and each point of interest
mentioned is as follows.
Line
on the psychrometric chart is tangential to the satu-
ration line at Point A corresponding to the expected minimum
outside temperature (–20°F [–29°C]), which is considered the
theoretical no plume process line.
Any moist air process taking place
along this line will not be subject
to vapor condensation formation at
Point A, preventing the formation of
a vapor plume.
Point C represents the gas leav-
ing the direct contact economizer
after being cooled by heat plate
exchangers, which is 10°F (5.6°C)
higher than the grey water supply.
Point B represents the temperature
of the dilution air system (100%
transferred air, dry and heated at
100°F [37.8°C]).
The mixture of gases released to
the atmosphere (humid exhaust gases
with dry air) corresponds to Point E on the chart and will pre-
vent the creation of a vapor plume. The proportion of dilution
air (line
) to total evacuated gases (line
) is high (85%).
Considering that the dilution air has to be heated before being
mixed and then exhausted, leads to an important reduction of
the overall efficiency of the process. Therefore, the process line
was considered as the theoretical no-plume process line.
After discussions and results from wind
studies, it was agreed that a certain visible
plume could be tolerated, as long as this
plume was not opaque. A proportion of
60/40 (line
) shows on the psychromet-
ric chart an area where condensation can
take place. This fog will be apparent only if
outside air is still and will be immediately
dispersed if any wind is present.
Psychrometric calculations show that
7,650 cfm of 100°F (3610 L/s of 37.8°C)
air is the optimal amount of dry air to be
added to flue gases. Heat exchanger (Ex3)
is supplied with LTW on one side and a
50% glycol solution on the other side,
giving a total exchange of 2,772 MBtu/h
(812 kW). The glycol solution supplies
the heating coil that strictly supplies dry
heated outside air for dilution.
The primary philosophy of the heating
plant concept is to use coherent and simple
systems along with standard components
to lower the cost of operation and main-
tenance. The components installed are
Figure 4: Flue gas plume reduction installation (direct
contact economizer at left and heat plate exchangers
for heat recovery of gray water at right).
46
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
Reducing Energy Costs
With Condensing Boilers
& Heat Recovery Chillers
A
condensing boiler saves energy by reducing hot water system
 design temperatures. It also makes it possible to use a heat
recovery chiller. Together, they make a great combination on hot
and chilled water systems that have cooling load in the winter and
heating requirements in the summer. They make possible the elimi-
nation of year-round operation of many chiller and boiler plants by
using lower design temperatures for hot water heating systems.
About the Author
James B. (Burt) Rishel, P.E., is owner of Pumping
Solutions in Cincinnati. Benny (Ben) L. Kincaid
is Senior Applications Engineer, Fluid & Thermal
Systems, Indianapolis.
For many years, the minimum al-
lowable temperature for gas-fired, hot
water boilers was around 140°F (60°C).
Any temperature less than that caused
condensing and corrosion in the boilers.
Most of the boilers then were cast-iron
and steel. The steel boilers were fire tube
or water tube. The dew point for the flue
gases from the combustion of natural
gas is around 135°F to 138°F (57°C to
59°C), depending upon the amount of
methane, ethane, etc., in the natural gas.
These flue gases contain carbon dioxide
and water vapor, so when condensation
forms in a boiler, these two elements
combine to form carbonic acid. The
result is corrosion of the final tubes and
flue collectors, which are not designed
for it. This meant that the supply water
temperature to the heating system had to
be at least 160°F (71°C) and often as high
as 240°F (115°C).
Condensing Boilers
Unlike cast-iron and steel boilers, the
condensing boiler is designed to use the
By James B. (Burt) Rishel, P.E., Fellow/Life Member ASHRAE; and Benny (Ben) L. Kincaid
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2007. © Copyright 2007 American Society of Heat-
ing, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may
not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
March 2007
ASHRAE Journal
47
condensing as the means of achieving higher thermal efficien-
cies—more than 90% instead of as low as 75%. Figure 1 is a
typical thermal efficiency curve for a condensing boiler. The actual
efficiency of such a boiler should be secured from the manufacturer
whose boiler is under consideration for a particular project. The
maximum thermal efficiency for a non-condensing boiler is around
87% with careful control of the percentage of excess air required
for clean combustion. Condensing boilers are configured to accept
the condensation without damage, and with them, supply water
temperatures can be as low as 120°F or 130°F (49°C or 54°C) and
return water temperatures around 80°F to 90°F (27°C to 32°C).
Condensing boilers are more expensive than non-condens-
ing boilers, but there are redeeming factors that make them
economically feasible. The piping is much simpler since no
need exists for warm-up procedures that are necessary for
most non-condensing boilers. These include primary pumps,
a primary bypass, and a secondary three-way blending valve
with override control to allow the primary loop to warm
up to minimum boiler return operating temperature before
releasing the boiler to normal operation. These additional
costs eliminate much of the additional cost of the condens-
ing boilers themselves. The condensing boiler also allows the
water system designer to select the optimum water tempera-
tures for a particular heating system without concern for the
boiler construction or proper boiler return temperature. The
design temperature differential between the supply and return
temperatures can be selected to provide the most economical
answer for a specific installation.
Figure 2 describes the reduction in header size as this tem-
perature differential increases from 20°F to 100°F (11°C to
56°C). It is for a 1,000 MBtu/h (293 kW) system and shows
that 3 in. (75 mm) steel pipe headers would be recommended
at a 20°F (11°C) differential and only 2 in. (50 mm) steel pipe
at a 60°F (33°C) differential. This freedom of design reduces
the differential in first cost for the condensing boilers over that
for non-condensing boilers.
Using lower operating temperatures increases the cost of
heating coils on four-pipe systems with separate heating and
cooling coils. The contemporary two-pipe system for schools
and other buildings has one coil for both heating and cooling,
so the cost for coils does not increase when these lower tem-
peratures are used.
Benefits of Lower Water Temperatures
Using lower design water temperatures opens up a number
of opportunities to improve the overall operation of a building.
The most significant benefit results from the opportunity to
use the heat from chiller condensers. It is the largest source
of heat for recovery in the HVAC industry. Another source
of heat is flue gases emanating from all types of boilers. The
development of the condensing-type boiler has all but elimi-
nated the hot water boiler as a source of heat for recovery
since much of the water vapor from combustion is condensed
already. Steam boilers can be sources of heat recovery from
flue gases, and the cost of fuels may provide the impetus to
evaluate the economics of economizers on the stack connec-
tions of them. The costs of heat recovery were not conducive
to do so in the past.
New equipment, digital control, and sensible selection of
operating temperatures have resulted in heat recovery that
does make economic sense. No longer is it viewed as neces-
sary to have 180°F to 200°F (82°C to 93°C) water to heat
55°F to 70°F (13°C to 21°C) air to 90°F to 95°F (32°C to
35°C) air required by modern buildings with much improved
building envelopes. Heating is about the most simple heat
transfer process in our industry, since it is simply a sensible
heat transfer. Cooling, on the other hand, requires that both
sensible and latent heat transfer functions be provided and
controlled. If a heating design in modern systems requires a
temperature differential between entering water and leaving
air of 90°F (50°C) (180°F [82°C] water and 90°F [32°C] air),
why does cooling only require 10°F (5.5°C) (45°F [7°C] water
and 55°F [12.5°C] air)? Obviously, this is tradition for heating
air, not necessarily the most logical method. Also, over the
years, the first cost of heating coils and piping probably was
lowest for the 90°F (50°C) difference. With the cost of energy
increasing, operating costs will receive greater consideration
than in the past.
One word of caution about these reduced design water tem-
peratures. The temperature range of 80°F to 140°F (27°C to
60°C) is ideal for the amplification of Legionella bacteria. To
minimize the risk to the service personnel, it is recommended
a biocide be added to these water systems. Note: This also
includes the temperature range for most domestic hot water
systems serving showers.
Condensing boilers are more expensive
than non-condensing boilers, but there
are redeeming factors that make them
economically feasible.

Flow, gpm
The Logic of Heat Recovery
Is it not illogical to have a source of heat injected into a building
when an appreciable amount of heat is being rejected from that
building? Now that we can correct this imbalance with the condens-
ing boiler, we should proceed to develop systems that will enable
us to recover the heat from interior spaces via chiller condensers in
lieu of rejecting the heat through economizers (or so called “free-
cooling” air-side systems) to relief vents and reduce appreciably the
needed heat from boilers or direct-fired air heaters. The basic rule
of heat recovery is to use recovered heat wherever economically
possible before consuming electricity or fossil fuels.
Heat Recovery Chillers
The greatest advantage for condensing boilers appears when
they are coupled with heat recovery chillers to capture some of
the heat from building interiors, especially high-rise buildings
with water-side economizers, through the use of water-cooled
heat recovery chillers. The actual design of the heat recovery
chiller is not of concern here. What is important is to describe
the great benefit that can be secured when they are operated
with the condensing boiler. When the condensing boiler is
operated at 130°F (54°C), the condenser water from the heat
recovery chiller can be used to inject heat upstream of the
boilers and supplement or replace heat from the boilers.
Most of the contemporary chillers are designed to reject
their heat at temperatures around 95°F (35°C), while heat
recovery chillers have condenser water leaving temperatures
from 90°F to 135°F (32°C to 57°C). The kW/ton of water
cooled chillers is now in the range of 0.55 to 0.65 (0.16 to
0.18 kW/kW) while the heat recovery chiller has a higher
Figure 1 (left): Typical condensing boiler efficiencies. Figure 2 (right): Variation in flow and pipe size with temperature difference.
energy requirement of from 0.65 to 1.3 kW/ton (0.18 to 0.37
kW/kW). The actual energy rate is dependent upon the leaving
condenser water temperature. All of these figures are at full
load with 45°F (7°C) chilled water leaving the chiller.
Heat Available from Chillers
The heat available from a chiller is the 12,000 Btuh/ton (1
kW/kW) plus the thermal equivalent of the energy consumed
which, for electricity, is 3,412 Btuh/kW (1 kW/kW). Equation
1 provides this.
Heat available for recovery =
12,000 + (kW/ton · 3,412) Btu/ton·hour (1)
If the kW/ton for a water-cooled, heat recovery chiller is
around 0.9 (0.25), the heat equivalent would be 3,000 Btu/h
(0.9 kW) or 15,000 Btu/h (4.6 kW) total. For precise calcula-
tion, the actual kW/ton (kW/kW) for the heat recovery chiller
should be determined for the operating range of the chiller
when heat is being recovered.
Computing the Recovered Heat
Figure 3 describes the instrumentation required to compute
the heat balance for a heat recovery chiller. The flow and
temperature measurements are as follows:
Chilled water flow, in gpm:
f1
Condenser water flow, in gpm:
f2
Chilled water supply temperature:
t1, °F
Chilled water return temperature:
t2, °F
Condenser entrance temperature:
t3, °F
Condenser leaving temperature:
t4, °F
50
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
The condenser entrance temperature and flow transmitters
are installed before any return water from the cooling tower is
mixed with the water from the heat recovery processes. Tem-
perature transmitter t5 controls valve V2 to flow water through
the heat exchanger on the tower to maintain a maximum return
temperature to the condenser.
Piping for a Heat Recovery Chiller
Figure 4 provides the typical piping for a heat recovery
chiller. The use of the heat recovery will be shown in Figure 5.
The heat recovery chiller can be piped in series or parallel with
the other chillers. Valve V1 can be furnished if there may be a
reason for both series and parallel operation. Valve V2 provides
connection to a cooling tower or closed-circuit cooler in event
that the condenser heat is greater than that needed for the heat
recovery processes. Seldom is an open cooling tower connected
directly to the heat recovery chiller. The heat recovery circuit
should be closed through the use of a heat exchanger with a
cooling tower or a closed-circuit cooler.
Figure 5 describes the piping arrangement for a heat recov-
ery chiller providing heat to a hot water heating system and a
domestic water installation. The control of the heat recovery
chiller, through valve V3, would select either the domestic
water heat exchanger or the hot water heating system for re-
ceiving the recovered heat. Normally, the heat recovery chiller
would be base loaded for the cooling load in the summer. In
the winter, this chiller would produce only the cooling required
to satisfy the heating load. So, in the winter the amount of
heat available for recovery would depend upon the building
internal cooling load. This demonstrates that a need exists for
a careful evaluation of all of the data for a particular instal-
lation to determine the total effect of using the condensing
boiler with a heat recovery chiller.

Figure 3: Instrumentation for heat recovery chiller.
Heat Recovery Equations
The basic equations for heat recovery are simple, assuming
the specific heat of water to be 1.0 and the density to be 8.33
lb/gallon (1000 kg/m3), the multiplier for the product of the
flow times the temperature difference is 500. Therefore,
Heat recovered = 500 · f2 (t4 – t3) Btu/h
(2)
The simultaneous generation of chilled water in Btu/h is:
Cooling in Btu/h = 500 · f1 (t2 – t1) Btu/h
(3)
The total useful energy for the heat recovery chiller is a
combination of Equations 1 and 2.
Total useful energy = 500 [f2 (t4 – t3) + f1 (t2 – t1)]
(4)
Following is actual data derived from a 50 ton heat recovery
chiller used for alternative space heating as well as heating
domestic water. First, for space heating:
Chilled water flow, f1 = 100 gpm;
Condenser flow, f2 = 81 gpm;
Chilled water temperatures: t2 = 56°F; t1 = 44°F;
Condenser water temperatures: t4 = 130°F; t3 = 110°F;
kW input = 60 kW.
Inserting these values in Equation 4 provides the total useful
heat and cooling for the chiller when it is providing heat to
the hot water system at 130°F (54°C).
Total Useful Energy =
500[100(56 – 44) + 81(130 – 110)] = 1,410,000 Btu/h
March 2007
ASHRAE Journal
51
This compares to the 600,000 Btu/h (176 kW) for the 50
tons (176 kW) of cooling generated. The 810,000 Btu/h (237
kW) recovered would be equivalent to more than 1 million
Btu/h (239 kW) of fuel if generated by a non-condensing
boiler operating at 80% efficiency. Roughly, this is equivalent
to 1,000 ft3 (28 m3) of natural gas.
If the cooling had been accomplished with a standard chiller
operating at 0.56 kW/ton (0.16 kW/kW), the electrical con-
sumption would have been 28 kW instead of 60 kW for the
heat recovery chiller. At $0.08 per kWh, this would be a loss
of 0.08 × 32 or $2.56 while gaining $8 for 1,000 ft3 (28 m3)
of natural gas. (Note: Energy & Power Management magazine
quoted August 2005 natural gas prices in a range of $6.87 to
$11.10 for large office buildings.).
The greatest use of recovered heat is for domestic hot water for
heavy domestic water heading loads, such as hospitals, hotels, and
dormatories, since the leaving condenser water temperature for
the heat recovery chiller can be reduced to 90°F (32°C) with an
energy rate of 0.68 kW/ton (0.19 kW/kW). This can be demon-
strated by using actual information from a heat recovery chiller
that was providing 72 gpm (4.5 L/s) of condenser water to a heat
exchanger for domestic hot water. The chiller was producing
50 tons (176 kW) of cooling as well. At the same energy rates

Figure 5: Heat recovery piping.
for the earlier example, the electrical cost for the conventional
chiller again would be $2.24 and the boiler would be producing
720,000 Btu/h (211 kW) at a fuel rate of 900,000 Btu/h (264
kW) or around 900 ft3 (25 m3) of natural gas at a cost of $7.20.
The total cost would be $9.44 compared to $2.72 for the heat
recovery chiller for a savings of around $7 per hour.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
52
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
In this example, the actual useful heat is greater for the
domestic water, 720,000 Btu/h (211 kW), than the thermal
equivalent for the chilled water, 600 MBtu/h (176 kW). Not
included in this calculation is the reduction in cooling tower
energy and maintenance costs, since the cooling tower flow
has been reduced from around 150 gpm (9 L/s) to less than
80 gpm (5 L/s).
A particularly attractive application of the heat recovery
chiller is with an installation of air-cooled chillers whose
COP is around 2.9. The heat recovery chiller can increase
substantially the COP of the entire plant when recovering
heat from theses chillers.
There may be installations with sizeable domestic water loads
and little winter cooling that still justify the installation of heat
recovery chillers to defray the higher costs of natural gas or fuel
oil during the cooling season. Water storage tanks, particularly
for domestic water, may be an economic answer for extending
the hours of operation for the heat recovery chiller.
Obviously, these calculations of energy savings are specific
examples that should be supplanted by those based upon in-
formation from a particular installation. They do demonstrate
the economic possibilities of using the condensing boiler with
a heat recovery chiller. Possibly, an even greater savings is the
ability to shut down a boiler plant in the summer and a main
chiller plant in the winter.
Other heating loads can use recovered heat such as reheat
coils for tempering, as in hospital applications with con-
stant airflow or higher than normal minimum airflow rates
to maintain required pressure relationships, reheat coils for
dehumidification, natatorium water heating and space dehu-
midification, process water heating loads, makeup air heating,
snow melting, etc. Any number of uses can be connected to
a heat recovery chiller. For example, in higher heating water
design systems, it could be used for snow melting in the winter
and reheat in air handlers and VAV boxes in the summer.
Elimination of Non-freeze, Preheat Coils
An unusual circuit that is made possible with the condens-
ing boiler and the heat recovery chiller is the elimination of
preheat coils in the air-handling units. The condensing boiler
with operating temperatures below 140°F (60°C) made this
possible by preventing high water temperatures from reaching
the chiller evaporators. This circuit was developed by Tom
Durkin, P.E., Member ASHRAE, and Ben Kincaid.
Figure 6 describes a system with 150 tons (528 kW) of
cooling that achieves heating and cooling without preheat
coils. Outdoor air is provided for ventilation. This system has
condensing boilers and a heat recovery chiller. This shows the
circuiting and particular instrumentation for this operation.
The hot and chilled water piping is of the four-pipe design with
supply and return pipes for the hot water and chilled water.
The chilled water coils are designed for 44°F/56°F (7°C/13°C)
temperatures and hot water coils for 130°F/110°F (54°C/43°C)
temperatures. The system includes standard equipment such
as air separators, expansion tank and makeup water apparatus.
Valve V1 is provided to ensure minimum flow in the chiller
evaporator. Reheat is provided by coils in the air-handling units
for single-zone applications or terminal reheat coils for VAV
applications. Their heat source is the condenser water from
the heat recovery chiller augmented by heat from the boilers
as required. Actually, this application returns colder water than
usual to the condensing boilers when operating in the freeze-
protection mode. Since these boilers’ efficiency is improved by
lower return water temperatures and/or reduced load, this ap-
plication improves their efficiency. In this case, the return water
temperature is dropped from 110°F to 99°F (43°C to 37°C) with
an increase of around 1.5% in boiler efficiency.
The heating and cooling systems operate in a normal four
pipe system mode with the heat recovery chiller format until
the outside air temperature is at or below 35°F (2°C). When
the outdoor air temperature drops below 55°F (13°C), the
system will allow airside economizer “Free-Cooling?” op-
eration only if the building heating loads are satisfied. This
is accomplished by reversing the conventional free-cooling
sequence of operation.
To allow the heat recovery chiller to have the first opportu-
nity to transfer the heat before it is ejected through the building
relief vents, the cooling coil control valve modulates to 100%
open on a call for cooling before the outside air damper is

Figure 6: Elimination of preheat coils.
March 2007
ASHRAE Journal
53
allowed to open beyond minimum outside air requirements.
This provides the ability for the heat recovery chiller to
recover all useful heat for exterior space heating, domestic
water preheating, pool heating and dehumidification, or other
loads, and provide all or part of the cooling load for internal
heat gain spaces. The economizer system must be controlled
by the AHU discharge air temperature and not by the mixed
air temperature for heat recovery applications. However,
minimum mixed air temperature override control is suggested.
As the outside air temperature falls, some air-handling units
will have a sufficiently high percentage of outside air that
they will need preheat while the remaining units on a lower
percentage of outside air continue to need cooling. At some
point ,the amount of preheating required will exceed the load
for internal cooling. As the chilled water temperature falls, the
heat recovery chiller will cycle off due to insufficient chilled
water return temperature.
Below 35°F (2°C)—The chilled water supply temperature
is constantly monitored, and if the temperature falls below a
reset schedule of 50°F at 35°F (10°C at 2°C) outside air tem-
perature, indicating low or no internal loads, such as during
unoccupied hours of operation, it will reset linearly to 60°F
at 0°F (16°C at –18°C) outside air temperature; each cooling
coil has the ability to convert to a preheat, non-freeze coil.
This is accomplished by blending hot water from the boilers
with the return water from the chilled water system. This is
done by use of valve V2 that is controlled by a temperature
transmitter, t1. The supply temperature is varied to meet the
required air supply temperature to the VAV reheat coils or
single zone air-handling unit reheat coil. Each unit’s cooling
control valve is converted according to the mixed air tempera-
ture of the air-handling unit.
If the mixed air temperature is above 55°F (13°C), the
control valve functions as a cooling coil control valve. If the
mixed air temperature is below 53°F (12°C), the control valve
functions as a preheat coil control valve. Necessary interlocks
are provided such as ensuring that the chillers do not run
whenever valve V2 is open. Freeze stats are still used to shut
the outside air damper and shut down the fan on indication
of near freezing temperatures downstream of the cooling/
preheat coil. The chilled water pumps operate continuously
and, therefore, the valve is driven open completely during a
freeze-stat event.
The motivation for this system was the experience of a “too
successful” heat recovery system. It appeared that lot of cool-
ing was being provided and also a lot of heating. Responding
Advertisement formerly in this space.
March 2007
ASHRAE Journal
55
to a service call due to loss of flow, which had been resumed,
the chilled water temperature was 105˚F (41˚C) on an 85˚F
(29˚C) day! It didn’t take long to find that the auditorium air
handling unit (AHU) preheat coil control valve had failed
open, by design. Before this discovery, the AHU was capable
of maintaining the space dry bulb set point, but was not ca-
pable of controlling humidity. In order to solve this problem
on future heat recovery projects, it was decided to eliminate
the possibility of this uncontrolled injection of heat in the
chilled water system by simply eliminating the preheat coil.
This makes you wonder, “How many preheat coils have control
valves that have failed open, and it is not apparent?”
This system eliminates the need for special preheat coils of
the non-freeze type, and, for practical purposes, the concern
for freezing of coils. On top of this, appreciable first cost is
eliminated by not having preheat coils in the air-handling
units, including the piping, control valves, controls, and as-
sociated fittings. This system reduces the pump capacity and
piping distribution system sizes. It also eliminates the need for
two expansion tanks and makeup connections. It also allows
the most efficient transfer of internal heat to exterior spaces
without the operation of the heat recovery chiller, when the
cooling and heating loads balance.
If the chiller serving the facility is an air-cooled, packaged
chiller with the evaporator packaged outside, this system can
provide winter freeze protection as insurance against a failure
of normal heat tracing systems for both the evaporator and
the chilled water piping outside.
Summary
The combination of the condensing boiler and the heat
recovery chiller provides a number of opportunities to reduce
energy bills for buildings with summer heating and winter
cooling. Obviously, the sizing of the heat recovery chiller
is contingent upon careful calculation of these summer and
winter loads, both in size and duration.
This is just the beginning of the use of recovered heat from
water-cooled chillers. As designers of HVAC water systems
become comfortable with condensing boilers and heat re-
covery chillers, new procedures will be discovered to use the
heat rejected from chiller condensers and make buildings
more energy efficient. Finally, using condensing type boil-
ers and heat recovery chillers enables the designer of HVAC
water systems to meet, and usually exceed, the requirements
of Paragraph 6.5.6 of ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard
90.1-2004, Energy Standard for Buildings Except Low-Rise
Residential Buildings.
Bibliography
Durkin, T.H., J.B. Rishel. 2003. “Dedicated heat recovery.” ASHRAE
Journal 45(10):18 – 23.
Durkin, T.H. 2005. “Evolving design of chiller plants.” ASHRAE
Journal 47(11):40 – 50.
Rishel, J.B., T.H. Durkin, B. Kincaid. 2006. HVAC Pump Handbook,
Second Edition. N.Y.: McGraw-Hill.

T
he centennial of the founding of ASHRAE’s predecessor society,
ASHVE, was celebrated in 1994. That celebration included pub-
lishing Heat & Cold: Mastering the Great Indoors. The original manu-
script featured a chronology of significant innovations in HVAC&R.
However, space limitations precluded its inclusion in the book. For the
American Society of Refrigerating Engineers centennial in 2005, the
original chronology was updated with the AC&R information excerpted.
The new version is available at www.ashrae.org/chronology.
Keeping Cool
Timeline of Refrigeration & Air Conditioning
By Bernard Nagengast, Member ASHRAE; and Gerald Groff, Fellow/Life Member ASHRAE
Here is a sampling of the chronology,
with some illustrations added, covering
some technical innovations of a century
ago.
1904
Public Debut of Air Conditioning: Mis-
souri State Building, St. Louis World’s
Fair. To commemorate the centennial of
The Lousiana Purchase, a World’s Fair
Bernard Nagengast is a consulting engineer in
Sidney, Ohio, and a past member of ASHRAE’s
Historical Committee. Gerald Groff is a principal
at Groff Associates in Cazenovia, N.Y.
About the Authors
Figure 1: The Missouri State Building was the public’s first demonstration of mechanical cooling.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2007. © Copyright 2007 American Society of Heat-
ing, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may
not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
March 2007
ASHRAE Journal
65
was held in St. Louis in 1904 – 05. Officially titled The Louisiana
Purchase Exposition (informally known as the St. Louis World’s
Fair), the fair was the site of the public debut of comfort air con-
ditioning. Although some comfort air-conditioning installations
had been engineered before 1904, the Missouri State Building
was the first place where the general public experienced the
benefits of mechanical cooling en masse (Figure 1).
When planning for the fair commenced, a proposal was made
to cool many public buildings and food concessions. However,
this was thought as too ambitious by the fair’s managers, and
the cooling plan was scaled back to only one building.
At this large public gathering place, the Missouri State Build-
ing contained a rotunda and 1,000 seat auditorium, which was
cooled during the summer.
Thirty-five thousand cfm (16
517 L/s) of partially recircu-
lated air, cooled by direct ex-
pansion, was delivered through
mid‑height wall registers.
No doubt this installation
impressed fair visitors, many
of whom experienced comfort
cooling for the first time. But
it really caused a stir among
refrigeration engineers, and
the trade journal Ice and Re-
frigeration ran an extensive
article on the installation. The
editors concluded, “The practi-
cal application of mechanical
refrigeration to air cooling for
the purposes of personal comfort, no doubt has a field ... and the
day is at hand, or soon will be, when the modern office building,
factory, church, theater and even residence will be incomplete
without a mechanical air cooling plant.”1
First Air-Conditioned Bank: Hanover National Bank, New
York, by Alfred Wolff. 1904 was the site of another comfort
cooling installation. For the first time, a bank was air condi-
tioned. New York consulting engineer Alfred Wolff designed
the system. (Wolff was no stranger to the new science of air
conditioning, having designed large comfort cooling systems
for the Cornell Medical College in 1899 and the New York
Stock Exchange in 1901.)
Wolff designed a cooling system in 1903 for the Hanover
Bank in New York. The system was in full operation by 1904,
using a 100 ton (352 kW) ammonia absorption refrigeration
system to furnish cold brine to pipe coils in a 2,560 ft2 (238
m2) bunker, through which the building air was circulated. The
conditioned air was distributed near the ceiling through orna-
mental grillwork. A heating coil was located in the airstream
parallel to the cooling coil, with the airstream regulated with
a damper so the air could be switched either to the heating
or cooling coil. The system was regulated by thermostat and
humidistat.
Initially, the cooling system was designed to maintain an in-
door temperature of 70°F (21°C), but when the outdoor summer
temperatures approached 100°F (38°C), the occupants reported
that the 30°F (17°C) of thermal shock “…was too sudden, and
severe colds and rheumatic twinges were the immediate results.”
The system control was changed so that the indoor temperature
was maintained 10°F to 15°F (5.5°C to 8°C) below the outdoor
temperature.2
1904 – 05 Founding of ASRE. “The first general meeting of the
American Society of Refrigerating Engineers was a most agree-
able surprise to everybody, including even the officers and found-
ers.… It gave promise of being the first of many annual meetings
which will become the guide of
the student and the operating
engineer in the science of main-
taining temperatures below that
of the atmosphere. There was an
honest ring to the papers and
discussions which betokened
the purpose of America’s fore-
most experts on refrigeration
to get down to facts regardless
of anybody’s pet theories or
interests, but it was also evident
that the search for Truth will
be fostered without malice or
prejudice.”3
The ASRE was founded in
1904 at the suggestion of Wil-
liam H. Ross, who was associ-
ated with the trade journal Cold Storage. The first organizational
meeting was held December 1904 in New York City. Ross was
secretary of the organization from 1905 through 1927.
Self-Contained Electric Refrigerator Displayed at St. Louis
World’s Fair. At the turn of the 20th century, most refrigeration
technology was applied to large installations and refrigeration
for homes and stores was in its infancy. Many entrepreneurs
were attempting to solve the technical problems stymieing the
development of a foolproof refrigerator.
One of the earliest successful small size refrigerating systems
was designed by Richard Whittaker of New Brunswick, N.J. Whit-
taker applied for a number of U.S. patents in 1904 and 1905 cover-
ing the elements of such a system (U.S. patents: 795,014; 795,015;
821,563; 848,277; 911,635; 932,599; 936,065 and 1,013,348).
The Brunswick Refrigerating Co. began manufacturing small
refrigeration systems for homes and butcher shops in 1900. In
particular, the company advertised a refrigerator that had the
refrigerating system self-contained with the refrigerator, an
unusual design for the period, as early as 1904. Normally, the
refrigeration compressor and condenser were located remote
from the refrigerator.
The company displayed a model of the refrigerator (Figure 2) at
the 1904 – 05 St. Louis World’s Fair. “A good sized box, between
Figure 2: The Brunswick self-contained refrigerator.
66
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
5 ft and 6 ft (1.5 to 1.8 m) high has at one side a small compart-
ment, about a foot-and-a-half (0.45 m) wide, but extending the
height of the refrigerator, in which is placed a self-contained, 300
pound (136 kg) capacity (this refers to pounds of ice equivalent),
refrigerating machine built by the Brunswick Refrigerating Co.,
New Brunswick, N. J., with coils of brine piping in the upper sec-
tion of the refrigerator. The machine is operated by a little motor,
attached, and is said to work very satisfactorily, easily keeping the
temperature in the refrigerator at 40°F (4.4°C) or under.”4
The Brunswick Refrigerating Co. manufactured small-size
refrigeration equipment for many years, later merged with
Kroeschell Brothers Ice Machine Co. to form Brunswick-
Kroeschell, which merged with Carrier Engineering Corp. to
form Carrier Corporation.
Introduction of Enthalpy and Plotting of Calculation of
Refrigerating Machines by Richard Mollier. Thermo-
dynamic Properties of Carbon Dioxide and Ammonia
Published. The term enthalpy was introduced into theoretical
thermodynamics by J.W. Gibbs in the
1870s. Richard Mollier introduced it
into applied thermodynamics in 1902
and used it as one coordinate of his
thermodynamic diagrams (the other
was entropy or pressure) beginning
in 1904. Mollier (1863 – 1935) was a
professor at the University of Dresden
who, like Willis Carrier, saw the need to
provide engineers with more easily used
tools for designing thermodynamic
systems. Mollier published an article
entitled “New Diagrams for the Tech-
nical Caloric Theory” in a 1904 issue
of the VDI magazine. His charts were
recognized to have given great impetus
to the advancement of thermal engine
development. He subsequently applied
this approach to his “New Tables and
Diagrams for Water Vapour.” In 1923
the International Congress of Thermo-
dynamics, held in Los Angeles, passed a resolution to designate
all diagrams that represent the heat capacity on a coordinate,
after Richard Mollier.
1905
Modern Automatic Expansion Valve Patented by Albert
Marshall. As the application of mechanical refrigeration spread
to uses requiring small systems, the need to control refrigerant
flow became evident. Various schemes to control refrigerant
flow began to appear in the 1880s, and one of the first successful
approaches that saw production and use was the constant pres-
sure expansion valve (commonly referred to as the automatic
expansion valve) invented by Albert T. Marshall who filed for
a U.S. patent in 1904 (U.S. Patent 785,265 granted in 1905)
The patent text says the invention “relates to an automatically
controlled valve for regulating the admission of anhydrous
ammonia or other refrigerating medium to the expansion coils
of a refrigerating apparatus.”
Marshall’s expansion valve was used by The Automatic
Refrigerating Co., a pioneer in development of small refriger-
ating systems. Marshall invented other automatic controls for
refrigeration, including a constant superheat (thermostatic)
expansion valve, patenting it in 1911.
1906
Apparatus for Treating Air Patented by Willis Carrier. Wil-
lis Carrier’s first attempts to control humidity in heating and
ventilating systems at the Sackett-Wilhelms printing plant used
pipe banks to dehumidify the air. Carrier “…observed that as
dehumidification was taking place, the air was in contact with
water on the pipe surfaces.… Why should we not, then, spray
the cold water into the air stream, thus increasing the surface
of contact and reducing the resistance to air flow.”5
Although the idea of using an air washer to control humidity
had been proposed as early as 1894 by
Hermann Rietschel, and air washers
commonly were used to clean and
humidify air in heating systems for
large buildings, Carrier realized that
the idea of controlling humidity with
an air spray was an engineering and
marketing feasibility. He applied for a
U.S. patent in 1904, and was granted
patent 808,897 in 1906. His patent
“Apparatus for Treating Air” says, “The
object of the invention is to provide an
efficient practical apparatus of simple
construction which will thoroughly
separate all solid impurities, floating
particles, and noxious material from
the air either with or without altering
its temperature and humidity.”
Carrier’s improved air washer was
marketed by Buffalo Forge Co. and
was used by Carrier in most of his early
industrial air-conditioning installations.
Psychrometric Chart Developed by Willis Carrier. In 1906,
Willis Carrier developed a hygrometric chart to aid in the de-
sign and operation of air-conditioning installations (the term
“air conditioning” was actually not coined until 1906 by Stuart
Cramer). In his work on the Sackett-Wilhelms printing plant
installation in 1902 – 03, Carrier was frustrated with the lack
of engineering tools to calculate process state points for his
system. Available data on moist air properties were limited to
Marvin’s psychrometric tables, published by the U.S. Depart-
ment of Agriculture in 1900.6 These tables consisted of 67 pages
of data. The relation of humidity to dew-point depression only
could be searched out by laborious subtractions—but it was
not self-evident in the tabulated data.
Figure 3: First use of term “air conditioning.”
68
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
The first exposition of Carrier’s chart was in a 1906 catalogue
of the Buffalo Forge Company’s air washers and humidifiers. On
Carrier’s chart, curved lines representing “percent of saturation”
were plotted against “temperature of dry bulb” and “grains of mois-
ture per cu. ft. of air.” Total heat was not shown but otherwise this
chart established the basic form of the psychrometric charts used
today. Carrier formally published this chart in his landmark ASME
paper “Rational Psychrometric Formulae” in December 1911.
When ASHRAE undertook, around 1960, to establish an
official Society chart, the academic community insisted that
dry-bulb temperature (which Carrier had used successfully as a
surrogate for enthalpy in his charts for practical reasons) should
be replaced as a major coordinate by enthalpy. Thus, we now
have enthalpy and specific humidity as the main coordinates
used in air-conditioning process calculations.
The Term “Air Conditioning” is
Coined by Stuart Cramer. Stuart
Cramer, a textile mill engineer from
Charlotte, N.C. who is credited by Willis
Carrier as the contemporaneous discov-
erer of some of the basic relationships of
humidity and temperature in air, was the
first to use the term “air conditioning”
(Figure 3). Cramer filed for a U.S.patent
in April 1906 (U.S.Patent 852,823). The
patent was titled “Humidifying and Air
Conditioning Apparatus.” Cramer’s
invention was a device to automatically
regulate the temperature and humidity
in factories.
Cramer also used the term in his May
1906 address before the American Cotton
Manufacturers Association in Asheville,
N.C. Excerpts are reprinted in his 1906
book Useful Information for Cotton
Manufacturers. “…I have used the term
‘Air Conditioning’ to include humidify-
ing and air cleansing, and heating and
ventilation.”7
Fascinated by this history? See more
of this proud heritage, beginning more
than 2,000 years ago, at www.ashrae.
org/history.
References
1. Anonymous. 1904. “At the World’s Fair.”
Ice and Refrigeration, 27(11): 179 – 180.
2. Anonymous. 1903. “Cooling a modern
bank building.” Cold Storage, 10(8):51.
3. Anonymous. 1905. “First meeting of
ASRE.” Cold Storage and Ice Trade Journal,
14(12):100.
4. Anonymous. 1904. “Refrigerating plants
at the Louisiana Purchase Exposition.” Ice and
Refrigeration, 27(9):89.
5. Carrier, W. 1929. “Air conditioning—its
phenomenal development.” Heating and Ven-
tilating, 26(6):116.
6. Lewis, LL. 1956. “The romance of air
conditioning.” Unpublished paper.
7. Cramer, Stuart. 1906. Useful Information
for Cotton Manufacturers. 2nd. Ed. Charlotte,
North Carolina: Queen City Printing and
Paper Co. III, p. 1208.

First Place Winners
Matt Younger, P.E., Associate Member ASHRAE
Project: Washington Department of Veterans Affairs,
Retsil, Wash.
Category: Health-Care Facilities (New)
The Washington State Veterans Home is a 160,000 ft2 (15
000 m2), 259-bed skilled nursing and assisted-living facility
in Retsil, Wash., that achieved an innovative exception to
Washington Administrative Code (WAC) design comfort
standards. Current WAC mandates the use of a mechanical
cooling system and does not recognize engineered natural,
passive cooling as a design option. The project uses a
complementary mechanical ventilation system that works in
parallel with the natural ventilation process. The mechanical
air-handling systems provide only the minimum outdoor air
ventilation requirements needed to compensate for natural
ventilation when the windows are closed.
Pierre Roussel, Associate Member ASHRAE; and Jacques
Lagacé, Ing., Member ASHRAE
Project: Montréal-Trudeau International Airport,
Montréal, QC, Canada
Category: Industrial Facilities or Processes (New)
A high-efficiency heating plant was needed as the 40-year-
old system could no longer keep pace with growth at the
Montréal-Trudeau International Airport. The new plant
consists of four chillers connected in pairs to increase
water temperature differential and reduce the main piping
network size. Waste heat is reclaimed for use in the low
temperature, hot water system. Saturated flue gases are
mixed with hot, dry air to avoid forming a visible plume of
smoke that would reduce visibility near the airport’s control
tower. A seasonal efficiency of nearly 99% is expected from
the fuel-dependent part of the system.
Daniel Pare, Associate Member ASHRAE
Project: Enerstat/IBM, Bromont, QC, Canada
Category: Industrial Facilities or Processes (Existing)
The IBM Bromont semiconductor manufacturing facility
needed to replace its 25-year-old chillers, which used
CFC-12. The new plant uses a thermal energy system
with phase-change materials combined with free cooling,
a variable frequency drive chiller and predictive algorithm
control. Phase-change materials are substances that can
accumulate and release energy during phase change, which
in this case is a change from liquid to solid. The design is
anticipated to produce energy savings of 6% annually, in
part by using artificial phase-change materials in the chiller
with different melting points between 28°F and 40°F (–2°C
and 4°C). The system also uses a natural cooling exchanger,
which runs from September to May.
T
  he ASHRAE Technology Awards recognize
outstanding achievements by members
who have successfully applied innovative
building design in the areas of occupant comfort, indoor
air quality (IAQ) and energy conservation.
Their designs incorporate ASHRAE standards for effective
energy management and IAQ. Performance is proven through one year’s actual, verifiable
operating data. The following are descriptions of projects from the ASHRAE Technology
Award winners and honorable mentions. Articles about these projects will be published
in this and future issues of ASHRAE Journal.
Outstanding Achievements

Honorable Mentions
Norman J. Brown, P.E., Member ASHRAE
Project: Marion Oliver McCaw Hall, Seattle, Wash.
Category: Public Assembly (New)
Seattle’s performance center was the result of an $80
million renovation of the former Seattle Center Opera
House. ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1’s Energy Cost
Budget Method was used through computer-calculated
hourly energy simulations to achieve an annual energy
cost savings of $31,000. Energy-saving features include an
automatic interior solar blind, airflow modulation, variable
speed pumping, underfloor air displacement and high
equipment efficiencies.
Ronald Gagnon, Member ASHRAE
Project: Comptoir Richelieu Botanix, Sorel-Tracy, QC,
Canada
Category: Commercial Buildings (New)
The Comptoir Richelieu Botanix is a 12,397 ft2 (1150 m2)
home and garden retail store that uses heating and cooling
slabs, a dedicated outside air ventilation system, a ground-
coupled closed loop heat exchanger, extended range water-
to-water heat pumps, natural humidification system and
primary-only loop hydronic system. The project uses less
than one-third of the energy in a similar type of building.
The owners moved from their previous 3,200 ft2 (300 m2)
building to this 12,397 ft2 (1150 m2) building with no change
in their energy budget.
Ronald Henning, Associate Member ASHRAE
Project: University of Michigan Life Sciences Institute,
Ann Arbor, Mich.
Category: Institutional Buildings (New)
The University of Michigan’s new laboratory building
features a “house air” system where animals are located.
This system, believed to be the first used in the U.S.,
reduces outside air requirements by delivering a specific
quantity of air directed to each rack of cages. The building
system also recovers waste heat while meeting the
stringent air quality standards required for laboratory
fumes. Exhaust fans built onto the roof do not increase
noise levels for nearby residence halls.
George Karidis, Member ASHRAE
Project: Visteon Village Corporate Headquarters, Van
Buren Township, Mich.
Category: Commercial Buildings (New)
Visteon is an automotive supplier that designs, engineers and
manufactures various products for vehicle manufacturers.
Visteon Village has 800,000 ft2 (74 000 m2) of office and lab
buildings. Lake water offers chiller condensing at typically
lower temperatures than a set of cooling towers could
offer, without incurring fan motor loads. An underfloor air-
distribution (UFAD) system offers a low-energy air-distribution
approach with linear perimeter UFAD supply diffuser system
to accommodate extensive perimeter loads.
Comptoir Richelieu Botanix
The Comptoir Richelieu Botanix is a 12,397 ft2 (1150 m2) home
and garden retail store that uses heating and cooling slabs, a
dedicated outside air ventilation system, a ground-coupled closed
loop heat exchanger, and other technologies to provide energy
savings and good air quality.
University of Michigan Life Sciences Institute
A “house air” system, believed to be the first used in the U.S., reduces
outside air requirements by delivering a specific quantity of air directed
to each rack of cages.

ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
\* See also Stanke, D. 2006. “Standard 62.1-2004—System

48(12):24.
Overhead Heating
Revisiting a Lost Art
V
AV terminals provide a measured quantity of conditioned
air to a space, in response to a control signal from a ther-
mostat or room sensor. This air may be tempered with a reheat
coil, plenum air, or both. The means and selection of parameters
for this reheat leads to much of the complexity and questions
in selecting and specifying VAV terminals. To avoid problems,
selecting the reheat design parameters requires an understand-
ing of the limitations of the reheat coil (hot water or electric)
and the means of air distribution.
When these systems were first designed
and installed in the late 1970s, several
manufacturers extensively researched the
parameters for effective overhead heat-
ing. The results of all the research were
similar, and a consensus recommenda-
tion was included in the 1979 ASHRAE
Handbook—Fundamentals. The recom-
mendation has been in every edition
since. (From the 2005 edition, Chapter
33, p. 33.17: “All researchers found less
than optimum performance with high
discharge temperatures [greater than
15°F above ambient]…. Under heating
load conditions, the supply air tempera-
ture must be limited to avoid excessive
thermal stratification.”)\* Unfortunately,
discussions with design engineers from
Missoula, Mont., to San Antonio, and
from Los Angeles to Boston reveal that
the preponderance of systems is designed
for discharge temperatures in excess of
100°F (38°C).
Figure 1 illustrates a common misap-
plication. Air is discharged at around
100°F (38°C), and never reaches the cold
airstream falling down the window. In
this situation, ventilation air often short
By Daniel Int-Hout III, P.E., Member ASHRAE
Daniel Int-Hout III, P.E., is the chief engineer for
Krueger in Richardson, Texas.
About the Author
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2007. © Copyright 2007 American Society of Heat-
ing, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may
not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
March 2007
ASHRAE Journal
57
circuits back through the return ceiling plenum, and never
reaches the occupants. The thermostat, meanwhile, may take
as long as an hour to respond to load changes.
Discharging air at a temperature more than 15°F (8°C) above
the room (90°F in a 75°F [32°C in a 24°C] room) likely will
result in significant unwanted air temperature stratification and
will void compliance with ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 55-2004,
Thermal Environmental Conditions for Human Occupancy. In
addition, ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2004, Ventilation for
Acceptable Indoor Air Quality, has been modified to require
increased outside air when heating from the ceiling if this rule
is not followed (Table 1).
This is because hot air tends to stay at the ceiling, and may
short-circuit directly back to the room exhaust without mixing in
the room. Indeed, using the ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 129-1997,
Measuring Air Change Effectiveness, mixing effectiveness
values as low as 20% (or lower) have been observed when the
supply to room differential exceeds 15°F (8°C). Calculations
will show that in most cases, it only requires 85°F (29°C) air
to handle a typical winter design perimeter load at 1 cfm/ft2 (5
L/s per m2) air supply rate (the airflow rate we recommend for
good ventilation mixing and comfort).
When properly operated, a discharge less than 15°F (8°C)
above room temperature results in higher required airflows,
longer throws, and the room air gets mixed. The thermostat
responds in a few minutes to changes in loads.
The need to rapidly warm a space following a night setback
has another set of requirements. The space needs to be heated
as rapidly as possible, with a maximum of mixing, without too
much regard for occupant comfort. This often results in both a
high ∆T and a high airflow.
However, the hotter the air temperature, the longer it will
take to heat the room at a given heat delivery rate! This is due
to stratification of hot air at the ceiling. Morning warm-up may
happen more rapidly with air temperatures lower than conven-
tional wisdom dictates.
The engineer needs to ensure that occupied heating and
morning warm-up situations are covered in his design with
proper staged or proportional electric heat or proportional
water coil valves.
Some energy codes (in Florida and California) and ANSI/
ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1-2004, Energy Standard for
Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings, prohibit reheat-
ing cooled air at more than 30% of design cooling flows. It is
unlikely that meeting this requirement will result in satisfactory
occupant comfort or ventilation mixing. In temperate climates,
this may be less than satisfactory, but an understood design
compromise. In cold climates, this may not be acceptable. Of
course, the Standard 90.1-2004 prohibition only covers reheating
previously cooled air. When the system is in economizer mode, air
is not being reheated. In fact, it may be argued that with today’s
better wall insulation and glass, few systems should be in com-
pressor cooling mode when perimeters require reheat.
The reheating of cold primary air seems, on the surface, to be
a wasteful practice in terms of optimum use of energy resources.
Many situations exist where it is necessary, beneficial, and can
save considerable energy. These include the following:
• Providing comfort with a great diversity of loads. When
both heating and cooling is required from a single air han-
dler, due to climate and building design factors, reheat is
often an economical solution. As it is only used in a few
locations, and only part of the time, the energy penalty for
reheat is minimal.
• Supplementing baseboard perimeter heat. Baseboard
heating systems can be an effective means of offsetting
perimeter heating demand loads. At times, peak heating
demand loads may exceed the installed baseboard capacity
and supplemental overhead heat can be supplied.
• Maintaining minimum ventilation rates. The benefit of
an installed reheat coil in non-perimeter zones becomes
apparent when a minimum ventilation rate exceeds the
cooling demand. This happens when the quantity of supply
air to a space required to provide proper ventilation exceeds
that required to offset local heat sources, such as when
the ratio of occupants to equipment (which requires little
ventilation air) shifts toward occupants, as in conference
rooms. In these cases, the required quantity of ventilation
air may subcool the zone.
• A slight amount of controlled reheat can prevent this
subcooling. The alternative, reducing supply air tempera-
ture at the air handler, may result in other spaces that cannot
be cooled at design maximum airflows, and also tends to
increase space relative humidity.
• Controlling Humidity. Humidity control can be enhanced
using reheat coils, just as for ventilation requirements. When
the local humidity is too high, then drier and cooler air can
be added, then slightly reheated to avoid subcooling.
Hot Water Heat
Most manufacturers of VAV terminals offer a hot water coil
selection program, and the output is often at odds with scheduled
capacities based on rule of thumb calculations. Unlike custom
air handlers, the fin spacing, circuiting, and tube spacing are
usually fixed for VAV box coils. This means only one solution
exists for a given gpm, number of rows and airflow rate, for a
given coil size.
When selecting a coil, one can pick only one independent
variable, with different parameters for increasing the number
of rows, gpm, etc. The selection of the best path almost always
requires a discussion with the engineer. It is necessary to know
58
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
which item on the provided schedule is to be met. While this
should probably be the Btu/h, it is often the gpm due to prior
pump selection. Both scheduled gpm and Btu/h often cannot be
met, unless the calculation method is based on verified method-
ology, which should be available from box manufacturers.
As discussed earlier, the coil leaving air should be fixed so
as to not exceed ASHRAE’s recommended 15°F (8°C) ∆T
maximum (except maybe in unoccupied morning warm-up)
for effective air distribution in the room. This often requires
adjusting the heating cfm to achieve the desired room Btu/h at
a discharge air temperature that will promote good room air
distribution and ventilation mixing. Apparently, this action is
seldom performed, but should be.
Water Coil Issues
For fan boxes, the mixed air temperature (the combination
of primary and induced air temperatures) is dependent on the
coil location:
Series Fan Boxes. As the coil is almost always on the discharge,
the mixed air quantity (and the coil entering air temperature) is
based on the fan cfm. The coil entering air temperature is calcu-
lated based on the minimum primary at one temperature and the
induced airflow (which is the fan cfm less the minimum primary)
at another temperature. With series flow units, the coil leaving
air temperature and the box leaving air are the same.
Thermostat
Figure 1: Typical overhead heating scenario.
Air Distribution Configuration
Ez
Ceiling supply of warm air 15°F (8°C)
or more above space temperature
0.8
and ceiling return.
Ceiling supply of warm air less than 15°F (8°C)
above space temperature and ceiling return
provided that the 150 fpm (0.8 m/s) supply
1.0
air jet reaches to within 4.5 ft (1.4 m) of
floor level. Note: For lower velocity supply air,
Ez = 0.8.
Table 1: Excerpt from Table 6.2, ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2004.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
60
ASHRAE Journal
March 2007
Parallel Fan Boxes. With some parallel
fan-powered units, the coil location may be
on the plenum inlet, so the coil entering air
is always the plenum air temperature. How-
ever, mixing occurs after the coil. The unit’s
discharge air temperature is therefore a mix
of primary air, typically at the minimum
primary airflow rate, and plenum air heated
by the coil, at the fan airflow rate.
Most parallel units have the water coil
on the discharge, but this may result in
a pressure penalty at the primary air
handler. When located on the plenum
inlet, the often high minimum primary air
setting (to meet ventilation minimums)
requires that the coil leaving air tempera-
ture may exceed 120°F (49°C), and there
is a high likelihood of “cooking” the fan
motor, forcing the coil to be relocated to
the unit discharge.
Leaving Water Temperature. Leaving
water temperature should not be used in
water coil selection. Cooling coils often
are selected on the basis of water coil
∆T, where the discharge temperature is
controlled. This technique is not recom-
mended for heating coils in VAV boxes,
where the discharge temperature is seldom
controlled. It is recognized that noncon-
densing boilers require a high entering
water (return water) temperature to avoid
“shocking” the system. This should be
accomplished through three-way valves,
secondary loops, or other means, not
through coil selections. At part load, when
the water valve is throttled, coil leaving
water temperatures will always be less
than recommended by these boilers.
Fluid Type. Three hot fluids are
commonly used: water, ethylene and
propylene glycol. The use of glycol will
significantly increase the minimum gpm
allowed for turbulent flow. A good coil
calculation computer program will com-
pute a Reynolds number, which must be
greater than 5,000 for a valid selection.
Maintaining this Reynolds number likely
will increase the minimum gpm as the
percentage of glycol increases.
Coil Load vs. Room Load. A water
coil often is selected based on a given
Btu/h. Two loads can be used for this
calculation. One is the coil load, which
is based on the airflow rate and the sup-
ply to discharge temperature differential.
The other is the room load, which is the
difference between the room temperature
and the discharge temperature, and may
be calculated from skin losses less inter-
nal loads. Often, it is not clear which is
being specified. Most software assumes
the Btu/h load is the coil load.
Electric Heat
The electric heater provided with most
single duct VAV boxes essentially is a
rated duct heater installed in an elongated
single duct unit. This longer unit provides
for developed flow, after the damper, and
a relatively uniform airflow across the
coil elements. At low flows, a minimum
airflow and a maximum kW must be con-
sidered. The heater has a safety switch that
prevents the heater from engaging unless
there is a minimum sensed pressure in the
duct. Normally, this is a velocity pressure,
although it sometimes becomes a total
pressure sensor in practice.
At low airflows, there may be insuffi-
cient total pressure in the unit to “make”
the contactor in the flow switch. This
may be due to probe location, damper
position, low discharge static pressure or
likely, a combination of all (for fan-pow-
ered terminals, the minimum fan setting
is sufficient to operate the electric heat).
The kW selected at minimum flow must
also avoid exceeding the maximum UL-
listed coil temperature of 120°F (49°C).
Overhead Heat and the Diffuser
Besides the minimum flow to activate
the heater safety circuits, there is an issue
of the diffuser performance. All diffusers
have a specific performance envelope in
cooling mode, which can be determined
through ADPI analysis.1 With VAV sys-
tems, diffusers should be selected, so
that at full flow, they are near the limit
of objectionable sound to allow for opti-
mum performance at reduced flows. VAV
boxes also are selected at as high an inlet
velocity as possible, for the same reason.
When heated air is being discharged
from a ceiling diffuser, the outlet velocity
needs to be as high as possible, to prevent
stratification. Airflows even close to the
manufacturer’s electric heating minimum
airflow are unlikely to be satisfactory from
an air-distribution standpoint. Short cir-
Advertisement formerly in this space.
62
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
cuiting of ventilation air and excessive temperature stratification
are likely, regardless of the resultant discharge temperature.
It some cases, it may be desirable to decouple the heating
and cooling air supply to the room. One option is to use down
blow nozzles (Figure 3) or slots at the window supplied from a
heating-only fan coil. A parallel fan box controller on the single
duct VAV terminal used for cooling can control this fan coil.
With fan boxes, the fan’s minimum flow rate is sufficient to
permit electric heater operation, so there is seldom a minimum
setting or requirement. Additionally, as there is minimum reheat,
energy codes are satisfied, and diffuser performance is maintained.
As a result, when restrictive energy codes are in place, fan-pow-
ered terminals may be required for acceptable environments and
ventilation mixing, instead of shut-off single duct units.
As mentioned earlier, the ASHRAE Handbook recommends
a maximum discharge temperature of 90°F (32°C) (in a 75°F
[24°C] room) with overhead heating to avoid excessive strati-
fication (and ventilation short-circuiting). Many times, simple
logic can be applied to select suitable conditions of airflow and
reheat that maintain room air mixing, diffuser performance and
Thermostat
Diffusers
Nozzles
Figure 2 (left): Proper overhead heating design. Figure 3 (right): Alternate overheating design.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
March 2007
ASHRAE Journal
63
air-change effectiveness, within the factory
airflow and kW limitations for units with
electric heaters. An airflow setting of about
1 cfm/ft2 (5 L/s per m2) as a heating flow
rate in perimeter zones is recommended
to achieve optimum air distribution when
heating.
Heat Control
The control of heat to a space is primar-
ily the responsibility of the supplied unit
controls. The controller provides staged
contacts for heat to an electric heater, and
either a single contact for on-off, or two
contacts for three point floating outputs to
a hot water valve. The control is most often
open loop, in that the controller relies on
feedback from the room sensor to modify
the heater control signal. If a room is strati-
fied (as when the discharge air temperature
is greater than ASHRAE’s recommended
15°F [8°C] ∆T) the time response may be
very slow, resulting in considerable room
temperature swings (in addition to the
unacceptable room temperature stratifi-
cation). The room sensor will indicate
acceptable temperature at all times.
New technologies permit proportional
discharge temperature controlled heat for
VAV boxes, at a low cost. The controller
is connected either to a proportional hot
water valve, or a single-stage, digital relay
electric heater. When tied with a discharge
temperature sensor, this allows the control-
ler to set the desired discharge tempera-
tures as a function of the DDC controller’s
demand. This technology allows for a
better control of space temperature than
most other methods. It can be driven by
a variety of application sequences using
proportional (0 – 10 vdc or 0 – 20 mA) out-
put, pulsing or staged 24 vac output. The
use of a three-point floating heater control
option—with either hot water or electric
heat—often reduces the cost of the DDC
controller by as much as $100 compared
to a proportional output on the DDC con-
troller. Some DDC manufacturers do not
have an analog output option available at
all on VAV box controllers.
When the discharge temperature sen-
sor is installed, one can control discharge
temperatures proportionally between the
no-heat duct temperature and a set maxi-
mum temperature.
Summary
Overhead heating has been the primary
method of offsetting perimeter winter
loads with VAV systems for 30 years.
Research was conducted on the “rules” to
avoid excessive stratification and provide
occupant comfort back then, and have
been included in the ASHRAE Handbooks
ever since. In spite of this, engineers
continue to design systems delivering
low velocity, high temperature air at the
ceiling. These designs are guaranteed to
result in spaces that do not meet the mini-
mum requirements of Standard 55-2004,
and also fail to consider new ventilation
requirements in Standard 62.1-2004 (and
may fail to comply with the U.S .Green
Building Council’s LEED®-NC Indoor
Environmental Quality [EQ] credit 7.1).
Electric and hot water reheat coils are
provided on many types of VAV termi-
nals. Selection criteria for each should
be considered to ensure proper unit
performance and to distribute the heated
air properly into the space. With all of
these, however, the engineer should en-
sure that the discharge temperatures and
air quantities will provide a comfortable
space, and provide ventilation mixing as
well. It often will be necessary for the
equipment supplier and control contrac-
tor to discuss these issues with the design
engineer before making a final selection
to ensure that everyone is making the
same assumptions
Meeting Standard 62.1-2004 and Stan-
dard 55-2004, without violating Standard
90.1-2004, requires proper selection of
heating control and heating equipment, as
well as air delivery devices. As Standard
62.1-2004 is a prerequisite of the version
2.2 LEED-NC requirements, and likely to
be incorporated into the next version of
the International Mechanical Code, it is
essential that these issues be understood.
And code issues aside, if the vertical
temperature requirements of Standard
55-2004 are not met, occupants will
use their own means of providing space
comfort (the dreaded 1500 W heaters),
or worse, move out.
References
1. Int-Hout, D. 2004. “Best practices for
selecting diffusers.” ASHRAE Journal 46(6):
S24 – S28.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
76
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
Using Internal-Combustion Engines
For Distributed Generation
By Robert Zogg, P.E., Member ASHRAE; Chad Smutzer; Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; and
James Brodrick, Ph.D.; Member ASHRAE
M
uch attention is lavished on using novel technologies
such as microturbines and fuel cells in distributed
generation (DG) applications. While using inter-
nal-combustion engines (ICEs) for DG applications receives
comparatively little press, ICEs’ technology maturity, proven
performance in DG application`s, rapid start-up and shutdown,
relatively high efficiencies, and low costs make them competi-
tive with newer technologies for many DG applications. ICEs
are, in fact, the dominant prime-mover technology used in DG
applications under 1000 kW generation capacity.1
Characteristics and Development Status
Although ICEs are used extensively in transportation and
backup generator applications, DG applications (including
combined heat and power, or CHP) generally require longer
life, higher efficiency, lower emissions, and longer maintenance
intervals (based on run time). The fuel available for the majority
of DG applications in buildings is natural gas. In the U.S., two
major government-supported programs were launched in 2001
to address key ICE R&D needs for DG applications:
• The U.S. Department of Energy, Advanced Reciprocating
Engine Systems (ARES) Program; and
• The California Energy Commission, Public Interest En-
ergy Research (PIER) Program, Advanced Reciprocating
Internal Combustion Engines (ARICE) Collaborative.
Both programs target generation efficiency improvements,
emissions reductions, and maintenance reductions for ICEs in
the 500 to 6,500 kW capacity range (based on engine output
power) while reducing costs. Under ARES and ARICE, the
key technology approaches to improved efficiency are various
combinations of:
• Increased specific power output;
• Variable-valve timing (VVT) for improved part-load ef-
ficiency;
• Homogeneous charge compression ignition (HCCI);
• Friction and parasitic-loss reduction;
• Chemical recuperation of exhaust heat to reform fuel;
• Exhaust heat recuperation and/or compound turbo-
chargers; and
• Advanced engine control strategies.
The key technology approaches to increase maintenance
intervals include:
• Advanced ignition systems (including laser ignition and
HCCI); and
• Advanced lubricants.
The key technology approaches to lowering emissions
include:
• Advanced combustion techniques (including HCCI);
and
• Novel after-treatment approaches.
Figure 1 shows typical current installed costs and electric
generation efficiencies for DG systems using ICEs. The ca-
pacity range suitable for commercial building applications
is at the lower end of the capacity range shown in Figure
1. Current generation efficiencies range from 30% to 37%
(HHV) (33% to 41% [LHV]). Efficiencies will increase over
the next few years, assuming the ARES and ARICE programs
achieve their performance targets. Current installed costs
range from about $1,000/kW to $700/kW for generation ca-
pacities between 100 kW and 5,000 kW. Some estimates are
higher—e.g., $1,200/kW to $1,700/kW.1 Adding combined
heat and power (CHP) capability increases installed costs by
roughly 30% to 45%.2
R&D for small ICEs (1 to 10 kW) for DG applications is
taking place primarily outside of the U.S., targeting residential
and light-commercial DG applications. One U.S. company,
offering a 1 kWe, Japanese-made engine/generator packaged
with an air-handling unit for residential CHP, claims:
• About 17% (HHV) (19% [LHV]) electric generation ef-
ficiency;
• Sound levels of 44 dBA at 3 ft (1 m); and
• Maintenance intervals of 6,000 hours (about every one to
two years).3
Energy and Emissions Considerations
The efficiency of grid-supplied electricity (including
generation, transmission, and distribution losses) averaged
31.5% (HHV) in the U.S. for 2004.4 Based on the data shown
in Figure 1, current ICE DG systems over 300 kW can meet
or exceed the generation efficiency of the grid. Future sys-
tems will offer greater efficiency advantages, assuming that
the ARES and ARICE programs achieve their performance
targets. For example, Figure 2 illustrates the efficiency im-
provement achieved by one advanced engine using the Miller
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2007. © Copyright 2007 American Society of Heat-
ing, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may
not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
78
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2007
Cycle, where variable-valve timing is used to decrease the
effective compression ratio while the nominal expansion
ratio is maintained. Figure 2 also shows that efficiency de-
creases modestly with decreasing load to about 50% of full
load. Below 50% of full load, however, the efficiencies of
both conventional and advanced ICEs drop precipitously—a
characteristic that will be important in many DG applications
depending on the DG system capacity, the load profile, and
the operational strategy.
Incorporating CHP with advanced engines can reduce pri-
mary energy consumption by 30% or more in large commercial
buildings.7 However, as noted earlier, there is a corresponding
increase in installed cost. In most commercial building appli-
cations, only about 30% to 45% of the engine reject heat can
be recovered and used on an annual basis, even if the recovered
heat serves both heating and cooling loads.7 ICEs reject heat
primarily through the engine jacket cooling system and the
exhaust stream—each containing about 50% of the recover-
able heat. Exhaust gas temperature is typically on the order of
1,100°F (600°C) and coolant temperature is typically 180°F
to 250°F (80°C to 120°C), with the higher end of the range
requiring a pressurized cooling system. This generally limits
thermally activated cooling technology choices to single-effect
absorption or desiccant regeneration, unless only the exhaust
heat is used or the absorption system is custom-designed to
use both the exhaust heat and jacket heat.
ICE emissions meet regulatory requirements in most of
the United States.1 Regulatory requirements, however, are
becoming stricter in many areas. For example, a California
Air Resources Board (ARB) regulation sets strict emissions
limits for DG systems. Fossil-fuel-fired DG systems sold in
California must be certified to meet the following emissions
standards (based on electric output):
• NOx: 0.07 lb/MWh (32g/MWh);
• CO: 0.10 lb/MWh (45 g/MWh); and
• Volatile Organic Compounds: 0.02 lb/MWh
(91 g/­MWh.8
While the 2007 performance targets for the ARICE Collab-
orative discussed previously are consistent with these limits9,
ARB currently lists no ICE DG systems that are certified for
sale in California.8
Cost Challenges
ICEs are generally the lowest-cost technology available for
DG systems. One source estimates that a 100 kW microturbine
DG system costs over 50% more fully installed compared to
an ICE DG system.2 As reported in recent Emerging Technolo-
gies columns,10,11 fuel cells are even more expensive. Main-
tenance costs for ICE DG systems, however, are significant,
ranging from about $0.01/kWh to $0.02/kWh, depending on
capacity.2
Promising Applications
Relatively low cost and good efficiency make ICEs the op-
timum choice for DG systems in most commercial building
applications, assuming that prevailing emissions regulations
do not preclude their use. New technologies such as fuel cells
may be needed, however, in regions where emissions regula-
tions for DG systems are more stringent.
References

Laboratory (NREL). 2003. www.nrel.gov/docs/fy04osti/34783.
pdf.

Summer Study on Energy Efficiency in Buildings.
4. 2006 Buildings Energy Data Book. 2006. Prepared by D&R
International for the U.S. Department of Energy. http://buildings
databook.eere.energy.gov.
5. Saito, H., et al. 2003. “Development of High-Efficiency Co-Gen-
eration System—High Efficiency Gas Engine for Co-Generation.” 22nd
