ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2007
William Goetzler is an associate director at Navi-
gant Consulting, Burlington, Mass. He has performed
consulting services for VRF manufacturers.
About the Author
V
ariable refrigerant flow (VRF) systems, which were introduced
in Japan more than 20 years ago, have become popular in
many countries, yet they are relatively unknown in the United
States. The technology has gradually expanded its market presence,
reaching European markets in 1987, and steadily gaining market
share throughout the world. In Japan, VRF systems are used in
approximately 50% of medium-sized commercial buildings (up to
70,000 ft2 [6500 m2]) and one-third of large commercial buildings
(more than 70,000 ft2 [6500 m2]).1
Variable
Refrigerant
Flow Systems
By William Goetzler, Member ASHRAE
Although vigorous marketing of VRF
systems in the U.S. began only two to
three years ago, several thousand systems
likely will be sold in the U.S. this year,
amounting to tens of thousands of tons
of capacity. Of course, the market is still
very small compared to the chiller mar-
ket, but VRF systems are marketed in the
U.S. by at least five manufacturers.
The success of the VRF in other coun-
tries, and its historically limited market
presence in the U.S., has several sources,
including:
• Differences in construction practices;
• The long history and large installed base
of ducted direct expansion (DX) systems
and chillers in the U.S. compared, for ex-
ample, to Europe, where many buildings
did not have air conditioning until recent
decades;
• Differences in regulatory environment
(e.g., regulations that discourage electric
chiller installations in Japan); and
• VRF technology has been developed and
promoted by Asian companies, which
had limited market presence in the U.S.
until recently. Also, building owners are
wary of HVAC suppliers whose parts
availability, service and technical support
infrastructure is uncertain.
What is VRF?
Many HVAC professionals are familiar
with ductless minisplit products. A varia-
Figure 1: Typical VRF configuration
in an office building.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, April 2007. © Copyright 2007 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning
Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
April 2007
ASHRAE Journal
25
tion of this product, often referred to as a multisplit, includes
multiple indoor evaporators connected to a single condens-
ing unit. Ductless products are fundamentally different from
ducted systems in that heat is transferred to or from the space
directly by circulating refrigerant to evaporators located near or
within the conditioned space. In contrast, conventional systems
transfer heat from the space to the refrigerant by circulating
air (in ducted systems) or water (in chillers) throughout the
building.
VRF systems are larger capacity, more complex versions of
the ductless multisplit systems, with the additional capability of
connecting ducted style fan coil units. They are inherently more
sophisticated than multisplits, with multiple compressors, many
evaporators, and complex oil and refrigerant management and
control systems. They do not provide ventilation, so a separate
ventilation system is necessary.
The term variable refrigerant flow refers to the ability of the
system to control the amount of refrigerant flowing to each of
the evaporators, enabling the use of many evaporators of dif-
fering capacities and configurations, individualized comfort
control, simultaneous heating and cooling in different zones,
and heat recovery from one zone to another. This refrigerant
flow control lies at the heart of VRF systems and is the major
technical challenge as well as the source of many of the system’s
advantages. Figure 1 illustrates a standard VRF configuration,
while Figure 2 shows a heat recovery unit providing simultane-
ous heating and cooling.
VRF Benefits
VRF systems have several key benefits, including:
• Installation Advantages. Chillers often require cranes for
installation, but VRF systems are lightweight and modu-
lar. Each module can be transported easily and fits into a
standard elevator. Multiples of these modules can be used
to achieve cooling capacities of hundreds of tons. Each
module (or set of two) is an independent refrigerant loop,
but they are controlled by a common control system. The
modularity also enables staged, floor-by-floor installations,
for example, if a building is only partly occupied, which is
similar to currently available self-contained VAV systems.
The relatively light weight of the system also may reduce
requirements for structural reinforcement of roofs. Because
ductwork is required only for the ventilation system, it can
be smaller than the ducting in standard ducted systems,
reducing building height and costs.
In cases where operable windows are present and meet
code requirements for ventilation, VRF systems are also par-
ticularly suitable for retrofitting historical buildings without
disturbing the structure or for older buildings with no air con-
ditioning. Finally, because the condensing units are normally
placed outdoors, no need exists for a machine room.
• Design Flexibility. A single condensing unit can be con-
nected to many indoor units of varying capacity (e.g., 0.5
to 4 tons [1.75 to 14 kW]) and configurations (e.g., ceiling
recessed, wall-mounted, floor console). Current products
enable up to 20 indoor units to be supplied by a single
condensing unit. Modularity also makes it easy to adapt
the HVAC system to expansion or reconfiguration of the
space, which may require additional capacity or different
terminal units.
• Maintenance and Commissioning. VRF systems with
their standardized configurations and sophisticated elec-
tronic controls are aiming toward near plug-and-play com-
missioning. Because they are DX systems, maintenance
costs for a VRF should be lower than for water-cooled
chillers, so water treatment issues are avoided. Normal
maintenance for a VRF, similar to that of any DX system,
consists mainly of changing filters and cleaning coils.
However, chillers, which often operate for 20 to 30 years,
normally would be anticipated to have a longer life expec-
tancy than a DX system such as a VRF.2 The large number
of compressors in a VRF may create a higher probability
of compressor failure, although the redundancy also leads,
in many cases, to a greater ability to continue to occupy
the space while repairs are made.
• Comfort. Many zones are possible, each with individual
setpoint control. Because VRF systems use variable speed
compressors with wide capacity modulation capabilities, they
can maintain precise temperature control, generally within
±1°F (±0.6°C), according to manufacturers’ literature.
• Energy Efficiency. The energy efficiency of VRF sys-
tems derives from several factors. The VRF essentially
eliminates duct losses, which are often estimated to be
between 10% to 20% of total airflow in a ducted system.3
VRF systems typically include two to three compressors,
one of which is variable speed, in each condensing unit,
enabling wide capacity modulation. This approach yields
high part-load efficiency, which translates into high sea-
sonal energy efficiency, because HVAC systems typically
spend most of their operating hours in the range of 40%
to 80% of maximum capacity.
At the present time, no ARI-certified rating system exists
for measuring the efficiency of a VRF system, so simple
EER comparisons to other systems can not be quoted here.
A careful review of all engineering data must be performed
to make accurate, quantitative comparisons.
For buildings requiring simultaneous heating and cooling,
heat recovery VRF systems can be used. These systems circulate
refrigerant between zones, transferring heat from the indoor
units of zones being cooled to those of zones being heated.
Each manufacturer has its own proprietary design, but most
use a three-pipe system (liquid, suction and discharge) and
special valving arrangements. Typically, extra heat exchangers
in distribution boxes are used to transfer some reject heat from
the superheated refrigerant exiting the zone being cooled to the
refrigerant that is going to the zone to be heated.
26
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2007
The modularity of the VRF also enables relatively simple
submetering of electricity (i.e., placing an electric meter on
one or a few condensing units is relatively simple, accurate and
inexpensive, whereas metering chilled water or refrigerant is
more complex). Though dif-
ficult to quantify, submetered
VRF systems may encour-
age energy-saving behavior
in multitenant buildings if
energy costs are charged ex-
plicitly to each tenant rather
than being hidden in overall
leasing costs.
First Costs
As with chilled water sys-
tems, installed costs for VRF
systems are highly variable,
project dependent, and difficult
to pin down. Total installed costs
for VRF systems are estimated
by some sources to be 5% to
20% higher than for chilled water systems providing equivalent
capacity,2 but actual costs are highly project dependent. The same
reference describes a 100,000 ft2 (9300 m2) office building project
in Brazil where the VRF system was approximately 15% to 22%
more expensive than a comparable chilled water system, but notes
that the cost was skewed by the high import tariff on the VRF. Also
cited is a 43,000 ft2 (4000 m2)
German hotel where the VRF
was approximately equivalent
in price to an air-cooled screw
chiller system. Equipment costs
in the U.S. would probably
be similar to those in Europe.
However, at the present time,
American contractors would
likely bid a higher installation
cost than their European coun-
terparts because they are less
familiar with the product and
need to build in a larger contin-
gency. As their experience and
comfort level with the product
increases, installation costs
should converge. A comparison
done by a VRF manufacturer of a 200 ton (700 kW) VRF to both
air- and water-cooled chillers in a U.S. application also showed an
Heat
Recovery
Unit
Figure 2: Heat recovery VRF system.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
28
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2007
installed cost premium of approximately 5% to 20% for the VRF,
but this was based on expected, rather than actual, costs.1
All these estimates apply to new construction. Replacing a
chiller and air-handling unit with another chiller and air handler
normally will be much less expensive than replacing it with a
VRF and ventilation system. The major cost issue is that a VRF
would need new refrigerant piping while the chiller already
would have its water piping installed.
In summary, it is likely that, at present, in the U.S., a VRF
system would involve a cost premium over a chiller in appli-
cations where the VRF is competitive. In some cases, where
chiller installations are particularly problematic (e.g., if access
for installing a new chiller requires major expense or demoli-
tion, if water piping is deteriorating and difficult to access for
repair) the VRF may have the lowest installed cost. In many
others, the VRF will not be suitable at all.
Energy Efficiency
As with installed costs, the energy efficiency of VRF systems
is application dependent. Both field tests and simulations can
be skewed by factors such as climate or the choice of baseline
systems for comparison.
For example, one article cited an installation in a government
building where much of the space is unoccupied during much
of the day when workers are out in the field.4 A rooftop VAV
was used on one side of the building and a VRF on the other.
The energy consumption of the VRF was approximately 38%
lower than for the VAV. However, other project details, including
the baseline system efficiency, were not published.
The general applicability of simulations also can be question-
able, due to the many different scenarios encountered in the real
world, and the limitations of current simulation tools.
A full year, hourly simulation, using standard spreadsheet
software, of a 538 ton (1892 kW) VRF compared to both screw
and centrifugal chillers yielded high energy savings for the VRF
relative to the other options.5 The cooling energy savings of about
30% may be explained by the relatively temperate Brazilian
climate. Another recent simulation compared a state-of-the-art
200 ton (700 kW) VRF system to both an air-cooled screw chiller
and a water-cooled chiller.1 The highest efficiency, newest R-410a
VRF system achieved energy savings of 30% to 40% compared
to the chillers, but an older R-22 VRF system showed little or no
savings compared to the chillers. The VRF system savings are
due to their high part-load efficiency. The improved efficiency
of the newest VRF systems compared to older generations is
due to component changes such as variable speed fan motors
and compressors that use ECM motors. The chiller efficiency
is higher than that of the VRF only at >90% load, but 80% of
the chiller operating hours occurred at 45% to 80% load. Vari-
able speed compressors in chillers are now common, but other
components such as pumps are often single speed.
Applications
VRF systems are generally best suited to buildings with
diverse, multiple zones requiring individual control, such as
office buildings, hospitals, or hotels. A VRF system does not
compete well with rooftop systems in a large low-rise build-
ing such as a big box retail store. Although VRF heat pumps
operate at ambient temperatures as low as 0°F (–18°C), as in
all heat pumps, their efficiency drops off considerably at low
temperatures, so they are less cost effective compared to gas
heating in very cold climates.
Market Acceptance Issues\*
Previous studies,2 recent experience in marketing the VRF,
as well as focus groups conducted with engineers, contrac-
tors, and facility managers, have revealed several important
concerns regarding the application of VRF systems in the U.S.
As manufacturers have intensified their education and training
efforts to address these concerns, and have explained some of
the technical features of the VRF that address these issues,
many of these concerns have been alleviated.
Lack of Awareness of Energy Efficiency Advantages. Most
industry professionals refer only to EER or kW/ton ratings
when considering efficiency. The more subtle energy efficiency
advantages of VRF systems, such as the reduction in duct
losses, the ease of electrical submetering, and even the higher
part load efficiency, frequently are overlooked. Engineers rarely
have the time and inclination to undertake complex building
energy simulations, and current non-proprietary tools such as
Energy Plus cannot address VRF systems. Furthermore, ARI
has not yet established a certification program for VRF system
performance, although it is expected by 2008.
First Cost. In many cases, the initial cost of a VRF system
is higher than that of a chilled water system, water source heat
pump, or rooftop DX system. Building owners often have no
incentive to accept higher first costs even if the payback period
is short, and they can be skeptical of energy savings predictions.
While a VRF may be cost competitive for new construction,
in a chiller replacement situation with existing water piping,
replacing the chiller would normally be less expensive than
installing a VRF.
Gas Heating for Cold Climates. Currently available VRF
systems have no integrated gas heating option. This factor
hampers their acceptance in cold climates for buildings with
substantial heating loads. Integration of the VRF with hydronic
heating works well and is sometimes done when such systems
already exist. However, using gas or oil heating with a VRF
requires a separate furnace or boiler, which is more expensive
than a gas-fired rooftop system or a chiller/boiler system.
Refrigerant Piping. Contractors are concerned about long
refrigerant piping runs for several reasons. They believe
that compliance with ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 15-2001,
Safety Standard for Refrigeration Systems, is difficult. Long
refrigerant lines also raise the specter of refrigerant leaks,
which can be difficult to find and repair, particularly in inac-
cessible spaces. These are legitimate concerns, but they can
be overcome.
\*Some of the information in this section is derived from Reference 2.
April 2007
ASHRAE Journal
29
Compliance with Standard 15-2001 is accomplished in
the same manner as with any DX system. The total refriger-
ant charge in the refrigerant loop must be within the limits
prescribed by Standard 15-2001 for the smallest zone served
by the system to ensure the safety of occupants if the entire
charge is released. In practice, the charge in a single system’s
refrigerant loop (condensing unit and multiple fan coil units)
is usually not problematic, except when serving relatively
small spaces. In those cases, one solution is to use a single
fan coil unit for multiple rooms, with ducting to each room.
In that case, the space to be considered in the safety calcula-
tion is the total space of the multiple rooms served by the
fan coil unit. Other suitable design approaches also exist.
Compliance with Standard 15-2001 can be achieved through
careful system design.
Minimization of leaks is also critical. VRF manufacturers
have developed products and protocols to address this concern.
Typically, all joints are brazed joints with no flared fittings.
Headers and splitters are specifically designed for the product
and do not require flaring or changing wall thicknesses. Con-
tractor training includes protocols that require pressure testing
of each refrigerant circuit during commissioning.
Long refrigerant piping loops also raise concerns about oil
return, and VRF products are designed to ensure proper oil
return. Typically, each compressor has its own oil separator,
which is optimized for the VRF system. Periodically, the VRF
goes into oil retrieval mode, during which time the thermo-
static expansion valve opens, and the compressor cycles at
high pressure to flush oil out of any locations where it has
accumulated.
Manufacturer Support. VRF systems were introduced by
Japanese companies with limited presence in the U.S., par-
ticularly in the large commercial HVAC market. In the past,
they have had limited sales and support for these products in
the U.S. Today, however, at least five manufacturers offer VRF
systems in the U.S., and several acquisitions involving U.S.
and Japanese manufacturers in recent years also should reduce
this concern.
Code Compliance Issues Specific to the U.S. Europe and
Japan, where VRF systems are widespread, have code require-
ments analogous to Standard 15-2001 and ANSI/ASHRAE
Standard 62.1-2004, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air
Quality, and VRF installations are engineered to meet those
requirements.
Future Directions
VRF manufacturers will target several additional challenges
over the next few years.
• ARI Rating Standard. Currently, no approved ARI
standard exists for a performance rating of VRF sys-
tems. Consequently, manufacturers need to apply for
waivers from the Department of Energy to market their
products in the U.S. Although these waivers have been
granted, new applications need to be submitted for new
product groups. Furthermore, an ARI standard will en-
Advertisement formerly in this space.
April 2007
ASHRAE Journal
31
able stakeholders to compare different manufacturers’
products more accurately and will provide them with a
greater comfort level with the products. A draft provi-
sional standard is under review with final publication
expected in 2008.
• Energy Modeling Tools. Current, non-proprietary
building energy simulation tools like Energy Plus and
DOE-2 cannot model VRF systems. Manufacturers are
working to resolve this issue. In the meantime, only
proprietary tools are available,
which some stakeholders view with
skepticism.
• Integration of Outside Air. Cur-
rently, ventilation systems used in
conjunction with VRF systems are
engineered separately on a case-
by-case basis. Manufacturers are
evaluating potential approaches for
an integrated solution, incorporat-
ing controls to ensure adequate
outside air and economizing, while
optimizing overall performance.
• Broaden Installer Base. The short-
age of skilled installers is problem-
atic for the HVAC industry as a
whole, but expanding the number
of installers who are comfortable
with extensive refrigerant piping
work is particularly critical for the
VRF market.
Conclusions
VRF systems are not suitable for
all commercial building applications.
However, they are an excellent option
for certain projects, and one more tool
for engineers to consider. As more
VRF units are installed and we gain
further operating experience in the
U.S., many of the concerns expressed
by industry professionals are likely
to diminish.
References
1. Smith, L. 2006. “A Daikin Perspective
on VRF.” Daikin Air Conditioning U.K.
2. Roth, K.W., et al 2002. “Energy Con-
sumption Characteristics of Commercial
Building HVAC Systems Volume III: En-
ergy Savings Potential.” Report prepared by
TIAX LLC for DOE Building Technologies
Program.
3. Fisk, W.J., et al. 1998. “Duct Systems in
Large Commercial Buildings: Physical Char-
acterization, Air Leakage, and Heat Conduc-
tion Gains.” LBNL Report, LBNL-42339.
4. Air Conditioning, Heating, & Refrigeration News. January 14,
2002. “Digital variable multi A/C technology passes test.”
5. Interact Ltda. of Brazil for DK Systemas De Ar Condicionado
Ltda. 2002. ���������������������������������������������������������
“Estudo comparativo de alternativas de climatizacao para
o predio Cardoso de Mello (Comparative Study of Alternative Air
Conditioning Systems for Predio Cardoso de Mello).” February.

ashrae.org
April 2007
Donald C. Erickson is president of Energy Con-
cepts Company in Annapolis, Md.
About the Author
Extending the Boundaries of
Ammonia Absorption Chillers
‘There is Nothing New in AAR’
In reply to this assertion, consider the
six AAR demonstration plants fielded in
the past two years shown in the sidebar
Ammonia Absorption Refrigeration
Examples.
Each plant demonstrates new AAR
capabilities, as detailed later. Collec-
tively, they show the boundaries of what
is possible with ammonia absorption
cycles are beyond the limits imposed by
conventional wisdom.
‘AAR is Inefficient’
It is frequently said that vapor com-
pression refrigeration (VCR) delivers
a coefficient of performance (COP) of
2.5 to 5, whereas ammonia absorption
refrigeration (AAR) is in the 0.5 to 0.8
range. Therefore, AAR is perceived as
less efficient. This misrepresents the ther-
modynamic reality, because it equates the
electricity required by VCR with the fuel
or waste heat required by AAR.
Consider what would happen if one
tried to power a VCR with the 200°F
(93°C) heat used for AAR. One would
need a low-temperature power cycle,
which would convert the low-temperature
heat to power, at about 8% efficiency.
Then the electricity is applied to the VCR
with an electrical COP of 4. The result-
ing heat-based COP of the VCR is 0.08
× 4 = 0.32, which is only half that of the
AAR. Without the power cycle, the low-
By Donald C. Erickson, Member ASHRAE
A
mmonia absorption refrigeration’s (AAR) ability to be powered by
  waste heat means this more than 150-year-old technology has an
important role to play in reducing energy consumption and greenhouse
gas emissions. Nevertheless, the engineering community has mostly
discounted or ignored AAR, usually because of a variety of misper-
ceptions. This article addresses those misperceptions, and hopefully
reveals the promise of AAR sufficiently to invite further investigation.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, April 2007. © Copyright 2007
American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is pre-
sented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed elec-
tronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
April 2007
ASHRAE Journal
33
Example 1: 100 Ton Heat Pump/
Chiller, Livingston, Calif.
This unit supplies 1.2 million Btu/h
(100 tons) of chilling and 3.2 million
Btu/h water heating from a steam
input of 2 million Btu/h (2,000 lb/h).
The hot water and chilled water are
required 20 hours per day, five days
per week at a poultry processing plant. The $200,000 instal-
lation provides $120,000 per year utility savings. Pacific Gas
& Electric (PG&E) and the California Energy Commission
(CEC) are participating in this project.
Example 2: 160 Ton Heat-
Actuated Chiller, Blythe, Calif.
This unit is part of an 830 kWe
distributed energy project. Electric
power is produced by natural gas-
fired reciprocating engines. The
exhaust heat and jacket heat are
used to make 160 tons of 26°F (563
kW of –3°C) refrigeration for a cold storage warehouse.
The refrigeration increases the customer’s electrical sav-
ings by more than 20%, and boosts the effective efficiency
of converting natural gas to electricity to above 40%. The
refrigeration is used to produce 32°F (0°C) chilled water
using a direct-expansion Baudelot coil. Desert Power Inc. is
the project developer.
Example 3: 60 Ton Solar Chiller,
Douglas, Ariz.
A solar thermal collector supplies
210°F (99°C) heat to this chiller,
which supplies 60 tons of 44°F (211
kW of 7°C) chilling to Cochise Com-
munity College. The COP of 0.75
makes this the highest efficiency
low-temperature heat-activated chiller available. Further
savings are realized from the low parasitic power, as the
required utility flow rates are only about 60% as high as for
conventional heat-activated chillers. Industrial Solar Technolo-
gies and APS are participating in this project.
Example 4: 16 Ton Geothermal
Chiller, Chena Hot Springs, Alaska
The only year-round ice hotel is
kept frozen in the summer during
24-hour sunlit days by this chiller.
It is powered by 155°F (68°C) geo-
thermal hot spring water. It supplies
0°F (–18°C) brine to an air handler
which keeps the hotel at 24°F (–4°C). Heat is rejected to
55°F (13°C) river water.
Example 5: 85 Ton Chiller, Bloom-
field, N.M.
Light ends (propane and gasoline)
are recovered and a refinery flare is
extinguished by this unit, installed at
a 25,000 barrels per day oil (bpd) re-
finery. The 300°F (149°C) waste heat
from the refinery powers the chiller,
which provides 50 tons (176 kW) of refrigeration at –25°F
(–32°C) and 35 tons at +25°F (123 kW at –4°C). More than
150 bpd of liquid product is recovered, in lieu of being flared.
The U.S. Department of Energy Office of Industrial Technology
provided cost-share support to this project.
Example 6: 19 Ton Thermocharger,
Gainesville, Fla.
This unit is part of a trigeneration
system that produces power, chilling,
and water. The air chiller is integrated
with a semi-closed power cycle com-
prised of a high pressure regenerative
microturbine. Heat from a mixture of
high-temperature recirculating exhaust and fresh air powers the
AAR. The mixture is then cooled to below ambient temperature
in the evaporator. The chilling produces 1.4 kg of water for each
kg of fuel consumed. The chilled air flows to the high pressure
turbine. Because of the inlet air cooling, a negligible amount of
power and efficiency are lost on a hot day. The direct contact
heat recovery vapor generator and evaporator are compact
with low-pressure drop. This system is being tested under a
U.S. Department of Defense SBIR Phase II project.
Ammonia Absorption Refrigeration Examples
grade heat is only a waste product from the VCR perspective,
but it can serve as an energy source for AAR. That is why it
is claimed that AAR provides “energy-free” refrigeration (i.e.,
not requiring any prime fuel or electricity).
The meaningful efficiency comparison is between AAR and
any other technology capable of converting low-grade heat to
refrigeration. Many candidates exist, such as adsorption, ther-
moacoustics, jet ejectors, Rankine-reverse Rankine cycles, etc.
AAR outperforms them all, both from an efficiency perspective
and from the cost perspective. Only lithium bromide (LiBr)
absorption comes close, and becomes competitive with AAR
in only one niche—water-cooled air conditioning.
As an example, among the demonstration plants in the side-
bar, the Douglas, Ariz., 60 ton (211 kW) solar thermal chiller
(Example 3) has a COP of 0.75. Furthermore, the Livingston,
Calif., 100 ton unit (352 kW) (Example 1) is more than 200%
34
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2007
efficient (on a first law basis, i.e., useful heating and chilling
out divided by input heat).
The most exciting development regarding AAR efficiency
is the advent of the heat-activated heat pump/chiller.1,4 This
apparatus supplies 1.4 units of heat pumping and 0.6 units of
chilling for every Btu of input fuel. In other words, it increases
water heating efficiency from the 90% level of current water
heaters to 140% (AAR heat pump only) or 200% (chilling
plus heat pumping). This doubling of energy efficiency is
quite revolutionary, even exceeding what hybrids are doing
for automobile efficiency. Furthermore, the input heat needn’t
be prime fuel. It can be waste heat or solar heat. With these
low-temperature heat sources, somewhat higher temperatures
are necessary to accomplish both chilling and heat pumping,
compared to the temperature required for heating only. In other
words, whereas 180°F (82°C) heat can be used to either make
hot water or to make chilling individually, it requires 250°F
(121°C) heat to make both chilling and heat pumped hot water
simultaneously.
‘AAR is Large, Heavy, and Costly’
The industrial AAR plants built 80 years ago had these char-
acteristics. Of course, the competing VCR plants of that era did
also. Since then, great improvements have been made in VCR,
such as the invention of halocarbon refrigerants, the shift to copper
exchangers and components, and improvements in compressors.
In the meantime, industrial AAR was in a state of arrested devel-
opment, while VCR rapidly improved and became dominant. In
an era when electricity prices were trending toward being perhaps
too cheap to meter, no incentive existed to modernize a competing
refrigeration technology that could be fired by waste heat.
In the 1950s through the early 1970s, when gas prices were
low, gas-fired absorption air conditioning was developed. This
included LiBr units for large commercial applications (typically
above 100 tons [352 kW]) and AAR units for residences (3
to 5 tons [10.5 to 17.6 kW]). The AAR units were air-cooled,
whereas the LiBr units could only be water-cooled. Great strides
were made in reducing the size and weight of the AAR units,
but only at that small (3 to 5 ton [10.5 to 17.6 kW]) scale. The
large industrial AAR units of that era still used low-performance
shell-and-tube heat exchangers, and were much larger, heavier,
and costlier than comparable LiBr units.
During the past few decades, it became clear to many working
in this field that waste heat-fired AAR could save significant
energy by heat pumping and supplying energy-free refrigera-
tion. Accordingly, attention was focused on larger AAR units
with the objective of modernizing them, along the lines of what
had been done to VCR and to small-scale AAR, to reduce size,
weight and cost. New AAR units are half the weight and foot-
print of comparable state-of-the-art LiBr units. At comparable
levels of production, they cost less because of their lighter
weight and lower cost materials. For example, the Livingston,
Calif., 100 ton (352 kW) unit weighs 5,000 lb (2268 kg) and
has a footprint of 5 ft by 5 ft (1.5 m by 1.5 m).2 This is half the
size and weight of 100 ton (352 kW) LiBr units.
With VCR, the total cost is dominated by the cost of the
compressor. Mass production of mature design compressors
yields low cost. AAR will not reach capital costs on par with
mass-produced VCR until comparable levels of production
are achieved. Until that time, a tradeoff will exist between the
capital cost premium and operating cost savings.
Custom AAR units start at about $1,000 per ton ($284 per
kW) of refrigeration. The cost of AAR per ton increases gradu-
ally with lower refrigeration temperature. However, the cost of
VCR increases rapidly with lower temperature, so AAR gains
cost advantages the lower the temperature. One study shows a
substantial capital cost advantage for AAR at –30°F (–34°C)
compared to VCR. This is in addition to the operating cost
advantage, because the AAR operates on waste heat.5
As an indicator of cost, the 100 ton (352 kW) Livingston,
Calif., unit and the 160 ton (563 kW) Blythe, Calif., unit had a
cost of manufacture of $1,000 per refrigeration ton ($284 per
kW), and incorporated a substantial degree of custom design
and unique features.
‘High Temperature Driving Heat Is Required for AAR’
For a variety of reasons, many industrial AAR plants have been
powered by 100 psig (690 kPa) steam. This (approximate) steam
pressure is commonly available, and occasionally the steam is in
excess, so it makes sense to power AAR from it rather than simply
to vent it. The condensing temperature of this pressure steam is hot
enough to enable the AAR to reach –40°F (–40°C), which covers
most industrial refrigeration applications. However, it would be a
mistake to conclude that all AAR cycles require this driving tem-
perature. New AAR cycles allow use of much lower temperature
heat sources, and especially those having a temperature glide.
Glide refers to a change in temperature as an object gives up
its heat. Sensible heat usually has a glide, whereas latent heat is
frequently isothermal. Waste heat is frequently in the sensible
heat category, i.e., has a temperature glide. It is desirable to be
able to use waste heat with a low starting temperature, as that
creates more opportunities. It is also desirable to extract a lot of
heat from the waste heat source, i.e., cool it down considerably,
to gain the maximum benefit from it. Modern AAR plants ac-
complish both of these objectives, which is part of their promise.
The 16 ton (56 kW) Chena Hot Springs, Alaska, unit (Example 4)
is powered by 155°F (68°C) geothermal water, using it to make
0°F (–18°C) refrigeration.6 The 60 ton (211 kW) Douglas, Ariz.,
unit (Example 3) uses 200°F (93°C) solar hot water, cooling it
below 160°F (71°C).
‘The Large Ammonia Charge Makes AAR Unsafe’
The two implications here are: that ammonia is an unsafe
refrigerant, and that AAR requires a lot of ammonia.
Ammonia is one of the largest commodity chemicals in the
world, with annual production of more than 30 million tons (27
million metric tons). The primary safety concern is its toxicity,
and it is rated as a toxic gas. That toxicity, which is relatively
low compared to other toxic gases, is more a function of the
extensive use of this chemical, rather than its level of toxicity.
April 2007
ASHRAE Journal
35
Safe procedures have been developed for its handling and use,
such that it is the most commonly used industrial refrigerant in
the world. Ammonia is classified a nonflammable liquid by the
U.S. Department of Transportation. It is a naturally occurring
chemical, present in the water and air everywhere.
Modern industrial AAR plants using compact heat exchang-
ers have an ammonia charge as low as 1.5 lbs/ton (0.19 kg/kW)
of refrigeration capacity.
The Livingston, Calif., unit (a 100 ton [352 kW] heat pump/
chiller) is one example of this, and has an ammonia charge
of 150 lb (68 kg). Installations with 10,000 lbs (4536 kg) of
ammonia must institute a formal safety program referred to as
“Process Safety Management.” Ammonia is lighter than air,
so outdoor releases are dispersed rapidly. Accidental ammonia
releases exceeding 100 lb (45 kg) per 24 hours must be reported
to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency.
‘AAR IS Rare’
There is no question that AAR is not in the mainstream of
heating and refrigeration technologies. However, AAR does
have several well-established niches, and is more widespread
than most people realize. The numerically largest success story
is the small heat-actuated refrigerator. These refrigerators range
from the propane-fired units found in camper vans, to the elec-
tric resistance heated units found in almost all upscale hotel
rooms. They have been manufactured at a rate approaching 1
million units per year for more than 50 years.
Another niche market is the gas-fired residential air condi-
tioner referred to earlier. These units reached a peak production
rate of about 500,000 per year in the early 1970s. Production
declined drastically when gas became unavailable during the
1970s gas crisis, and eventually dropped below 5,000 units per
year, but is now recovering.
The third niche is the oldest, and accounts for the most cumula-
tive tonnage, although the smallest number of installations. This
is the industrial AAR market. The initial industrial AARs were
developed in France in the 1850s by the Carre brothers.3 In the
early 1860s, the Confederate States of America imported the first
AAR units into the Americas, to New Orleans. Virtually every year
since then a handful of industrial AAR units have been fielded. The
attributes were never enough to make them a booming success, but
they were good enough to keep them from disappearing.
AAR is no longer rare. More important is what the future may
hold, and the prospects that AAR presents for efficient use of
waste heat. There are many waste heat sources in the world, count-
ing both first law and second law opportunities: engine, furnace,
and boiler exhaust; fuel-fired water heaters and process heaters;
process streams; solar-heated surfaces; and geothermal heat.
AAR can produce energy-free refrigeration from any of those
sources. The 160 ton (563 kW) Blythe, Calif., unit (Example 2)
and the 85 ton (300 kW) Bloomfield, N.M., unit (Example 5) are
powered directly by waste heat. AAR is robust, able to produce
heat pumping and drying from the same heat sources. This makes
it possible, and even likely, that at least one-third of all future
refrigeration and heating plants will be based on AAR.
Conclusions
Modern ammonia absorption refrigeration units are compact,
lightweight, efficient, economical, robust and safe. They can be
powered by exceptionally low-temperature heat, whether waste
heat, solar heat or geothermal heat. Demonstration plants are
operating that verify these attributes. These demonstrations go
beyond the boundaries conventionally assigned to this technology,
and even more applications are possible. With aggressive devel-
opment and deployment, AAR can contribute substantial global
reductions in fuel consumption and greenhouse gas emissions.
References
1. Erickson, D.C., et al. 2002. “Prototype commercial hot water
gas heat pump (CHWGHP)—design and performance.” ASHRAE
Transactions 108(1):792 – 798.
2. California Energy Commission Public Interest Energy Research
Program. 2005. “Gas Fired Heat Pump for Heating and Refrigeration in
Food and Beverage Industry.” http://tinyurl.com/2howjq or www.energy.
ca.gov/2005publications/CEC-500-2005-094/CEC-500-2005-094.pdf.
3. Carre, P. 1857. French Patent.
4. Mannapperuma, J.D. 2006. “Energy-saving heat pump produces
scalding, chilling water.” WATT Poultry USA (12):18 – 19.
5. Amelse, J., et al. 2005. “Green PX processes that use waste heat
powered ammonia absorption refrigeration.” AIChE Proceedings.
6. Erickson, D.C., et al. 2007. “Ice kept nice.” Appliance Design
(1):28 – 32. www.nxtbook.com/nxtbooks/bnp/ad0107/.

36
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2007
About the Author
HVAC
Controls
After an Outside Contaminant Release
R
ecently (notably since Sept. 11, 2001), awareness has in-
creased of vulnerabilities to intentional chemical, biological,
and radiological (CBR) releases. Many guidelines have been written
regarding the control of HVAC equipment in the event of a release.
Two common recommendations are
to close the outside air intakes, and shut
off HVAC equipment after the detec-
tion of a release outside a building in
an attempt to prevent the release from
entering the building via outside air ducts
of air-handling units (AHUs).1,2,3,4 It is
typically recommended that the effects
of such actions and system interactions
be considered before implementation
of these strategies. The effectiveness of
implementing both recommendations
was studied at an Army building and the
results presented here.
Two tests were conducted to study
the effects of HVAC system operation
on contaminant uptake and distribution
for two scenarios: a release outside the
building that goes undetected so that
the HVAC system continues to operate
normally, and a release outside the build-
ing that is immediately detected by a
perfect sensor such that the HVAC AHUs
bringing in outside air are immediately
shut down, and outside air dampers are
closed in an attempt to prevent intake and
dispersion throughout the building of the
released contaminant.
Building Description
The building in which the tests were
performed is a classroom/administration
facility, constructed in 1997, located in
the Midwest United States. The two-story
building has approximately 30,000 ft2
(2800 m2) of floor space. Approximately
26,500 ft2 (2460 m2) of this are climate-
David Underwood, P.E., is a principal investigator
at the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Engineering
Research and Development Center, Construction
Engineering Research Laboratory (ERDC-CERL)
in Champaign, Ill.
By David Underwood, P.E., Member ASHRAE
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, April 2007. © Copyright 2007 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or
in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
April 2007
ASHRAE Journal
37
controlled, occupied areas. Climate-controlled building volume
is 344,565 ft3 (9760 m3). The building envelope is primarily
brick-clad concrete masonry unit (CMU) construction with a
flat membrane roof.
A forced air HVAC system provides heating and cooling
to the occupied areas as shown in Figure 1. Control of all air
handlers was accomplished by single loop digital controllers.
The system consists of two supply air-handling units (AHU1
and AHU2), an exhaust air handler (AHU3), and a network of
22 VAV boxes. Two of the VAV boxes were inoperative. The
opening and closing time of their dampers was approximately
90 seconds. Both the OA AHU (AHU2) and the exhaust AHU
(AHU3) are located in a mechanical room on the ground floor
on the north side of the building. Both outside air intakes are
on the north side of the building. The mixed AHU (AHU1) is
located in a mechanical room on the second floor on the north
side of the building. The zone terminal units are located in
the plenum space above the zones. Hot and chilled water are
provided by local boilers and chillers.
The OA AHU (AHU2) provides 100% conditioned outside air
for the required ventilation for indoor air quality requirements
to the zones’ fan-powered terminal units under the control of
room occupancy sensors. It is controlled to provide 0.8 in. w.c.
(200 Pa) duct static pressure. The mixed AHU (AHU1) also is
controlled to 0.8 in. w.c. (200 Pa) duct static pressure and can
provide a mixture of outside and return air when in economizer
mode. When not in economizer mode, AHU1 provides 100%
return air. AHU1 supplies the fan-powered terminal units to
offset space cooling loads. The exhaust fan is controlled at a
speed of 72% of AHU2.
Each terminal unit is a commercially available VAV box that
includes a “series” fan that operates continuously and an inlet
to allow it to recirculate plenum return air (PRA) (Figure 1) to
its zone. Since each VAV box has a constantly operating single
speed fan, the supply airflow rate to each space (SS) is nearly
constant, but the source of the supply air varies as the thermal
load and/or occupancy varies. When the room cooling load is
reduced, the supply air to the room (SS) consists of less supply
air and more PRA. During the heating season there is no SA,
but supply airflow rate to the room (SS) remains nearly constant.
Similarly, when the occupants vacate a room, VA is reduced to
zero and is replaced in SS by increased PRA. Each terminal
unit also has hot water reheat (not shown in the schematic) to
provide space heating/reheating as needed.
The space above the drop-tile ceiling on each floor serves as
a return air plenum. Air enters the plenum from the individual
spaces, primarily through return grilles located in the drop
ceiling frame. The upper and lower floor return air plenums are
connected by a transfer duct running from the ceiling of the
upper and lower classrooms on the north side of the building to
the second floor mechanical room. This transfer duct provides
the return air (RA1 and RA2) to AHU1.
Toilet exhausts operate continuously and independently of
the AHUs.
Test Setup and Instrumentation
The test consisted of a gas release mechanism and eight
ambient air analyzers calibrated for sulfur hexafluoride (SF6).
The release was a single point release at the intake to the out-
side air duct on the north side of the building for the OA AHU
(Photograph 1). A mass flow controller, operated via a laptop
computer was used to control and measure the release of SF6.
Figures 2 and 3, respectively, show the locations of the gas
analyzers in the lower and upper levels:
• LowClassN is located in the middle of a classroom on the
north side of the building on the lower level;
• LowHall is located in the middle of the hallway on the
lower level midway down the length;
• LowAdmin is located in the middle of an administrative
area on the lower level;
• UpClassN is located in the middle of a classroom on the
north side of the building on the upper level;
• UpClassS is located in the middle of a classroom on the
south side of the building on the upper level;
• UpHallM is located in the hallway on the upper level,
midway down the length;
• UpHallS is located in the hallway on the upper level on the
south end of the hallway, just north of the lobby; and
• UpAdmin is located in the middle of an administrative area
on the upper level.
These locations were chosen because they were believed to
best represent the airflow patterns within the building. Since
the sensor instrumentation was costly (approximately $20,000
for each location), only a limited number of locations could be
monitored. UpClassN and LowClassN were considered espe-
cially important for two reasons. The inlet for return ducting
for the mixed AHU was located in the ceiling plenum of these
rooms. These locations also were the closest to the air handlers,
and consequently had the shortest duct runs.
Ambient Air Analyzer
The analyzers use a single-beam infrared spectrophotometer.
A detection limit of 0.01 ppm and accuracy of ±10% of read-
ing are claimed by the manufacturer. The units were factory
calibrated approximately two months prior to tests described
here. Manufacturer recommends calibration yearly. Data was
later downloaded from the analyzers to a laptop computer.
SF6 Release Mechanism
The SF6 release device consisted of a 13.85 lb (6.28 kg) cyl-

inder of 99.996% pure SF6, a manual needle valve, and a NIST
traceable calibrated laminar mass flow controller including an
electronic solenoid valve interfaced to a laptop computer for
control and data collection of release. The controller had a con-
trol range of 0 to 10 standard liters per minute (1271 cfm).
Outside Release With No Response
Release 1 consisted of a two-minute release at the outside air
duct intake as shown in Photograph 1. The rate of release was
set at 0.35 cfm (10 standard liters per minute) and resulted in
a total release of 0.25 lb (113.7 g) of SF6. The HVAC system
was operated normally both during and after the release. The
mixed AHU economizer mode was off. The VAV box controls
were overridden to mimic a full cooling requirement (VA and
SA setpoint set to maximum design). This is the operation
expected with all zones occupied and outside air temperatures
such that full cooling is required. The airflows were measured
under these conditions prior to the release and are shown in
Figure 1 (see measurements in green).
Outside Release With Control Action
Release 2 consisted of a four-minute release at the outside air
duct intake as shown in Figure 1. The rate of release was set at 0.35
cfm (10 standard liters per minute) and resulted in a total release
of 0.5 lb (231.8 g) of SF6. Release 2 was larger than Release 1
to compensate for anticipated effect of the control action, which
would allow such small amounts of contaminant into the build-
ing that its detection would be difficult. The HVAC system was
operated as it was for Release 1, except that: (1) the OA AHU was
shut down by throwing the fan disconnect switch, shutting the fan
motor off instantly, and (2) the outside air damper was commanded
to its closed position. Both of these were done simultaneously
Mixed Air

Figure 2: Lower level sampling locations.
Figure 3: Upper level sampling locations.
with the beginning of the release. As was the case with Release
1, the exhaust fan was left running. Because the building was not
occupied during release, airflows were not remeasured. This was
intended to simulate the scenario in which a perfect sensor (one
that reports the presence of a harmful airborne substance with zero
delay time) in the outside air duct coupled with a control system
responds to the positive test by shutting down the air-handler unit
(including shutting the fan off and closing the outside air damper)
at the moment a positive sensor reading is present.
Several aspects of this simulated control response should
be noted:
• OA AHU fan motor and fan did not immediately stop spin-
ning due to inertial forces.
• The outside air damper did not immediately close due to
the response of its pneumatic actuator. Exact measurements
of these two aspects were not taken. Visual inspection indi-
cated an approximately seven-second closing time for the
outside air damper and 10 seconds for the fan to stop.
• The exhaust fan AHU and fan-powered VAV boxes re-
mained running because the power wiring and controls
made simultaneously shutting them down unfeasible.
• No such sensor exists for many CBR releases. Presently,
many substances that can be detected require significant
time for analysis (several hours or more is typical).
These factors point to several aspects of such a system that
would have to be improved for the simulated control response
to be possible and effective. (The building did not begin the
release with zero concentration of tracer gas due to time limita-
tions in purging it prior to this second release.)
Weather Conditions
Because weather conditions can affect infiltration and ex-
filtration, weather data was recorded using a portable weather
station several hundred feet away from the test building. During
the tests the maximum wind speed recorded was 5.4 mph (8.6
km/h). Wind direction was from the southeast.
Data Analysis
Figure 4 plots the tracer gas concentrations increase from
time of release for the two releases over time in the eight loca-
tions, and the total volume of releases. As expected, the tracer
gas concentration increases nearly simultaneously with the first
release of 19.6 L. The HVAC system does an excellent job of
distributing it. The highest concentration during Release 1 was
found at UpClassN (classroom on the north side of the building
upper floor). This is likely due to the available return airflow
paths to the mixed AHU. The ceiling of UpClassN contains the
ducted return air for this floor for the mixed AHU. This duct not
only draws air from this room for return air, but it also draws air
from other zones through doorways and hallways. The result is
that air from all zones on the upper floor tend to follow a path
leading to UpClassN.
Release 2 (from 10:38 to 10:42) of an additional 40 L reveals
an unexpected result. During and after Release 2, the OA AHU
was shut down (fan off and outside air dampers closed), yet
the tracer gas concentrations increase nearly as quickly as they

did in Release 1. Several possible
contributing factors exist:
• The fan motor and fan did not
immediately stop spinning
due to inertial forces.
• The outside air damper did
not immediately close due to
the response of its pneumatic
actuator.
• The exhaust AHU continued
to operate, creating nega-
tive pressure in the building,
inducing airflow through the
VAV boxes which have a
direct airflow path to the OA
AHU.
Exact measurements of these
locations and both releases. The PCIV at LowClassN was 175
ppb/L for Release 2 vs. 88 ppb/L for Release 1. Shutting down
the OA AHU actually increased the effect of a release on the
concentration of the tracer gas at LowClassN. This was likely
due to two combined factors:
1. The ducting from the OA AHU to the VAV box serving
LowClassN has a shorter length than any other VAV box.
Shutting down the OA AHU allowed the tracer gas that
was able to enter the ducting to most easily be drawn into
the VAV box for LowClassN.
2. The OA AHU was providing less air for dilution.
The opposite effect was seen at UpClassN where PCIV
was 136 ppb/L for Release 1 and 74 ppb/L for Release 2. The
designed OA AHU and MA AHU flow rates may, in some un-
known way, be the reason for this. Although the average ratio
of OA/MA design flow rate for all zones within the building is
71%, it is 150% for UpClassS and 38% for LowClassN.
The time it takes for a release to reach various areas of the
building also is of great interest. This information has the
aspects were not taken. Visual inspection indicated an approxi-
mately seven-second closing time for the outside air damper
and 10 seconds for the fan to come to a complete stop. Previous
measurements of the exhaust air AHU indicated a flow rate of
3,550 cfm (1675 L/s).
Although the releases are not directly comparable due to the
different release durations (two and four minutes), the duration
as compared to air-change rates was relatively small for Release
1 (1.6 air changes per hour). Air change rate for Release 2 was
not measured, but since the outside air AHU was shut down, it
is reasonable to assume it was much smaller. Both releases can
be treated as instantaneous and, therefore, are comparable.
Another interesting result was that LowClassN reaches an in-
crease in peak concentration of 7,012 ppb after Release 2, more
than four times the increase in peak concentration of 1,523 ppb
reached after Release 1 despite that OA AHU was not running
and the outside air dampers were closed. It is speculated that this
was due to the ducting from the OA AHU to the VAV box that
serves LowClassN is shorter in length than any other VAV box.
Significant infiltration into the building driven by outside wind
conditions in the vicinity of the release is considered unlikely. The
wind was relatively calm with a maximum speed of 5.4 mph (8.6
km/h) from the southeast. Release that did not enter the outside
air intake would have been blown away from the building.
A study of peak tracer gas concentrations before and after
release for the two releases also proved interesting. Peak concen-
tration increase to volume of release (PCIV) was defined as:
PCIV = (PCar – Cbr) / Vr
where
PCar = the peak concentration after release
(ppb)
Cbr = the near steady state concentration
before release (ppb)
Vr = the volume of release (liter)
Units of PCIV = (ppb/L)
PCIV then relates changes in concentration peak to volume
of release. Figure 5 shows the calculated PCIV for all eight

Figure 7: Normalized exposure for 30 minutes after release.
potential to guide occupants (identify safe areas or routes) in
the event of a contaminant release. Figure 6 shows the elapsed
time from release to the first detection at the various locations
for the two releases. Four of the sample locations have a sig-
nificant change in the delay time for the two releases, while
the other four locations have a relatively small change.
Also of interest is the exposure over time that one would
experience in the various locations. Figure 7 shows the total
integrated increase in SF6 concentration for 30 minutes af-
ter the two releases. Comparison of Figures 4 and 7 shows
that peak concentration differences for the two releases do
not necessarily correspond to a similar change in exposure.
The location with the highest exposure (LowClassN) also
experienced the highest absolute peak. However the increase
in PCIV of 99% corresponded to an increase in exposure of
212%. UpClassS, which experienced a 23% decrease in peak
concentration for Release 2, experienced only a 1% decrease
in exposure. UpClassN experienced a 45% decrease in PCIV
and a 26% increase in exposure.
This building, like the majority of existing buildings, is
unprotected by constant CBR filtration and is unlikely to
ever be. Therefore, the outside air intake is one of the most
vulnerable locations. Because of this, the degree to which an
outside release would enter the building through the building
envelope was not investigated. However, pressurization tests
(blower door tests) were performed. These indicated that in
the case of this building, only a small percentage of building
envelope leakage was on the side of the building where the
outside air intakes exist.
Summary and Conclusions
Two tracer gas experiments were performed on an existing
building simulating an external release. The first experiment
involved no action by the building controls. The second in-
cluded shutting the outside air-handling unit off along with
closing of the outside air damper (sometimes informally re-
ferred to as “The Big Red Button,” representing a single action
affecting several systems) simultaneously with the beginning
of release. The results showed that, for this particular build-
ing, this simplistic response is inadequate. For this strategy
to be effective, several additional operational issues would
have to be addressed such as: shutting off all fan-powered
VAV boxes (This was not feasible in this building because
of the way the fans are powered and controlled.); braking of
fan motors; fast-acting, well-sealed dampers; system interac-
tions; control of exhaust fans; and infiltration. Furthermore
the tests showed that, depending on the location within the
building, the resulting concentration of the tracer gas can be
increased by this control strategy. The results also indicate
that, for this building and the test release scenario, the delay
time from release to detection within the building is largely
unaffected by the control strategy in some interior building
locations, and largely affected in other building locations.
The effectiveness of shutting off fans and shutting outside air
dampers is dependent upon many variables. Which ones have
the greatest effect was not investigated. Further research on
this control strategy is recommended.
References
1. U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Naval Facilities Engineering
Command, and Air Force Civil Engineer Support Agency. 2004.
Security Engineering: Procedures for Designing Airborne Chemical,
Biological, and Radiological Protection for Buildings. UFC 4-012-11
pp. 14, 22.
2. Department of the Army U.S. Army Corps of Engineers. 1999.
Design of Collective Protection Shelters to Resist Chemical, Biological
and Radiological (CBR) Agents. ETL 1110-3-498. pp. B-2.
3. ASHRAE. 2003. Report of Presidential Ad Hoc Committee for
Building Health and Safety under Extraordinary Incidents on Risk
Management Guidance for Health, Safety, and Environmental Security
under Extraordinary Incidents. www.ashrae.org/homelandsecurity.
4. Felker L. 2004. “Smoke-control systems and homeland security.”
Heating/Piping/Air Conditioning Engineering 76(7).

ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2007
Using Microturbines
By Robert Zogg, P.E., Member ASHRAE; John Bowman; Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; and
James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
M
icroturbines use small combustion turbines to
provide electric power for distributed generation
(DG) applications, including combined heat and
power (CHP). Compared to conventional gas turbine genera-
tors, microturbines typically have lower electric generation
capacities (30 to 500 kW versus more than 1 MW), lower
pressure ratios (about 4 versus 8 to 10), radial-flow designs
(versus axial-flow designs), and incorporate recuperators
(versus typically unrecuperated designs). Microturbines
are promoted as lightweight, compact, low noise/vibration,
low emissions, and fuel-flexible compared to compet-
ing DG technologies such as internal-combustion-engine
generators.
Characteristics and Development Status
Figure 1 depicts a schematic of a typical microturbine
integrated with a CHP system that provides power, heating,
and cooling. The compressor section raises air pressure to
about four atmospheres and the air then enters a recuperator
that preheats the air using turbine exhaust. Subsequently, the
heated air enters the combustor, where pressurized fuel is
injected and burned. Since commercial buildings are typically
supplied with low-pressure natural gas, a compressor generally
is needed to pressurize the gas prior to injection. After combus-
tion, the exhaust products enter the turbine, which provides
shaft power to the compressor and a generator. The generator
typically produces high-frequency power that is converted to
Advertisement formerly in this space.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, April 2007. © Copyright 2007 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or
in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
April 2007
ASHRAE Journal
49
60 Hz ac power at the desired voltage. The turbine exhaust
(typically about 1,100°F [600°C]) then enters the recupera-
tor. The recuperator exhaust (typically about 500°F [260°C])
is then released to the atmosphere (in DG systems) or enters
one or more heat-recovery heat exchangers to provide heating
and/or cooling loads (in CHP systems). CHP systems using
absorption chillers require a reliable bypass damper to avoid
damaging the chiller with hot exhaust gases when little or no
cooling load exists.
Microturbines have been commercially available for almost
20 years. The dominant microturbine manufacturer was founded
in 1988 and claims more than 3,500 microturbine cumulative
sales.1 Given the market dominance of this manufacturer,
cumulative installations from all manufacturers probably do
not exceed 5,000 units. Typical efficiencies (including gas-
compressor parasitics) range from about 23% to 26% (higher
heating value [HHV]) (25% to 29% [lower heating value
{LHV}]) for generation capacities ranging from 28 to 100
kW, respectively.2
The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) launched its Advanced
Microturbine Program in 2000, including projects with five
manufacturers.3 The goals of the program were to achieve:
• Fuel-to-electric conversion efficiency of at least 36%
(HHV) (40% [LHV]);
• Less than 7 ppm NOx emissions (using natural gas);
• 11,000 hours of reliable operation between major over-
hauls;
• 45,000 hours of service life;
• System costs of less than $500/kW; and
• Fuel flexibility (including natural gas, diesel, ethanol,
landfill gas, and biofuels).
Key strategies to improve efficiencies used by developers
working with the DOE include:
• Increasing generation capacity to 200 kW to 300 kW while
maintaining tight tolerance control, reducing leakage-
related losses as a fraction of total output;
• Achieving higher turbine inlet temperatures by using
temperature-resistant materials, such as high-temperature
alloys and ceramics;
• Improving recuperator effectiveness and temperature re-
sistance; and
• In at least one case, using a Rankine bottoming cycle to
extract additional power from the microturbine exhaust.
The DOE program ended in 2006. Although project final
reports are not yet public, some performance information is avail-
able. For example, one developer reports achieving 30% (HHV)
(33% [LHV]) generation efficiency with a 200 kW (nominal)
simple-cycle microturbine and 35% (HHV) (38% [LHV]) when
Advertisement formerly in this space.
50
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2007
using two 200 kW microturbines and a water-cooled, organic
Rankine bottoming cycle (total capacity of 465 kW).4
Energy and Emissions Considerations
The efficiency of grid-supplied electricity (including
generation, transmission, and distribution losses) averaged
31.5% (HHV) in the U.S. for 2004.5 Commercially available
microturbines (see previous efficiency ranges) are less ef-
ficient than the grid and, therefore, do not save energy when
operating as power-only systems. Even when used in a CHP
system providing both cooling and heating to a large office
building, detailed simulations suggest that a 24% (HHV) (26%
[LHV]) microturbine can provide only marginal primary en-
ergy savings.6 In the same study, even the high thermal loads
associated with hospitals provided minimal primary energy
savings. Further complicating the efficiency story, microtur-
bine efficiencies drop when operating above the rating-point
ambient-air temperature of 59°F (15°C), resulting in lower
generation efficiencies during peak summer days. Clearly,
efficiency improvements, such as those pursued under the
DOE Advanced Microturbine Program, are needed before
microturbine-based CHP systems can reduce primary energy
consumption in most commercial building applications. Mi-
croturbines, however, may be able to lower energy costs by
reducing grid purchases during peak periods.
While the emissions characteristics of microturbines gener-
ally meet regulatory requirements, requirements are becoming
stricter in many areas. For example, a California Air Resources
Board (ARB) regulation sets strict emissions limits for DG
systems. Natural-gas-fired DG systems sold in California must
be certified to meet the following emissions standards (based
on electric output):
• NOx: 0.07 lb/MWh (32 g/MWh);
• CO: 0.10 lb/MWh (45 g/MWh); and
• Volatile Organic Compounds: 0.02 lb/MWh (9 g/MWh).6
The ARB regulation, however, allows a generous emis-
sions credit for CHP systems. ARB currently lists only one
microturbine product (a CHP system) that is certified for sale
in California.7
Cost Challenges
One source estimates that the installed costs of microturbines
(power only) range from about $2,600/kW for a 30 kW capacity
to about $1,800/kW for a 100 kW capacity.2 The same source
estimates the installed cost of a 100 kW internal-combustion-
engine system (power only) at about $1,000/kW—56% less

Robert Zogg, P.E.; John Bowman; and Kurt Roth, Ph.D.;
are associate principals with TIAX LLC, Cambridge, Mass.
James Brodrick, Ph.D., is a project manager with the Building
Technologies Program, U.S. Department of Energy, Washing-
ton, D.C.
than a microturbine of the same capacity. However, as noted
earlier, microturbines are currently produced in low volumes.
It is reasonable to expect that increased sales volumes could
appreciably lower costs.
Promising DG Applications
Despite the efficiency and cost challenges, potentially attractive
niche markets for microturbines include:
• California CHP systems, where
using microturbines (rather than
internal-combustion engines) may
facilitate meeting ARB emissions
regulations;
• Greenhouse CHP systems where the
microturbine is exhausted directly into
the greenhouse, providing inexpensive
and very efficient heating plus carbon
dioxide to enhance plant growth;
• Power generation from landfill gas,
where tolerance of low-energy-
content fuels is essential; and
• Some roof-mounted DG or CHP
systems where the compact, light-
weight, and low noise/vibration
characteristics of microturbines
may be essential to avoid expensive
structural reinforcement and vibra-
tion-isolation measures.

Matthew Younger is a principal at Stantec Con-
sulting in Seattle. The project’s design won a first
place 2007 ASHRAE Technology Award.
About the Author
Natural Ventilation
In Nursing Facility
T
 he Washington State Department of Veterans Affairs’ mis-
sion is, “Serving Those Who Served.” In keeping with this
mission, planning began in 1998 with the department and NBBJ
Architects for a new skilled nursing facility. The Veterans Affairs
existing campus located in Retsil, Wash., required an additional
259-bed facility to serve its assisted living needs. The project
goals included the following components:
• Provide the best possible living envi-
ronment for the residents;
• Create a state-of-the-art facility;
• Construct a non-institutional facility
that fosters aging in place;
• Balance a respect for privacy with the
need for socialization;
• Design outdoor spaces that enhance the
quality of life for residents and staff;
and
• Build a facility that attracts and
retains staff.
Once the entire design team was as-
sembled as part of the predesign phase,
all members participated in a charette
that focused on sustainability. The simple
concepts of connections from the indoors
to outdoors, access to fresh air, views,
daylighting, and passive/unobtrusive
systems would contribute to each of the
project goals. To summarize, sustainable
design was not the objective. However, it
enabled the design to achieve the goals.
One key goal that emerged was to
naturally ventilate as much of the proj-
By Matthew Younger, P.E., Associate Member ASHRAE

ect as possible. As part of the charette, the building form
evolved from the basic principles of natural ventilation, day-
lighting, access to fresh air, orientation of building blocks,
mitigating direct solar gain, and integration of program
requirements while creating a space that helps veterans feel
at home. The result was an articulated building layout that
successfully integrates these principles. The primary chal-
lenge was to maintain these strategies without any increase
in the project budget.
The 160,000 ft2 (14 870 m2) facility completed construction
in spring of 2005 and has since been awarded Leadership in
Energy and Environmental Design® (LEED) V2.0 Gold Cer-
tification by the U.S. Green Building Council.
With proximity to Sinclair Inlet and its sea breezes, this
natural resource was recognized as an opportunity to
benefit the facility’s residents. Twelve years of data
from the existing powerhouse records had been
collected, enabling the design firm to analyze mi-
croclimate conditions including local wind and am-
bient temperature tendencies. Based on this collected
data, a weather data file for the thermal modeling was
modified to custom fit the site. Accurate, site specific weather
information was essential in demonstrating the effectiveness
of the natural ventilation systems to both the owner and local
building officials.
Assisted by the site’s mild climate with consistent breezes
blowing from Sinclair Inlet near the Puget Sound, this project
has achieved an innovative exception to Washington Adminis-
trative Code (WAC) design comfort standards. Current WAC
mandates the use of a mechanical cooling system and does
not recognize engineered natural, passive cooling as a design
option. Working closely with the Washington State Depart-
ment of Health, acceptable alternative comfort parameters
were used to file a code exemption. By designing within the
temperature range requirements instead of following the letter
of the code, the consultant team negotiated a performance-
based requirement and fulfilled it using engineered natural
ventilation.
The comfort standard that the building met was defined within
the WAC, which states that “all resident spaces must meet 75°F
(24°C) within two and a half percent of the time.” Setting a
precedent for a state project, natural cooling was approved by
the department of health for this project. The chief financial
officer for the Washington Department of Veterans Affairs has
been an integral supporter of this project and its design. The
local department of health has also been supportive in enabling
the passive cooling system to be pursued.
The skilled nursing facility has a complementary mechani-
cal ventilation system working in parallel with the
natural ventilation process. Minimum ventilation heat-
ing only air handlers have high-efficiency filtration
capability to provide excellent indoor air quality,
as required by the state guidelines. However, the
mechanical air-handling systems provide only the
minimum outdoor air ventilation requirements needed
to compensate for natural ventilation when the windows are
closed. This reduced capacity in both natural ventilation and
minimum mechanical ventilation modes. Restroom exhaust
serving resident rooms are used for relief air in conjunction
with heat recovery for outside air preheat within the ventila-
tion air-handling units.
Large, independently operated windows facilitate the pas-
sive cooling system, providing a greater number of ventila-
tion air changes than would be achieved with a conventional
mechanically cooled system. The double-hung windows are
counterweighted and are easily operated by the occupants. By
simply lifting the lower window a small amount, the top window
drops in proportion to allow an effective means of providing
Photo Credit: Michelle Litvin
Investing in the structure of the
building has a number of positive
implications due to the decreased
mechanical system requirements.
Annual utility costs are reduced (by
an expected 45%), and the mechani-
cal system capital costs are less than
a conventional system. Replacement
costs and maintenance require-
ments are minimized as well.

Figure 1: Building section illustration showing major components of the air-side heating and cooling concepts.
single-sided natural ventilation. In general, the residents are ex-
pected to operate their living spaces in the same manner as any
homeowner. These windows were also selected to provide more
accessibility to daylight. The main building spine is oriented
from east to west, with shading devices in the form of overhangs
and fins to control solar heat gain from the south. The nursing
wings have occupant-controlled, motorized, exterior venetian
blinds to control solar gain on the north and south sides.
Cost transfer was a key to implementing many of these fea-
tures. Based on the need for thermal mass to enable passive
cooling strategies, a detailed analysis of a steel structure and
a precast concrete structure showed that a $400,000 premium
was required to obtain the necessary thermal mass within the
building structure. However, the cost transfer to offset this
premium was easily demonstrated by comparing the cost of
the $800,000 premium for a fully air-conditioned building to
a naturally cooled facility with ducted minimal ventilation.
The project used a general contractor/construction manage-
ment approach, enabling the design team to source accurate
construction cost data for determining the effectiveness of cost
transfer concepts.
At the department of health’s request, rough-ins for split
system air-conditioning units were added in common areas
throughout the building (to address a handful of marginally
acceptable spaces), however, these were not purchased or in-
stalled. Acceptable comfort conditions have been demonstrated
during the past two years using passive cooling techniques only,
and supplemental mechanical comfort cooling in the common
areas is not required.
Within resident rooms, high ceilings, concrete mass and
high and low operable windows combine to create a radiant
cooling effect in conjunction with natural cooling air currents
that are effective even on warmer, windless days. In the dining
and multipurpose rooms, the building is designed to promote
cross-ventilation. The concrete structure is used in these areas
as well. High and low windows facilitate natural convection
currents of warm air rising to flow out of the high windows with
cool fresh air entering at the height of the occupants.
Mechanical System Features
Minimum ventilation air-handling units. These AHUs
supply minimum ventilation air to each building wing. In heat-
ing mode, the rooftop units temper the ventilation air through
hydronic heating coils. In summer mode, these units continue
to provide 100% outside air (without added cooling energy)
when activated by CO2 sensors. For the remainder of the time,
the spaces operate in natural ventilation mode. Heat recovery
(captured from the residence restroom exhaust) is used to pre-
heat this ventilation air.
Steam heat exchanger. The campus had an existing district
steam heating system. The hydronic heating water from the heat
exchangers supplies hot water to all heating components.
Baseboard heaters. To allow for increased user control and
ultimately a higher degree of individual comfort, each occupied
space was designed with adjustable baseboard heaters. These
units are controlled in each space by the occupant and are de-
signed to compensate for skin heat loss as well as to provide
heating of ventilation air through operable windows.
This building section illustration (Figure 1) shows the major
components of the air-side heating and cooling concepts. High
and low level operable windows allow for increased air circu-
lation and passive cooling. To control solar heat gain, motor
controlled exterior blinds were installed.
Energy Efficiency
Energy conservation was achieved as a result of a whole
building, integrated, high-performance design. Key efficiency
factors included the deletion of air conditioning for cooling,
