Actual peak demand for the month of August. School in full session based on early August start date; high-efficiency lighting is installed at the Pepperell site (1.125 W/ft2) and this light-
ing efficiency was used for both designs; actual HVAC power consumption from trended Modbus data (part of the on-site instrumentation); conventional baseline system design as per
Figure 5 (does not include total energy recovery addition).
comparison highlights the reduction in energy consumption
associated with the advanced design. It averages only 34% of
the energy estimated for the conventional approach and 44%
of the energy required by the conventional system with total
recovery integration.
Most of this reduction in energy use comes from compres-
sor load during the cooling season, and electric heating load
during the heating season. The regeneration energy used by
the IADR is low; less than $0.90/hour at the peak cooling state
point. Although desiccant regeneration of the main classroom
IADR units is accomplished using waste heat from the onsite
natural gas driven engine generator (see sidebar DOE Pilot
System), this economic benefit is not reflected in the analyses
discussed in this article.
One of the most important contributions to this research
investigation is the level of system performance data provided
by the control/data acquisition system. Particularly useful is the
energy-use monitoring provided by a Modbus communications
network that reports the real-time electrical use of each indi-
vidual component of the HVAC system as summarized in Figure
7. This information should be of interest to facility managers
and design engineers because few facilities are instrumented,
trended and analyzed after construction to see how the HVAC
designs actually perform. For example, the executive director of
facilities for the Floyd County School District was anxious to
see the data resulting from this investigation so it could be used
to guide the direction of future HVAC designs. Figure 8 pro-
vides an example of the electrical consumption data as reported
by the DDC system integrated into each IADR system.
Peak Electrical Demand Reduction
To estimate the reduction in peak electrical demand offered by
the advanced system, the actual HVAC peak demand measured
at the Pepperell project is compared against that estimated for
the conventional system providing comparable performance. The
peak cooling season electrical demand of the advanced approach
is 49% of that estimated for the conventional approach.
Figure 9 shows the difference in peak electrical demand
between the two HVAC system options. It also shows how the
overall peak electrical demand is dominated by the HVAC system

when the conventional approach is used. This
clearly conveys the fact that any desire to re-
duce energy use within a school facility must
be accomplished by optimizing the HVAC
design, especially if comfortable conditions
are to be maintained and the building codes

Given the magnitude of peak energy re-
duction summarized by Figure 9, an attempt
was made to obtain actual electrical demand
data from a school designed and operated
as modeled for the conventional design. A
high school complex having a similar size
and located in Rome, Ga., but operated by a
different school district was identified. This
school used vapor compression for cooling,
a heat pump cycle for heating and electri-
cal reheat as needed during the heating
season operation. The school was designed
to deliver approximately one-third of the
outdoor air volume used for Pepperell High
School. As importantly, no active humidity
control was incorporated (i.e., no cooling
season reheat).

the actual cooling season conventional data

that the facility would have been eligible for approximately 43

shown in Figure 10 once adjusted to reflect the reduced outdoor
airflow and lack of active humidity control or cooling season
reheat energy use. The analysis also suggests that the advanced
system can provide a significant reduction (44% and 47% for
the cooling and heating seasons respectively) in peak HVAC
electrical demand while simultaneously providing Standard
62-2001 ventilation and active humidity control.
Indoor Environmental Advantages
Trended data collected over the first full cooling season, and
reflected by Figure 3 proved the advanced system capable of
maintaining the desired space dew point of 55°F (13°C) over
a wide range of outdoor air conditions (Figure 6). As reported
in an earlier ASHRAE Journal article,4 lower space humidity
produced comfort conditions at elevated space temperature
setpoints (i.e., 75°F [24°C]).4,5,15
Indoor Air Quality (TVOC and CO2 testing)
To benchmark the indoor air quality maintained within Pep-
perell High School, air quality samples were collected from
all three main classroom areas using adsorbent-filled retention
tubes and analyzed by Georgia Tech Research Institute. The
results of this total volatile organic compound (TVOC) testing
showed the air quality in the school to be excellent, averaging
less than 200 µg/m3, better than the best of those reported on in
Reference 4. Similar results were found for CO2 data collected
at the site coincident with the TVOC testing.
A natural gas-driven engine generator was installed at
Pepperell High School in Georgia to power four IADR
systems coupled with total recovery modules and oper-
ated as dedicated outdoor air units. This DOE-sponsored
pilot system combines an electrical efficiency of 33.4%
with a 37.2% thermal efficiency to provide a 70.5% over-
all system efficiency, far higher than the 45% efficiency
typical of a commercial distribution grid. Heat recovered
from the engine jacket is used to regenerate the active
desiccant wheels during the cooling season and for heating
the supply air during the winter months. As a result, peak
electrical demand at the site is reduced by more than 100
kW. Cooling and heating season energy costs for the four
IADR systems will be reduced by approximately 21% and
29% respectively based on an electric cost of $0.075/kWh
and gas costing $0.75/therm. Neither the cost nor energy
savings associated with the engine demonstration are re-
flected in this article.

Background Classroom Noise
Another important factor in maintaining
a desirable teaching environment is con-
trolling background noise in classroom
areas. Much industry controversy has
surrounded a new ANSI standard (ANSI/
ASA Standard S12.60-2002) requiring
background noise levels to be maintained
below 35 decibels. Many commonly
used HVAC designs will not allow the
background noise to approach this level.
Testing was conducted in 10 representative
Pepperell classrooms using a Brüel and
Kjær sound meter reporting sound power
levels as dBA. The average background
noise level within the unoccupied rooms
measured was 34.5 dBA. The maximum
level measured was 39.5 dBA and the
minimum was 29.7 dBA.
Maintenance Concerns
The advanced system introduces ad-
ditional components. Some of which are
unfamiliar to the maintenance staff, and
the system complexity is increased over
a more conventional system. However,
important advantages are provided that
may outweigh any initial concerns.
Advantages include the soft-start on all
fans and most compressors, the elimina-
tion of belts on the IADR fans, the elimina-
tion of most condensate in the main AHUs,
improved defrost cycles and full refrigera-
tion monitoring with built-in safeties.
The most important maintenance benefit
may be the comprehensive monitoring in-
tegrated into the control system. Automatic
monitoring of the system alerts the service
department when a system is not function-
ing correctly, is not achieving setpoint or
in need of service or adjustment. Project
history has shown that most problems can
be addressed remotely or automatically.
This remote monitoring is accessible to
the IADR equipment manufacturer. If the
service department has a question, the
problem can be quickly addressed with the
equipment supplier acting as a long-term
partner. This has allowed for effective
overall systems optimization during the
commissioning process and beyond.
Since the equipment can be positioned
outdoors, it is easily accessible, simplify-
ing any maintenance function. This also
resulted in architectural enhancements
including freeing up several mechanical
rooms for usable floor space.
Construction Cost Analysis
Few would argue that school facilities
should be built and operated in accor-
dance with ASHRAE recommendations
and the building codes. They should be
comfortable, free from humidity prob-
lems, microbial concerns and well ven-
tilated. An inefficient school will remain
so for the life of the structure, which is
usually in excess of 30 years. Energy
efficiency should be a top priority. The
question is can all of these criteria be
satisfied in a cost effective manner?
The mechanical construction cost for
the advanced HVAC design applied to
the Pepperell project totaled $11.64/ft2.
This project cost includes an unusually
high level of controls, programming and
instrumentation, customized remote
heat pump condensing units and other
premium components.
Figure 11 summarizes the installed
construction cost estimate for various
more conventional systems commonly
used for school facilities. This analysis
shows the advanced approach to be sur-
prisingly competitive with other design
schemes that are less efficient and do
not actively control humidity. Designers
will have to complete their own analyses
to determine actual costs for any given
project but the results of this analysis
provide solid support for considering the
advanced system for school facilities.
Smaller Schools or Zones
Smaller schools or zones, like the B
wing classroom areas of this Pepperell
site can be designed with the IADR sys-
tem operated to process all of the systems
airflow needs and operated as a VAV
system. In this way, the system integrates
the dedicated outdoor air system and the
main VAV system into a single system.
Figure 12 shows actual performance data
Advertisement formerly in this space.
46
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2007
from the B Wing IADR system during the
peak cooling mode.
This same total conditioning approach
was applied to serve the Pepperell band and
choral area, the auditorium, the gymnasium
and the cafeteria. This design option is
well suited to retrofitting existing school
facilities since existing packaged equip-
ment can be easily replaced using a custom
curb adapter. Retrofit IADR systems have
since been installed in this manner at two
additional Floyd County schools.
Conclusions and Recommendations
The advanced design approach de-
tailed in this article suggests an effective
roadmap for constructing and operating
comfortable, healthy, energy-efficient
and cost-effective schools that follow
current ASHRAE recommendations and
meet all code requirements.
Decoupling the latent load from the
main VAV system, applying a dedicated
outdoor air system with low dew-point
capabilities, using high-efficiency vapor
compression—heat pump systems and
advanced control algorithms showed
measurable improvements when com-
pared to a conventional baseline system.
Based upon analyses completed, the
required cooling capacity was reduced
by 44% (475 tons versus 850 tons [1670
kW versus 2990 kW]), cooling season
peak demand was cut by approximately
51% (671 kW vs. 1,372 kW) and an-
nual energy costs were reduced by an
estimated 43% ($0.73/ft2 vs. $1.28/ft2)
when compared to the baseline system
operated without total energy recovery
preconditioning.
The mechanical equipment and installa-
tion was found to be cost competitive with
more conventional designs that consume
more energy and are less capable of control-
ling space humidity. The operating cost of
the Pepperell High School is low compared
to more conventional systems. The 20-year
life-cycle analysis comparing the advanced
and baseline system was completed using
the ASHRAE-recommended procedure and
assumed a 2% annual increase in energy
cost from the current levels of $0.055/kWh
and $0.75/therm. The analysis projected
$2,647,000 in energy savings over 20 years
with the advanced approach and a net cash
present value of $1,054,000.
Less quantifiable benefits include
increased performance and reduced
absenteeism associated with improved
indoor air quality, more state funding
resulting from higher attendance and a
reduction in costs associated with poor
humidity control.

About the Authors
School HVAC
Retrocommissioning
For a University Library
C
alifornia State University, East Bay, in Hayward, Calf., par-
ticipated in the 2004 – 05 University of California/California
State University/Investor Owned Utility Energy Efficiency Partnership
Program1 through the implementation of monitoring based com-
missioning (MBCx) at the university’s library building. The 2004 – 05
MBCx program was a pilot retrocommissioning program targeted
towards the 25 UC and CSU campuses located in the service terri-
tory of California’s four investor-owned utilities. The goal was overall
energy savings of 9 million kWh and 500,000 therms per year and
peak demand reduction of 1 MW.
The MBCx program differs from tradi-
tional retrocommissioning in that it will
install sufficient equipment to ensure an
extensive and comprehensive built-in
measurement and verification capability
and become a “continuous commission-
ing” program, i.e., institutionalized at the
campuses for the foreseeable future.2
The primary objectives of this MBCx
project were to:
• Obtain cost-effective energy sav-
ings from implementing low-cost
measures and optimizing how the
building’s energy using systems are
operated;
• Identify previously recognized
inefficiencies in building and plant
system operations;
• Enhance building system perfor-
mance and building comfort;
• Measure and document energy
savings from resulting operational
improvements; and
By Rishabh Kasliwal, P.E., Associate Member; and Ed Spivey, P.E., Member ASHRAE

ASHRAE Journal
49
Rotating Firing Order Example
• Facilitate ongoing recommissioning of systems to ensure
persistence of savings.
This article examines the identification and rectification of
some of the mechanical system design and control flaws using
the approach of retrocommissioning at the 248,000 ft2 (23 040
m2) library. The building’s energy management system (EMS)
has more than 500 points and controls most of the mechanical
equipment including the building’s chillers, hot water boilers,
air-handling systems and some zone terminal units (most of
the zone terminal units use pneumatic control). This case study
examines the MBCx process applied to the library’s hot water
(HW) loop and condenser water (CW) loop.3
Hot Water Loop
The HW loop retrocommissioning work began by reviewing
the available documentation, e.g., mechanical plans and control
shop drawings, followed by a detailed system walkthrough to
generate a system schematic.
The library’s hot water plant consists of six heating hot water
boilers of 1.8 MMBtu/h input each and a nominal efficiency
of 84%. The design flow for each boiler is 75 gpm (1189 L/s),
and the design temperature difference is 40°F (22°C). Each
boiler has four stages and is served by a small dedicated 0.5
hp (0.37 kW) pump. The secondary HW loop is served by two
constant flow centrifugal pumps (15 hp or 11 kW each) that
operate in a lead/standby fashion. The six boilers are piped in
a series/parallel arrangement (Figure 1) such that the output
from the first boiler, after blending with the system hot water
return, becomes the entering water to the downstream boiler
(see sidebar Rotating Firing Order Example).
The HW loop serves 16 heating coils spread over two build-
ings, the library building and the adjacent 11-story administra-
tive building. Each HW coil also possesses a dedicated coil
booster pump typically sized at 0.33 hp (0.25 kW).
The spaces served by these air handlers are used for vari-
ous purposes such as reading and study rooms, book stacks,
reception and checkout areas, a filming studio, book storage
and warehousing, the university police station (a 24 by 7 zone)
and office spaces.
Background
The suboptimal performance of the HW loop system was
one of the primary factors for nominating the Library building
as a candidate for the pilot monitoring based commissioning
program. Many of the remote zones, especially those served by
air handlers whose heating coils are farthest from the boilers in
the HW loop, would experience depressed zone temperatures
during the heating season resulting in an excessive number of
cold calls from the occupants.
Interviews with the building engineer and the campus controls
specialist revealed that the boilers fired to maintain a system
HWST setpoint of 180°F (82°C) leaving the HW plant. The
EMS enabled the individual boilers to maintain the system HW
supply temperature. Each boiler operated on its own internal
staging controls. The HWST setpoint was reset linearly between
a maximum of 180°F (82°C) to a minimum of 130°F (54°C) on
an increase in OAT from 40°F (4°C) to 70°F (21°C). The loop
was never allowed to run colder than 130°F (54°C).
Each boiler had four stages of control set in 5°F (2.8°C)
increments (Table 1). For example, the setpoints for Boiler 1
were 200°F, 195°F, 190°F and 185°F (93°C, 90°C, 88°C and
85°C). The first stage was enabled whenever the leaving water
temperature at Boiler 1 was less than 200°F (93°C), the second
stage was enabled when the leaving water temperature was less
than 195°F (90°C), third stage was enabled when the leaving
water temperature was less than 190°F (88°C) and the fourth
stage enabled at a leaving water temperature of less than 185°F
(85°C). The individual stages staged off when the leaving water
temperature exceeded its respective setpoint.
The six boilers operated on a fixed firing order, based on the
physical location of each boiler’s connection to the loop. The order
is 1, 6, 4, 3, 5 and 2 (Figure 1). The facilities staff had to resort to
a fixed firing order, even though they were aware that the boilers
would not share the load equally, as historically, implementing a
If the energy management system decided to fire the boil-
ers in the following sequence to meet the load 1, 2, 3, 4,
5 and 6 (see pre RCx staging setpoints in Table 1) and with
the entering water condition at Boiler 1 (75 gpm at 135°F
[4.7 L/s at 57°C]), the same as those in Table 1, all four
stages will fire and raise the leaving water temperature to
175°F (79°C). This leaving water will mix with the water
in the loop (at 135°F [57°C]) and result in 142°F (61°C)
entering water temperature for the subsequent boilers .
Boiler 2, which is next per the desired sequence, will receive
142°F (61°C) entering water and as a result, two of its stages
will fire to increase the leaving water temperature to 162°F
(72°C) (20°F [11°C] ΔT). This would mix with the loop such
that the loop leaving water temperature is 145°F (63°C).
Boiler 3, which is next in the sequence, actually is piped
prior to Boiler 2, and as a result it will receive 142°F (61°C)
entering water (from mixing of loop water and water from
Boiler 1). With the programmed setpoints of Boiler 3, three
stages will fire raising the leaving water temperature from
Boiler 3 to 172°F (77°C) (30°F [17°C] ΔT). Thus, the enter-
ing water temperature to the next boiler in the loop will be
147°F (64°C).
Returning to Boiler 2, which will now receive 147°F (64°C)
water, will result in firing only Stage 1 (down from two stages)
resulting in the second stage cycling off.
This example illustrates that the short cycling problem with
only three boilers firing. As more boilers will come online to
try and meet the loop setpoint, it will result in accentuating
this problem and short cycling of more stages.

rotating firing order resulted in short cycling of the boilers.
The boilers as well as the AHU heating coils with three-way
valves were designed to operate at a 40°F ΔT (160°F to 200°F)
but were found not operating at a HWST of greater than 163°F
(73°C) due to the existing controls setpoints and piping con-
figuration. Due to the poor selection of an existing boiler piping
arrangement, we settled on a less than original building’s design
HWST such that the last boiler in sequence was required to
produce 210°F (99°C) leaving water temperature, which for this
system was the limit of safe and stable operation as the boiler
manufacturer’s high limit cutout is 230°F (110°C).
Retrocommissioning Diagnostics
Three phases of RCx diagnostic tests were performed, namely
prefunctional testing, functional testing, and post-implementa-
tion verification.
A system inspection as well as safety and operation check on
the boilers was conducted during prefunctional testing. Three
boiler stages were noted to have failed and had been disabled
and were under repair.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
May 2007
ASHRAE Journal
53
The analysis of the system included a mass energy balance
to understand the full-load operating temperature conditions
of the HW plant. Using the programmed setpoints for each
of the stages and assuming that the boilers were able to meet
their design ΔT of 40°F (22°C) (i.e., a heat output of 1,512,000
Btu/h [440 000 W) the entering and leaving water temperature
at each boiler in the loop was estimated. Each boiler has four
stages of capacity control with 10°F temperature rise per stage
at design conditions.
The current series/parallel piping arrangement resulted in
higher entering water temperatures at the boilers located down-
stream at the boiler plant. The elevated entering water tempera-
ture coupled with the depressed hot water supply temperature
(HWST) setpoints of the boilers farther down the loop prevents
certain stages of the downstream boilers from firing on a regular
basis, which was consistent with the staff’s observations.
For example, consider Boiler 1 in Table 1. The entering water
conditions are 75 gpm (1189 L/s) at 135°F (57°C). As the 135°F
(57°C) entering water condition is lower than all four staging
setpoints, all four stages will fire and raise the leaving water
temperature by 40°F (22°C) (the design ΔT) to 175°F (79°C).
The entering water temperature at the next boiler in the loop,
Boiler 6, would be 142°F (61°C) due to mixing of 75 gpm (1189
L/s) of 175°F (79°C) water from Boiler 1 with 375 gpm (5945
L/s) of 135°F (57°C) water from the system return header. Fol-
lowing this logic to Boiler 4, the entering water conditions are
75 gpm (1189 L/s) at 148°F (64°C). The first three stages of the
boiler will fire to raise the temperature by 30°F (16.5°C), as-
suming the 10°F/stage design ΔT. However, water exiting Stage
3 at 178°F (81°C) is warmer than the firing setpoint of stage 4
(175°F, 79°C). Thus, Stage 4 does not fire and the water exits
Boiler 4 at 178°F (81°C) to mix with the system return water
and enter the next boiler at 153°F (67°C) and so on.
With the programmed setpoints, shown in Table 1 the esti-
mated HWST would be 160°F (71°C), significantly lower than
the desired setpoint of 180°F (82°C). The previous analysis was
conducted assuming an average hot water return temperature
(HWRT) of 135°F (57°C). The resulting theoretical system ΔT
is closer to 25°F (14°C) instead of 40°F (22°C). The HWRT of
135°F (57°C) was consistent with field observations (Table 1).
Functional Diagnostic Testing
During the functional testing phase of the diagnostic testing,
trend data was collected for the HW loop for a period of two
weeks from Nov. 7 to 21, 2005. The trend interval was set at 10
minutes. A more frequent trend interval was not possible due to
control system memory and data transfer constraints (the only
means of data transfer was by 3.5 in. diskettes). Trending dur-
ing November was favorable as the system could be observed
during high heating demand conditions.
Our preliminary findings were confirmed during the functional
testing. Based on the programmed sequences of operation and
existing HWST setpoints, our analysis (Table 1) had predicted
that the boilers located downstream in the loop would not fire on
a regular basis. The trend analysis shows that the last two boilers

rarely ever fired (2% of system runtime) even though the system
was not able to meet its HWST setpoint. The observed HWST
was typically around 150°F (65°C) (Figures 2a and 2b).
Figure 2a shows the loading of the boilers. For half of the trend
period the system operates with either three or four boilers firing
even though the system is unable to meet its setpoint. Figure 2a
shows the boiler loading during daytime periods, classified as 7

was closer to 150°F (65°C) instead of the theoretically predicted
values of 25°F (14°C) and 160°F (71°C), respectively. The
observed loop ΔT was 45% of the predicted theoretical ΔT and

30% of design ΔT. This was likely a result of two causes (a)

Implementation
As re-piping the boiler plant to a classic parallel arrangement was
not a feasible option within the low cost approach of the MBCx
program, the RCx team concentrated on
control changes.
The mass energy balance analysis sug-
gested that by changing the individual
staging setpoints, the loop ΔT could be
increased. The individual setpoints for
each boiler stage were calculated to
maximize the loop ΔT by allowing all
boiler stages to fire under high load
conditions. The analysis suggested that
with the proposed setpoints the system
would be able to achieve an overall
HWST of about 173°F (78°C) with the
same HWRT of 135°F (57°C). The high
temperature safety limit for the boilers
dictates the upper limit on the individual
setpoints. The overall theoretical loop ΔT would increase from
25°F (14°C) to 38°F (21°C).
The previous changes included reducing the differential
between boiler stages from 5°F (2.7°C) to 4°F (2.2°C), an
acceptable change. The boiler staging differential acts as a
dead-band between the individual stages and lowering it further
could result in short cycling and improper firing of the stages.
The analysis showed that the recommended setpoints were
high enough to enable the entire system (all 24 stages) during
peak heating load conditions. The recommended changes were
implemented in January 2006 per Table 2.
Verification Testing
For verification testing the same control points were trended
for another two week interval, Feb. 14 to 28, 2006. Trending
in February was advantageous as the heating degree days
in February (336) are similar to that in November (300) for
Hayward, Calif.
The post-implementation verification testing clearly showed
(Figure 3) that the system was indeed be-
ing loaded more evenly. Five out of six
boilers were firing more than 50% of the
daytime hours and all six boilers were
firing 13% of the time.
Consequently, the HWST had in-
creased to an average of 170°F (77°C)
(Figure 4). The system ΔT had in-
creased to roughly 22°F (12°C). The
observed HWST and observed ΔT were
still lower than the predicted theoreti-
cal values due to less than rated boiler
performance and certain disabled
stages. The observed loop ΔT had
increased to 22°F (up from 12°F) and
was now 55% of design ΔT (up from
30%). The heating coils at the air handlers were receiving
warmer water. The low-cost approach of diagnostic testing
based RCx helped alleviate comfort issues and suboptimal
performance of the HW loop, which had been plaguing the
system since its conception in the mid-1980s.
The measurement and verification (M&V) methodology
used for this project is per the International Performance
Measurement and Verification Protocol (IPMVP) Calibrated
Simulation Option.4 Per the M&V, this RCx measure resulted
in an increased gas consumption of approximately 37,246
therms per year.

Figure 4: Pre- and post-
retrocommissioning HW loop
performance comparison.
Time Series (Normalized)
Condenser Water (CW) Loop
Similar to the HW loop, the retrocommissioning effort at the
library’s condenser water loop started by reviewing available docu-
mentation and generating a system schematic based on a detailed
walk-through. The preexisting schematic is shown in Figure 5.
The library’s 700 ton (2461 kW) chiller plant consists of two
Advertisement formerly in this space.
May 2007
ASHRAE Journal
59
nominal 350 ton (1230 kW) centrifugal chillers. The chilled
water loop is pumped in a primary-only, constant flow ar-
rangement and serves the 17 air handlers in the two buildings,
the library and the administration building. The chiller plant
is circa 1960s except for the chillers, which were replaced in
2001. The condenser water loop has two 4,875 MBH centrifu-
gal blow-through cooling towers. Each tower has two 20 hp
(15 kW) fan motors, which are equipped with variable speed
drives (VSDs). Three 25 hp (19 kW) condenser water pumps
are piped in parallel from a common header. Each chiller has
a dedicated condenser water pump with one pump acting in a
standby capacity for the other two.
Background
Historically, at chiller plant shutdown, the cooling towers
suffered from sump overflow and subsequent problems upon
starting the condenser water pumps. At startup the tower basin
volume combined with the makeup water at full flow are not
able to keep up with the flow of the condenser water pump and
as a result the sump runs dry. Essentially, the volume of the
CW system piping is much larger than the volume of the sump,
and the CW pump flow is much larger than the makeup line
capacity. The facilities staff was forced to operate the pumps
manually. As a result the staff had resorted to operating the
condenser loop pumps on a continual basis. This situation was
among the primary reasons to nominate the library building for
the utility MBCx program.
The original system design schematic (Figure 5) shows that
the 10 in. (0.25 m) condenser water return header to the cooling
towers is slightly inclined towards the cooling tower.
For some years the campus staff believed that the sump over-
flow was caused by the draining of water from the condenser wa-
ter return header piping at CW pump shutdown. Subsequently,
to avoid overflow and start-up problems, the campus installed
a gooseneck (whose top is higher than the highest point in the
condenser water return header) downstream to the cooling tower
inlet (Figure 6). However, this did not rectify the problem.
Retrocommissioning Diagnostics
The RCx team initially suspected that one or both of the check
valves downstream of the condenser water pumps (Figure 5)
was faulty, allowing water to leak back to the tower sump. As
a result, at plant shutdown, the water would flow back through
the valve and the pump, into the sump and overflow through
the sump. The staff believed that the check valves were not the
source of the problem. Evaluating the trend data on pump and
tower operation did not provide any insights into this problem.
The team then conducted a manual functional test by observing

60

ashrae.org

the system as it was cycled through
various operating modes. During
the test, the CW pumps were cycled
on and off to determine the path of
water back to the sump at CW pump
shutdown.
The team found that the real source
of the problem was neither the drain-
ing of water in the condenser water
return header piping nor a leak at the
check valve, but rather an improper
placement of a small ¾ in. (20 mm)
PVC chemical treatment line (Figure
5). At CW pump shutdown the water
would continue to flow from the
supply header to the sump via the
chemical treatment line and drain
the system after a prolonged system
shutdown.
Implementation
The chemical treatment line was
raised to a level above that of the con-
denser water header with the installa-
tion of an air vent at the highest point
(Figure 7). The air vent was required
to break the siphon effect which would
cause the water to continue flowing
through the chemical treatment line at
condenser water pump shutdown. This
modification successfully prevents the
CW system piping from draining at
CW pump shutdown.
The low cost RCx measure was
implemented shortly thereafter, solv-
ing the long standing problem and as a
result the University is saving approxi-
mately 123,525 kWh of electricity an-
nually by allowing the condenser water
pumps to be cycled with the chillers.
Conclusion
This case study shows how moni-
toring-based commissioning with its
three tiers of diagnostic testing (Pre-functional, Functional and
Verification) can be an extremely valuable tool in understanding
the root cause of problems occurring in building HVAC systems.
Commissioning in many cases, often leads to straightforward
and inexpensive solutions resulting in a better building with
higher occupant comfort and improved system operation while
meeting the program goals of capturing energy savings.
Acknowledgments
We would like to acknowledge the support of Dan Franke,
Operations Manager, Michael Tadevich, Engineering Manager
and the facilities staff at California State University, East Bay,
CA during the MBCx project.

Systems Manual. info@cogentenergy.com.

About the Author
University Laboratory System
T
he University of Michigan is one of the premier research institu-
tions in the United States. In May 1998, the university embarked
on a study of the rapidly developing fields of the Life Sciences by
forming the President’s Commission on the Life Sciences, which led
to the enhancement of collaboration and exchange of ideas across
a range of academic and medical disciplines. To achieve the desired
collaboration, the northeast corner of the university’s central campus
required a physical transformation. As a result, the Life Sciences
Institute was created to foster basic and translational research.
Ronald Henning, P.E., serves as the chief mechani-
cal engineer for SmithGroup’s Science & Technology
Studio in Detroit. Henning won an honorable men-
tion for the 2007 ASHRAE Technology Awards.
By Ronald W. Henning, P.E., Associate Member ASHRAE
The Palmer Drive Development is a
complex of buildings including the Life
Sciences Institute (LSI), the Undergradu-
ate Science Building, and Palmer Com-
mons. It also includes a 1,000 car parking
structure; a 100,000 ft2 (9290 m2) plaza
and walkway system; and the creation
of a pedestrian bridge that spans across
a major city thoroughfare. The Palmer
Drive Development creates a physical and
intellectual “bridge” across what used
to be a “chasm” of traditional academic
studies, medical research and the educa-
tion of future scientists.
The LSI building presented the chal-
lenge of creating the right work environ-
ment for the building using a mechanical
and electrical system that would be flex-
ible enough to accommodate revisions
and upgrades over the life of the building.
It was also important that the building
met the energy efficiency requirements
of the university.
Life Sciences Institute
The LSI is a 235,000 ft2 (21 831 m2)
biomedical research facility consisting of
six floors and a mechanical penthouse.
The building houses three major func-
‘House Air’ system improves vivarium air quality, reduces energy loads and is architecturally expressed at the Life Sciences Institute.
School HVAC
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, May 2007. ©Copyright 2007
American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is
presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed
electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
May 2007
ASHRAE Journal
23
tional groups; 43,400 ft2 (4030 m2) of vivarium space located
on the lowest level of the building, 135,000 ft2 (12 540 m2) of
laboratory and laboratory support spaces on the third through
sixth floors; and 25,000 ft2 (2320 m2) of office/interaction
spaces distributed across the second through sixth floors. Major
mechanical and electrical spaces are located on the second floor
and penthouse floor of the building.
Central Plant Heating and Cooling
The LSI building in the Palmer Drive Development receives
steam from an existing central power plant at two pressures, 40
psi and 9 psi (276 kPa and 62kPa). All condensate is metered
and returned to the central power plant.
Discussions with the university and the initial analysis of the
Palmer Drive Development established that a central chilled
water plant that could be expanded to include future buildings
would be more efficient and cost effective than individual
chillers at each building. The
design resulted in the chiller
plant being included as part of
the Palmer Commons Building
and included multiple electric
centrifugal chillers. The plant
has a maximum capacity of
6,000 tons (21 100 kW) and
delivers 42°F (5.6°C) chilled
water via a primary-second-
ary piping system and through
underground tunnels.
Design Considerations
The building ventilation sys-
tem is designed so that each of
the three major function groups
in the building can operate in-
dependently of each other for
maximum flexibility. There are two air-handling units (25,000
cfm [11 798 L/s] each) for the office and general building areas;
two air-handling units (135,000 cfm [63 706 L/s] each) for the
laboratory and laboratory support spaces; and four air-handling
units (25,000 cfm [11 798 L/s] each) to serve the vivarium
space. All the air-handling units are variable air volume type
with chilled water cooling coils, pumped hot water heating
coils, minimum 65% efficient filters and steam humidifiers to
maintain proper relative humidity levels within the space.
The laboratory/laboratory support and vivarium air-handling
units are 100% outside air design with glycol runaround energy
recovery coils in the air-handling units and in the respective
exhaust streams for these systems. A bypass around the exhaust
side energy recovery coils allows the exhaust air to bypass the
coils whenever the energy recovery system is not active, reduc-
ing the operating static pressure of the exhaust system.
The two air-handling units serving the laboratory/laboratory
support spaces each contain two supply fans and the supply
ductwork is cross connected at each of the floors. This cross
connection of the supply ductwork provides a common supply
duct from the two air-handling units and allows for redundancy
in the system with the four total supply fans.
One of the four air-handling units serving the vivarium is a
redundant unit and the system can operate at 100% capacity
with only three of the units in operation. However, an analysis
of the system energy use indicated that the normal control op-
eration for the system is for all four units to be in operation at
all times. This allows each unit to operate at a reduced capacity
which results in less total operating fan energy than if only three
units were operating at full capacity.
The design consideration for the laboratory/laboratory support
exhaust air systems needed to be flexible and include redundancy.
The resultant design allowed
for a four exhaust fan group for
each of the lab floors and for
the vivarium. Separate exhaust
risers for each floor extend up
to the penthouse, through the
energy recovery coils and then
out to the associate exhaust fan
group located on the roof. Each
system was designed to operate
on three of the four fans set at
maximum load with the fourth
fan in each group as a redun-
dant unit. This would allow the
systems to be maintained with
no loss in capacity or operation
on any of the lab floors.
The air-handling units
serving the office/interaction
spaces, are return air type units and use enthalpy control to
modulate the outside air dampers, in addition to CO2 (carbon
dioxide) monitoring. Using the CO2 monitoring allows units
to adjust the amount of outside air being introduced into the
spaces whenever CO2 rises above the prescribed limits.
All motors for the air-handling units, as well as the main
chilled water and heating hot water pumps, are operated through
variable speed drives.
Two separately pumped perimeter heat systems divide the
building into north and south zones to match the major build-
ing exposures. Each perimeter system water temperature is
controlled, based on the outside air temperature, via a high
limit override at each above-grade floor on the south zone. A
separately pumped hot water heating system provides reheat
to the VAV supply valves.
‘House air’ system minimizes exhaust and energy use.
24
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2007
Figure 1: Typical open holding animal room control system.
A heat recovery system is part of the design to reduce heating
loads in the winter and electric cooling loads in the summer
on the 100% outdoor air systems. A pumped glycol runaround
coil loop was determined to be the best option due to space
constraints because of the distance between airstreams and cross
contamination concerns with the laboratory exhaust. Annual
Advertisement formerly in this space.
May 2007
ASHRAE Journal
25
Advertisement formerly in this space.
26
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2007
savings are estimated to be $144,000 based on 2001 energy
rates with an average airflow of 200,000 cfm (94 380 L/s) for
the laboratory and 75,000 cfm (35 392
L/s) for the vivarium. The resulting simple
payback on the increased project cost was
just over six years.
Vivarium Space Design
LSI’s vivarium uses a house air system
that is believed to be the first operational
system of its kind in the U.S. University
criteria established the vivarium design
to be based on a ventilated cage rack sys-
tem. The house air design was compared
against the manufacturer’s system that
included individual supply and exhaust
fans placed on top of each cage rack to
ventilate the individual cages in the rack.
The supply fan would generally draw
room air as the ventilation air for the
animal cages and the exhaust fan would
either discharge back into the room or to an exhaust duct. The
house air system eliminated the fan noise in the rooms, has the
benefit of not requiring maintenance staff to enter the vivarium
space and was designed to improve the quality of space for the
staff and animals, while minimizing energy
consumption and maintenance expenses for
the systems with the highest operating cost
in the building.
The ventilated animal holding cages are
directly connected to the building’s HVAC
system, with laboratory airflow control
valves controlling the supply and exhaust
air. Each supply control valve includes
a reheat coil to control the discharge air
temperature into the animal cages within
the racks. An exhaust air valve is also con-
nected to the rack to ensure a slight negative
pressure at the cages.
This system also minimizes the quantity
of ventilation air required in the animal hold-
ing room, since a specific quantity of air is
delivered to and extracted from each rack,
rather than turning over large volumes of
room air as required with traditional design approaches. It also
allows the room temperature to be controlled by the technicians
Figure 2: Barrier rooms.
Iris Dampers
Inline Thermostat
Reheat Coil
Valve
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
28
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2007
working in the room since the tempera-
ture within the animal cages is controlled
independently.
A manifold of racks in a room on a
common supply and exhaust air valve
enables each project to be able to use con-
ventional air valves, which reduces cost,
while providing improved temperature
control and monitoring. This approach
reduces maintenance costs, and allows
for maintenance access from outside the
holding areas, while maximizing space
use within the rooms.
Another positive feature of the
scheme is that it eliminates the need
for two bio-pack fans on top of each
rack—one for supply and one for the
exhaust from the cages. Without the
fans, the racks are lighter and easier to
move, reducing the potential for work-
related injuries. An additional benefit is
that both room noise and noise in the
racks are reduced. The elimination of
any fan noise within the animal holding
rooms improves the environment for
the animals.
The system also provides better iso-
lation between the room and the air in
the animal cages, resulting in a cleaner,
more odor-free environment, making
the vivarium staff much happier with
their working conditions. It is clear that
the animals also like this innovation.
Based on over one year of operation,
the reproductive rate for the animals in
this vivarium is considerably higher than
other vivariums on campus.
Laboratory/Laboratory Support
Space Design
All laboratory and laboratory support
spaces are provided with tracking VAV
supply and exhaust air valves to maintain
a proper negative pressure relationship
with adjoining office and corridor areas.
Fume hoods for the laboratory spaces
are located in alcoves adjacent to the
open laboratory spaces. This creates a
localized area for control of the fume
hoods that is separate from the open
laboratory spaces. The fume hoods were
designed as VAV-type with sash position
sensors to control the exhaust airflow
through the hoods.
Operations and Maintenance
All major pieces of equipment such
as the lab exhaust fans, air-handling unit
supply fans and pumps have some level of
redundancy to allow maintenance to oc-
cur without having to shut down services
to the facility. The equipment rooms were
designed with appropriate space to allow
the proper maintenance of all equipment.
All equipment is monitored by the build-
ing management system for runtime
status to ensure proper maintenance can
be maintained. The static pressure drop
across all filters is monitored to ensure
proper service and replacement of filters
is maintained.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
May 2007
ASHRAE Journal
29
Environmental Impact
The LSI was evaluated against Lead-
ership in Energy and Environmental
Design® (LEED) 1.0 criteria during
the design process to see how well the
university’s design guidelines for labo-
ratories fit the goals of sustainability.
The university required that satisfaction
of any LEED criteria would need to be
accomplished within the project budget
and schedule. If pursued, LSI would
have come within a few points of certi-
fication, even though LEED 1.0 was not
designed for a complex research labora-
tory. This is a significant accomplish-
ment for a laboratory building designed
to maximize the research space within
the facility. The following sustainable
design concepts were included as part
of this effort.
• A heat recovery system is part of
the design, significantly reducing
the amount of heat loss. This HVAC
system also meets laboratory safety
criteria of passing through the building
only once.
• Sustainable growth wood is used for
much of the project’s woodwork, in-
cluding the extensive wainscoting in
virtually all of its public areas.
• The project is located within a reha-
bilitated “brown field” site, creating a
well-used complex on what was once
an underdeveloped impervious surface
parking area.
• The facility includes 20 major exhaust
fans on the roof, thereby not increasing
the perceptible noise to the 4,000 oc-
cupants of the residence halls located
within 500 ft (152 m).
Conclusions
The university and its stakeholders in-
vested in the creation of LSI, the state-of-
the-art research facility, in consideration
of the emerging field of life sciences.
To entice potential scientists to join the
fledgling organization, the institute need-
ed to present an image of permanence
and longevity. In addition, the design of
the laboratory spaces was unique to other
campus facilities, making them more at-
tractive to potential supporters.
In addition, the university is currently
tracking nine technologies that may lead
to patents and/or licensing agreements
with the pharmaceutical industry. The
technologies range from new research
techniques, to cell, gene and protein
interaction, to possible therapies. After
only two years in operation, the results
are impressive. Overall, the investment
of $96 million in the Life Sciences Insti-
tute is paying dividends for the students,
faculty and staff of the university, result-
ing in more jobs for the region and an
economic shot in the arm for the State
of Michigan. The university believes it
made a very cost effective forward mov-
ing decision with their research into the
life sciences and with its decision to build
this facility.

The Perfect Wall
By Joseph W. Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., FASHRAE
T
he perfect wall is an environmental separator—it must
keep the outside out and the inside in. To do this the wall
assembly must control rain, air, vapor and heat. In the old
days, we had one material to do this: rocks. We would pile up
a bunch of rocks and have them do it all. But over time, rocks
lost their appeal. They were heavy and often fell down. Heavy
means expensive, and falling down is annoying. So, construc-
tion evolved. Today, walls need four principle control layers,
especially if we don’t build out of rocks. They are presented in
order of importance:
• A rain control layer;
• An air control layer;
• A vapor control layer;
and
• A thermal control layer.
A point to this order of
importance, if you can’t keep
the rain out, don’t waste your
time on the air. If you can’t
keep the air out, don’t waste
your time on the vapor, etc.
To protect the structure,
the best place for the con-
trol layers is on the outside
of the structure (Figure 1). When we built out of rocks, the
rocks didn’t need much protection. When we build out of steel
and wood, we need to protect the steel and wood. And, since
most of the bad stuff comes from outside, the best place to
control the bad stuff is on the outside of the structure before
it gets to the structure.
Also, after generations of building out of rocks, folks some-
how got the idea that they wanted to be comfortable and figured
out that rocks were not the best insulation. I mean rocks are
not that bad compared to windows (note to architects: you
can’t build an energy-efficient green building out of glass, but
you can get design awards, and we all know which is more
important). Back to rocks, they are heavy and you need many
to make the wall have any decent thermal resistance, so we
invented thermal insulation.
But, where to put the insulation? If we put the insulation
on the inside of the structure, the insulation does not protect
the structure from heat and cold. Expansion, contraction, cor-
rosion, decay, ultraviolet radiation, and almost all bad things
are functions of temperature. So, all the control layers need
to be on the outside. Keep the structure from going through
temperature extremes and protect it from water in its various
forms and ultraviolet radiation, and life is good.
What about air control? Well, air can carry a lot of water, and
water is bad for the structure. So, we must keep air out of the
structure because of the air-water problem. Or, if we let it get
into the structure, we must make sure it does not get cold enough
to drop its water. Now, one other item, and this is important; if
you intend on living in the building or working in the building
or keeping things safe in the building, we might want to control
the interior environment.
We especially ought to be
concerned about what is in
the interior air because when
we are in the interior we tend
to breathe it. Well, it turns
out that we can’t control air
until we enclose it. So, we
need an airtight enclosure to
provide conditioning such as
filtration and air change, and
temperature and humidity
control. And , once again,
the best place to control air
is on the outside of the struc-
ture—but under the insulation layer, so the air does not change
temperature. Presto! Now we have the perfect wall—a water
control layer, air control layer and vapor control layer placed
directly on the structure and a thermal control layer over the top
of the other control layers (Figure 1 ).
This was figured out long before I was born—I think the
Canadians figured it out first,1 but the Norwegians have some
claims to this, as well as the Russians. I am going to go with
Canadians on this, because as a Canadian I am biased and proud
of it. For a more detailed discussion of the physics of all of this
go to the old masters: Hutcheon and Handegord2 and the new
kids on the block, Burnett and Straube.3
In a beautiful bit of elegance and symmetry, if you lay down
the perfect wall you get the perfect roof (Figure 2) and then
when you flip it the other way, you get the perfect slab (Figure 3).
The physics of walls, roofs and slabs are pretty much the same.
This insight was given to a whole generation of practitioners
by Max Baker4 when I was first getting started.
Notice in the perfect roof assembly the critical control layer
or membrane for rainwater control and air control, and vapor
control is located under the thermal insulation layer and the
Cladding
Control Layers
Structure
Figure 1: The Perfect Wall.
In concept, the perfect wall
has the rainwater control
layer, the air control layer,
the vapor control layer and
the thermal control layer on
the exterior of the structure.
The cladding’s function
is principally to act as an
ultraviolet screen.

Control Layers
Stones
Earth
Figure 3: The Perfect Slab. The perfect slab has a stone layer that
separates it from the earth and acts both as a capillary break and a
groundwater control layer. This stone layer should be drained and
vented to the atmosphere just as you would drain and vent a wall
cladding. (Note to the Environmental Protection Agency: you are
doing well by venting this layer; don’t fold on this one.)
Ballast
Filter Fabric
Control Layers
Roof Structure
Figure 2: The Perfect Roof. The perfect roof is sometimes
referred to as an “inverted roof,” since the rainwater control
layer is under the insulation and ballast (i.e., roof cladding).
Personally, I don’t view it as inverted. Those other folks got
it wrong by locating the membrane exposed on top of the
insulation.
stone ballast (i.e., “roof cladding”), so that it is protected from
the principle damage functions of water, heat and ultraviolet
radiation. Why we put the most critical control layers on roofs
on the very, very top where they can be trashed by these damage
functions never fails to amaze me. Yes, I know, they are easier
to replace when they are located there, which is the standard an-
swer for our disposable, unlimited-resource-available society.
Most problems in building enclosures occur where roofs
meet walls. The classic roof-wall intersection is presented
in Figure 4 (with both credit and apologies to Max Baker—I
took some liberties). Notice that the control layer for rain on
the roof is connected to the control layer for rain on the wall,
the control layer for air on the roof is connected to the control
layer for air on the wall, and so it goes. Beautiful. And, when
it is not, it’s ugly.
Time to put some meat on the bones of Figure 1. How should
this perfect “conceptual” wall actually be built? Three ways.
The best can be found in Figure 5a. This is a special wall. It
is expensive, but it is often worth it. It is the type of wall that
you save for special buildings such as those that are passed
down from one generation to the next: museums, art galleries,
courthouses, and libraries. These are institutional buildings,
and I call this wall the “institutional wall.”
Perhaps once or twice in an entire career you may be fortu-
nate enough to use it. It is sweet in that it can be constructed
in any climate zone. The only thing that is changed because
of climate is the level of thermal insulation. My advice here
is simple: whatever you think the right amount of thermal
insulation should be, double it and shut up. If you love your
kids, don’t argue with me.
The second wall should be the “meat and potatoes” wall for
commercial buildings. It’s the wall every commercial building
should use, and the base wall that our infrastructure should
depend on. So, it’s no surprise that I call it, yes, you guessed
it: the “commercial wall.” It has a conductive structure—metal
studs. All of the insulation should—and must—be located on
the outside. It is a thermodynamic obscenity to insulate within
a conductive structural frame. Again, you can build it anywhere
in any climate location. Just consider the insulation levels (see
previous, particularly the part about loving your kids).
The third wall is the “residential wall.” Notice the structural
cavity is insulated. That is because we are using a relatively
nonconductive structural frame. The structure is wood and
wood-material based. Wood is not particularly conductive,
which is why we do not have wood frying pans, but we do
have steel ones.
For this third wall to work almost everywhere (except Alaska,
where we would not insulate even within a relatively non-con-
ductive wood structural frame), we would split the thermal
resistance of the insulation on the exterior of the structural frame
with this insulation within the structural frame at least 50:50. So
in an R-20 wall—at least R-10 or more is on the outside of the
nonconductive structural frame. And, no vapor barrier is used
on the inside of the assembly. Repeat after me, no vapor barrier
is used on the inside of the assembly. We want the assembly to
dry inwards from the control layers—and to dry outwards from
the control layers. Always. Everywhere.
So where are we today? We have learned that we need to have
a drainage gap over a continuous drainage plane, a structurally
sound and continuous air barrier, a thermal layer (insulation)
Roof Control
Layers
Figure 4: The Roof-Wall Connection. Notice that the control
layer for rain on the roof is connected to the control layer for
rain on the wall, the control layer for air on the roof is connected
to the control layer for air on the wall, and so it goes. Thank
you, Max Baker.

how to construct. It works everywhere in all climate zones.

erywhere—except in extreme cold climates where we would not
insulate within the wood structural frame, and then it works
there, too.

with no major conductive penetrations, and a vapor control
layer located to allow drying in both directions if wetting events
happen. All of this is outside of the structure. That is the perfect
wall. We need to use it now more than ever. We have energy
security to deal with and a planet to worry about. So let’s get
with the program and be perfect.

Europe’s Hazardous Substances Restrictions and HVAC
Design for Environment
Jennifer Shepherd is a principal at Canyon Snow
Consulting in Los Gatos, Calif.
About the Author
T
he European Union’s Restriction of Hazardous Substances
(RoHS) Directive bans placing on the EU market new electrical
and electronic equipment containing more than agreed levels of
certain restricted substances. Although the directive focuses on
electronic equipment (e.g., computers, servers, and cell phones),
it is important to consider how RoHS may affect HVAC equipment
and building operations.
By Jennifer Shepherd
The RoHS is part of a group of legisla-
tion passed in recent years that has had
a signiﬁ cant impact on the electronics
industry. EU legislators have focused
on removing hazardous substances from
electronic equipment and creating systems
for recycling discarded equipment.
The RoHS is easily the most important
piece of legislation affecting electronic
products today, especially as it relates to
product engineering and materials sourc-
ing. The law does not allow a producer
to sell certain products after July 1, 2006
that contain:
• Lead and its compounds;
• Mercury and its compounds;
• Hexavalent chromium and its com-
pounds;
• Cadmium and its compounds;
• Polybrominated biphenyls (PBBs);
and
• Polybrominated diphenyl ethers
(PBDEs).
Violators are subject to fines and
restriction from market access until the
violation can be corrected.
Impact on Supply Chain
Electronics producers have been furi-
ously “scrubbing” their bills of materials
for the last few years in an effort to learn
whether or not the components they buy
contain any restricted substances. Under
the law, the presence of a restricted sub-
stance in only a single component makes
it illegal to sell the ﬁ nished product in
Europe. Considering the complexity and
scale of the supply chain, this has caused
some serious difﬁ culties.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, June 2007.
©Copyright 2007 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational
purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed
electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
June 2007
ASHRAE Journal
61
Producers and suppliers had to set common expectations
around RoHS compliance. Since some companies were given
an exemption for lead in solder, they only needed to be con-
cerned about the remaining ﬁ ve substances, and so created a
specialized query for suppliers. Other companies anticipated
that additional hazardous chemicals eventually would be subject
to restriction, and so created queries that covered far more than
the “RoHS six.”
At the same time, producers were dealing with suppliers who
were uninformed or unwilling to provide the information. In
many cases, determining a reliable answer required costly labo-
ratory analysis. Adding to the pressure was that customers and
suppliers could not agree on which party should be responsible
for damages such as lost revenue and market share in the event
of a non-compliance.
Impact on HVAC Industry
The most important question for any company in examin-
ing RoHS requirements is: do these requirements apply to my
products, to my company, and to my supply chain? The answers
are not always straightforward for several reasons. First, the
applicable products covered by RoHS are listed as categories
in the Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE)
Directive. Ten categories are listed in WEEE (http://ec.europa.
eu/environment/waste/weee/index\_en.htm) and described brieﬂ y.
Each is accompanied by a non-exhaustive list of product ex-
amples considered a part of that category. Categories 8 and 9
are excluded from RoHS at present. In addition to the category
exclusions, many technical exemptions exist, which typically
apply to speciﬁ c applications of one or more RoHS substances.
As with the category exclusions, these exemptions are not
designed to last forever.
Here are the categories worth a closer look for the HVAC
industry:
• Category 1: Large Household Appliances. This includes
large cooling appliances; large appliances used for cooking
and other processing of food; electric heating appliances;
large appliances for heating rooms; beds; seating furniture; air
conditioner appliances; and other fanning, exhaust ventilation
and conditioning equipment.
• Category 5: Lighting Equipment. This includes luminaries
for ﬂ uorescent lamps; straight and compact ﬂ uorescent lamps;
high intensity discharge lamps including pressure sodium
lamps and metal halide lamps; and low pressure sodium
lamps.
• Category 6: Electrical and Electronic Tools. This notably
excludes large-scale stationary industrial tools, but it includes
drills; equipment for processing wood, metal and other
materials; tools for riveting, nailing, screwing, welding or
soldering; tools for mowing or other gardening activities.
• Category 9: Monitoring and Control Instruments. It in-
cludes smoke detectors; heating regulators, thermostats, and
other monitoring and control instruments used in industrial
installations, such as control panels.
If your company produces equipment in any of these catego-
ries—even if not listed in the examples—you will be respon-
sible for RoHS and WEEE compliance. Category 9 is not yet
included in RoHS, but it is eligible for later inclusion. Also, if
your company buys equipment in any of these categories and
re-brands it, a similar responsibility exists. Finally, if you buy
and use these equipment types, you’ll need to determine RoHS
compliance from the producer.
While these impacts concern European purchases and instal-
lations of equipment, two important reasons exist for under-
standing RoHS outside of Europe:
1. For many multinational companies, RoHS compliance has
become the de facto standard for all equipment, and many
buyers expect RoHS compliance for any equipment they plan
to purchase; and
2. European environmental policies have been inﬂ uential in
setting laws and policies in other regions, including North
and South America, China, Korea, and Japan.
What Applies to HVAC Equipment
The most reasonable category for HVAC equipment is Cat-
egory 9 because heating regulators and thermostats are listed
as examples. As discussed earlier, Category 9 products are
excluded from RoHS. A consultancy hired by the EU Com-
mission to review the issue of inclusion of these products has
recommended that doing so should require testing and valida-
tion to be certain technical requirements are met. Its recom-
mendations place the probable inclusion of Category 9 products
no earlier than 2012.
Furthermore, equipment that is part of a ﬁ xed installation is
General trends from Europe are becoming widespread and are
affecting substances used to make products, methods that
products are handled at end-of-life, and amount of energy
products consume while in use.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
June 2007
ASHRAE Journal
63
excluded from RoHS. A more comprehensive deﬁ nition of a
ﬁ xed installation, provided by the European Commission, is
“a combination of several equipment systems, ﬁ nished prod-
ucts and/or components (hereinafter called ‘parts’) assembled
and/or erected by an assembler/installer at a given place to
operate together in an expected environment to perform a
speciﬁ c task, but not intended to be placed on the market as
a single functional or commercial unit.” To the extent that
this deﬁ nition describes the types of installations you install
and operate, this is further support of a position that RoHS
does not apply to HVAC equipment. Finally, cadmium-plated
electrical contacts in HVAC equipment are speciﬁ cally exempt
from RoHS if they are used to improve the HVAC equipment’s
reliability.
The importance of an exclusion is that the excluded equip-
ment is completely exempt from RoHS. For example, there is
no restriction on circuit boards with lead-based solders used
in HVAC equipment. Similarly, hexavalent chromium, which
is used as a pretreatment in paint processes, will remain unre-
stricted while HVAC equipment is excluded from RoHS.
So, with a Category 9 exclusion, a ﬁ xed installation exclu-
sion in Category 6, and a speciﬁ c exemption for cadmium
plating in electrical contacts, it seems the HVAC industry
is well-insulated from the rigors and requirements of RoHS
compliance. Any aspect of the ﬁ eld that does not ﬁ t into these
three cases should be examined more closely, and soon.
Next Steps
After considering the general purpose of RoHS and the types
of equipment considered to be in scope of the requirements,
you should assess and document how these rules affect your
organization. If no part of RoHS affects your products directly,
it can be useful to make an explanatory note to describe the
reasons for future reference and inquiries by others.
If you do ﬁ nd your organization has a responsibility for
compliance with RoHS, you should consider bringing a
cross-functional team together to educate your colleagues on
the requirements. As a group, you can best create a strategic
plan for compliance that takes into account your products,
operations, locations, and the requirements for each.
It is important to maintain working familiarity with emerg-
ing legislation that can affect your company’s products and
processes. General trends from Europe are becoming wide-
spread and are affecting substances used to make products,
methods that products are handled at end-of-life, and amount
of energy products consume while in use.

35
By John Ruiz
E
mployees need to be connected to customers and colleagues throughout the entire business
day. Whether they are commuting, at their desk, in a meeting, or conversing at the water cooler,
instant communications are expected. The tools to meet this need, cellular phones, pagers, PDAs,
laptops, and other devices have become an integral part of our professional and personal life. With
the growth of reliable cellular networks that provide coverage globally, individuals and organizations
have grown to rely on these wireless services and expect them to work wherever they are. Since
building systems have begun to converge, the same needs for reliability, ﬂ exibility, and mobility make
wireless connectivity twice as critical.
Unfortunately, wireless coverage within a building or cam-
pus of buildings can be unreliable due to building locations,
construction techniques, physical location within the building,
network loading, and external interference. Tightly spaced
buildings block external wireless networks while commercial
construction techniques and materials attenuate wireless fre-
quencies in the ranges used by cell phones and computers. The
HVAC ductwork, glass, steel, and concrete building materials
used in most large structures absorb energy sent from phones,
radios, and computers as they pass through. That strong “ﬁ ve-
bar” wireless signal you enjoy outside quickly fades when you
are indoors. It is even possible that too much cellular coverage
may cause interference. For example, in high-rise buildings
two or more outdoor cellular network towers may penetrate
the building. A wireless phone may continually hunt between
them for the best signal.
As wireless technologies become more reliable and cost
effective, more of a building will become wireless. Although
the technologies differ, many building control manufacturers
have a line of wireless products that provide HVAC controls,
lighting controls, and security. These systems sometimes
face an even more demanding environment than cell phones
because the products (and hence the wireless radios) can be
attached directly to wireless unfriendly materials, such as
metal ductwork.
Ironically, to make wireless communication networks
reliably function indoors, you need wires. Fortunately, these
wires can be hidden in the ceilings of the building to give
you an even distribution of wireless signals across the build-
ing without compromising the building’s aesthetics. Known
as in-building wireless distribution systems, these systems
distribute wireless signals throughout a building and can
connect to one or more outdoor cellular networks or enter-
prise information technology (IT) networks. Over the years,
in-building wireless distribution systems have evolved into
three distinct technologies based on their use of cabling media
and their ability to support multiple wireless services.
The three main competing technologies in the in-building
wireless connectivity market are distributed antenna systems
(DAS), Pico cells, and repeaters.
Each technology has different characteristics and capa-
bilities (e.g., reliability, cost, bandwidth) to meet the speciﬁ c
needs of the customer.
Distributed Antenna Systems
DAS are the premier solution for improving wireless cov-
erage within a building. They offer the greatest amount of
connectivity, the most extensibility beyond cellular, and the
highest reliability, but at the highest initial cost.
As shown in Figure 1, a DAS is composed of three main
components, a base station interfaces to the wireless services
that are being distribute across the building, a vertical dis-
tribution system that takes the communications signals and
transports them to the areas where coverage is being provided,
and a horizontal distribution system that covers the area with
the wireless signal.
The base station typically is located where the telecom-
munications services enter the building and connects to one
or more wireless cellular phone service providers, paging
systems, emergency radio systems, or specialized mobile
radio (SMR) systems. These communications signals are
About the Author
John Ruiz is a senior wireless program manager for the Building Efﬁ ciency
Group of Johnson Controls in Milwaukee.
36
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u n e 2 0 0 7
interfaced to a wireless provider’s external network
via a head-end unit. One or more head-end units
are connected to a DAS base unit where the com-
munications signals are either passed (in a passive
DAS system) or converted to optical or electrical
signals (in an active DAS system) and combined
for transport on the vertical distribution system to
the telecommunications closets on each ﬂ oor of
the building. Here the signals are transformed by
the DAS remote unit to their original RF form and
sent horizontally to broadband antennas located
on the ceiling of the covered area. The number and
type of antennas needed depends upon the type of
signals being sent and the application (e.g., cellular,
paging, emergency radio, 802.11 (WiFi), ZigBee®,
etc.). A wireless site survey is normally performed
to determine where antennas need to be placed to
ensure adequate coverage within the space.
Broadband antennas are distributed across the
covered space.The horizontal distribution system
also can be used to provide wireless coverage for
most in-building wireless systems. For example, the
IT department can use this distribution system to

enhances the connectivity of multiple types of in-building wireless systems.

38
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u n e 2 0 0 7
provide wireless local area network (LAN) services. The facili-
ties department can also use the system for a wireless HVAC
network. Although additional equipment may be needed to
support these applications, wireless coverage for these systems
can be improved dramatically.
The three types of DAS systems are active, passive, and hy-
brid. Active DAS systems use powered components to acquire,
amplify and distribute the wireless signals. A passive system
propagates wireless signals throughout a building without any
powered components. A hybrid DAS uses a mixture of both
active and passive components. Although active and passive
DAS’s perform the same function, the technical characteristics
are quite different; and depending upon customer needs, one
technology may ﬁ t better than the other. Table 1 compares the
characteristics of active vs. passive systems.
Pico Cellular Networks
Pico cellular networks, also known as Pico cells, are used to
distribute cellular networks within a building. The technology
mimics an outdoor cellular network but on a miniature scale.
A Pico cell network extends a cellular
carrier’s network into a building using
small distributed cellular radios. If you
need more space covered, add an ad-
ditional cellular radio in the space. It is
inherently different from a DAS, which
has a centralized cellular base station
radio and small distributed antennas
placed in the covered space.
As shown in Figure 2, a Pico cell is typi-
cally a mini base-station radio connected to
a carrier’s wired network. While it can be
run on its own private cabling system, the
mini base-stations are typically connected
to the building’s enterprise network to a
central point where an external connec-
Characteristic
Active
Passive
Vertical Distribution
Pair of ﬁ ber strands per service and area being
covered (to remote unit). Fiber provides low
signal loss.
Broadband coaxial riser cable. All services (current and
future) are transported on a single coaxial cable.
Filtering
Active ﬁ ltering reduces interference. Signal
strength can be boosted to reduce signal loss.
Passive ﬁ ltering reduces interference with slight
degradation to signal.
Signal Strength
Ampliﬁ cation can be added to boost signal
strength and tune building coverage.
No ampliﬁ cation is provided. Coverage can be increased
by adding new antenna segments.
Network Management
Network management can be provided to centrally
monitor for system failures.
Network management is not provided. Since the system
is not powered, system failures are uncommon.
Horizontal Distribution
Uses non-powered broadband antennas to deliver
signals.
Uses a radiating (or leaky) coaxial cable and non-
powered antennas to deliver signals. This system offers
highly contained coverage but is more difﬁ cult to install.
Distance Scalability
Ampliﬁ cation of the signal can be used to expand
the size of the system.
In a pure passive system, the signal degrades as it gets
farther from the source.
Occupied Space
Scalability
Additional antennas can be added or moved
easily.
Once installed, the system is difﬁ cult to reconﬁ gure.
Application Scalability
New applications can be added incrementally by
adding new active components. New applications
that require higher signal strength than originally
planned can be added incrementally.
System has wide bandwidth so new applications can
be added with minimal expense. New applications that
require higher signal strength than originally planned are
difﬁ cult to accommodate.
System Reliability
Active components can fail over time.
Highly reliable since components are passive.
Total Cost of Ownership
Higher total cost of ownership due to power re-
quirements and possible additional environmental
needs (e.g., cooling, redundant power).
No power requirements for the DAS; no cooling required,
small space needed, and lower monthly operating cost.
Table 1: Comparison of the characteristics of active versus passive systems.
