in Alameda, Calif.
About the Author
By Reinhard Seidl, Member ASHRAE
Universal Translator
L
ast year, I wrote an article1 published in ASHRAE Journal
illustrating problems commonly encountered when attempting
to gauge the performance or energy efﬁ ciency of buildings using
energy management and controls systems (EMCS).
One of the main problems is the need to process large amounts of
data from the EMCS. The Universal Translator (UT) is a tool that allows
ﬁ ltering, analysis and visualization. It is produced by representatives
of the Paciﬁ c Gas and Electric Company (PG&E), under the umbrella
of the energy savings and incentives program2 that is sponsored by
the California Division of Ratepayer Advocates.3
Using the UT simpliﬁ es commission-
ing of facilities and veriﬁ cation of energy
savings, and is a useful addition to the
services that PG&E provides under its
energy rebate program.
This free tool can be downloaded at
www.utonline.org. The UT has been
available for several years, but has under-
gone some drastic changes and improve-
ments in the last six months, making it
essentially a new product.
The most recent version of the UT
(version 2.2.25) is still going through a
beta-test period. The program sometimes
crashes, but rarely loses any data as a re-
sult. An automated user feedback forum
lets users upload their comments and fea-
ture requests through the UT Web site.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, July 2007. ©Copyright 2007 American Society
of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes
only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission
of ASHRAE.
July 2007
ASHRAE Journal
13
In addition, a series of video tutorials is being developed
for release in fall 2007, which will make it easier to use the
UT. Step-by-step slides developed for a recent public training
session can be downloaded free of charge from the author’s
ftp site.4
This article introduces the current functionality of the UT,
and highlights some of the future goals of the ongoing devel-
opment process.
Functionality
The essential functions of the UT are:
Read large amounts of data from EMCS databases;
Group trends in a nested hierarchy to mirror the logical
structure of the building systems;
Transform trend data into a database with uniform time-
stamps so mathematical calculations and comparisons can
be performed between trends or families of trends;
Graphically show trend data in time series or scatter plots;
and
Perform automatic analysis to evaluate economizer perfor-
mance and ability of all equipment to maintain setpoints
over time.
Future functionality, currently under development is as fol-
lows:
Read exported data from all major EMCS platforms, data
loggers, electrical meters, etc., in formats such as text ﬁ les,
CSV ﬁ les, spreadsheets, etc.;
Read data from all major EMCS platforms in native for-
mat, i.e., directly from Microsoft® Access or SQL format
databases; and
Read data from “live” systems using SOAP calls.5
The main advantage of the UT is that a facilities engineer,
commissioning agent or owner only needs to be familiar with
one tool, which is available for free. Once he or she is comfort-
able with the functions of that single application, any building
can be examined. This examination includes both a “helicopter
view” of the all of the building’s components, and a detailed
view of individual equipment performance.
In this way, it will become possible and much easier to test dif-
ferent control strategies, and to trace errors and malfunctions.
Even though the graphical abilities of many EMCS platforms
have improved over the last few years, many legacy systems
have poor or no support for visualizing data. In addition, even
modern systems rarely allow mathematical operations between
data and the analysis of data, unless speciﬁ cally programmed
into the control system of a project. Such programming must
be speciﬁ ed clearly before implementation and usually requires
additional discussions between the owner or operator and the
programmer.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
Tools are becoming available that allow automated interpreta-
tion of building trend data. However, these tools generally are
not part of the EMCS, but are separate programs that must be
purchased.6 Some EMCS vendors offer additional packages
to perform high-level energy analysis when integrated with
their control system. Some analysis tools can be downloaded
for free, but are limited to predeﬁ ned analysis for particular
applications.7
Executive level results can help evaluate decisions such as, “If
I raise chilled water temperature by 3°F (1.7°C) on cold days,
how much money do I save,” or “If I change the staging points
of my chillers so that I operate the plant on a single chiller more
hours per year, how much money do I save?”
A simple example would be kW/ton data for a chiller plant—
to evaluate how efﬁ ciently a chiller plant operates: Add the kW
from each major piece of equipment: chillers, pumps and cool-
ing tower fans. Then, divide this number by the total cooling
capacity produced, typically found by multiplying the chiller
water ﬂ ow rate by the chilled water temperature differential:
Q =
Equation 1: Central plant cooling tonnage calculation
where
Q = Cooling capacity, tons
Φ = Flow, gpm
CHWS = Chilled water supply temperature, °F
CHWR = Chilled water return temperature, °F
So that the overall plant efﬁ ciency is:
ε = 
Pi
Q
Equation 2: Central plant efﬁ ciency calculation
where
ε = Central plant efﬁ ciency, kW/ton
Pi = Power consumption per component, kW
Q = Cooling capacity, see Equation 1
If this formula is programmed into the EMCS as part of an
energy page, then it is easy to display the resulting kW/ton
values on the screen. However, altering the logic, or using a
different metric to look at data, would require a programmer.
The UT has a ﬂ exible calculated channel interface that lets
the user create mathematical formulas with a new trend channel
as output, shown in Figure 1.
Accuracy of Analysis Results
As a general precaution, the UT does not verify the accuracy
of source data. Therefore, the results of any analysis will depend
φ × [CHWR – CHWS] × 500
12,000
(1)
(2)
14
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
on the validity of the input, and factors
such as sensor calibration and accuracy.
It is possible to test some source
data for obvious errors. One example
might be the comparison between an
air handler mixing plenum temperature
sensor and an outside air sensor. When
in economizer mode with correctly
operating dampers, the mixing plenum
temperature cannot be lower than the
outside air temperature.
The UT allows the user to test for such
relationships by creating mathematical
formulas, and by offsetting trends by
a ﬁ xed value to correct for calibration
errors. Such formulas are not created
automatically, so the user must rely on
experience to detect and correct sensor
errors.
Other errors are less obvious. When
using sensor data in a mathematical
formula, the errors in the various data
series are compounded.8,9 This effect
must be accounted for by the user for a
true measure of accuracy.
For each sensor, the end-to-end uncer-
tainty10 will be greater than the sensor un-
certainty itself because of the combined
errors introduced by the sensor probe,
transducer, signal errors in wiring and
electrical components of the EMCS.
Although it is important to be aware
of these errors, an energy analysis is fre-
quently aimed at providing results before
and after a retroﬁ t or change in controls
sequence, and looks at overall energy
values11,12 rather than uncertainties.
Building-Wide Airﬂ ow Analysis
Let’s look at an example of a tenant
with temperature complaints. Even
though the tenant claims to have hot
areas during the summer, it’s not clear
whether the complaints are valid. When
a technician is sent to respond to a hot
complaint, it is typically hours or days
later, and the condition is gone, so there
is no real ability to check.
Rather than responding to incidental
reports, a systematic approach to resolv-
ing complaints requires “charting” the
behavior of the entire building. This
requires an analysis of all temperatures,
their associated setpoints, and all air
terminals over time.
Here are the steps, using the UT, to see
overall building performance:
• Read the available trend data from the
EMCS;
• Logically group the trends so that all
VAVs are recognized as logical entities
and become available for analysis;
• Sum up the airﬂ ows from all VAVs to
get an idea of total airﬂ ow over time;
• Perform a setpoint analysis for all VAVs
to determine their ability to maintain
room temperature setpoints and airﬂ ow
setpoints;
• Ensure that data is ﬁ ltered so the analy-
sis only takes place when the AC unit
fans are running.
The screenshots show various stages
of this type of analysis from a campus
project in San Francisco.
Figure 2 shows the typical UT layout,
with a central graph window and four data
windows arranged around the center. The
four windows are, in clockwise direction
starting from the bottom left:
1. Raw data window: this shows the
ﬁ le path of each source data ﬁ le from
a data logger or building automation
system.
Figure 1: Mathematical formula interface.
Figure 2: Template graph without ﬁ lter.
July 2007
ASHRAE Journal
15
2. Channel window: this shows the im-
ported data, each trend is one channel.
3. Test window: this contains tests,
which are collections of trends with the
same timestamp (see Step 2)
4. Filter window: contains deﬁ nitions
of either schedule ﬁ lters which work by
time, or data ﬁ lters, which take a trend as
the trigger for ﬁ ltering other trends.
Step 1: Read building data. As this
article is being written in January 2007,
the UT is capable of reading the native
output formats of all sensors and tools
in the PG&E lending library,13 as well
as the comma-separated values (CSV)
format,14 which many systems produce.
Spreadsheets can also be imported. Im-
port templates allow sets of multiple ﬁ les
(typically, hundreds or thousands of ﬁ les)
to be imported in a single step. Other
formats are being developed and are
expected to be included over the course
of the next year.
Step 2: Transform data to the same
timestamps and intervals so different
trends can be compared, added, etc.
Since most of the computations on trend
data are done to perform some kind of
test, the collections of transformed data
are called tests. Tests show up in the top
right window of the UT interface. The
time stamps and intervals for all trends in
a test are synchronized by interpolating
values between recorded measurements.
Status trends (also commonly called
change of value trends [COV]) are au-
tomatically recognized and converted to
on/off type format.
Step 3: Group the trend data using
categories and attributes so that logical
groupings of trends result. Each VAV
zone in our example has eight trends: a
damper position, airﬂ ow, airﬂ ow setpoint,
discharge air temperature, reheat valve
position, room temperature, cooling set-
point and heating setpoint. By assigning
the right attributes to each trend, we iden-
tify its function—namely what kind of
data it represents. In other words, we let
the UT know what trends contain damper
position information, and what trends
contain room temperature information,
and so on. Thanks to the interface, this
doesn’t have to be done for every trend.
Rather, it is done for families of trends,
and the UT automatically generates a
separate category for each VAV terminal.
The categories make up the project tree
and are somewhat analogous to directo-
ries in a Windows® Explorer ﬁ le tree.
Figure 3 shows the attribute deﬁ nition
window. From the initial point names, at-
tributes can be “distilled” without having
to be retyped by isolating parts of the ﬁ le
names and assigning these to columns
so that the attributes are assigned for all
trends. The example shows attributes
“Device,” “I/O address” and “Property,”
where the device is a number that desig-
nates an actual controller ID in the ﬁ eld,
the I/O address is the physical input/out-
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
18
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
put hardwired address for each point on
the VAV controller, and “Property” is
the plain-text description of the trend,
all isolated from the ﬁ le name. In cases
where the ﬁ le names are less descriptive,
ﬁ lling multiple rows of trend descriptions
with similar attributes such as “Damper
Position” still allows fairly fast attribute
assignments for a whole dataset.
Figure 4 shows the UT window with
categories of trends, which is normally
the top left window. These categories
can be semiautomatically generated.
For example, the VAV category can be
generated by grouping all VAV trends
by device (VAV controller) number. The
UT then generates a new category for
each new controller ID, and populates
each of these with all trends for one VAV
terminal. The user still names the overall
category (VAVs) and decides whether the
ﬁ le tree is organized by building geom-
etry, equipment type, or a mixture. But
again, a large part of the tedious manual
assignments is eliminated.
Step 5: Using the logical structure
established in Step 4, we now let the
UT analyze all VAV terminals. To de-
termine how well zones maintain their
temperature, we identify which trend
represents room temperature, which
trend represents cooling setpoint (high
setpoint), and which trend represents
heating setpoint (low setpoint). The UT
takes this assignment and looks at each
VAV terminal in turn, automatically
recognizing which categories on the tree
structure are relevant. Note that the UT
only looks at temperatures during occu-
pied hours, by ﬁ ltering out all data when
the AC unit fan is off.
Figure 5 shows the same temperature
and airﬂ ow data as Figure 2, but now
the schedule ﬁ lter is turned on, and data
for nights and weekends is no longer
included in the analysis. It can clearly be
seen that the unﬁ ltered trend in Figure 2
appears to show a room that is often too
cold. In contrast, Figure 5 shows that the
low temperatures occur mostly during
unoccupied periods, so the room is in fact
not too cold on Thursday, November 19
and Friday, November 20, although the
room warms up too slowly on Monday,
November 22.
A table with performance results is
automatically generated that gives us an
instant overview of the whole building.
Figure 6 shows an example of such a
table. For each terminal, we see how well
the setpoints were maintained over time
to answer the following: Was the zone too
hot? Too cold? If so, for how long? By
how much were the setpoints exceeded?
Each of these questions is answered by
results in one of the table’s columns. The
rows can be sorted to show the hottest
zones, or the coldest zones, or the zones
that were too cold for the longest time.
In Figure 6, the Result Score is the
overall performance of the terminal. The
Figure 5: Template graph with ﬁ lter.
Figure 4: Categories.
higher this value, the worse the overall
performance. It is made up of the cooling
offset and the heating offset. The cool-
ing offset shows an integral over time
(ﬁ ltered time, occupied periods only). It
is the temperature above setpoint times
the number of seconds above setpoint.
The same applies in reverse for the heat-
ing offset—it is the temperature below
setpoint multiplied by the number of
seconds below setpoint. The mathemat-
ics behind this method was described in
detail in the author’s previous article.14
Table 1 is sorted by overall result score.
Terminal 5133 scores worst on percent-
age of time over setpoint, because it is
Figure 3: Attribute setup.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
20
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
always too hot (100% of tested time).
However, its overall score is better (lower)
than that of Terminal 5333. That is because
terminal 5333 is over setpoint for 88% of
the time, but it is 7.6°F (4.2°C) more than
the setpoint on average, compared to 6.1°F
(3.4°C) more than setpoint for Terminal
5133. Its highest temperature during this
period is shown in column “Max Value” at
86.3°F (30.2°C). Figure 7 shows Terminal
5333, and it can easily be seen that the
zone is always too hot.
Note that all of the terminals shown in
this example are at or over 78°F (25.6°C)
during the tested period, so that user
complaints in these areas are certainly
reasonable. Many other terminals showed
no problems. Sorting for the coldest zone
returns Zone 5137. Figure 8 shows this
zone, and again it is readily apparent that
there are heating problems.
Minimum values of 0°F (–17.8°C) for
the lowest temperature in the results table
indicate data loss occurred, and zero values
were returned as a result. The spikes in setpoints on November
17 are the result of on-site testing. In case some data is missing
(trends are not recorded or imported into the UT), the column
“Errors” returns the value “Failed to Retrieve xyz Test Channel,”
where “xyz” describes which channel is missing. This lets the
user complete the trend database in a targeted manner.
Using Figure 6, and sorting by various parameters allows the
commissioning agent or building engineer to gain rapid insight
into where problems arise and why.
The same exercise can be repeated for airﬂ ow analysis. We
identify which trend represents actual airﬂ ow, and which trend
represents the airﬂ ow setpoint. The analysis then reveals which
terminals are the most “starved.” Not surprisingly, Terminal
5333 is among the worst performers. Surprisingly, Terminal
5135 is actually more starved but performs well in spite of its
lack of airﬂ ow, indicating that it was probably over-designed.
Step 6: Once the worst performers are identiﬁ ed by exam-
ining and sorting the analysis results table in various ways,
these terminals can be shown graphically. A template graph
is a single graph, which is set up once to determine the format
and the type of trends viewed. It can then be used multiple times
to see different pieces of equipment. For our example, we’ve
set up a template graph with two charts. It shows airﬂ ow ver-
sus setpoint in the bottom chart, and room temperature versus
heating/cooling setpoint in the top chart. Using this template
graph, we can step through the project tree of VAV terminals.
Figures 2, 5, 7 and 8 show such template graphs.
An experienced user can analyze an entire building in about
four to eight hours using this approach. A building operator
with a family of template graphs can quickly see a useful mul-

tipoint history of problem
systems.
Depending on the size of
the building, this used to
require one to two weeks
using manual analysis. Even
after this investment of time,
the results were not as con-
clusive as the tables with
analysis results produced
by the UT.
Economizer Analysis
In addition to the example
shown earlier, the UT also
has an automatic econo-
mizer analysis module. A
typical result of economizer
operation is shown in Fig-
ure 9.
Almost all ofﬁ ce buildings
feature systems with econo-
mizers, and often, these
economizers either perform
poorly or are completely
inoperable. They determine
the actual amount of outside
air that has to be conditioned
and as such have a major
inﬂ uence on building energy use.
A quick way to assess their perfor-
mance is given in the UT. We assign the
following trends in the analysis interface:
outside air; return air; mixed air; supply
air; and CO2 (if used).
Since the correct operation of an
economizer produces a well-defined
relationship between these temperatures,
it is possible to show in a graph how an
ideal economizer would operate. The UT
draws this ideal economizer graph, and
then overlays the actual economizer op-
eration—the farther away from the ideal
graph we are, the worse the performance
of the actual economizer. Since a single
snapshot is produced that shows perfor-
mance over the entire trend period, this is
a useful and simple tool. The ﬁ lters allow
us to discard temperatures while the unit
is not in operation.
Figure 9 is an example of the econo-
mizer analysis graph. It shows outside
air temperature on the x-axis against
mixed air temperature on the y-axis. The
blue solid line shows what the mixed-air
temperature should be in the ideal case.
The dashed blue lines above and below
show offsets from the ideal line by 2°F
(1.1°C), to represent what we would con-
sider acceptable results based on sensor
inaccuracy. The red line shows where the
mixed air would be for heating condi-
tions, and the red and black dots show the
actual unit performance in cooling and
heating operation, respectively.
The graph is divided up into three
ranges from left to right.
Range 1 (left) for cold outside air
corresponds to the temperatures during
which the economizer modulates to
control supply air temperature from its
minimum position (up to 100% open).
Range 2 shows a line sloping at 45°
upward and is a 100% open economizer.
In this range, the mixed air temperature
equals outside air temperature.
Range 3 represents high outside air
temperatures. In this region, the econo-
mizer is disabled and closes to minimum
position, resulting in a mixed air tem-
perature that slowly rises with outside
Figure 8: Terminal 5137.
Figure 7: Terminal 5333.

ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
air temperature. The slope of the line depends
on the percentage of minimum outside air. For
0% outside air, the ideal economizer operation
line would be horizontal (mixed air would equal
return air), and for 100% minimum outside air,
the ideal economizer operation line would be 45°
upward, superimposed on the green line shown
in the ﬁ gure. Any real operational values will fall
between a horizontal line and the green line in
this third range.
The net result is a rough step-shape made by the
blue line, and scattering of actual red temperature
values around this line. The more distance we
ﬁ nd between the blue line and the red points, the
worse our economizer operation. The same applies
for the red solid line, and the black dots, which
represent ideal mixing temperature in heating
mode, and actual temperatures in heating mode.
Figure 9 shows an example of good economizer
performance in the 100% outside air setting, with
a higher than intended minimum outside air set-
ting—the cloud of red points on the right side of
the graph is consistently above the blue line. This indicates the
outside air damper is open too much at its minimum setting,
wasting energy.
An added advantage of seeing data in this manner is that it
allows us to gauge the actual outside air percentage that is being
introduced at minimum outside air damper position. Measuring
actual outside air amounts is notoriously difﬁ cult for package
units. But, with a sample of several weeks’ worth of data, a red
“cloud” of temperatures emerges in the third range on the right
side of the graph. Changing the slope of the blue line until it
runs roughly through the center of this cloud tells us the actual
amount of outside air the unit is operating with.
Conclusions
The Universal Translator (UT) is a free software tool that
can be downloaded from www.utonline.org. It allows users to
look at and analyze large amounts of data from building energy
management and control systems. A team of programmers has
added a broad array of functions to the UT over the last six
months, and the programming efforts are continuing into the
future, funded by California taxpayers. The newest functions
of the UT are highlighted in this article.
If you are a building engineer, commissioning engineer or
owner who wishes to gain more insight into your building’s
operation in an affordable manner, the UT may be just what
you’ve been waiting for.
Instructions for the UT are available from the Web site. In addi-
tion, UT training Web casts are given by the Paciﬁ c Energy Cen-
ter15 and you can sign up for them anywhere in the country.

ASHRAE Journal

J u l y 2 0 0 7
By Michael A. Reed, Member ASHRAE; and Cedreck Davis
Chilled Water Plant
Savings at No Cost
NASA Marshall Space Flight Center has been involved in projects such as producing this laboratory module for the International Space Station.
N
ASA at its Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Ala., contracted an energy consulting
company to evaluate the operation of the central chilled water plant (CCWP) built in 1999 to
ascertain if any improvements could be made to reduce operating cost with no expenditure of capital
funds. Only no-cost changes were considered. This was a decision made by the owner to ensure
existing systems were operating at peak performance before extensive capital expenditure projects
were evaluated. The consultant’s study showed that the chilled water system was not producing the
ΔT that matched the chillers’ design during off-peak conditions. This condition is often called low ΔT
syndrome and has been written about in many articles.1,2,3,4
Photo Credit: NASA
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, July 2007. ©Copyright 2007 American Society
of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes
only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission
of ASHRAE.
July 2007
ASHRAE Journal
37
Michael A. Reed is an associate at Sain Engineering Associates in Birmingham,
Ala. Cedreck Davis is the energy program manager for NASA Marshall Space
Flight Center in Huntsville, Ala.
About the Authors
The impact of this condition causes the secondary ﬂ ow to be
larger than the primary at times, and warm water from the return
was mixing with cold water from the chillers as it was leaving
the plant. When this happens, another chiller was started even
before the operating machines were fully loaded. This article
describes the results at the CCWP of changing the logic to
control the mixed temperature in the chilled water secondary
with the chiller discharge temperature, rather than starting an-
other chiller to deal with low ΔT syndrome. The logic change
allows the operating chillers to be fully loaded before starting
the next machine. Implementation of the recommendation was
accomplished in November 2005.
The CCWP was constructed in 1999 to consolidate 32 re-
mote chilled water plants with a combined capacity of 17,000
tons (59 789 kW) into one 10,000 ton (55 170 kW) plant.5 The
facility has been in operation for approximately seven years.
This article describes a speciﬁ c portion of the operation of the
plant that relates to the sequencing of the chillers and pumps
for optimum performance.
This site is the propulsion research and support center for
NASA’s space missions, and consists of more than 216 build-
ings and is spread over 1,800 acres (728 ha). A CCWP with an
operational peak connected capacity of 10,000 tons (35 170
kW) was designed with eight nominal 1,250 ton (4396 kW)
chillers and a ninth chiller as backup. During the ﬁ rst summer
season, which was in 2000,5 the peak load was approximately
7,200 tons (25 322 kW).
Existing System
A study was made before the CCWP was built, which pre-
dicted that the plant’s secondary ΔT would be approximately
12°F (6.7°C) at peak-load conditions. The designed hydraulic
system was a primary/secondary type with a secondary ﬂ ow
capacity equivalent to a 10°F (5.6°C) system at 10,000 tons (35
170 kW) and individual chiller primary pumps with an 11°F
(6.1°C) ΔT at the nominal 1,250 ton (4396 kW) capacity.5 Since
the CCWP’s construction, all coil designs have been at a 13°F
(7.2°C) ΔT for peak conditions. The buildings now connected to
the CCWP range in year of construction from 1964 to 2005.
The CCWP has a reasonably simple and orderly layout as
indicated by Figure 1.
Existing System Operation
At the beginning of the investigation, the consultant requested
historical trend data from the site. A full year’s worth of hourly
data from May 1, 2003 to April 30, 2004 was received from the
Utility Controls System (UCS) group for several requested key
points at the CCWP. This amount of available data is exceptional
and is directly responsible for the ability to implement the logic
change described later. Additionally, the data was exceptionally
clean; a total of 8,725 hours were usable out of a leap year of
8,784 total hours.
Examination of the load proﬁ le of outdoor air temperature
(OA) vs. load (tons) in this trend data indicates that the site
load has increased from the peak of 7,200 tons (25 322 kW) in
2000 to a value closer to 8,500 tons (29 895 kW) in 2003–04.
Additionally, a Loop B was added to the plant since this
2003–04 data. The owner continues to connect new buildings
to the CCWP.
As the efﬁ ciency of the CCWP was being evaluated, a need
existed to determine if the site was experiencing low ΔT syn-
drome. The answer to this requirement is contained in the trend
data received from the UCS system. A scatter plot was created
of OA vs. secondary ΔT using the trend data received from
the UCS. Figure 2 shows that the site does suffer from low ΔT
syndrome during off-peak conditions.
The red line on the scatter plot is a graphical average overlaid
on the chart and shows that at peak operation the ΔT is reach-
ing the value of the design for the chillers at 11°F (6.1°C) ΔT.

38
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
However, the amount of time
the system operates at peak is
extremely small compared to
the hours per year it operates
not at peak. As the OA and load
decreases, the secondary ΔT
reduces to approximately 7°F
(3.9°C) at its minimum. This
is a graphical representation of
the low ΔT syndrome that exists
at this site.
How does the presence of low
ΔT syndrome impact the opera-
tion of the CCWP? Figure 3 is a
simpliﬁ ed schematic of the site’s chilled water system showing
the hydraulics of what is happening around the system with a
7°F (3.9°C) example secondary ΔT. Example loads and ﬂ ows
based on the site’s plant system size will be used in this section
to help explain the impact of low ΔT on the primary/secondary
system at the CCWP. This is not what is actually happening at all
times, and this example is only used to simplify the hydraulics
within the system for describing this project.
For the example schematic, as the ﬂ ow in the secondary
increases and if no action is taken to start another chiller, the
mixing of water ﬂ ows will change from the system return-side
intersection of the decoupler to the supply-side intersection.
This is not what really happens at the CCWP, but it is what
would happen if another chiller were not started.
Figure 3 shows the hydraulics around the example schematic
system at the nominal full load of 1,250 tons (4396 kW) for a
single chiller (shown in green text).
The secondary ΔT is still 7°F (3.9°C), but the ΔT that the
chiller needs to see to reach full load is 11°F (6.1°C) as seen
in the formulas at the bottom of Figure 3. When the ﬂ ow in
the secondary exceeds 2,750 gpm (168.6 L/s) the water in the
decoupler will ﬂ ow as shown in Figure 3, which is reversed
from what is occurring for loads below 802 tons (2821 kW).
At a full 1,250 tons (4396 kW) and a 7°F (3.9°C) ΔT second-
ary, the ﬂ ow is 4,286 gpm (262.7 L/s). Of the total 4,286 gpm
(262.7 L/s) of secondary ﬂ ow returning from the site, 1,536
gpm (94.2 L/s) is passing through the decoupler and mixing at
the system supply-side intersection of the decoupler as shown
in Figure 3 with 2,750 gpm (168.6 L/s) of 44°F (6.7°C) chilled
water leaving the chiller.
A common description of this situation from operators is that
the “chiller can’t handle the load.” The chiller is set for 44°F
(6.7°C), but the chilled water leaving the plant is 48°F (8.9°C)
as seen in Figure 3.
The on-board chiller control panel is responsible for maintain-
ing the temperature leaving the machine and is not aware of the
temperature in the secondary. This example schematic shows
that the secondary temperature would be 48°F (8.9°C) if another

39
chiller was not started, and the
existing machine(s) were loaded
to 100%. However, this would not
be acceptable because the desired
temperature is 44°F (6.7°C) in the
secondary loop. To deal with this
situation, the UCS control logic
calls for the next chiller to start
when the secondary ﬂ ow is greater
than the primary. If the primary
loop is always moving more water
ﬂ ow than the secondary, then the
temperature of the water leaving
the chiller will be what is delivered
to the site in the secondary. Thus,
starting the next chiller is how the
CCWP deals with the effect of low
ΔT syndrome at this primary/sec-
ondary chiller plant. Using the example schematic for a 7°F (3.9°C)
ΔT secondary, the next chiller would be started when the running
machine is at 802 tons (2821 kW) or 64% loaded.
Using six months of the hourly trend data provided from the
UCS, Figure 4 shows a scatter plot of the number of chillers
running plotted as compared against the OA temperature. Only
December through May from the pre-project trend data was used
in Figure 4 to match with the same months of data available in
the after logic code changes.
Trend data for the pre-project operation showed that there
were many hours in which the minimum site load was below
1,000 tons (3517 kW) even down to approximately 500 tons

(1759 kW). Yet Figure 4 shows that on all but two one-hour
events, there was always a minimum of two chillers running.
As previously noted, the next chiller for this site was started
when the secondary ﬂ ow exceeded the primary.
UCS Code Logic Change
Is there a controls logic change that could be implemented at
the CCWP that would enable the chillers to control the secondary
temperature setpoint and load to 100% despite the impact of low
ΔT syndrome? The CCWP’s product is chilled water that is distrib-
uted to users around the site, which speciﬁ cally is the secondary
chilled water system. Logically speaking, shouldn’t the secondary
temperature be directly monitored
and controlled to the desired
setpoint? This, in simple terms,
is exactly what the new logic
code does. The new logic for the
CCWP is to directly maintain and
control the secondary setpoint
using the UCS system. This is
done by modulating the chiller(s)
discharge temperature to maintain
the mixed water temperature in
the secondary system to the de-
sired setpoint.
Figure 5 illustrates this logic
change and shows what is hap-
pening hydraulically around the
system as well as the tempera-
tures at key areas.
As the water reverses in the
decoupler, for loads above 802 tons (2821 kW) as seen by the
arrows in Figure 5, the temperature in the secondary supply
starts to increase. With the new code in control on the actual
system, the existing temperature sensors on the secondary loops
detect this increase and the UCS system modulates the chiller
discharge temperature setpoint to maintain the desired mixed
water temperature in the secondary. As the load increases, and
more mixing occurs at the supply side of the decoupler, the
chiller(s) discharge temperature is lowered through a typical
control loop from the UCS to maintain the secondary sup-
ply setpoint. This means that during some part of the day the
chiller discharge temperature could be lower than the secondary

July 2007
ASHRAE Journal
41
controlled setpoint. When the true maximum capacity (100%)
for the chiller is reached, it can do no more work. At this point
the chilled water will begin to rise above the desired setpoint.
When the secondary actual temperature is 1°F (0.6°C) above
the secondary setpoint for ﬁ ve minutes, the UCS system will
start the next chiller.
How did this new control logic alter the operation of the
CCWP? From the data collected around the time of the imple-
mentation of this logic change, it was apparent the change
was activated between 10 a.m. and 11 a.m. on Nov. 28, 2005.
Trend data for after the logic change was made is data from
Dec. 1, 2005 to May 31, 2006. Figure 6 is formatted the same
as Figure 4 but is based on the data collected after the logic
change was made.
Figure 4 shows that there were only two hours in which one
chiller was operational. After the change in logic was made,
there were 120 occasions in which one chiller maintained the
site load. Prior to the logic control change, the third chiller
was needed at an OA temperature above approximately 28°F
(–2.2°C). After the logic control change, the third chiller was
not needed until the OA was above approximately 47°F (8.3°C).
Because of the nature of this site’s mission, the chilled water
system is used for more than administrative building support.
Research areas and technical equipment cooling uses are some
examples of requirements for chillers to operate all year.
At each step of another chiller start, the new code enables the
next chiller to be started at a higher OA reference temperature
than the previous code. The seventh chiller was never started
with the new logic code even with an OA as high as 96°F
(35.6°C) and the addition of the new Loop B. Before the change
was made, the seventh chiller was needed for temperatures
above 89°F (31.7°C).
It could be argued that each chart is against a different site
load and different weather conditions and can’t be technically
compared this simply, which is partially true.
Another way to examine this change would be to try to elimi-
nate the actual load and weather as a factor in the evaluation.
The percent load of the operating chiller(s) in the immediate
hour before the next chiller was started was evaluated from
the trend data to normalize this situation. Previous to this code
change, the average percent loaded of the operating chiller(s)
in the hour before the next started for January and February
was 57.7%. After the code change, this average percent loaded
of the operating chiller(s) increased to 86.4%. Since the data
was only logged hourly at an instantaneous value, in reality, the
chiller could have been started 59 minutes or just one minute
before the data was logged. The same random starting of chillers
existed in relation to the clock hour for both cases so the differ-
ence between the 57.7% and 86.4% is a true improvement in
the starting sequence for the chillers at this CCWP. Evaluating
Advertisement formerly in this space.
42
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
the change in operation in this way removes the impact of the
weather and load.
Several methods were used to verify this improvement in
efﬁ ciency of the new logic code. One method that proved to
be interesting was a side-by-side 24-hour-by-hour comparison.
A 24-hour day from the previous operational trend data (May
16, 2003) and one from after the change (May 23, 2006) were
selected that was as close as possible to the same in OA dry-
bulb temperature and is shown in Table 1.
A reset chilled water schedule up to 44°F (6.7°C) was also
implemented during this change that enabled the lowering of the
chiller discharge temperatures. The following can be observed
from this side-by-side comparison in Table 1:
The day started with one less chiller operating with the
new code;
The new code did start the ﬁ fth chiller sooner in the day
than the previous code, but the OA temperature was 3.6°F
(2°C) higher;
The sixth chiller was started by the new code two hours
later than the previous code;
•
•
•
The previous code operated the sixth chiller for six hours
during the day versus only one hour for the new code;
The new code dropped back to only two chillers running
versus four at the end of the day for the previous code;
and
In the previous code, the flow in the primary was con-
trolled to always be more than the secondary and was
always “positive” in the decoupler. The flow in the
decoupler was “negative (or opposite)” in the after logic
change, representing a greater flow in the secondary
than the primary for most hours of the day.
This side-by-side comparison shows that fewer chillers were
operated between the data before the code change (2003–04) and
the data after (2006). As previously stated, the site added a Loop B
to the CCWP in the summer of 2005, which means that even with
an increased load, the new code operated with fewer chillers.
Energy Savings
Where are the savings coming from for this new logic change?
The majority of savings is coming from the equipment that is

ASHRAE Journal
43
not started with the next chiller. Starting a chiller at the CCWP
requires a 75 hp (56 kW) primary pump and 100 hp (74.6 kW)
condenser pump to be started.
What about the fact that for the new code the chiller will at
times have to run at a lower setpoint than the secondary tem-
perature, requiring more energy? Using the example ﬁ gures,
which are not the real system but represent generally what is
happening for this CCWP, the following simpliﬁ ed, single-point,
winter season evaluation is presented:
In the example ﬁ gures used in this article, the load at 802
tons (2821 kW) and 2,750 gpm (168.6 L/s) was the balance
point with zero ﬂ ow going through the decoupler. With
the previous code at ﬂ ows above 2,750 gpm (168.6 L/s)
in the secondary, another chiller would be started with its
corresponding primary and condenser water pumps. In the
example, Figure 5, to accomplish the mixing temperature of
44°F (6.7°C) in the secondary at a load of 1,250 tons (4396
kW) requires that the chiller run at 40°F (4.4°C). Manufac-
turers’ data for the nine identical chillers was used to esti-
mate the potential increase at the chiller to operate at 40°F
(4.4°C) vs. 44°F (6.7°C). This increase is approximately 23
kW as seen below in the simple example calculation (assume
70°F [21.1°C] site condenser water supply (CWS) setpoint
achievable in winter):
1,250 tons × 0.548 kW/ton (at 40°F and 70°F CWS) = 685.0 kW
1,250 tons × 0.530 kW/ton (at 44°F and 70°F CWS) = 662.5 kW
Δ = 22.5 kW
With the old logic code, at 802 tons (2821 kW), another
chiller and its corresponding pumps would be started for a total
additional kW larger than the increased kW to run the chiller
at a lower chilled water setpoint.
(Measured 56.7 kW primary pump + Measured 65.8 kW con-
denser pump) = 122.5 kW total
Under the old logic code when the decoupler reverses direc-
tion at 802 tons (2821 kW) another chiller was started and an
additional 122.5 kW was required immediately. Therefore, the
Δ in kW between the old and new code varies between 122.5
kW at 802 tons (2821 kW) and 100 kW (122.5 kW–22.5 kW) at
1,250 tons (4396 kW) for each chiller running with the new code
always requiring less kW when low ΔT syndrome is occurring.
As noted in Figure 2, the low ΔT syndrome effect varies as the
OA temperature changes. Additionally, as previously noted, the
average percent load of the chillers is higher in the after code
operation (86.4%) versus the pre-code change operation (57.7%),
which results in an improvement in chiller performance. Due to
the many variations in load, weather, chiller starts, etc., throughout
Advertisement formerly in this space.
44
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
the year, only looking at the total annual consumption before and
after this code change reveals the total savings.
Four electrical meters log the kWh consumption for the
CCWP. Comparing the one year preceding this code change
(Dec. 1, 2004 to Nov. 30, 2005) to one year after (Dec. 1, 2005 to
Nov. 30, 2006), there was a 6.3% annual savings in kWh energy
use. During the summer of 2005, Loop B was connected to the
CCWP and placed into operation, which means that a full year
of operation of the increased site load is not in the pre-change
energy use. Even with this, the annual savings between 2005
(before change) and 2006 (after change) the savings was ap-
proximately $88,850 based on the cost of energy at the facility
when the project was implemented.
The greatest savings for this code change occurs during the
winter when the low ΔT syndrome is at its lowest point. When
examining the kWh consumed, each of the winter months of
December, January and February after the code change to the
same months in the previous year the savings average was 15.7%.
These three months (after code change) averaged lower than the
same months for all the years back to 2002. Only data back to this
time was requested from the UCS group. This included the fact
that the load increased in 2005 with the addition of Loop B.
This control logic change was implemented using on-site
staff. Therefore, no added cost was incurred to reprogram the
UCS system to the new logic code. So, in almost every way
this project is examined, the owner is achieving a signiﬁ cant
amount of savings for a no capital cost project.
Conclusion
Is your facility a multiple chiller primary/secondary hydraulic
designed system that appears to be affected by low ΔT syn-
drome? If the running chillers are not fully loaded and the next
chiller is commanded to start, either manually by an operator,
or by an automated system, then your facility could possibly be
suffering from the effects of low ΔT syndrome. Other systems,
such as fully variable primary-only designs, are not included
in this statement. This article describes the results of this code
change for this speciﬁ c CCWP that is hydraulically designed
as a primary/secondary system.
The new logic code reduced the impact of low ΔT syndrome
at its CCWP and saved energy. Low ΔT syndrome did not
change or go away. Its existence is a result of what is happen-
ing at the use points around the site. This article describes how
this site’s CCWP deals with the impact of low ΔT syndrome
at the plant.
To implement the logic control change as noted in this article,
the following items were necessary:
Ability to monitor the chilled water secondary temperature
at the UCS;
Ability to start and stop each chiller (and pumps) from the
UCS;
Ability to reset the discharge temperature setpoint at the
chiller from the UCS;
Ability to monitor kW or percent load of each chiller for
when to shut down a chiller; and
A person knowledgeable enough about chilled water sys-
tems to properly code, implement, and tune the system for
optimum performance.
Several ways exist to deal with the impact of low ΔT syn-
drome at central plants as well as trying to increase the ΔT by
altering conditions at the using source, all of which generally
cost money to implement. This logic code change option was
chosen for this site because it met the criteria of no capital cost
for this owner.
The annual 6.3% improvement in performance as noted
in this article is not huge, but it was worth it for this owner
because of the ability to implement it at no capital cost. The
results as described in this article were previously presented at
a conference6 in 2006.
References
1. Kirsner, W. 1998. “A check valve in the chiller bypass line? two
views on this question.” Heating/Piping/Air Conditioning Engi-
neering 70(1):128 – 134.
2. Coad, W.J. 1998. “A fundamental perspective on chilled water systems.
A fundamental physics of chilled water systems from a perspective of
engineering philosophy.” Heating/Piping/Air Conditioning Engineer-
ing 70(8):59 – 66.
3. Avery, G. 2001. “Improving the efﬁ ciency of chilled water plants.”
ASHRAE Journal 43(5):14 – 18.
4. Taylor, S.T. 2002. “Primary-only vs. pri-
mary-secondary variable ﬂ ow systems.”
ASHRAE Journal 44(2):25 – 29.
5. Leonard, P.L. and R. Brunell. 2002.
“Centralized chillers: a case study. de-
sign of new central chiller facility for
NASA’s Marshall Space Flight Center
presented unique challenges.” Heating/
Piping/Air Conditioning Engineering
74(3).
6. Reed, M. 2006. “Under the Hood: Op-
erations and Maintenance. Heavy Metal:
Worth the Upkeep…or a Boat Anchor to
Efﬁ ciency?” Workshop Session, Energy
2006.

About the Authors
By Bill Gaines, Martin R. Orban, and David R. Welch
15
Things to Know
About Cooling
Water Treatment
A
n objective evaluation of competing cooling water proposals
only can be made when they are uniform. An unambiguous
speciﬁ cation would seem ideal for this purpose. Yet each facility
is unique and industry standards of quality and performance still
leave open areas of interpretation due to those unique system
characteristics. How can a facility manager know when a change
in suppliers or chemical program is warranted?
of course, be economical and provide
value to the customer. A cooling water
treatment program must have the same
qualities. Poorly specifying performance
metrics is unwise and imprudent, yet
writing entirely prescriptive requirements
for a cooling water program is, at best,
difﬁ cult and impermanent. Constantly
changing regulations, variable manufac-
turing ﬂ uids/processes, and development
of new treatment chemistries demand
that a treatment program be ﬂ exible to
reﬂ ect optimum conditions. A compre-
hensive system speciﬁ cation that rules
out discovery, experience, and innova-
tion minimizes the opportunity to use a
supplier’s expertise. It also sets the stage
to focus on the speciﬁ cation rather than
actual system performance.
Does this mean a well-deﬁ ned per-
formance speciﬁ cation isn’t necessary?
Indeed not! The need for an explicit
specification is critical to protect the
integrity of process equipment. The indi-
vidual elements of what must be included
have been well documented in previous
literature.1,2 Unfortunately, industry
standards of quality and performance still
leave areas open to interpretation due to
unique system requirements, and create
signiﬁ cant variations between apparently
similar proposals. Given this situation,
This article provides a practical guide
to determine the value of a water treat-
ment program and to perform a critical re-
view of alternative programs. It is equally
suitable when proposals are solicited or
when an end-user wants to compare a new
supplier to an existing program.
In the auto industry, a “tight” part spec-
iﬁ cation is a necessity. Components must
meet rigid tolerances, be of high qual-
ity, perform to expected standards, and,
50
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, July 2007. ©Copyright 2007 American Society
of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes
only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission
of ASHRAE.
July 2007
ASHRAE Journal
51
how can a facility manager know when a change in suppliers
or chemical program is warranted?
Five Front-End Elements to Establish
There is no single “right” solution to selecting a treatment
regime. Even the “best” program involves tradeoffs between
cost and effectiveness. However, certain elements always im-
prove the likelihood of success:
1. System Characterization
Nothing is more important than an accurate system character-
ization -- it is the foundation upon which all other decisions are
made, and provides the basis for proposals that optimize cost
and performance. Even greater beneﬁ t is gained when suppliers
are offered the opportunity to survey the system to gain ad-
ditional information. Essential system characteristics include:
The volume of water used in the previous year (if unknown,
start measuring it);
The number of operating days and seasonal variation;
The total system volume (easy to determine);
A complete makeup water analysis (if you don’t have one,
get one);
The recirculation rate (if unknown, pump horsepower or
tons of cooling capacity can be used to estimate it until it
is measured);
Present cycles of concentration (CoC); and
A list of the materials of construction for all wetted parts
within the system.
Water usage is a source of difﬁ culty in bid preparation
and evaluation. Left to their own, suppliers who may have to
estimate makeup and blowdown will skew cost estimates. In
the absence of reliable metered water usage, it is essential that
annual water usage be deﬁ ned in the speciﬁ cations.
2. Restrictions
Any restrictions due to discharge requirements or corporate
policies must be communicated (e.g., zinc and/or molybdate
not allowed). Equally important is when there is a bias toward a
particular program (e.g., halogen). In this case, the active chemi-
cal and its control range must be deﬁ ned, while still allowing
voluntary alternate programs that use supplier experience.
3. Performance Metrics
The current metrics (if satisfactory) or expectations (if unsat-
isfactory) must be communicated. Remember that high demands
escalate costs and may promote future dissatisfaction. The best
method for determining performance metrics is to combine in-
formation garnered during the bidding process with knowledge
from the vendor, end-user and industry organizations.
High performance cannot be sustained without proper train-
ing. Vendors need to provide a manual to administer the program
safely and as designed. Such a manual needs to include at a
minimum:
Emergency contact information;
Chemical treatment programs and control limits;
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
Test procedures, interpretation of results, and corrective
actions;
Product bulletins and material safety data sheets (MSDSs);
and
Routine service reports.
4. “Face Time”
Treatment programs are provided on either a full service or
limited (namely, advisory) basis. Under a full-service agree-
ment, the supplier provides total management of the program.
Under a limited agreement, the supplier recommends a program,
provides chemicals and offers consulting to the site. Facility per-
sonnel maintain the equipment, administer the chemicals, and
monitor water quality, operational characteristics, and program
performance. The primary responsibility of a water treatment
supplier is to manage the water treatment program. The supplier
should ensure the completeness of analytical tests, know how
to interpret them and foresee potential problem areas.
“Face time” is the frequency and duration of “service” pro-
vided by the supplier. In many cases, this is the most costly
of all the program components. Therefore, interview the rep-
resentative as if you were hiring a full-time employee. Real-
ize that when you specify service frequency, you are directly
impacting the program cost and inviting a cost that may not
connect to your desired program value. The experience of the
representative plays a critical role in program success and cost.
Although dollars spent divided by hours of service may be a
helpful calculation, it is the quality of service that determines
the largest portion of program value.
Alternatively, one must ask “What are the components of the
present service program? What actually happened in the hour
of service that I received and how much am I willing to do in
support of the program that I specify?” Many high-performing
programs are 90% self-managed.
5. Deliverables
Operator logs and vendor service reports provide valuable
data that in conjunction with water, deposit, and other analyses,
measure progress against deﬁ ned performance metrics. Regular
reviews of the program cover performance, costs, problem areas
and corrective action plans. Therefore, it is common to request
a copy of a review authored by the representative you are con-
sidering. Also, a water treatment company’s corporate support
structure impacts its pricing. Be aware of the type and amount
of additional support that you most likely will need.
Five Elements to Establish During the Selection Process
Uniformity among the various proposals is crucial to allow
objective evaluation. Seeking an apples-to-apples comparison
works well for apples, but not always for water treatment pro-
grams. Each facility is unique and industry standards of quality
and performance still leave many areas open to interpretation
based upon those unique system characteristics. Once propos-
als are received, the process of evaluating them can be quite
challenging. Five areas requiring evaluation are:
•
•
•
52
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
1. Product Evaluation
MSDSs don’t provide sufﬁ cient information to compare
products. End-users usually address this is by requiring full
disclosure with submittals. Full disclosure allows the end-user
to examine programs in terms of suitability, health and safety,
and material compatibility. Once the proposals and product
information are in hand, one can compare them to the previ-
ous years’ total pounds of each chemical used, cost per pound,
total program cost, and the number of hours that the supplier
spent on site. One must consider the product recommendation
and its impact on CoC. The product concentration (dilution)
accounts for wide variations in unit pricing, making it impos-
sible to compare usage and cost. Therefore, respondents must
also provide annual use rates. Maximum cycles also vary
depending on the treatment chemistry. Similar programs from
different vendors ought to yield similar CoC. If a program has
greater cost but higher CoC, then the evaluation should reﬂ ect
the reduced makeup. An insightful metric is total annual cost
divided by million pounds of blowdown.
Ideally, deviations are addressed during the pre-bid meeting
and walk-through. But if a reasonable irregularity surfaces after
the fact, then bidders need to be offered an opportunity to make
appropriate revisions. However, they should not be allowed
license for changes outside the irregularity identiﬁ ed.
2. Method of Application
Just as the choice of chemistry must be appropriate to the
individual system, so too must be the method of addition. Pro-
posals must address:
a. Control Method: Is the feed and monitoring equipment ap-
propriate for the chemical program? Will it be on a continuous
or intermittent basis, or a combination of both? If additional
equipment is required, several options are available:
Direct Purchase: End-user purchases and installs required
equipment;
Lease-to-Own: A third-party lease agreement;
Added to Price of Chemical: This is popular when capital
budgets are limited. However, both parties must understand
the term and/or volume limits for which the adder is ap-
plied (a depreciation schedule is advantageous if there is
a need to change vendors before satisfying the terms of
the agreement); and
Vendor Supplied: The vendor retains ownership and mainte-
nance and folds the cost into the chemical price. This method
is gaining in popularity as more end-users view this as an op-
portunity to keep up with advances in control technology.
b. Inventory Management: Proposals must be consistent with
the end-user inventory needs and storage of each product appro-
priate for its usage. They must also demonstrate that adequate
•
•
•
•
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
54
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
but not excessive inventory levels will be
provided. Many companies do not want
to dispose of containers when drums are
used, and some vendors have responded
with “drumless” programs.
c. Ordering/Shipping: While most end-
users specify FOB delivered, this is often
not the most economical choice. Overall
charges are cheaper when higher volumes
are shipped, but some vendors apply a
higher freight charge in case only a few
drums are ordered. Usually it’s better to
specify prepay and add. This way, the
end-user enjoys the quantity freight dis-
counts for large orders, as well as, lower
charges when only a few containers are
shipped. Alternatively, it’s often advanta-
geous to ship on a consignment basis if
the end-user has storage space and the
parties agree on invoicing methods. The
vendor minimizes cost by shipping larger
truckloads less frequently and passes the
savings on to the end-user.
3. Who Do You (And Who Do You Not)
See?
Proposals must identify resources that
will be made available when the primary
representative is unavailable. They must
include names and biographical informa-
tion for the primary and backup support
personnel, as well as, the key corporate
support personnel to be assigned under
such conditions. Thoroughly check all
references!
Evaluation of services cannot be made
by a predetermined formula that applies
to all cases. However, certain questions
aid in the review:
Does the service representative have
the skill to identify and correct prob-
lem areas as they arise?
Does the proposal consider plant
procedures, safety training and lock-
out procedures?
What is supplier turnover rate, and
how long have the personnel been
assigned to the local area?
Is there an allowance for emergency
service? If so, are separate charges
clearly delineated?
Is periodic (typically annual) in-
depth equipment inspection provid-
ed? Proposals should describe how
this will be done and what inspection
equipment is to be used.
4. Corrective Measures for Routine
‘Out of Control’ Parameters
Given the highly dynamic nature of plant
systems, an unexpected event will occur.
When the program is collaborative, service
is proactive, and standard operating proce-
dures have been written; then personnel fol-
low uniform methods to “act and correct”
following an upset. The answer to a water
treatment problem is not always the applica-
tion or increased usage of a chemical. The
representative must always be cognizant
of physical or operational changes, as well
as, chemical upsets. It has been reasonably
stated that “…80% of chemical problems
are mechanical.” Above all we must try to
•
•
•
•
•
Advertisement formerly in this space.
July 2007
ASHRAE Journal
55
remember that, in the real world, change is sometimes necessary.
When this occurs, the method of change should be thoroughly
considered, rather than a knee-jerk reaction. The management of
change is best achieved when a systematic approach is applied to
each process and subprocess.
5. Non-routine Service
If one accepts the premise “anything that can fail will ultimately
fail,” then it follows that a well-designed program must allow
for unforeseen and unscheduled equipment outages. It is in the
best interest of both parties that non-routine service be addressed
upfront. Although there is no single correct formula for deﬁ ning
this, the most common method resembles the following: Basic
contract allows for x hours of routine service plus y hours of non-
routine/emergency service in conjunction with z hours of analytical
laboratory work for a contracted price. Work required beyond these
allowances to be charged at a predeﬁ ned hourly rate.
Five Ongoing Elements
Once a program is established and underway, there are ongo-
ing aspects that require continuing evaluation:
1. Meeting Performance Metrics
The most fundamental measure of supplier performance is
that all metrics and deliverables identiﬁ ed in the front-end ele-
ments are being met. Proof that tests were performed at their
scheduled frequency, all parameters were within prescribed
control limits, and performance objectives were achieved,
must be clearly documented.
2. Risk Management
A risk management program (that should have been jointly
developed) includes a hazard analysis and risk assessment. Each
portion needs to be reviewed annually to ensure it remains cur-
rent for changes made during the previous year.
The hazard analysis is intended to identify safety and envi-
ronmental hazards by rating their severity and likelihood of oc-
currence, and then establishing controls to reduce or eliminate
them. It is fundamental to evaluate and improve the safety of
cooling water systems. A process ﬂ ow diagram needs to indi-
cate where spill prevention is required and where personnel
exposure potential exists. Sensible control for strategies for
hazards must be included. A Legionella-speciﬁ c system assess-
ment must review all water systems for their aerosol potential,
temperature, physical condition, cleanliness, and location.3
Risk assessment data should include: makeup and system water
analyses, system design, operating characteristics, and potential
for equipment fouling due to scale and bioslime.
3. Exhibiting Innovation and Foreseeing Problems
The subjective aspect of supplier evaluation is the most
difﬁ cult. However, one can examine a supplier’s ability to
continually demonstrate eagerness and innovation. When the
representative is engaged in the treatment program, he’s not
concerned solely with routine testing, but rather strives to
improve results and reduce costs. He “crawls upstream” to
discover manufacturing ﬂ uid and process changes, and then
reacts ahead of time. He becomes involved in water treatment
issues and checks performance of equipment outside the scope
of his services. The representative must recognize downsides to
proposed changes. For example, reducing circulating pumps to
save energy may decrease ﬂ ow velocities and turn heat exchang-
ers into sedimentation tanks. Isolating (no ﬂ ow) loops for energy
savings may promote corrosion and uncontrolled microbiologi-
cal growth. Cycling standby equipment at greater than one week
frequencies may allow proliferation of bacteria to inoculate the
system upon startup. It takes an experienced water-treater to
know when these types of changes are prudent.
Methods have been developed that quantify the subjective por-
tion of the evaluation. Most of these involve a matrix consisting of
a comprehensive list of qualities in the ﬁ rst column, a weighting
factor determined by the end-user in the second, and a force-ranked
rating in the third. Multiplying each score by its weighting factor
and totaling all scores produces a single ﬁ nal score.4
4. The Happiness Factor
The most difﬁ cult portion of supplier evaluation to examine
is the “happiness” factor. How much of my present satisfaction
with the program is unrelated to system performance or cost?
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
July 2007
ASHRAE Journal
57
If there is a component of satisfaction that is not covered in
the speciﬁ cation, then examine whether or not that criteria is
appropriate. For instance, some managers want all information
immediately communicated and want to be involved in every
decision (high supplier visibility); while others value quarterly
reviews and exception reporting, knowing the supplier is manag-
ing the details of the program (supplier invisibility). An ongoing
program evaluation process helps to reduce the reliance on the
“happiness factor” for supplier retention or change.
5. Knowing When a Cost Increase is Justiﬁ ed
Owners are often approached for a cost increase due to a
“changed condition” from the speciﬁ cation. The question is
whether or not the so-called “changed” condition should have been
foreseen during the bidding process. Manufacturing ﬂ uids and
processes are constantly changing, so sometimes a cost increase is
justiﬁ ed. Other times, it is an unjustiﬁ ed ploy by the supplier to in-
crease sales or unnecessarily push one up the technology scale.
Equipment and chemicals are closely interrelated. A sticking
solenoid valve can cause chemical use to dramatically increase—
which is not a water treatment problem but rather a maintenance
issue. New production equipment, accidental contamination of the
cooling water (for example, by hydraulic oil), and new discharge
restrictions are examples of changes that the supplier would not
have foreseen (and for which they must be compensated).
However, many common occurrences ought to have been antici-
pated and are not to be compensated. These include: 1) Sediment
buildup from wind-blown dirt into a cooling tower causing ad-
ditional biocide usage; 2) A makeup ﬂ oat that became stuck open
but was repaired in a timely manner; and 3) Legionella entering a
cooling tower requiring immediate disinfection.
Conclusions
No single “correct” method exists for selecting and manag-
ing a cooling water program. High-performance programs can
range anywhere from nearly 100% vendor-managed to almost
completely self-managed. Under any condition, however, it
is a well-deﬁ ned set of performance metrics, a disciplined
data acquisition and analysis approach, and a mutual sense of
ownership that drives success. Knowledge and application of
several key elements during the front-end development, selec-
tion process, and ongoing periods can ensure a solid platform
for balance of cost and performance.
References
1. Chesney, W.W. 1996. “Preparation of bidding speciﬁ cations for
chemical services relating to cooling water and boiler treatments in
industrial facilities.” 57th International Water Conference, p.333.
2. Simon, D.E. 1997. “Comprehensive speciﬁ cations for contracted
water treatment service programs.” 58th Annual International Water
Conference, p.172.
3. Turvey, T., Kuchinski, J. 2006. IWC-06-01 “The Legionella shift:
how the risk from Legionnaires’ disease has changed the perception
of water treatment.” 67th Annual International Water Conference.
4. Howard, K., Winters, M. 2002. “A performance-driven approach
to cooling water treatment evaluation.” 63rd International Water
Conference, p. 629–635.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
July 2007
ASHRAE Journal
129
PCM Technology for
Building Materials
M
ost buildings in the developed world use conventional
building materials: bricks and mortar, concrete (with
or without steel reinforcement), or steel or wood
framing for structural components, insulation to reduce space
conditioning loads, and plaster or gypsum wallboard ﬁ nishing
indoors.
Although insulation can enable the different wall types to
have similar insulating values (thermal conductivity), the ther-
mal mass of walls, i.e., the quantity of heat it takes to increase
or decrease the temperature, can vary greatly. For example,
a concrete wall has much more thermal mass than a framed
wood wall per unit volume. In buildings, thermal mass reduces
temperature swings, which increases occupant comfort and can
reduce cooling energy consumption and peak cooling loads in
some climates.
For around 30 years, researchers have investigated ways to
increase the thermal mass of building structures by incorporat-
ing phase-change materials (PCMs) into building materials.
The latent heat required to cause the material to change phase
is much larger than the speciﬁ c heat of the material, e.g., the
latent heat capacity of wallboard with 30% PCM by weight is
about ﬁ ve times greater than the heat uptake of conventional
wallboard for a 10°F (5.5°C) temperature change. Consequently,
the PCM effectively increases the thermal mass of the building
material when temperatures rise above or below the transition
temperature of the PCM. IEA (2005) provides data for key
performance attributes of commercially available PCMs.1
PCMs studied for building materials include organic materi-
als, such as parafﬁ ns, and salt hydrates.1,2,3,4 Although much
effort has focused on wallboard imbibed with PCM, ﬂ oor tiles,
ceiling tiles, particle board, foam insulation board, plaster, radi-
ant barriers, cellulose insulation, solar blinds, and concrete con-
taining PCM also have been investigated.1,3,4,5,6,7 IEA (2005)
summarizes several current commercial uses of PCMs.1
Most PCMs evaluated for use in building materials typically
change phase from solid to liquid phase at a temperature some-
where in the acceptable comfort range for building occupants,
i.e., from 70°F to 80°F (21°C to 27°C). Ideally, phase change
occurs at a precise temperature, but, in practice, most PCMs
change phase over a range of temperatures. For parafﬁ n-based
PCMs, the range of the transition temperature depends on
the purity of the materials, i.e., higher purity decreases the
range.1,2,9
Energy Savings Potential
Because of their high thermal mass, PCMs can achieve
substantial reductions in peak cooling loads on days when the
PCM-containing building components begin the day in the solid
phase. When the outdoor temperature rises and solar energy
strikes the building, heat ﬂ ows through the building and is
absorbed by the PCM, as are internal heat sources (e.g., lights,
ofﬁ ce equipment, people). Thus, until enough heat has ﬂ owed
into the building component to change the phase of the PCM,
the component will remain at the phase change temperature
(or temperature range) of the PCM. If the indoor and outdoor
temperatures decrease below the phase-change temperature
before the PCM has completely changed phase, heat will then
ﬂ ow out of the PCM. Thus, PCM-containing components can
mitigate indoor temperature swings, decrease cooling loads, and
strongly reduce or shift the peak load until later in the day.\*
The inﬂ uence of PCMs on building cooling and heating loads
varies greatly between building types, that is, they have a greater
impact in buildings of light construction such as frame buildings
or motor homes. Climate also has a strong inﬂ uence on PCM
energy savings, as moderate climates where the outdoor tem-
perature often passes through the PCM transition temperature
(particularly at night) will realize greater savings than climates
where temperatures during the cooling season remain predomi-
\* As with Thermal Energy Storage, shifting an appreciable portion of electric load to off-peak periods can realize signiﬁ cant primary energy savings for those hours because lower temperatures
increase AC efﬁ ciency; utility generators operating at night typically have higher electricity generation efﬁ ciencies than peaker plants; and electricity transmission and distribution losses are
usually lower. Limited data for California suggests that electricity generated off peak consumes approximately 20% less energy at the power plant that on-peak generation.11
By Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; Detlef Westphalen, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE;
and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
The following article was published in ASHRAE
Journal, July 2007. ©Copyright 2007 American
Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for
educational purposes only. This article may not
be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
130
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
J u l y 2 0 0 7
nantly above or below the transition temperature. Notably, PCM
achieves greater savings in moderate climates with large diurnal
temperature swings and lower summertime humidity levels, i.e.,
where nighttime ventilation can be used to cool and re-solidify
the PCM without creating moisture issues.
There have been limited evaluations of the energy-saving
potential of PCMs in building materials. One study carried out
simulations of PCM-containing building components. Simula-
tions for a Dayton, Ohio, climate of 12 in. (305 mm) concrete
building walls imbibed with 10% PCM (average 78°F [26°C]
transition temperature) found a 13% reduction in annual cooling
loads through the wall. Simulations of a steel roof with two 1
in. (25 mm) thick isoboards imbibed to 28% weight with the
same PCM could decrease annual cooling loads by ﬂ owing
through that building component by14%. Simulations of a
frame wall found that gypsum board imbibed with PCM (also
to 28%) reduced component annual cooling loads through the
wall by about 9%. A related simulation of whole house loads
found that the PCM-imbibed walls (again to 28%) reduced an-
nual cooling loads by about 4%. When using a night-ﬂ ushing
strategy, i.e., ventilating the indoor spaces with cooler outdoor
air, the reduction in the annual cooling load was much larger,
17%. In all cases, heating energy saving were much smaller
or negligible.6
Overall, available data suggest an initial estimate that PCMs
in building materials can reduce annual residential cooling
energy consumption by as much 10% and does not appreciably
reduce residential heating energy consumption. Similarly, a 10%
reduction for half of commercial buildings may be attainable.
Many commercial building have substantial building mass al-
ready and commercial buildings typically have larger internal
heat gains than residences. Assuming this level of energy use
reduction for cooling loads, national annual primary energy
savings (the total energy consumed by an end-user, including
the energy used in the generation and transmission of electricity)
of approximately 0.4 quad might be possible with PCM tech-
nology. Additional, more comprehensive analyses are needed
to better understand the energy-saving potential of building
materials with PCMs.
Market Factors
Building materials with PCM have positive attributes be-
yond energy savings that increase their market attractiveness.
As mentioned earlier, the thermal mass provided by the PCM
maintains the PCM-containing surfaces at an approximately
constant temperature until the phase change has completed.
This mitigates indoor temperature swings and, in turn, tends
to increase occupant comfort. In addition, PCMs can help to
reduce peak loads in some climates. Moreover, in some moder-
ate climates with low humidity levels, PCM-based materials
could have the potential to eliminate the need for mechanical
air conditioning. For example, simulations of PCM-imbibed
wallboard used in a living room in the San Francisco Bay area
found that wallboard PCM with nighttime ventilation could
maintain the indoor temperature within an acceptable comfort
range during a warmer period of the year without mechanical
cooling. Extracting the stored latent heat at night to resolidify
the PCM can be a challenge, however, due to limited convective
heat transfer between the walls and indoor airﬂ ows.4
Several factors have kept PCMs from being used in main-
stream building materials. Developers of building materials
with PCMs, such as parafﬁ n-based PCMs, have concerns about
ﬂ ammability. Notably, Kosny et al.3 note that building materi-
als “must demonstrate resistance to smoldering combustion
and pass other ﬂ ammability tests.” For this reason, building
materials parafﬁ n-based PCMs likely will need to incorporate
ﬁ re retardant to pass ﬁ re codes, particularly at higher PCM
concentrations (e.g., greater than 20%). Developers of a cellu-
lose-based insulation that incorporates up to 30% PCM report,
however, that the their material passed the ASTM smoldering
combustion tests in initial testing.
Cost is another major barrier. For example, one developer
estimated that parafﬁ n-based PCM with melting temperatures
near room temperatures cost approximately $2 per pound in
the late 1990s, which could result in a cost premium of $2,000
to $4,000 for a 2,000 ft2 (186 m2) residence.5 As oil prices
have increased dramatically since the time of these estimates,
presumably so have the costs of the PCM. The average U.S.
home with central AC in 2001 consumed about $250 worth of
electricity to power the AC.10 If materials with PCM achieve
a 15% (likely high-end) reduction of AC energy consumption,
this suggests a payback period on the order of several decades.11
If building materials with PCM can eliminate the need for me-
chanical cooling entirely, the economics would improve. This
does not mean, however, that they would be more cost-effective
than other approaches that could work in such climates, such
as swamp coolers or other forms of thermal mass.
Building materials with PCM ultimately will need to have
demonstrably similar long-term reliability and material proper-
ties, such as strength and ease of use and installation, to typical
building materials; otherwise, designers and contractors likely
would not use a new and unproven product. This includes the
stability of the PCM transition temperatures, e.g., the transition
temperature of one PCM increased by about 5°F (3°C) over a
10+ year period.2
References

Implementing Agreement on Energy Conservation through Energy
Storage, Final Report.
2. Kissock, J.K., et al. 1998. “Testing and simulation of phase change

(or http://intraweb.stockton.edu/eyos/energy\_studies/content/docs/
