A 17 m3/s (36 000 cfm) central HVAC unit, including a 175
kW (50 ton) condensing unit with five circuits and HCFC-22
as refrigerant, a 40 kW backup electrical heater, and a 256
kW (873,472 Btu/h) heat recovery brine-to-air coil allows
heating and cooling the outdoor fresh air prior entering the
supermarket. A small 2.2 kW (3 hp) pump circulates the
warm secondary fluid through the heat reclaim coil. The
integrated secondary loop refrigeration and HVAC system
has been monitored during a 12-month period to determine
its thermodynamic parameters and energy performances in
a Canadian cold climate.
Primary Refrigerant Cycles
Both low- and medium-temperature primary refrigerant loops
have operated with stable and normal thermodynamic cycles.
The average condensers’ subcooling (4.6°C [8.3°F]) proves
that the primary refrigerant charges and secondary fluid flow
rates were correctly designed and balanced. The evaporating
temperatures of the low- and medium-temperature refrigerant
loops respectively averaged –33°C (–27°F) (Figure 3), and
–10°C (17°F). It can be seen that the average suction tempera-
tures (–19°C [–2.2°F]) of low-temperature rack were higher
than designed (28.9°C [–20°F]), but no excessive discharge
temperatures have been observed.
The compressors’ suction pressures were stable year-round,
but their discharge pressures and temperatures excessively
increased up to 2100 kPa, r (304 psig) only during a few hot
summer days (Figure 4). This situation caused frequent unde-
sirable shutdowns of the unique reversible heat pump installed
on the low-temperature heat rejection loop when operating in
cooling mode.
Freezing and Refrigeration Loops
The average temperatures of the secondary fluid
(potassium formate) at the outlet of the low-temperature
evaporators were stable at –25.2°C (–13°F), while its
return temperatures were almost constant at about –21.6°C
(–6.9°F). The outlet average temperature approach of 7.8°C
(14°F) proves that the low-temperature evaporators were
slightly undersized. The temperatures of the secondary
fluid (35% propylene glycol/water) leaving the medium-
temperature evaporators were stable at –6°C (21.2°F), and
the average return temperatures were also constant at –2°C
(28.4°F) year-round (Figure 5).
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
48
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 7
Heat Rejection/Heat Recovery Loops
The temperatures of the low-temperature warm secondary
fluid were high enough during the cold weather to provide
efficient heat recovery with heat pumps. These temperatures
have never dropped below 25°C (77°F) during the winter
(Figure 6), and all heat pumps have operated in heating mode
with coefficients of performance (COPs) as high as 4.6, while
the outdoor temperatures dropped to –25°C (–13°F). As a
comparison, at such low outdoor temperatures, a conventional
air-to-air heat pump will operate with COPs of about 1.2.
Consequently, it will be totally inefficient as a heating device.
However, during a few days in summer, the low-temperature
heat rejection loop had attained more than 40°C (104°F) because
of high discharge pressures. Consequently, the reversible heat
pump operating in cooling mode had frequently stopped on its
high pressure limit. This suggests that it would be appropriate
to avoid using heat pumps in cooling mode, even connected on
the low-temperature rejection loop.
The temperatures of the medium-temperature warm second-
ary fluid were kept high enough in the winter to provide efficient
heat recovery. These temperatures have frequently exceeded
30°C (86°F) between January and March, providing useful
waste heat for makeup air preheating with the central HVAC
unit brine-to-air heat exchanger (Figure 7). However, without
artificially raising the condensing temperatures/pressures, less
energy savings are possible in cold climates with such a direct
heat recovery heat exchanger. In this application, no waste heat
was used to preheat domestic hot water.
Power Demand
The supermarket daily average power demand varied between
a peak of 800 kW (July 2004) and 610 kW, with an average
of 96 W/m2/yr (versus sales area) (Figure 8). Compared to
two Canadian improved multiplex refrigeration systems,5 this
performance represents a specific power demand reduction of
14% and 20% respectively. The refrigeration system (including
low- and medium-temperature compressors, secondary fluid
circulating pumps and liquid coolers fans) but excluding all
display cases and cold rooms, assumed 28.9% (or 27.8 W/m2)
from the store annual average power demand.
On the other hand, the low- and medium-temperature refrig-
eration compressors only assumed together about 11% of the
supermarket annual power demand. During typical very cold
winter days, the low-temperature compressor rack has operated at
about 55% of its maximum power input rated at 114 kW (Figure
9), while in summer, this percentage increased to about 70%.

Entering Heat Recovery Heat Pumps
At the same time, the medium-temperature compressors have
run at about 40% in winter and at 80% during the hottest summer
day (Figure 10) of their maximum rated power input (165 kW).
Energy Consumptions
The supermarket annual electrical energy consumption
amounted 5.8 MWh/yr (or 841 kWh/m2/yr versus the store
total sales area) with a peak of approximately 550,000 kWh in
July because of increasing refrigerating and air-conditioning
demands (Figure 11). In the Canadian conventional direct
expansion (multiplex) systems, the specific supermarkets’
consumptions (versus total sales areas) generally exceed 1000
kWh/m2/yr. From this store’s total annual energy consumption,
13.3% was assumed by the low-temperature (freezing) zone and
16.9% by the medium-temperature (refrigeration) zone. These
consumptions include all refrigeration compressors, secondary
fluid pumps and liquid cooler fans.
The heat recovery heat pumps (compressors, fans, backup
electrical heating coils and their secondary fluid pumps) (Table
1) assumed 2.6% of the supermarket annual energy consump-
tion, while the central HVAC unit (compressors, associated
secondary fluid pumps and backup electrical heater) consumed
6% (Figure 12).
The electrical ovens, basket fryers and two water heaters al-
together assumed 2.3% of the total store’s energy consumption.
A percentage of 59.8% of the store’s total annual energy con-
sumption (including display cases and freezing rooms, lighting,
supplementary heating and cooling devices, and ice maker) was
not measured. The low- and medium-temperature refrigeration

zones (excluding all display cases, lighting, auxiliary heating
and cooling devices and the ice maker) consumed 1.8 GWh/yr
or 30.2% of the supermarket annual energy consumption.
This percentage is comparable to the energy consumption of
Canadian conventional multiplex refrigeration systems where
the refrigeration compressors and condensers fans generally
assume between 35% to 40% of the total supermarket annual
energy consumption. From this amount of energy, the low- and
medium-temperature refrigeration compressors only assumed
together 57.7%.
Because of the large number of store parameters, it’s generally
difficult, if not impossible, to compare the energy consumptions
of two or more supermarkets. However, as an example, it can
be noted that the specific energy consumption of the second-
ary fluid supermarket (841 kWh/m2/year) was approximately
8% lower compared to the specific energy consumption of a
Canadian improved multiplex system that used a cascade heat
recovery method with desuperheating coils and refrigerant-to-
air heat pumps.5
By using heat pumps, this improved system has eliminated
the traditional direct heat recovery from the superheated dis-
charge gases, as well as the the need for natural gas or oil as
backup energies for space heating. Such energy savings may be
attributed to the close coupling of the compressor racks to the
condensers and evaporators, resulting in increased efficiency
of thermodynamic compression cycles. The more efficient
and shorter defrosting cycles, higher average evaporating
temperatures, proper insulation of the building and secondary
fluid piping, and more efficient lighting also have probably
contributed to improving the supermarket’s annual specific
energy performance.
Primary Refrigerant Charge
The total charge of the primary refrigerant on both refrigera-
tion and freezing zones was of 454 kg (1,000 lbs) or 1.63 kg (3.6
lbs) per kW of compressors’ total rated power input. Compared
to multiplex baseline refrigeration systems where the refrigerant
charge averages 4.15 kg/kW,2 this charge represents a reduc-
tion of about 61%. However, because the system proved able
to operate at stable parameters, the primary refrigerant charge
may be further lowered (up to 70%), notably by sizing smaller
liquid receivers and refrigerant piping.6

Operating Experiences
Relatively few mechanical defaults and no major leakage
of primary refrigerants have been observed. Any leakage of
propylene and ethylene glycol brines, respectively, used as
secondary fluids in medium-temperature refrigeration loop and
the heat rejection loops, hasn’t been recorded. However, at the
level of several valve stations that ensure alternate distribution
of potassium formate to the low-temperature display cases for
freezing and defrosting, fluid leakages have been found dur-
ing the first two years of operation. The main cause was the
bad quality of original ball and solenoid valves chosen, and of
soldering materials. The piping contraction/dilatation cycles
corresponding to frequent changes, from freezing with a very
cold fluid to defrosting mode with a much warmer fluid, allowed
Advertisement formerly in this space.
September 2007
ASHRAE Journal
55
the brine to leak through the moving parts
of some ball valves. These small leakages
have corroded the nearby piping soldered
joints. This problem has been solved by
replacing the initial plumbing-type ball
valves with refrigeration-type ball valves.
With all new ball valves and soldering
materials, no additional potassium for-
mate leakage was discovered.
Second, about 20% of the potassium
formate solenoid valves have peri-
odically generated electrical sparks be-
cause of the humidity freezing between
their coils and pilots. The electrical
sparks have damaged the valves’ control
boards and probably pierced the valve
bodies, provoking small brine and/or
vapor leakages. The initial solenoid
valves, presenting such phenomenon
have been replaced with new models
and the problem was fixed. During the
first two years of operation, about 850
L (224 gallons) of potassium formate
have been added to the low-temperature
freezing loop. The total fluid cost aver-
aged $4080 per year ($4.8/L). Because
of potassium formate leakages and
frequent replacements of electronic
boards, the annual maintenance cost of
the completely secondary fluid refriger-
ation system averaged $21,500 per year
during the first two years. For instance,
this cost is similar to the maintenance
costs of conventional multiplex sys-
tems, but the system managers estimate
that, with less or without potassium
formate leakages, the annual main-
tenance cost may be lower compared
to the cost of traditional refrigeration
multiplex systems.
Greenhouse Gas Emissions
The concept of total equivalent warm-
ing impact (TEWI) combines direct CO2
emissions, due to refrigerant leakages,
and indirect emissions associated with
the production of energy. By assuming
an annual 5% to maximum 10% rate of
leakage for the completely secondary
fluid refrigeration system, and a regional
conversion factor (Quebec province) of
0.00122 kg CO2/kWh, a simple TEWI
analysis for a 20-year lifespan shows that
the secondary fluid refrigeration system
would achieve 79.6% in CO2 emission
reductions compared to equivalent base-
line multiplex systems with 20% annual
leakage rates and similar annual energy
consumptions.
Conclusions
The completely secondary loop
supermarket refrigeration concept is
distinguished by its simple configuration.
It uses standard refrigeration components,
is not difficult to operate and control, and
has less refrigeration valves and fittings
than most of conventional systems.
However, this concept represents a
significant technological change, so
trained refrigeration technicians and
operators are needed.
The total quantity of the primary
refrigerant (R-507) was reduced by
61% compared to baseline multiplex
refrigeration systems. By reducing
the liquid receivers’ capacities and
optimizing refrigeration piping
diameters and lengths, the primary
refrigerant charge can be further
reduced, up to 70% compared to
multiplex systems. The heat recovery
method with brine-to-air heat pumps
on the low-temperature rejection
loop provided efficient space heating
because of the heat pumps’ high
coefficients of performance (4.6) at
the operating conditions in a cold
climate winter. The back-up electrical
coil of the central HVAC unit has
never operated during the winter for
makeup outdoor air preheating, and
small quantities of heat were rejected
outdoors during the winter.
Both low- and medium-refrigera-
tion zones have operated with stable
parameters, while the thermodynamic
cycles of primary refrigerants were
normal according to thermodynamic
refrigeration rules. This performance
is attributable to the thermal inertia of
secondary fluids, to the correct adjust-
ment of primary refrigerant charges and
of secondary fluid flow rates, and to a
simple and efficient control strategy.
The main heat exchangers (evaporators,
condensers, subcoolers and desuper-
heaters) have been designed correctly
Advertisement formerly in this space.
56
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 7
www.info.hotims.com/12650-51
and have operated according to the
state-of-the-art of heat transfer.
Even though the new system includes
refrigerant-to-brine evaporators and
secondary fluid circulating pumps, and
thus, is subject to additional heat transfer
irreversibility and energy consumptions,
the specific annual energy consumption
of the new supermarket (841 kWh/m2/
year) seems to be in the same range of
magnitude and even lower compared
to Canadian conventional multiplex re-
frigeration systems with desuperheating
coils and fossil fuels as backup energy
sources.
The main components of the low- and
medium-temperature refrigeration zones
(compressors, secondary fluid pumps and
liquid coolers fans) have assumed 30.2%
of the supermarket’s total annual energy
consumption. Small leakages of freez-
ing secondary fluid (potassium formate)
have been caused by the poor quality the
initially selected ball and solenoid valves,
and of soldering materials. Two years
after the replacement of defective valves
and soldering materials, the potassium
formate leakage problem seems fixed.
However, a long-term survey will help us
to know if the problem was completely
solved or not.

About the Author
B
uilding owners seeking to create healthier work environments
or to obtain the U.S. Green Building Council’s (USGBC) Lead-
By Tom Hudson, Associate Member ASHRAE
This study is an example methodology
providing energy cost of office buildings
complying with the LEED credit, EQc2,
Increased Ventilation, that prescribes out-
door airflow rates that exceed ASHRAE
Standard 62.1-2004 minimum rates by
30%. Office buildings in Portland, Ore.,
Chicago, Phoenix, and Atlanta, are mod-
eled using eQUEST®, a freeware building
ership and Environmental Design (LEED)® certification often want
to know the effect of higher ventilation levels on energy use and
cost. Unfortunately, owner decisions are often based on opinions
rather than engineering analysis.
energy use analysis program designed to
estimate energy consumption of proposed
and existing buildings using an hour-by-
hour simulation procedure.
For LEED credit EQc2, the outdoor
air ventilation rates to the breathing zone
of all occupied spaces are increased by
at least 30% above the minimum rates
required by ANSI/ASHRAE Standard
62.1-2004, Ventilation for Acceptable
Indoor Air Quality. It has been shown
that increasing ventilation rates decreases
respiratory illness and associated sick
leave,1 reduces sick building syndrome
(SBS) symptoms,2 and improves pro-
ductivity.3 Lawrence Berkley National
Laboratory reported that almost all stud-
ies found that ventilation rates below 20
cfm (10 L/s–1) per person in all building
types were associated with statistically
significant worsening in one or more
health outcomes.4
Complying with LEED credit EQc2
won’t necessarily have a negative impact

Figure 1: Typical 25,000 ft2 (2322 m2) floor plan.
N
on the ability to achieve credits under EAc1. In the ANSI/
ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1 2004, Energy Standard for
Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings, modeling
protocol, Appendix G3.1.2.5 ventilation rates are considered
energy neutral between the proposed and base model. You may
find a net increase in energy savings over baseline when you
condition more air with better equipment.5
This is a modeling study, subject to all the limitations and
inadequacies inherent in using models to reflect real-world
conditions that are complex and considerably more varied
than can be fully represented in a single study. Nevertheless,
it is hoped that this example can contribute to understanding
the relationship between ventilation and energy consumption,
so that engineers will be able to provide owners with a valid
assessment of energy costs.
Office Building
The four-story office building selected for this discussion is
a multiple-zone variable air volume (VAV) system. Each floor
has a central air handler, with
an outdoor air economizer; a
variable-volume supply fan; and
throttling VAV boxes with hot
water reheat. Heating and reheat
are provided at the zone. A hot
water boiler and a centrifugal,
water-cooled chiller serve the
building.
The minimum outside air-
flow at the central air handler
is determined by the designer
(in accordance with the Ventila-
tion Rate Procedure in Standard
62.1-2004) and set by the testing,
adjusting and balancing (TAB)
contractor. Likewise, the TAB
contractor verifies that VAV
boxes actuate between a design maximum and minimum flow.
In heating mode, the VAV box discharge temperature shall reset
to a maximum heating setpoint at minimum design flow. Upon a
continued call for heat, the VAV box resets the zone airflow set-
point from minimum to maximum design heating flow setpoints.
In cooling mode, zone temperature is sensed and maintained at
the cooling setpoint by adjusting the VAV box primary airflow.
VAV box airflow is sensed and maintained at the setpoint by
adjusting the position of the VAV box damper.
The total building size is 100,000 ft2 (9290 m2) with each
25,000 ft2 (2322 m2) floor plan consisting of identical pro-
gramming (Figure 1). Each floor has eight spaces including
high-density conference rooms and low-density office spaces.
Each space is an HVAC zone with a thermostat controlling one
or more VAV boxes.
Modeling
The energy analysis of the example office buildings was
performed using accepted, standard engineering calculation
procedures and eQUEST, which is an interface to the DOE-2.2
energy simulation engine. DOE-2.2 is the latest privately sup-
ported extension of DOE-2. While DOE-2 is generally accepted
as the most accurate energy simulation program available, the
predicted energy consumption is not meant to be an absolute
prediction of the actual usage.
For this example, the main emphasis is on the associated
costs to increase ventilation rates 30% above the Standard
62.1-2004 baseline.
The wall and roof insulation, glazing properties, and HVAC
efficiencies conform to the baseline prescribed by Standard
90.1-2004. The economizer is controlled via a dry-bulb set-
point in accordance with the standard, which varies from 65°F
(18°C) in Atlanta to 75°F (24°C) in Portland and Phoenix. The
cost per kWh and cost per therm were gathered from the “EIA
2006 Commercial Sector Average Energy Costs by State.” The
cost per kWh varied by less than
a penny across the four locations
with an average cost of $0.074 per
kWh. The average cost of natural
gas was $1.22 per therm and var-
ied by as much as $0.15.
Zone Ventilation Calculations
Buildings and spaces comply-
ing with the LEED rating system
must meet the minimum require-
ments of Sections 4 through 7 of
Standard 62.1-2004. To comply
with EQc2, our design calcula-
tions begin by finding the base-
line ventilation rates required
by Standard 62.1-2004 and then
adjusting those ventilation rates
upwards by 30%.
The Ventilation Rate Procedure in Standard 62.1-2004 has
specific calculations for multizone systems6 and a spreadsheet
is available at www.ashrae.org/vrpspreadsheet. The zone and
system characteristics are entered into the spreadsheet and
ventilation rates are calculated at heating and cooling design
conditions. The worst-case or highest required intake airflow
may occur at the design cooling condition or design heating
condition and, therefore, it is necessary to check both. In a VAV
system, the highest required intake airflow typically occurs in
the cooling mode and this condition is reflected here.
Standard 62, Table 6.1 (not shown) prescribes minimum
people outdoor air rate (Rp) and minimum building area
outdoor-air rate (Ra). Each space consists of office area and
conference area. Both spaces require 5 cfm/person (2.4 L/s per

1. The required minimum breathing zone outdoor airflow, Vbz, is found by solving Equation 6-1 and multiplying the zone area and zone population by their respective outdoor air rates.
For the south conference room (Zone 2), Vbz = 5 x 44 + 1,000 x 0.06 = 282 cfm.
2. The zone air-distribution effectiveness (Ez) is selected based on air-distribution design and is Ez = 0.8 for heating and Ez = 1.0 for cooling.
3. The zone air-distribution effectiveness modifies the breathing-zone outdoor airflow by solving Equation 6-2 to determine the minimum zone outdoor airflow, Voz. In cooling mode, the
south conference room (Zone 2) remains unchanged Voz = 282 /1.0 = 282 cfm.
3-adj. To satisfy the requirements of LEED EQc2, the breathing-zone outdoor airflow rates are increased by 30%. The minimum zone outdoor airflow, Voz (Step 3), therefore, is increased
30%. In cooling mode, the new minimum zone outdoor airflow for the south conference room (Zone 2) becomes Voz = 282 x 130% = 367 cfm.
4. eQUEST was used to estimate the design cooling load and associated design flow rate or zone primary airflow (Vpz ).
5. The minimum primary airflow settings (Vpz-min) were chosen at 30% of design cooling airflow except where noted below.

6. The zone primary outdoor air fraction (Zp) is given by Equation 6-5 (Zp = Voz  / Vpz-min ). For the office area (Zone 1), Zp = 1,274/2,153 = 0.59. Standard 62 allows the designer to use
a default or calculated value for system ventilation effectiveness (Zp). Table 6-3 (not shown) specifies the use of Appendix A for zone primary outdoor air fractions above 0.55. Appendix
A is used herein. The table values for zone primary outdoor air fraction (Zp) are disregarded and the zone discharge outdoor air fraction (Zd ) is calculated for each zone. The zone
discharge outdoor air fraction (Zd) is found by Zd = Voz /Vdz. For VAV systems, Vdz is the minimum expected discharge airflow for design purposes (or Vpz-min). For this example Zp = Zd.
6. (cont) When VAV systems serve spaces with varying densities, the high-density ‘critical’ zone drives the fraction of outdoor air that must be sufficient across all zones. For this
example, the highest zone discharge outdoor air fraction (Zd) occurs in the north conference room (Zone 8). The conference room’s minimum damper position was increased from 30%
to 40%. This decreased the maximum Zd (Zone 8) from 0.94 to 0.70.
7. An occupant diversity of 75% was estimated over the building. The sum-of-peak populations was 969, and that actual peak system population was only 727.
8. The uncorrected outdoor air intake flow, Vou, is from Equation 6-6 (Vou = D x [sum(Rp x Pz) + sum(Ra x Az)]).
9. The system primary airflow (Vps) for cooling was taken based on a 70% load diversity factor (LDF). Vps = sum Vpz x 0.70 = 18,884 x 0.7 = 13,219.
10. The average outdoor-air fraction (Xs) is Xs = Vou  / Vps = 2,409/13,219 = 0.18.
11. For each zone, the zone ventilation effectiveness (Evz) is Equation A-1 (Evz = 1 + Xs – Zd).
12. The system ventilation efficiency (Ev) is the minimum zone value found above (Ev = minimum Evz).
13. Finally, the outdoor air intake flow (Vot) for the system is Equation 6-8, Vot = Vou / Ev = 2,409 / 0.64 = 3,762.
13-adj. The new outdoor air intake flow (Vot) for the system is 5,882 cfm.
person) and 0.06 cfm/ft2 (0.3 L/[s·m2]). For this example, the
architect has provided the intended programming and densities
for the spaces. The design zone population (Pz) for the confer-
ence rooms is 44 persons/1,000 ft2 (93 m2) and the office space
is at 7 persons/1,000 ft2 (93 m2).
The Table 1 zone calculations follow the Ventilation Rate
Procedure for Portland. The procedural Steps 1 through 3 are
common for all locations. The population density being con-
September 2007
ASHRAE Journal
61
stant, the differences in the ventilation
rates occur based on differences in design
cooling load and associated design flow
rate (Step 4). eQUEST was used to esti-
mate these values. The design flow values
varied from 0.58 cfm/ft2 (2.9 L/[s·m2])for
the interior office (zone 5) to 1.42 cfm/ft2
(7.2 L/[s·m2]) for the south conference
room (Zone 2).
When VAV systems serve spaces with
varying densities, the high-density criti-
cal zones drive the fraction of outdoor air
that must be sufficient across all zones.
For this floor plan, the high-density
conference rooms returned the highest
outdoor air fraction (Zd). The confer-
ence rooms drive the minimum damper
position at the air-handling unit and as-
sociated outdoor air intake flow (Vot). By
increasing the minimum position of the
critical zone’s VAV box, the critical zone
receives additional ventilation rates and
the damper position at the air-handling
unit is decreased.
For this floor plan both conference
rooms minimum damper position was
increased from 30% to 40%.
Table 1’s results show that the percent
outside air intake at the air-handling unit
can be decreased by increasing the mini-
mum airflow of the VAV box that serves
the critical zone. Other considerations
include air-distribution design, accept-
able diffuser air speed at the terminal unit,
the first cost of increasing the terminal
units’ capacity and/or duct size, and an
energy balance between increased airflow
versus increased ventilation. In the above
case, during times the conference room
is not occupied, increasing the minimum
airflow may cause cycling. Increasing
supply rates to an unoccupied space re-
quires the reheat coil to cycle on. Another
exercise beyond the scope of this article
is to solve, with eQUEST, the energy bal-
ance between increasing the critical zone
ventilation verses increasing the overall
outdoor air intake flow.
A more energy-efficient technique
is to use transfer air on critical zones.
Appendix A and the spreadsheet both
allow credit to be taken for devices such
as transfer fans or fan-powered mixing
boxes that transfer return air from other
zones to the critical zone. Depending on
the quality of the return air, this technique
can substantially reduce or even eliminate
the need for primary air to the zone.
Demand-controlled ventilation (DCV)
based on CO2 or other sensors should be
considered to reduce the minimum VAV
box flow when applicable.
Operations Summary Results
Table 2 lists the ventilation summary
and simulated runtimes of various modes
of operation. eQUEST’s estimates for
design flow rates showed increasing de-
sign flow in locations with higher cooling
degree days. Phoenix returned the largest
design flow. Adjusting ventilation rates
upwards by 30%, increased the ratio of
outside air to design flow by ~10% at
each location. As expected, locations
with more heating degree days experi-
enced more hours at minimum outside
air introduction. Portland and Chicago
experienced more hours at minimum out-
side air during heating mode than either
Atlanta or Phoenix. In Phoenix, 97.4%
of the hours that operated at minimum
ventilation were due to the outside air
dry-bulb limit being exceeded (see Note
2, Table 2).
For LEED credit EQc2, the outdoor air ventilation rates to
the breathing zone of all occupied spaces are increased
by at least 30% above the minimum rates required by
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2004, Ventilation for Ac-
ceptable Indoor Air Quality.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
62
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 7
Simulation Results
The energy use to comply with the
increased ventilation rates correlated to
the amount of hours the HVAC system
was operating at minimum outside air
ventilation and in heating mode. This
varied widely by location. Portland
showed the highest proportion of hours
(78%, Table 2), operating in heating mode
and at minimum outside air ventilation.
eQuest returned the highest proportional
increase in energy consumption for this
location. When the minimum OSA rate
was increased for the Portland location,
the building’s natural gas consumption
increased 50% (Table 3). The natural gas
consumption for all buildings is for the
hot water boiler serving VAV box reheat
coils at the zone level. In heating mode,
as outside air is introduced to the space,
the reheat coils maintain discharge air
temperature.
In Phoenix, the HVAC system operated
at the minimum outside air rate for more
than 60% of the operating hours. Of those
hours, only 2.7% (Table 2) were in heat-
ing mode. When the minimum outside
air was increased, eQuest returned a neg-
ligible increase in energy consumption.
The system was in cooling mode for the
majority of hours the system operated at
minimum outside air. While operating
in cooling mode, increased introduction
of OSA incurs a cooling penalty. The
majority of the supply air is recirculated
and conditioned to meet internal heat
gains and solar heat gain. The energy
required to meet the internal heat gains
and solar heat gain overshadow the energy
associated with outside air conditioning.
When multiple zones require cooling,
the quantity of airflow introduced to the
mechanical equipment is small.
The simulated cost to comply with
LEED EQc2 during all occupied hours
ranged from $2.94 per occupant per
year in Phoenix to $25.50 in Portland.
The largest impact was on the heating
system. The heating energy consump-
tion increased close to 45% and 48% in
Atlanta and Portland respectively.
Summary
The simulation program eQUEST was
used to simulate the energy cost of LEED
credit, EQc2, Increased Ventilation on
office buildings with VAV multiple-zone
systems. The credit requires minimum
outdoor airflow rates increased 30%
above Standard 62-2004 minimums. The
primary impact on energy consumption
was increased natural gas consumption
during the heating season. In cooling
dominated climates, the energy required
to meet the internal heat gains and solar
heat gain overshadow the energy associ-
ated with OSA conditioning.
The increase in heating energy to com-
ply with LEED EQc2 ranged from 4% in
Phoenix to 50% in Portland. On a per-
person basis, the additional cost ranged
from $2.94 per occupant in Phoenix to
$25.50 per occupant in Portland. The
cost to improve indoor air quality may
be insignificant based on documented
increases in productivity and decreased
sick days. It is important for engineers
to provide a valid cost estimate so that
owners can make an informed decision on
whether to seek the LEED credit.
The increase in energy consumption
may not be small and will increase site
energy use intensity (EUI) and associ-
ated green house gas emissions. Using
Appendix A in Standard 62 allowed a
more precise calculation of the required
outside air intake. Increasing the mini-
mum design flow or transfer return air
to the critical zone are options worthy of
further investigation and energy study.
Adjusting the distribution of air to the
critical zone has a dramatic effect on the
percent outside air required at the system
level. Using heat recovery ventilators to
decouple the ventilation system from the
heating and cooling system is also worth
considering.
The first cost to comply with this credit
may be negligible. In heating-dominated
climates, equipment may need to be
upsized or life-cycle assessments may
justify heat recovery equipment. In cool-
ing-dominated climates, the loads may
be sufficiently diversified not to affect the
total system sizing.

Jim Poulos, P.E., is a principal at Poulos Energy
Engineers in Atlanta. He is a member of ASHRAE’s
Guideline Project Committee 30, The Commission-
ing Process for Existing HVAC&R Systems and past
chair of ASHRAE Technical committee 7.8, Owning
and Operating Costs.
About the Author
Existing Building
Commissioning
T
 o fix chronic problems, reduce energy costs or meet require-
ments of the U.S. Green Building Council’s (USGBC) LEED-
EB® rating system, building owners increasingly are requesting
commissioning of HVAC systems in their existing buildings. Com-
missioning providers offer services termed retro-commissioning,
recommissioning, ongoing commissioning, and others. However,
these variations in terminology and the disparity in the scopes
and fees associated with the services are confusing. In addition,
some in the building industry question if commissioning existing
buildings produces long-lasting benefits.
ASHRAE formed Guideline Project
Committee 30, The Commissioning Pro-
cess for Existing HVAC&R Systems, to
develop a guideline for the existing building
commissioning (EBC) process. ASHRAE
defines commissioning as a process,
whereas many building owners and service
providers use these commissioning terms
to define their services. This article is not
intended as an introduction to the guide-
line nor the process. Instead, this article is
intended to address concerns about EBC
services, including clarifying definitions
of the most common commissioning terms,
using energy savings as justification for
such services, and determining persistence
of benefits and ranges of costs.
Definitions
To address the confusion of the various
uses of the term commissioning, ASHRAE
published definitions in Guideline 0-2005,
The Commissioning Process.1 The follow-
ing are definitions for several of the most
common commissioning terms as defined
in Guideline 0-2005.
By Jim Poulos, P.E., Member ASHRAE
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, September 2007. ©Copyright 2007 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
September 2007
ASHRAE Journal
67
Commissioning Process: A quality-focused process [that]
focuses upon verifying and documenting that the facility
and all of its systems and assemblies are planned, designed,
installed, tested, operated and maintained to meet the
Owner’s Project Requirements (OPR).
Continuous Commissioning Process: A continuation of
the Commissioning Process well into the Occupancy and
Operations Phase to verify that a project continues to meet
current and evolving OPR.
Recommissioning Process: An application of the Commis-
sioning Process requirements to a project that has been
delivered using the Commissioning Process.
Retro-commissioning Process: The Commissioning Pro-
cess applied to an existing facility that was not previously
commissioned.
Table 1 compares these four commissioning terms. It shows
that commissioning applies to new construction. The other three
terms apply to existing buildings. Ongoing commissioning
and recommissioning only apply to previously commissioned
buildings. Retro-commissioning applies to buildings that were
not previously commissioned.
Those in the building industry use these terms in different ways.
Some use recommissioning to mean commissioning a building to
original OPR whether the building was previously commissioned
or not. Likewise, some use retro-commissioning to mean com-
missioning a building to OPR that is developed for the current
use of the facility (not necessarily original design OPR), whether
the building was previously commissioned or not.
The definitions presented in Guideline 0-2005, if used consis-
tently by the building industry, should end this confusion. The real
issue is defining the OPR. A building that was originally com-
missioned has documentation detailing the project’s functional
requirements and expectations of the building’s use when first
designed and constructed. However, the building’s functional use,
operation and architectural layout change over time. Therefore,
commissioning an existing building necessitates that the OPR be
updated (or developed if not previously commissioned) for the
building’s current and anticipated future use and operation. This
is one of the most important aspects of existing building com-
missioning—defining the current facility requirements, which
may be very different than the original design’s OPR.
Using Energy Savings to Justify EBC
The majority of documentation on EBC justifies the cost of
commissioning services based on energy savings and the return
on investment from achieving those energy savings. The most cit-
ed non-energy reasons for performing EBC include maintenance
cost savings, extended equipment life, improved productivity,
increased system reliability and improved indoor environment.
However, few publicly available documents detail EBC projects
that do not spotlight the energy savings achieved.
In fact, guidelines developed by other entities usually em-
phasize the energy savings aspects of EBC.\* Some define EBC
in terms of energy savings or other terms typically associated
with energy-saving opportunities. Some examples are:
[A] process … with the goal of reducing waste, obtaining
energy savings and fixing existing problems.
A process that identifies low cost operating and maintenance
improvements in an existing building. (This source empha-
sizes a task in the process to “Revise estimated energy savings
calculations.”)
[Existing Building Commissioning] activities usually result
in low- and no- cost repairs and adjustments that can be ad-
dressed and maintained by facility operations staff.
The LEED-EB (Leadership in Energy and Environmental
Design–Existing Buildings) rating system developed by the
USGBC requires existing building commissioning as a pre-
requisite. The prerequisite includes the following requirement
for the EBC process:
All low-cost and no-cost measures must be implemented
in the first two years.2
The terms energy savings, low cost and no cost are usually
associated with a different service: energy audits. The ASHRAE
Handbook—HVAC Applications, Chapter 35: Energy Use and
Management, describes three levels of Energy Audits.3 The
definition of the Level I assessment states:
Level I analysis identifies and provides a savings and cost
analysis of low-cost/no-cost measures.
The shared use of the terms energy savings, low cost and no
cost led this author to investigate the similarities and differ-
ences between existing building commissioning services and
energy audit services.
Energy-savings opportunities presented through energy audits
can typically be sorted into three categories: no-cost, low-cost
and capital retrofit projects.
No-cost opportunities are energy saving measures that can be
implemented by in-house staff without the need to purchase
materials. Only in-house labor is needed. No-cost opportuni-
ties typically entail modifying existing programmed control
sequences.
Low cost opportunities are those measures than can be paid for
through the facility’s operating budget (as opposed to its capital
budget). The measures can usually be accomplished by in-house
staff and typically have simple paybacks of less than one year.
Capital retrofit projects entail significant capital outlay, usu-
ally requiring funds from the facility’s capital budget. These
projects are almost always implemented by outside contractors
and usually have simple paybacks greater than one year.
The definition for energy audits notes that this service usu-
ally includes:
Identifying low cost operating and maintenance improve-
ments;
Reducing waste, obtaining energy savings and fixing existing
problems; and
Low- and no-cost repairs and adjustments that can be ad-
dressed and maintained by facility operations staff.
These are the same terms used in the definitions for Existing
•
•
•
•
•
•
\*These entities use the various terms presented earlier in this article. For clarity, existing building
commissioning is substituted in place of terms used in those documents.

Table 1: Commissioning terminology.
Building Commissioning presented by
other entities in their guidelines as noted
above. Hence, the objectives of existing
building commissioning services and en-
ergy audits have some common character-
istics. Table 2 compares the most common
objectives of these two services.
This table shows that EBC services and
energy audits address similar issues. The
only differences evident in the table are
that EBC services concentrate on O&M
improvements and no-cost/low-cost
savings opportunities, while energy audits
concentrate on the savings opportunities,
including no-cost/low-cost opportunities
and capital retrofit projects.
Other differences are not shown in the
table. Most notably:
1. EBC is a process that is intended to
allow the facility and all of its systems
and assemblies to be operated and
maintained to meet the current facil-
ity requirements, whereas an energy
audit is a systematic study to identify
how energy is used in a building and to
identify energy-saving opportunities.
2. EBC is intended to see the work all the
way through implementation, whereas
an energy audit typically ends upon
submission of a report that details the
energy savings opportunities that were
analyzed.
3. EBC is intended to be an ongoing
process, whereas an energy audit is
Service
Operations and
Maintenance
Improvements
No-Cost and Low-
Cost Savings
Opportunities

Savings
Opportunities

Commissioning

Table 2: Existing building commissioning versus energy audit objectives.

70
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 7
typically performed once, unless con-
ditions change or a long period of time
has passed.
Although EBC and energy audits
are very different services, the com-
monality of the energy savings portion
of these services remains. To further
explore these commonalities, the author
compiled all of the energy-savings op-
portunities that were recommended to
a large international client through a
series of energy audits performed over
a one-year period. These opportunities
were categorized as either no-cost, low-
cost or capital retrofit projects based on
the definitions presented earlier in this
article. Figure 1 presents the results of
this compilation: 12% of the total energy
savings (in $) is attributable to no-cost
measures and 29% of the savings is at-
tributable to low-cost measures. This
graph demonstrates that 41% of the
energy cost savings identified by energy
audits performed for one client’s facili-
ties were due to the same types of oppor-
tunities identified by EBC services.
Another demonstration of the similari-
ties between these services is a compari-
son of the energy-savings opportunities
most often cited in EBC reports and in
energy audit reports. Table 3 (Page 78)
compares the energy-savings opportuni-
ties listed in a document entitled “Retro-
commissioning’s Greatest Hits”4 (i.e.,
existing building commissioning) to a
compilation of the top 10 most common
energy saving measures identified in our
own energy audits.
This table shows that most of the
“greatest hits” directly align with the
top 10 energy-saving measures iden-
tified through energy audits. In both
cases, the most common savings oppor-
tunity is “scheduling HVAC systems”
(or the observation “equipment running
unnecessarily”). Other examples of
comparable techniques include con-
trolling outside airflows, mitigating
simultaneous heating and cooling and
other controls-related measures. In all
cases, savings can be achieved through
no-cost and low-cost implementation
measures.
This is not to say that the results of
these distinct types of services are the
same. EBC includes many aspects not
included in energy audits, including the
three cited earlier in this article:
Defining the current facility require-
ments and allowing the facility to be
operated and maintained to meet those
requirements;
Seeing work all the way through imple-
mentation; and
Ongoing monitoring.
Savings and Costs
The most cited non-energy reasons for
performing EBC include maintenance
cost savings, extended equipment life,
improved productivity, increased system
•
•
•
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
72
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 7
reliability and improved indoor environment. However, the
majority of publicly available documents that justify the cost
of EBC services use energy savings for that justification.
Table 4 (Page 78) presents summaries from a study that
used energy savings to justify EBC.5 This table shows sav-
ings ranging from $0.17 to $1.26 per square foot and simple
paybacks ranging from 4 to 24 months.
Another study, a meta-analysis of more
than 200 facilities, including schools,
lodging, higher education, laboratories,
hospitals, offices and retail, documented
savings ranging from $0.13 to $0.45
per square foot and an average simple
payback of 12 months.6
The savings and economic paybacks
presented in those documents include
energy savings only. They note that EBC
resulted in additional benefits such as increased system reli-
ability, improved indoor environment and reduced liability risk.
However, since it is difficult to place hard dollar savings on these
additional benefits, they are only mentioned subjectively.
The wide range of savings and implementation costs
shown through these studies demonstrates the diversity
of scopes of work associated with EBC in addition to the
inherent variation in energy savings opportunities available
in individual buildings.
Persistence of Benefits
One of the main concerns of building owners is the persis-
tence of the benefits resulting from EBC. Will the benefits last?
Or, will they degrade over time?
Obviously, the answer to this question
is dependent upon the level of ongoing
O&M processes and ongoing awareness
of the benefits of the commissioning
services previously provided. Once again,
most of the publicly available documenta-
tion on the persistence of benefits focuses
on energy savings. Statements regarding
persistence of savings in the studies cited
earlier include:
… it is clear that savings can manifest gradually and thus
analysts may underestimate savings if using data only from
the first post-commissioning year. … On the other hand, sav-
ings are also not permanent, and can erode as the building
falls back into disrepair …6; and
All the sites show increasing energy savings during years one
and two. … After the second year, the increasing savings trend
•
•
No-Cost
12%
Low-Cost
29%
Capital
Retrofit
59%
Figure 1: Energy audit savings sources.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
74
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 7
appears to flatten during year three,
then degrade in the fourth year.7
These statements point out two at-
tributes of the EBC process. First, the
benefits of the commissioning process oc-
cur over time. Therefore, the full amount
of savings may not be evident in the first
year after commissioning commences.
Second, the savings tend to peak in the
second or third year and begin to degrade
unless steps are taken to maintain the ben-
efits achieved through the commissioning
process. However, in all cases cited in
those studies, the economic payback for
the commissioning services was shorter
than the period of time that passed before
savings began to degrade.
A shining example of the importance of
ongoing awareness is from an ASHRAE
article and an ASHRAE seminar presenta-
tion made two years later.8,9 Together, they
demonstrate the need for awareness and
ongoing maintenance to ensure that the
benefits of the service persist.
The article demonstrated the energy
savings for a unique VAV control modi-
fication. The situation was:
Two air-handling units (AHU) serving
laboratories with 24-hour operation;
Each AHU provided 84,000 cfm (39
600 L/s) of 100% outside air;
Each of the supply fans were powered
by 100 hp (75 kW) motors and variable
speed drives; and
Zones included VAV boxes with reheat.
The existing AHUs provided varying
amounts of air based on space tem-
perature setpoints and pressure relations
between the labs and adjacent spaces.
Even when a lab was not being used, its
VAV box was controlled based on space
temperatures.
The energy-savings opportunity in-
cluded shutdown of the VAV boxes serv-
ing spaces that were not being used and
did not require airflow to maintain pres-
sure relations. This dramatically reduced
the supply fan, outside air conditioning
and reheat energy use. The first three
columns in Figure 2 show the electrical
consumption for the three years prior to
implementation of this control modifi-
cation. The next two columns show the
electrical consumption for the two years
after implementation of this control
•
•
•
•
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
76
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 7
modification. This figure shows that energy savings of 40%
was achieved and maintained for two years.
After two years, the client reorganized its maintenance
personnel and discontinued third-party monitoring of energy
use in the facility. These changes resulted in a loss of the
knowledge base in the maintenance and operation of these
systems. Furthermore, no one had responsibility for track-
ing the monitored energy use data or to do anything with
that information. As a research exercise, the engineer that
performed the original work asked the client for permission
to analyze the energy use of these systems since the reorga-
nization. The last two columns in Figure 2 show the results
of these analyses. The energy consumption of these systems
returned to levels that existed prior to implementation of the
energy-savings measures.
Further investigation revealed that the controls were bypassed
because the knowledge of the previous maintenance personnel
and the reasons for the controls sequence were not passed on
to the current staff. With the client’s permission, the control
modifications were re-enabled and the current maintenance
personnel trained in the systems operation. Short-term data
collection validated that the energy use of the system returned
to the earlier, lower-energy consumption profile.
This exercise demonstrates that benefits can persist, but only
if O&M processes are maintained and the knowledge base is
transferred through training and documentation.
Persistence of benefits is not limited to energy savings. The
fixes that are accomplished through the commissioning process
optimize system operation, comfort control and other indoor
environment conditions. Without ongoing awareness and
maintenance, these system optimizations degrade with time,
returning the systems and indoor environments to the conditions
that existed prior to implementation.
To ensure that the benefits of existing building commission-
ing persist, the building owner and maintenance personnel must

Table 3: Comparison of most common energy savings opportunities.
remain committed to an ongoing program
that keeps the systems, indoor environment
and personnel at optimal effectiveness and
efficiency. This program should include:
A champion who is vested with the re-
sponsibility for maintaining the ongoing
awareness and keeping the knowledge
base intact.
Continually updated documentation,
including drawings, O&M manuals,
equipment lists, system diagrams,
operation sequence and the EBC plan
documents. These documents should
be updated each time a change is made
within the building, the systems or their
operation.
Ongoing training of key personnel,
including the building manager and
all maintenance personnel. This same
training should be provided to new
personnel as they come on board. If
some of the maintenance is performed
by outside contractors, include them in
the training program.
Defined and scheduled O&M pro-
cedures plus tracking of the work to
ensure it is accomplished satisfactorily
and as scheduled. In other words, avoid
deferred maintenance.
Documentation and tracking of the
benefits is vital, whether utility use
and costs, indoor temperature trends,
equipment downtime, tenant com-
plaints and/or maintenance and repair
costs. Tracking the ongoing benefits
detects potential issues before they
become a major problem and helps
tell the story that justifies the ac-
complishments of the maintenance
personnel.
Summary
Existing building commissioning
is a catchall phrase that encompasses
recommissioning, retro-commissioning,
ongoing commissioning and other terms
for applying the commissioning process
to existing buildings. It is meant to pro-
vide benefits other than energy savings,
including reduced maintenance costs,
improved system performance, equip-
ment reliability improvement, enhanced
indoor air quality and even reduced
liability risks. However, essentially all
of the published data on EBC services
•
•
•
•
•
(as opposed to the process) cite energy
savings as the justification for those com-
missioning services.
EBC can reduce energy costs through
the identification and implementation of
no-cost and low-cost energy saving op-
portunities. A side-by-side comparison
of actual commissioning savings to the
results from a series of energy audits
performed for one international client
shows that these no-cost and low-cost
opportunities can account for about
41% of the total, economically feasible
energy-savings opportunities in existing
facilities.
Finally, there is concern about the
persistence of benefits achieved through
EBC services. Documented results show
that benefits can be maintained as long as
the O&M processes, and awareness of
the benefits and consequences of these
activities are maintained.

ASHRAE Journal

September 2007
What do you do when you have an old building and the
walls aren’t doing their job? What do you do when the walls
look bad, leak and are falling apart? You give them a face-lift.
We’ve been doing this to buildings for centuries.
Old mass walls were typically built with really good cor-
ners, and really good openings, but not so good sections in
between. You need good corners or the building tends to fall
down. Holes in walls need to be built carefully. You can’t just
poke a hole in a wall and expect the wall not to be affected.
Builders learned this early on. Large square stones or rocks
(ashlar blocks or dressed stone) were used to make the “cor-
ner true and strong.” The corner stones were referred to as
“quoins.” Carefully cut stones were also used to line open-
ings. The tops of openings tended to be a big deal. Builders
learned to use arches to span openings; and arches were fin-
icky things that needed carefully cut stones. Arches turned out
to be important—but that story is for another day.\*
Face-Lift for Old Buildings
By Joseph W. Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., Fellow ASHRAE
Builders being builders, no matter what the century, cheap-
ened the rest of the wall when they could get away with it.
Roman and Greek owners were just as annoyed at their con-
tractors as some of us are at ours today. Of course, the other
side of the “quoin” was also true, Roman and Greek owners
didn’t want to spend much money either. Sound familiar? So
the sections in the field of the wall between quoins were typi-
cally constructed with “rubble.”
If you were rich like a king or a pope you could have the en-
tire wall built with carefully cut stones with tight joints. And,
if you were a really important king or a rich pope, you could
afford to have your wall many layers thick with each layer be-
ing constructed of carefully cut stones with tight joints with
every joint off-set horizontally and vertically (Photo 1). Alas,
kings and popes often tended to be cheap. Building multilayer
assemblies out of shaped rock with tight joints really “rocked.”
Too bad not much was built that way. Buildings constructed
that way lasted, and will last almost forever. Forever is a nifty
“useful service life.”
Photo 1 (left): Multi-layer Stone Wall. A stone wall many layers thick with each layer constructed of carefully cut stones with nice tight
joints with every joint offset horizontally and vertically. Photo 2 (center): Quoin Assemblies. Good stone corners and good stone lined
“punched” openings with rubble comprising the infill of the wall. Photo 3 (right): Traditional Stucco. A protective layer of white stucco
applied to the stone-rubble walls. The stucco was typically applied principally to the rubble infill between the quoins. Eventually, even the
quoins got stuccoed, and the original function of the quoin was lost. Quoins became aesthetic rather than functional.
\*Ah, why wait?…a dome is the ultimate expression of an arch and the greatest dome of them all was not the Astrodome, but the Hagia Sophia in Istanbul, Turkey. The Hagia Sophia is something special, 102 ft in diameter and 184 ft in
height. My Italian friends will argue this point, and claim that Brunelleschi’s Duomo in Florence is the greatest of them all at 147 ft in diameter, and 463 steps high—I should know, I climbed each and every one of them; but the Hagia
Sophia was constructed in 563, and the Duomo was finished in 1436 about a 1,000 years later—I have to give this one to the older guys, it took almost a thousand years to do them one better.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, September 2007. ©Copyright 2007 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and

or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
September 2007
ASHRAE Journal
81
Photo 4: In Europe, stucco has a wonderful reputation and is associated with addressing
problem buildings experiencing rain leakage. Over time most of Europe got “stuccoed.”
Sometimes builders gave their clients
a nice outer layer and a nice inner layer
and threw a lot of stuff (i.e., “rubble”)
in between. Even more often, clients
only got good stone corners and good
stone-lined “punched” openings with
rubble comprising the majority of the
wall (Photo 2).
Unfortunately,
these
composite
stone-rubble walls tended to leak, and
the leakage tended to make the wall look
bad. Over time, the wall tended to fall
apart. When water gets into something,
bad things happen. This was very bad
if the building was a castle. It was not
a good idea to annoy a king. It became
pretty common to stucco over castle
walls so that the stucco protected the
mortar in the joints in particular, and the
wall in general. The “White Tower” of
the Tower of London got its name when
Advertisement formerly in this space.
82
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
September 2007
Henry III had it stuccoed and whitewashed to keep the water
out. “White Castle” in Wales got its name from the protective
layer of white stucco applied to the stone-rubble walls. The
stucco was typically applied principally to the rubble infill
between the quoins (Photo 3). Eventually even the quoins
got stuccoed and the original function of the quoin was lost.
Quoins became aesthetic rather than functional.
From the Middle Ages onward, stucco became the repair
material of choice to address leaking walls and deteriorating
walls, regardless of whether the walls were stone, stone-rub-
ble, stone-brick or all brick or combinations between. Over
time, almost all of Europe “got stuccoed” (Photo 4).
In an ironic twist, stucco in North America has a horrible
reputation and is associated with rain leakage, corrosion, de-
cay and mold; whereas in Europe, stucco has a wonderful rep-
utation and is associated with addressing problem buildings
experiencing rain leakage.
How did this happen? In short, confusion between “mass
walls” and “drained walls.” Mass walls are “massive” and
control rainwater entry by shedding, absorption and redistri-
bution. They are constructed from water-resistant assemblies
such as masonry, concrete, mutli-wythe brick and stone, and
are typical construction in Europe.
Drained walls have multiple layers with drainage spaces and
drainage layers located behind an exterior screen or cladding.
They are typically wood frame or steel frame, have a drainage
plane, such as a building wrap (tar paper in the old days) behind
a brick layer, or a metal cladding or a wood-based siding. Wood
frame and steel frame assemblies are water sensitive. Drained
walls are typical construction in North America.
Bottom line, in North America we learned that drained clad-
dings are necessary over water-sensitive materials.\*\*
What is the significance of this? If you were to import a
mass wall approach from Europe and use it with water sensi-
tive materials, you could get into a world of hurt—and that
is what happened when EIFS (“exterior insulation finish sys-
tems” aka “synthetic stucco”) came to North America. The
EIFS industry took a successful European approach where
stucco was applied over foam insulation, that was attached to
masonry, concrete or stone that in North America was applied
to wood and steel frame walls covered with oriented stand
board (OSB) or gypsum board.
In Europe, if this “insulating stucco” leaked a little bit, the
water entered a masonry, concrete or stone assembly, which
\*\*Marcus Vitruvius Pollio, 27 B.C., figured it out earlier. He assumed that Roman concrete was water sensitive (an
amazing perspective unless you think in terms of centuries) and pointed out that the best way to protect walls was to
install a cladding over an airspace that drained. Check it out: The Ten Books on Architecture, De Architecutura, Book
II, Chapter VIII, Methods of Building Walls.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
September 2007
ASHRAE Journal
83
was not affected by the water in any meaningful way. In fact,
the insulating stucco worked just like the traditional European
stucco repair approach (only better) and reduced water entry
into the mass wall, while also increasing thermal resistance.
In North America, when the same insulating stucco was
installed over OSB or gypsum board and a little water entered,
the result was dramatically different. The water that got in was
not absorbed and redistributed without damage as in the Eu-
ropean mass wall approach. Water that got in could not get
out and since the materials that comprise the wall were water
sensitive, significant damage occurred. The stucco industry
suffered a pretty impressive black eye.
Of course, Vitruvius had the answer, drain the stucco,
drain the EIFS. In fact, drain all claddings. The EIFS industry
figured it out and now offers insulating stucco that drains in
North America (Figure 1). We are still working on “traditional
hard coat stucco” systems installed in North America—they
need to drain as well. Unfortunately, most traditional hard-
coat stucco systems installed in North America do not drain.
Stucco should have a drainage gap behind it when it is used
with water-sensitive materials.
What can we do today if we take the lessons—good and
bad—from Europe and North America and apply them to
today’s old buildings? You can’t get worse than the build-
ing in Photo 5—an uninsulated mass wall in Boston that
leaks rain, is cold, uncomfortable, and an energy pig. Photo

6 is what you can do if you have a great architect (Brun-
er Cott). Add overhangs, a pitched and vented roof, and a
“stucco screen” that would make both Vitruvius and Henry
III proud (Figure 2).
Be prepared to see a resurgence of stucco “over cladding”
in North America. The over-cladding stuccos need to be
drained—drained and insulated is even better. EIFS is com-
ing back—and coming back in a good way—and just in time,
because we have many old buildings that will need it if we
are serious about climate change, sustainability and energy
security.
Joseph W. Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., is a principal of Building
Science Consulting in Westford, Mass.

ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 7
Chilled Beam Cooling
M
ost U.S. commercial buildings cool spaces by deliv-
ering air that is cooled before it flows into different
spaces. In contrast, chilled beam systems use chilled
water pipes in modular units mounted to ceilings. They differ
from radiant chilled ceilings in that they transfer heat primarily
via convection instead of radiation. Similar to radiant chilled
ceilings, to date, chilled beams have been used primarily in
Europe and, to some extent, Australia.1,2,3 Chilled beams come
in two distinct architectures: passive and active.
Passive chilled beams consist of a cooling coil with fins and
housing that is suspended from the ceiling (Figure 1). Chilled
water passes through the coil at temperatures typically from
55°F to 63°F (13°C to 17°C),3,4,5 cooling the air around the
chilled beam and causing it to descend toward floor level.
Passive systems have design sensible cooling capacities of
approximately 5.6 W/ft2 to 6.5W/ft2 (60 W/m2 to 70 W/m2) of
ceiling area covered by chilled beam units.1
Active chilled beams, also known as induction diffusers,3
are more complex than passive chilled beams (Figure 2). In
addition to a finned cooling coil, they have an integral air sup-
ply designed to meet minimum outdoor air (OA) requirements
(e.g., ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-2001, Ventilation for Accept-
able Indoor Air Quality). In this way they differ from fan-coil
units, which blow indoor air over cooling coils located in the
conditioned space and rely upon a separate system to meet OA
requirements. The supply air passes through nozzles, induc-
ing additional airflow from the conditioned space through the
cooling coil and down to the conditioned space. Due to forced
convection, active chilled beams achieve cooling densities about
twice (e.g., 12 W/ft2 to 14.8 W/ft2 [130 W/m2 to 160 W/m2]1)
those of passive chilled beams. The low cooling density of
passive chilled beams limits their ability to cool many build-
ings in many U.S. climates. For this reason, we focus on active
chilled beam systems.
Although they use higher chilled water supply temperatures
than conventional chilled water systems, chilled beams require
attention to building and HVAC system design to avoid con-
densation of moisture on the chilled water supply pipes and
cooling coils. In most commercial buildings, the strategy for
avoiding condensation on chilled beams is straightforward:
manage indoor moisture levels such that the dew point of the
indoor air is lower than the chilled water temperature.1,3,5,6 In
most building types and climates, ventilation outdoor air (OA)
and infiltration are the main sources of humidity. Consequently,
chilled beam systems in most climates usually require dedicated
outdoor air systems (DOAS) and tight building envelopes to
manage humidity. The DOAS handles the OA humidity loads
prior to introduction to the space, with enough additional hu-
midity removed to cover internal moisture generation.
Chilled beams are often well-suited for laboratories in
applications where heat generated by laboratory and process
equipment drives cooling loads and supply airflow levels. In
such cases, using chilled beams to meet internal heat loads can
appreciably reduce (often halving) required supply airflows and
the associated energy consumption. In laboratories where safety
requirements govern minimum airflow requirements, however,
chilled beams cannot reduce supply airflow.3 Conversely, chilled
beams are not recommended for buildings with high indoor
latent loads, such as restaurants, health clubs or theaters.1,6
Energy Savings Potential
Chilled beams can save energy in several ways. First, they
deliver sensible cooling directly to spaces, decoupling maxi-
mum air delivery from the cooling load and reducing ventila-
tion fan energy consumption. With sensible cooling separated
from ventilation, OA can be provided as needed to satisfy OA
requirements, greatly reducing the ventilation energy consumed
to deliver cooling (e.g., 0.25 to 0.5 inches of water [62 Pa to 124
Pa] versus 3 to 8 inches of water [746 Pa to 1990 Pa] for central
air systems3). Second, the combination chilled beam and DOAS
can meet Standard 62-2001 ventilation requirements with less
ventilation airflow due to the inherent precision of the DOAS
in delivering required ventilation flows in the aggregate and to
the individual zones in the building. This reduces the quantity
of OA to be conditioned and, hence, the energy consumed to
condition the OA.6 Third, because chilled beams use higher
chilled water temperatures than conventional air-conditioning
systems (55°F to 63°F [13°C to 17°C] versus 39°F to 45°F
[4°C to 7°C]), a chiller dedicated for chilled beam cooling has
a lower temperature lift and operates at a 15% to 20% higher
efficiency than for an conventional system (for cooling loads
independent of ventilation).3,6 Fourth, the combination of
higher chilled water system temperatures and the fact that ac-
tive chilled beams entrain large quantities of room air greatly
reduces or eliminates the need for energy-consuming reheat
of the cooled air.3,5
By Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; John Dieckmann, Member ASHRAE;
Robert Zogg, Member ASHRAE; and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, September 2007. ©Copyright 2007 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
86
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 7
On the other hand, the lower supply airflow volumes limit
the extent of outdoor air economizing possible1 unless this
capability is designed in separately.3
There are limited data available for the energy savings po-
tential of active chilled beam systems. One comparison of the
energy performance of active chilled beams with a variable-
air-volume (VAV) system for an office building in cooling-
dominated Sydney, Australia, found similar energy performance
for the two systems. During summer months, the chilled beam
system consumed less energy than the VAV system, but it con-
sumed more during the moderate (average daily temperature
~54°F [12°C]) winter months because the reduced quantity
of supply air precluded extensive use of airside economizing.
Simulations of other buildings in Australia found that the en-
ergy impact varied appreciably depending on the specifics of a
given project, but illustrated that HVAC energy savings as large
as 10% to 20% per year can be achieved.1 This is similar to
another estimate for the average U.S. energy savings of radiant
ceilings and chilled beams deployed with DOAS. Extrapolated
to the population of commercial buildings in the U.S., chilled
beams could reduce commercial building energy consumption
by approximately 0.6 quad (0.64 EJ).6
Market Factors
Although relatively common in parts of Europe, chilled
beams have achieved limited market penetration in the U.S.
Thus, most contractors have not worked with chilled beams,
increasing both their first and installed costs. At present and
considered independently of other building systems, chilled
beam systems cost more than VAV systems, e.g., comparative
evaluations of active chilled beam and VAV systems in major
Australian projects found that chilled beam systems cost about
$8/ft2 ($80/m2).1 This does not, however, consider savings pos-
sible from reduced building height and space requirements for
air handlers afforded by the smaller supply air requirements
for active chilled beam systems. For example, chilled beams
can reduce per-story building height by 0.33 ft to more than
1.3 ft (0.1 m to 0.4 m)1,3 and the size of air-handling systems
in offices by 20% to 30%.3
On the other hand, chilled beam systems have limited heat-
ing capacities and often require a separate perimeter heating
system.5 This increases the total cost of the HVAC system. In
some buildings, envelope and interior cooling loads would
require a density of chilled beams that exceeds the manufac-
turers’ installation recommendations to avoid flow restrictions
that compromise system performance.1,3 Unless a supplemental
cooling system is added (at additional expense), this precludes
the use of chilled beams in these applications.
In some instances, architects and end users may not like the
aesthetics of chilled beams. Specifically, chilled beams usually
cover 50% or less of the ceiling, in which cases occupants can
see the building services near the ceiling.1 To overcome these
aesthetic challenges, integrated service beams have come to
market that incorporate additional services, such as lighting;
cabling; and conduits for power, voice and data services; open-
ings for sprinklers, smoke detectors, and public address system
speakers; lighting control sensors; and closed-circuit television
cameras.4,5 Alternatively, the ceiling and in-ceiling services are
sometimes painted black to reduce their visibility.1

2. Barista, D. 2005. “Chill the ceilings and achieve cool energy
savings.” Building Design and Construction 56(11). http://tinyurl.
com/38tp23 (or www.bdcnetwork.com/article/CA6285393.html).
3. Rumsey, P. and J. Weale. 2007. “Chilled beam in labs: eliminating
reheat & saving energy on a budget.” ASHRAE Journal 49(1):18–
25.
4. Farthing, T. 2006. “Why demand for the beam is gathering steam.”
Building Services and Environmental Engineering. 28(27). http://
tinyurl.com/334xk8 (or www.bsee.co.uk/news/fullstory.php/
aid/3900/Why\_demand\_for\_the\_beam\_is\_gathering\_steam.html).
5. Schultz, C.C. 2007. “Next-generation cooling is looking up.”
Engineered Systems. 24(5). http://tinyurl.com/2syuw9 (or www.
esmagazine.com/Articles/Feature\_Article/BNP\_GUID\_9-5-
2006\_A\_10000000000000099257).

pdfs/HVAC3-FinalReport.pdf).

About the Authors
Part
Two
The Wal-Mart
Experience
McKinney, Texas, Wal-Mart supercenter entrance vestibule showing sustainability objectives.
I
n 2005, Wal-Mart opened major experimental stores in McKinney,
Texas, and Aurora, Colo. These two stores incorporate several ex-
This article focuses on the lighting
and renewable electricity generation ex-
periments. A related article by the same
authors in the September 2007 issue
develops a baseline and covers the HVAC
and refrigeration experiments.
Interior Lighting Systems
The typical Wal-Mart supercenter uses
standard two-lamp 4 ft T8 fluorescent
fixtures for sales area lighting, which are
By Michael Deru, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE, and Michael MacDonald, Member ASHRAE
Michael Deru, Ph.D., is a senior engineer at
the National Renewable Energy Laboratory in
Golden, Colo. Michael MacDonald is a staff
member at Oak Ridge National Laboratory in
Oak Ridge, Tenn.
The National Renewable Energy Labo-
ratory (NREL) is monitoring the Colorado
stores, and the Oak Ridge National Labo-
ratory (ORNL) is monitoring the Texas
stores. The construction and evaluation of
these stores are part of a commitment to
sustainability by Wal-Mart. The evaluation
also includes monitoring typical Wal-Mart
supercenters for a reference case in close
proximity to the experimental stores.
In Texas, the experimental store and
the reference comparison store are in
McKinney, north of Dallas. The Colorado
stores are in the Denver metro area, with
the experimental store in Aurora and the
reference store in Centennial.
periments using recycled materials and energy-saving technologies.
Over the three-year period from 2006 – 2008, the performance of
the experiments will be evaluated and lessons learned generated.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, October 2007. ©Copyright 2007 American
Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational
purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without
